topics.title: Introduction to Analysis of Deformable Bodies: Types of External Loads - Normal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT402 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Analysis of Deformable Bodies: Types of External Loads - Normal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Deformable Bodies — The Formal Definition

In **Strength of Materials (Mechanics of Deformable Solids)**, a *deformable body* is a solid body whose shape and dimensions change when subjected to external forces. Unlike the assumption in rigid body statics, real engineering materials (steel, concrete, wood, polymers) experience **displacement, strain, and internal stress** when loads are applied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *A deformable body is a solid whose geometry changes under the action of external forces. Strength of Materials is the branch of mechanics that deals with the internal forces, stresses, strains, and deformations produced by these loads, while ensuring the body remains within safe (elastic) limits.*

### 1.2 Concept of External Loads

An **external load** is any force, moment, or couple applied to a body from outside its boundaries. Loads are broadly classified by:
- **Direction of action** (normal, transverse, eccentric)
- **Type of response they induce** (axial, shear, bending, torsion)
- **Time-dependence** (static, dynamic, impact, fatigue)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why We Classify Loads:** Each load type produces a *characteristic internal stress and deformation pattern*. The same physical material responds *differently* to a pull than to a twist. Classifying loads allows us to apply the correct governing equations for design.

### 1.3 Normal Load — The Central Concept of This Topic

A **Normal Load** (also called an **Axial Load** or **Concentrated Axial Force**) is a force whose line of action coincides with the **longitudinal centroidal axis** of the structural member.

$$P_{\text{normal}} \parallel L_{axis}$$

**Two Sub-Categories of Normal Load:**

| Type | Direction | Effect on Length | Symbol Convention |
|------|-----------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Tensile (T)** | Pulls outward, away from cross-section | Elongation ($\delta L > 0$) | Positive (+) |
| **Compressive (C)** | Pushes inward, toward cross-section | Shortening ($\delta L < 0$) | Negative (−) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Terminology Note:** When the question says *"axial pull"* or *"axial push,"* the examiner is testing whether the student correctly identifies this as a **Normal Load** and applies **Normal Stress / Normal Strain** formulas. Getting the sign convention wrong is a common 1–2 mark deduction.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Rubber Band & The Brick Column

> **Intuition Box — Real World Analogy**
>
> *Imagine you are stretching a rubber band between your two hands. Your fingers pull the band along its length → this is a **Tensile Normal Load**. The rubber band becomes thinner and longer.*
>
> *Now imagine a concrete column supporting a heavy roof slab. The roof pushes down on the column along its length → this is a **Compressive Normal Load**. The column becomes microscopically shorter and slightly thicker (Poisson effect).*
>
> *In both cases, the force acts along the **line joining the centers of the cross-sections** at the two ends. That alignment with the longitudinal axis is the defining feature of a "normal" load.*

### 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Curriculum

Normal loading is the **simplest and most fundamental loading case** in Module 1. It serves as the **building block** for:
- Composite bars and thermal stresses (Module 2)
- Principal stresses and Mohr's circle (Module 3)
- Columns and buckling (Module 4)
- Deflection of beams (Module 5)

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome Mapping (CO1):** *This topic directly maps to CO1 of PCSFT402: "Understand the concepts of stress, strain, and deformation in axial members."* Examiners frequently set 3-mark and 14-mark questions from this area every semester.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Canonical Types of External Loads

In Strength of Materials, all complex real-world loadings are decomposed into four basic types. Mastery of this classification is essential.

**A) Normal / Axial Load (this module's focus)**
- Force acts **along the centroidal longitudinal axis**.
- Produces **uniform normal stress** on transverse cross-sections.
- Causes **uniform elongation or shortening**.
- Examples: Tie rod in a truss, piston rod, chain link, hanging cable.

**B) Transverse / Shear Load**
- Force acts **perpendicular** to the longitudinal axis.
- Produces **shear stress** ($\tau$) on cross-sections.
- Examples: Riveted/bolted joints, punching of a hole, scissors cutting paper.

**C) Torsional Load**
- **Couple / Moment** acting about the **longitudinal axis**.
- Produces **torsional shear stress** ($\tau_{tors}$).
- Examples: Drive shaft of a vehicle, drilling bit, screwdriver action.

**D) Bending Load**
- **Transverse force OR couple** producing curvature in the member.
- Produces **flexural (bending) stress** that varies linearly across depth.
- Examples: Simply supported beam, diving board, floor joist.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real loading is a combination.** A cantilever bracket may experience **Normal + Shear + Bending simultaneously**. The principle of superposition (valid for linearly elastic, small-deformation problems) lets us analyze each component independently and add the results.

### 2.2 Internal Forces — The Method of Sections

To find internal stresses, we use the **Method of Sections**: an imaginary cut divides the body into two parts. For equilibrium, the internal force on the cut face must balance all external forces on the free body.

For a member under pure normal load $P$ at the ends:

$$N(x) = P \quad \text{(constant along length, assuming no intermediate loads)}$$

where $N(x)$ is the **internal normal (axial) force** at section $x$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Under a pure normal load applied at the centroid of the end cross-section, the internal force is **uniform** along the entire length of a prismatic bar. This is what allows us to write $\sigma = P/A$ as a constant stress.

### 2.3 Average Normal Stress

The **average normal stress** on a cross-section of area $A$ is defined as:

$$\sigma_{avg} = \frac{N}{A}$$

- Units: **N/m² (Pa)**, typically expressed in **kPa, MPa, or GPa**.
- $1 \text{ MPa} = 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2 = 1 \text{ N/mm}^2$
- $1 \text{ GPa} = 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$

**Sign Convention (universally followed in KTU exams):**
- **Tensile stress** $\sigma > 0$ (member is being stretched)
- **Compressive stress** $\sigma < 0$ (member is being compressed)

### 2.4 Average Normal Strain

The **average normal strain** along a member of original length $L$ that elongates by $\delta L$ is:

$$\varepsilon_{avg} = \frac{\delta L}{L}$$

- It is **dimensionless** (length/length).
- Often reported in $\mu\varepsilon$ (microstrain, $10^{-6}$) or mm/m.
- **Tensile strain** $\varepsilon > 0$; **Compressive strain** $\varepsilon < 0$.

### 2.5 Hooke's Law for Normal Loading

For most engineering materials within the elastic limit:

$$\boxed{\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon}$$

where **E** is the **Modulus of Elasticity** (Young's Modulus), a material property measured in **GPa**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hooke's Law is a one-dimensional statement.** It applies directly only when stress and strain are uniform and uniaxial — exactly the case for a member under a pure normal load.

### 2.6 Elongation of a Uniform Bar (Integration Form)

Combining the definitions:

$$\delta L = \int_0^L \frac{N(x)}{A(x) \cdot E(x)} \, dx$$

For a **prismatic bar** (constant $A$, constant $E$, constant $N$):

$$\delta L = \frac{NL}{AE}$$

This is the most tested formula in Module 1.

### 2.7 Saint-Venant's Principle (Essential for KTU 3-Mark Questions)

> *The stress distribution on a cross-section far from the point of application of a load is **uniform**, regardless of how the load is actually applied at the ends.*

- This means we can assume **uniform normal stress $\sigma = P/A$** in regions **away from the loaded ends**.
- Near the loaded end, stress concentrations occur (this is Module 2/3 territory).

### 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Topic 1 (Normal Loads)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | SI Units |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|----------|
| 1 | Internal Normal Force | $N = \sum F_{axial}$ | Sum of axial forces on free body | N |
| 2 | Average Normal Stress | $\sigma = \frac{N}{A}$ | $N$ = axial force, $A$ = cross-section area | Pa (N/m²) |
| 3 | Average Normal Strain | $\varepsilon = \frac{\delta L}{L}$ | $\delta L$ = change in length, $L$ = original length | dimensionless |
| 4 | Hooke's Law | $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ | $E$ = Young's Modulus | Pa |
| 5 | Elongation (prismatic bar) | $\delta L = \frac{NL}{AE}$ | $L$ = length, $A$ = area, $E$ = modulus | m |
| 6 | Elongation (non-prismatic, in segments) | $\delta L = \sum \frac{N_i L_i}{A_i E_i}$ | $i$ indexes each segment | m |
| 7 | Poisson's Ratio (related) | $\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lat}}{\varepsilon_{axial}}$ | Lateral to axial strain ratio | dimensionless |
| 8 | Volumetric strain (small) | $e = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$ | Sum of normal strains | dimensionless |
| 9 | Stress–Strain energy density | $u = \frac{1}{2}\sigma\varepsilon$ | Strain energy per unit volume | J/m³ |
| 10 | Axial stiffness | $k = \frac{AE}{L}$ | Resistance to elongation | N/m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Formula (5) $\delta L = NL/AE$ is the single most important equation in Module 1. It directly gives deflection, which controls serviceability limits in design. The 14-mark KTU problems in this section almost always end with a numerical evaluation of $\delta L$ using (5).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Average Normal Stress $\sigma_{avg} = N/A$

We start from the most fundamental principle: **Newton's Third Law (Equilibrium of a Free Body).**

**Step 1 — Consider a bar under axial load P:**

Apply an external tensile force $P$ at the centroid of the right end of a bar of cross-section $A$.

**Step 2 — Make an imaginary cut at section $x$:**

This isolates the right-hand portion. The internal force exposed on the cut face is $N(x)$, acting along the axis (positive to the right, tensile).

**Step 3 — Apply equilibrium of the right portion ($\sum F_x = 0$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum F_x &= 0 \\
N(x) - P &= 0 \\
N(x) &= P
\end{aligned}
$$

The internal axial force equals the applied load (tension positive).

**Step 4 — Define average normal stress as force per unit area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{avg} &\equiv \frac{\text{Internal axial force}}{\text{Area of cross-section}} \\
\sigma_{avg} &= \frac{N(x)}{A} = \frac{P}{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic of the step:** Stress is an *intensive* measure (force intensity) that represents the average force transmitted per unit area of an imaginary internal plane. The bar is assumed to be prismatic, so $A$ is constant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "average"?** Real stress distribution on a cross-section can be non-uniform (e.g., near a hole or a load point). The "average" form $P/A$ is a *global* measure valid far from load introduction points (Saint-Venant's Principle).

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Average Normal Strain $\varepsilon_{avg} = \delta L / L$

**Step 1 — Define deformation:**

Let $L$ = original (undeformed) length of the bar, and $L' = L + \delta L$ = length after the load is applied.

For a tensile load, the bar elongates, so $\delta L > 0$.

**Step 2 — Define normal strain as a normalized deformation:**

Strain measures the *relative* change in length, so it is dimensionless and independent of the bar's absolute size.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{avg} &\equiv \frac{\text{Change in length}}{\text{Original length}} \\
\varepsilon_{avg} &= \frac{L' - L}{L} = \frac{\delta L}{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify units:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
[\varepsilon] &= \frac{[\delta L]}{[L]} = \frac{\text{m}}{\text{m}} = \text{dimensionless}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sign convention (CRITICAL for KTU marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon > 0 &\Leftrightarrow \text{Tension (elongation)} \\
\varepsilon < 0 &\Leftrightarrow \text{Compression (shortening)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forgetting the sign convention** is the #1 cause of "minus" mark deductions in KTU board papers. Always write: *"Since the load is tensile, $\varepsilon$ is positive"* before substituting.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Hooke's Law for Axial Loading: $\sigma = E\varepsilon$

**Step 1 — Empirical observation:**

In 1678, Robert Hooke observed that for many materials (steel, aluminum, copper, concrete within limits) the elongation $\delta L$ is **directly proportional** to the applied load $P$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta L &\propto P \\
\delta L &= C \cdot P
\end{aligned}
$$

where $C$ is a constant depending on geometry and material.

**Step 2 — Substitute the expressions for $\sigma$ and $\varepsilon$:**

Using $\delta L = \varepsilon L$ and $P = \sigma A$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon L &= C \cdot \sigma A \\
\sigma &= \frac{L}{C \cdot A} \cdot \varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the proportionality constant as $E$:**

Let $E = \frac{L}{C \cdot A}$. The constant $E$ has units of stress (Pa) and is a **material property** (Young's Modulus).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= E \cdot \varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify that the formula is dimensionally consistent:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
[\sigma] &= [E][\varepsilon] = (\text{Pa})(\text{dimensionless}) = \text{Pa} \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Express in terms of the given geometry:**

Substitute $\sigma = P/A$ and $\varepsilon = \delta L / L$ into $\sigma = E\varepsilon$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P}{A} &= E \cdot \frac{\delta L}{L} \\
\delta L &= \frac{PL}{AE}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the central working equation for KTU numerical problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning of $E$:** $E$ is the slope of the linear (elastic) portion of the stress–strain diagram. A high $E$ means a stiff material (small $\delta L$ for a given load). For example, $E_{steel} \approx 200 \text{ GPa}$ while $E_{rubber} \approx 0.01 \text{ GPa}$.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical — The KTU "Type Problem" for Normal Load

> **[KTU University Exam Pattern]**
> *A mild steel bar 2.5 m long, 40 mm wide, and 25 mm thick, is subjected to an axial tensile load of 180 kN. Determine:*
> *(a) The normal stress induced in the bar.*
> *(b) The total elongation of the bar.*
> *(c) The longitudinal strain in the bar.*
> *Take $E = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$ for mild steel.*

**Solution (Exhaustive, KTU Step-Mark Style):**

**Step 1 — Identify the data and convert to consistent units (N and mm):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 2.5 \text{ m} = 2500 \text{ mm} \\
b &= 40 \text{ mm},\; t = 25 \text{ mm} \\
P &= 180 \text{ kN} = 180 \times 10^3 \text{ N} \\
E &= 2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the cross-sectional area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= b \times t = 40 \times 25 = 1000 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the definition of average normal stress (Part a):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \frac{P}{A} = \frac{180 \times 10^3 \text{ N}}{1000 \text{ mm}^2} \\
\sigma &= 180 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 180 \text{ MPa (Tensile)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute longitudinal strain (Part c) using Hooke's Law:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{180 \text{ N/mm}^2}{2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2} \\
\varepsilon &= 9 \times 10^{-4} \quad (\text{dimensionless})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute total elongation (Part b):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta L &= \varepsilon \times L = (9 \times 10^{-4}) \times 2500 \text{ mm} \\
\delta L &= 2.25 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification using the direct formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta L &= \frac{PL}{AE} = \frac{(180 \times 10^3)(2500)}{(1000)(2 \times 10^5)} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^8}{2 \times 10^8} \\
\delta L &= 2.25 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Pattern Recognition:** Notice how the solution **always starts with unit conversion to N and mm** when working with $E$ in N/mm². Forgetting this is the #1 reason students get a "wrong answer, full marks" but still lose 1 mark for not showing the conversion step. Always write the data in a "Given" box at the top.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Map — Classification of External Loads

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[External Loads on Deformable Body] --> startB[Normal Axial Load]
    startA --> startC[Transverse Shear Load]
    startA --> startD[Torsional Load]
    startA --> startE[Bending Load]
    startB --> startF[Tensile Elongation]
    startB --> startG[Compressive Shortening]
    startF --> startH[Stress sigma = P over A positive]
    startG --> startI[Stress sigma = P over A negative]
    startH --> startJ[Strain epsilon = delta L over L positive]
    startI --> startK[Strain epsilon = delta L over L negative]
    startH --> startL[Hookes Law sigma equals E epsilon]
    startI --> startL
    startC --> startM[Shear Stress tau equals V over A]
    startD --> startN[Torsional Stress tau equals T r over J]
    startE --> startO[Fiber Bending Stress sigma equals M y over I]
    startL --> finalX[Elongation delta L equals P L over A E]
    startM --> finalY[Shear Deformation]
    startN --> finalZ[Twist Angle phi]
    startO --> finalW[Bending Curvature]
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** The flowchart shows the hierarchy from general *external load* to specific *deformation result*. The path highlighted through the central column is the **normal-load path** (Section 1 of the syllabus). All other paths become relevant in later modules.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Normal-Load Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Geometry L, A] --> procA
    inputB[Material E] --> procA
    inputC[Applied Load P] --> procA
    procA[Step 1 Internal Force N equals P] --> procB
    procA --> procC
    procB[Step 2 Average Stress sigma equals N over A] --> procD
    procC[Step 2b Lateral Strain via Poissons Ratio] --> procE
    procD[Step 3 Hookes Law sigma equals E epsilon] --> procF[Step 4 Strain epsilon equals sigma over E]
    procF --> procG[Step 5 Elongation delta L equals epsilon L]
    procE --> procH[Lateral Contraction nu epsilon L]
    procG --> outputA[Deformation Result]
    procH --> outputA
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** This is a *signal-flow* style diagram of the calculation pipeline. Every KTU problem on normal loading traverses this exact path. Memorizing the order **N → σ → ε → δL** is the key to scoring.

---

### 4.3 Schematic Description — Prismatic Bar Under Axial Tension

> *(Text-based representation in lieu of a physical drawing — students are expected to reproduce this in exams.)*
>
> **Components shown in the standard KTU diagram:**
>
> 1. **The bar:** Drawn as a horizontal rectangle. Dimensions labeled: length $L$, cross-section dimensions (e.g., $b \times t$ or diameter $d$).
> 2. **The supports/load arrows:** At the left end, an arrow pointing **leftward** (or simply a hatched wall) represents the reaction. At the right end, an arrow pointing **rightward** represents the applied tensile force $P$.
> 3. **The cut section:** A vertical dashed line drawn somewhere in the middle of the bar, labeled "Section $x-x$".
> 4. **Internal force on the cut:** On the right-hand free body, an arrow $P$ is shown acting **rightward** at the right end, and an internal force $N = P$ shown acting **leftward** on the cut face.
> 5. **Deformation:** A second, longer dotted outline shows the elongated bar, with $\delta L$ marked at the right end.
>
> **Always label:** arrow directions, $P$, $L$, $A$, $\delta L$, and the section cut.

---

### 4.4 Free-Body Diagram of Cut Section (Internal Force Exposure)

> *(Mermaid block-schematic showing equilibrium of a cut portion.)*
>
> ```
>  External Load P  → |======  N(x) (internal, on cut face)  |
>                     |======                                |  ←→ P
>                     |======                                |
>  Reaction ←        |======================================|
> ```
>
> **Caption:** The cut-face force $N(x)$ must equal the applied load $P$ for equilibrium. This is the basis of the entire method-of-sections approach.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the following terms: (i) Normal stress, (ii) Normal strain, (iii) Young's Modulus. State their SI units.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — distribute as 1 mark per definition):**

> **(i) Normal Stress:** The intensity of internal axial force per unit area acting on a plane perpendicular to the longitudinal axis. Mathematically, $\sigma = N/A$. **Unit: N/m² (Pa)**.
>
> **(ii) Normal Strain:** The ratio of change in length (deformation) to the original length of the member. $\varepsilon = \delta L / L$. **Unit: dimensionless**.
>
> **(iii) Young's Modulus (E):** The slope of the stress–strain curve in the linearly elastic region; a material constant representing stiffness under uniaxial loading. $\sigma = E\varepsilon$. **Unit: N/m² (Pa), commonly GPa**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State and explain Saint-Venant's Principle. Why is it important in the analysis of normal loading?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Statement:** *"The stress distribution on a cross-section sufficiently far from the point of application of a load is essentially uniform, regardless of the exact manner in which the load is applied at the boundary."*
>
> **Explanation:** When a tensile load $P$ is applied at the end of a bar, near the loaded end the stress is non-uniform (concentrated near the load application point). However, at a distance greater than approximately the largest cross-sectional dimension, the stress becomes uniformly distributed as $\sigma = P/A$.
>
> **Importance:** It permits us to assume a uniform normal stress distribution across the cross-section, simplifying the analysis of prismatic bars subjected to axial (normal) loads. Without this principle, the stress would be mathematically intractable for almost all practical problems.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. The student must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full.

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** *With neat sketches, classify the different types of external loads that can act on a deformable body. Explain the "Normal Load" case in detail, including the sign convention and the resulting internal stress distribution.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Classification of loads with sketches:**

A deformable body can be subjected to four canonical types of external loads:

| Load Type | Sketch Description | Internal Effect |
|-----------|---------------------|-----------------|
| **Normal (Axial)** | Force along longitudinal axis, arrows pointing outward at both ends (tension) | Uniform $\sigma$ over cross-section |
| **Shear (Transverse)** | Force perpendicular to axis | Shear stress $\tau$ on cross-section |
| **Torsion** | Couple about longitudinal axis (curved arrows) | Torsional shear stress $\tau$ |
| **Bending** | Transverse force OR couple causing curvature | Linearly varying flexural stress |

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Detailed description of Normal Load:**

A normal (axial) load is one whose line of action passes through the centroids of all cross-sections of the member. This guarantees the force produces *only* axial deformation (no bending, no twisting). It is called "normal" because the internal force on the cross-section is **perpendicular (normal)** to that cross-section's plane.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Sign convention:**

- **Tensile:** $\sigma > 0$, $\varepsilon > 0$, $\delta L > 0$
- **Compressive:** $\sigma < 0$, $\varepsilon < 0$, $\delta L < 0$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Internal stress distribution:**

By Saint-Venant's Principle, far from the loaded ends the stress is uniformly distributed: $\sigma = P/A$. The internal axial force at any section equals the applied load: $N(x) = P$.

---

**(b)** *A copper rod of length 1.5 m and circular cross-section of diameter 30 mm is subjected to a tensile axial load of 60 kN. Find:*
*(i) the normal stress,*
*(ii) the longitudinal strain, and*
*(iii) the total elongation.*
*Take $E_{Cu} = 100 \text{ GPa}$.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data (1 mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 1.5 \text{ m} = 1500 \text{ mm} \\
d &= 30 \text{ mm} \\
P &= 60 \text{ kN} = 60 \times 10^3 \text{ N} \\
E &= 100 \text{ GPa} = 100 \times 10^3 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (30)^2 = 225\pi \\
A &= 706.86 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Normal Stress (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \frac{P}{A} = \frac{60 \times 10^3}{706.86} \\
\sigma &= 84.88 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 84.88 \text{ MPa (Tensile)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Longitudinal Strain using Hooke's Law (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{84.88}{10^5} \\
\varepsilon &= 8.488 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Elongation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta L &= \varepsilon \cdot L = (8.488 \times 10^{-4}) \times 1500 \text{ mm} \\
\delta L &= 1.2732 \text{ mm} \\
\delta L &\approx 1.27 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification using direct formula (extra credit, not required):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta L &= \frac{PL}{AE} = \frac{(60 \times 10^3)(1500)}{(706.86)(10^5)} \\
\delta L &= \frac{9 \times 10^7}{7.0686 \times 10^7} \approx 1.27 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** *Define Hooke's Law. With the help of a labeled stress–strain diagram for a ductile material (mild steel), identify the salient points: Proportional Limit, Elastic Limit, Yield Point, Ultimate Stress, and Fracture Point. Write the relationship between elastic constants.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Definition of Hooke's Law:**

> *"Within the elastic limit, stress is directly proportional to strain."* $\sigma = E\varepsilon$, where $E$ is Young's Modulus.

**Step 2 (3 marks) — Stress–Strain Diagram Description (label in the sketch):**

| Region | Point / Zone | Description |
|--------|--------------|-------------|
| OA | **Proportional Limit** | Linear $\sigma$-$\varepsilon$ relation; Hooke's Law holds strictly |
| AB | **Elastic Limit** | Maximum stress up to which the body returns to original shape on unloading |
| BC | **Yield Point** | Sudden elongation without increase in load; upper yield point A*, lower B* |
| CD | **Strain-Hardening Region** | Plastic deformation with increasing stress |
| D | **Ultimate Stress** ($\sigma_u$) | Maximum stress the material can withstand |
| E | **Fracture / Rupture Point** | Material finally breaks |

> **Sketch Note:** A typical KTU diagram has the curve starting at origin, rising linearly to A, then curving slightly to B, then a small horizontal step (yield), then rising to D, then descending to E at the break.

**Step 3 (3 marks) — Relationship Between Elastic Constants:**

The three principal elastic constants are:
- $E$ — Young's Modulus (longitudinal)
- $G$ — Modulus of Rigidity (shear)
- $\nu$ — Poisson's Ratio (lateral/axial strain)
- $K$ — Bulk Modulus (volumetric)

The three fundamental relationships are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= 2G(1 + \nu) \\
E &= 3K(1 - 2\nu) \\
G &= \frac{3K(1 - 2\nu)}{2(1 + \nu)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Important limits (1 mark):**
- $-1 < \nu < 0.5$ for stable isotropic materials
- For steel: $\nu \approx 0.3$, $E/G \approx 2.6$

---

**(b)** *A stepped bar is made of two segments. Segment 1 (steel): length 1.2 m, area 600 mm². Segment 2 (brass): length 0.8 m, area 400 mm². The bar is subjected to an axial tensile load of 90 kN. Find the total elongation of the bar. Take $E_s = 200$ GPa, $E_b = 100$ GPa.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1 &= 1.2 \text{ m} = 1200 \text{ mm},\; A_1 = 600 \text{ mm}^2,\; E_1 = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
L_2 &= 0.8 \text{ m} = 800 \text{ mm},\; A_2 = 400 \text{ mm}^2,\; E_2 = 1 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
P &= 90 \text{ kN} = 90 \times 10^3 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Internal force in each segment (1 mark):**

Since the load is purely axial and applied at the ends (no intermediate forces), the internal axial force is the same in both segments:
$$N_1 = N_2 = P = 90 \times 10^3 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Elongation of Segment 1 (steel) (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta L_1 &= \frac{N_1 L_1}{A_1 E_1} = \frac{(90 \times 10^3)(1200)}{(600)(2 \times 10^5)} \\
\delta L_1 &= \frac{1.08 \times 10^8}{1.2 \times 10^8} = 0.90 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Elongation of Segment 2 (brass) (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta L_2 &= \frac{N_2 L_2}{A_2 E_2} = \frac{(90 \times 10^3)(800)}{(400)(1 \times 10^5)} \\
\delta L_2 &= \frac{7.2 \times 10^7}{4.0 \times 10^7} = 1.80 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Elongation (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta L &= \delta L_1 + \delta L_2 = 0.90 + 1.80 \\
\delta L &= 2.70 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on Normal-Load Problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–1 to –2 marks):** Mixing m and mm in the same calculation. *Always convert all lengths to mm and forces to N before plugging into $E$ in N/mm².*
> 2. **Forgetting the Sign (–1 mark):** Compressive loads must yield a negative $\sigma$, $\varepsilon$, and $\delta L$. Examiners check this explicitly.
> 3. **Missing "Given" Box (–0.5 mark):** Always list all data with units at the start of the solution.
> 4. **Saint-Venant's Principle Omission (–1 mark):** When asked why $\sigma = P/A$ is valid, students often skip explaining that it's valid only **away from the load application point**.
> 5. **Cross-section calculation for circular bars (–1 mark):** Forgetting $A = \pi d^2/4$ for circular sections.
> 6. **In Part (a) of a 14-mark question, students often write only a definition (1 mark) and not a labeled diagram (3 marks).** Always include a labeled sketch in long-answer questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Normal Loads in Deformable Bodies**

- A **deformable body** changes shape under load; rigid body mechanics is not sufficient.
- A **Normal Load** is an axial force aligned with the centroidal axis of the member.
- **Tension** elongates; **Compression** shortens. Sign convention: tension positive, compression negative.
- **Internal axial force** $N(x)$ is found using the **Method of Sections** and equilibrium.
- **Average normal stress:** $\sigma = N/A$. Units: Pa (N/m²), commonly MPa.
- **Average normal strain:** $\varepsilon = \delta L / L$. Dimensionless.
- **Hooke's Law (within elastic limit):** $\sigma = E\varepsilon$. $E$ is a material property (Young's Modulus).
- **Elongation of a uniform prismatic bar:** $\delta L = PL / AE$.
- For **non-prismatic or multi-material bars:** $\delta L = \sum (N_i L_i) / (A_i E_i)$.
- **Saint-Venant's Principle:** Stress is uniform only **far from** the load application point.
- **Standard values to remember:**
    - $E_{steel} \approx 200 \text{ GPa}$
    - $E_{aluminum} \approx 70 \text{ GPa}$
    - $E_{copper} \approx 100 \text{ GPa}$
    - $\nu_{steel} \approx 0.3$
- **Stress-strain curve points:** Proportional Limit, Elastic Limit, Yield Point, Ultimate Stress, Fracture.
- **Most-tested formula in KTU 2024 Module 1:** $\delta L = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$ — practice 5+ numerical problems using this.
- **Always show unit conversion to N and mm** at the start of every problem.
- **Elastic constants relationship:** $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ and $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$.
- **Diagram requirements for full marks:** every long-answer solution must include a labeled free-body diagram, an internal-force diagram, and (if relevant) a stress-strain curve.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shear
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT402 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Shear

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shear Stress ($\tau$)** is the component of stress that acts *tangentially* (parallel) to the cross-section of a material. It is defined as the **tangential (shearing) force per unit area** of the surface on which it acts. Its SI unit is **N/m²** or **Pa**, and in engineering practice, it is commonly expressed in **MPa** or **GPa**.

Mathematically:

$$\tau = \frac{V}{A}$$

where:
- $V$ = Tangential (shear) force acting on the plane (in **N**)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area resisting the shear (in **m²** or **mm²**)

## 1.2 Shear Strain ($\gamma$) — The Companion Deformation

When a shear force acts on a body, it does not stretch or compress the material — it *skews* it. The angular distortion produced is called **Shear Strain** ($\gamma$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Shear Strain ($\gamma$)** is the change in angle (measured in **radians**) at a point originally formed by two perpendicular line segments in a stressed body. For small deformations, $\gamma \approx \tan(\gamma) \approx \gamma$ (in radians).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **deck of playing cards** resting on a table. If you push the top card horizontally while holding the bottom card fixed, every card slides slightly relative to the one below it.

- The *force* you apply is the **Shear Force ($V$)**.
- The *area* of each card face is the **Shear Area ($A$)**.
- The *tilt* of each card relative to the vertical is the **Shear Strain ($\gamma$)**.

Another classic analogy: the action of a **pair of scissors**. The blades apply a tangential (shear) cutting action on paper — not a pulling or pushing action perpendicular to the surface.

## 1.4 Complementary Shear Stress

A critical insight (introduced by **Augustin-Louis Cauchy**) is that shear stresses on perpendicular planes are **equal in magnitude and opposite (or same) in sense** — they exist in complementary pairs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Complementary Shear Stress Theorem:** If a shear stress $\tau_{xy}$ acts on one face of an elemental cube, an *equal* shear stress $\tau_{yx}$ must act on a perpendicular face to maintain rotational equilibrium of the element.

This is why pure shear produces **pure shear stress states** on rotated axes — a foundational concept for deriving principal stresses.

## 1.5 Modulus of Rigidity (Shear Modulus, $G$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Modulus of Rigidity ($G$)** is the constant of proportionality between shear stress and shear strain within the elastic limit. It measures a material's resistance to *shape distortion* (as opposed to volume change).

$$G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma}$$

**Standard units:** **N/m²** (Pa) or **MPa**.

**Typical values of $G$:**
- Steel: ~**79.3 GPa**
- Aluminium: ~**26 GPa**
- Rubber: ~**0.0003 GPa**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

The mechanics of shear can be understood in 4 hierarchical steps:

**Step 1 — Force Application**
A tangential force $V$ is applied parallel to the surface of area $A$. Unlike normal stress, no stretching or compressing occurs along the line of force.

**Step 2 — Stress Generation**
The internal resistance mobilized per unit area gives the shear stress $\tau = V/A$. This stress acts *on* the plane and *along* the direction of the applied force.

**Step 3 — Deformation (Shear Strain)**
The originally right angle between two perpendicular edges of the element deforms by a small angle $\gamma$ (in radians). This angular change is the shear strain.

**Step 4 — Material Response (Hooke's Law in Shear)**
For linearly elastic materials within the proportional limit:
$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma$$

The constant $G$ (Modulus of Rigidity) is a fundamental **elastic constant** of the material.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following table is your **exam-ready reference** for any shear-related problem in KTU ESE and internal assessments.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Physical Meaning |
|---|----------|---------|------------|------------------|
| 1 | Shear Stress | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{A}$ | N/m² (Pa) | Tangential force per unit area |
| 2 | Shear Strain | $\gamma = \dfrac{\Delta x}{L} = \tan\theta \approx \theta$ | radians (dimensionless) | Angular distortion |
| 3 | Hooke's Law in Shear | $\tau = G\gamma$ | Pa | Linear elastic shear response |
| 4 | Modulus of Rigidity | $G = \dfrac{\tau}{\gamma}$ | Pa | Resistance to shape change |
| 5 | Shear Force from $G$ | $V = \dfrac{G \cdot \gamma \cdot A}{1}$ | N | Tangential force |
| 6 | Strain Energy Density (Shear) | $U = \dfrac{1}{2}\tau\gamma = \dfrac{\tau^{2}}{2G}$ | J/m³ | Energy stored per unit volume |
| 7 | Relation: $G$, $E$, $\nu$ | $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ | Pa | Inter-elastic constant relation |
| 8 | Complementary Shear | $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$ | Pa | Equality of orthogonal shears |
| 9 | Total Shear Force on Bolt | $V = \dfrac{P}{n}$ | N | $P$ = applied load, $n$ = number of shear planes |
| 10 | Shear Deformation | $\delta = \dfrac{VL}{AG}$ | m | Lateral displacement under shear |

**Key Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
- Shear stress is **zero** on *free surfaces* (traction-free).
- On a principal plane, the **shear stress is zero** and only normal stress exists.
- In a *pure shear* state, principal stresses are $\pm \tau$ on planes oriented at **45°**.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Riveted / Bolted Joints:** Shear governs the load-carrying capacity of bolts and rivets.
- **Punching & Shearing Operations:** Metal sheets are cut by exceeding the ultimate shear strength.
- **Shafts under Torsion:** Pure shear is the dominant stress in circular shafts transmitting torque.
- **Beam Webs:** Shear stress distribution in I-section webs is critical for design.
- **Adhesive Bonds & Welds:** Shear governs bond strength in structural adhesives and fillet welds.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Complementary Shear Stress Theorem

**Statement:** Shear stresses on mutually perpendicular planes of an elemental element are equal in magnitude.

**Consider** a small rectangular element of dimensions $dx \times dy \times t$ (thickness $t$). Apply a shear stress $\tau_{xy}$ on the face perpendicular to the $x$-axis.

**Forces acting on the element:**
- On face of area $dy \cdot t$: Force $= \tau_{xy} \cdot dy \cdot t$ (in $+y$ direction)
- On opposite face: Force $= \tau_{xy} \cdot dy \cdot t$ (in $-y$ direction)
- These form a **couple** with moment arm $dx$, producing a moment:
$$M_1 = \tau_{xy} \cdot dy \cdot t \cdot dx$$

This couple must be balanced by an equal and opposite couple from the shear stresses on the faces perpendicular to the $y$-axis.

**Equating moments for rotational equilibrium ($\Sigma M = 0$):**

$$\tau_{xy} \cdot dy \cdot t \cdot dx = \tau_{yx} \cdot dx \cdot t \cdot dy$$

**Cancelling common terms $dx \cdot dy \cdot t$:**

$$\boxed{\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This proves that shear stresses on perpendicular planes are **mutually complementary and equal**. The directions are such that they either both point *toward* the common edge or *away* from it.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Relation between $G$, $E$, and Poisson's Ratio $\nu$

This is one of the **most frequently asked derivations** in KTU exams.

**Consider** a square element of side $L$ subjected to pure shear $\tau$ on all four faces (as shown in the stress diagram).

**Step 1 — Identify the principal stresses**

In pure shear, the principal stresses are:
$$\sigma_1 = +\tau, \quad \sigma_2 = -\tau, \quad \sigma_3 = 0 \text{ (plane stress)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the diagonal strain using Hooke's Law (2D)**

The strain along a diagonal (say the direction making 45° with the $x$-axis) can be computed by resolving the principal strains:

$$\varepsilon_{45} = \frac{\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2}{2}\cos(90°)$$

Wait, let me use the strain transformation with angle $\theta = 45°$:

$$\varepsilon_{45} = \frac{\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\sin(2\theta)$$

With $\theta = 45°$, $\cos(90°) = 0$, $\sin(90°) = 1$:

$$\varepsilon_{45} = \frac{\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y}{2} + \frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Express strains in terms of stresses using generalized Hooke's Law**

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_x - \nu\sigma_y) = \frac{1}{E}(\tau - \nu(-\tau)) = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_y - \nu\sigma_x) = \frac{1}{E}(-\tau - \nu\tau) = \frac{-\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau}{G}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the diagonal strain expression**

$$\varepsilon_{45} = \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E} - \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}\right] + \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{\tau}{G}$$

$$\varepsilon_{45} = 0 + \frac{\tau}{2G} = \frac{\tau}{2G}$$

**Step 5 — Equate with direct geometric computation**

Geometrically, when the square shears by angle $\gamma$, the diagonal elongates by strain:
$$\varepsilon_{45} = \frac{\gamma}{2} = \frac{\tau}{2G}$$

For consistency with the same stress state, this must also equal the strain computed directly from principal stresses:

$$\varepsilon_{45} = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_{45} - \nu\sigma_{-45}\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[\tau - \nu(-\tau)\right] = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 6 — Equate the two expressions for $\varepsilon_{45}$**

$$\frac{\tau}{2G} = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $G$**

$$\boxed{G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **master relation** between three elastic constants. KTU examiners frequently give numerical problems where two of $\{E, G, \nu\}$ are known and the third must be found.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III: Shear Strain Energy per Unit Volume

The work done by a gradually applied shear stress $\tau$ in producing a shear strain $\gamma$ is stored as **strain energy** in the elastic body.

For a linearly elastic material ($\tau = G\gamma$), the energy per unit volume is the area under the $\tau$-$\gamma$ line:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \tau \cdot \gamma$$

Substituting $\gamma = \tau/G$:

$$\boxed{U = \frac{\tau^{2}}{2G} = \frac{1}{2}G\gamma^{2}}$$

This is directly analogous to $U = \sigma^{2}/(2E)$ for uniaxial tension.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Shear Stress System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Tangential Force V] --> B[Applied on Plane of Area A]
    B --> C[Shear Stress tau = V / A]
    C --> D[Internal Resistance of Material]
    D --> E[Shear Strain gamma appears]
    E --> F[Elastic Region: Hooke's Law]
    F --> G[tau = G times gamma]
    G --> H[Modulus of Rigidity G - material property]
    H --> I[Strain Energy Stored]
    I --> J[Energy Density = tau squared over 2G]
    C --> K[Complementary Plane]
    K --> L[tau xy = tau yx]
    L --> M[Rotational Equilibrium satisfied]
    C --> N[Principal Plane at 45 degrees]
    N --> O[Pure Normal Stress sigma = plus or minus tau]
    O --> P[Failure or Yield check]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Elastic Constant Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E[Young's Modulus E] --> R1[Relation 1: G equals E over 2 times 1 plus nu]
    NU[Poisson's Ratio nu] --> R1
    R1 --> G[Modulus of Rigidity G]

    E --> R2[Relation 2: K equals E over 3 times 1 minus 2 nu]
    NU --> R2
    R2 --> K[Bulk Modulus K]

    E --> R3[Relation 3: E equals 9 KG over 3K plus G]
    K --> R3
    G --> R3
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Element under Pure Shear

```
   ↑   τ_yx
   |←—————|
   |       |  →  τ_xy (tangential)
   |—————→|
   |  τ_yx ↓
       
   All four faces carry shear stress τ.
   The element distorts (skews) by angle γ.
   Diagonals: one elongates (+ε), one contracts (-ε).
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Deformation of Riveted/Bolted Joint

```
   Plate 1  →→→→  P (applied load)
   ======================== ← Shear Plane
   [BOLT]───τ = P/A_bolt──→
   ======================== ← Shear Plane
   Plate 2  →→→→  P

   The bolt resists the load through SHEAR at the
   interface plane. τ = P / (π·d²/4)
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Pattern]**
**Define shear stress and shear strain. State Hooke's law in shear.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Shear Stress ($\tau$):** It is the tangential (shearing) force per unit area acting on a plane in a body. Mathematically, $\tau = V/A$, where $V$ is the shearing force and $A$ is the area of the plane on which it acts. Unit: **N/m²** or **Pa**.

> **Shear Strain ($\gamma$):** It is the angular distortion (in radians) produced in a body due to shear stress. For small deformations, $\gamma \approx \tan(\gamma) \approx \Delta x / L$.

> **Hooke's Law in Shear:** Within the elastic limit, shear stress is directly proportional to shear strain:
> $$\tau \propto \gamma \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau = G\gamma$$
> where $G$ is the **Modulus of Rigidity**.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Pattern]**
**State the complementary shear stress theorem and explain its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Theorem:** If a shear stress acts on one plane of an element, an equal complementary shear stress must act on a plane perpendicular to it to maintain rotational equilibrium.
> $$\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$$

> **Significance:**
> - It implies that shear stresses **always occur in pairs** on orthogonal planes.
> - On a *free surface*, no shear can exist — hence complementary shear on adjacent internal planes must also be zero.
> - It forms the basis for **principal stress analysis** and the pure shear state on 45° planes.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Pattern: Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the relation $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ connecting the three elastic constants. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A circular bolt of diameter **20 mm** is used to connect two plates. A tensile force of **50 kN** is applied. Find:
   (i) The shear stress induced in the bolt.
   (ii) The shear strain, if the modulus of rigidity $G = 80$ GPa.
   (iii) The lateral deformation if the bolt length under shear is **30 mm**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$:**

*Step 1:* Consider a square element subjected to pure shear $\tau$. This produces principal stresses $\sigma_1 = +\tau$ and $\sigma_2 = -\tau$ on planes at 45°.

*Step 2:* The strain along the diagonal (45°) using principal stress formulation is:
$$\varepsilon_{45°} = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_1 - \nu\sigma_2) = \frac{1}{E}[\tau - \nu(-\tau)] = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

*Step 3:* Alternatively, the diagonal elongation due to pure shear can be geometrically expressed as half the shear strain:
$$\varepsilon_{45°} = \frac{\gamma}{2} = \frac{\tau}{2G}$$

*Step 4:* Equating the two expressions:
$$\frac{\tau}{2G} = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

$$\boxed{G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Diameter of bolt, $d = 20$ mm = $0.02$ m
- Tensile force, $P = 50$ kN = $50{,}000$ N
- $G = 80$ GPa = $80 \times 10^{9}$ N/m²
- Length under shear, $L = 30$ mm = $0.03$ m

**Step 1: Cross-sectional area of bolt**
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}d^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.02)^{2} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 2: Shear stress in bolt (single shear)**
$$\tau = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{50{,}000}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.5915 \times 10^{8} \text{ N/m}^{2}$$
$$\boxed{\tau \approx 159.15 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 3: Shear strain**
$$\gamma = \frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{1.5915 \times 10^{8}}{80 \times 10^{9}} = 1.989 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}$$
$$\boxed{\gamma \approx 1.989 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}}$$

**Step 4: Lateral deformation**
$$\delta = \gamma \cdot L = (1.989 \times 10^{-3})(0.03) = 5.968 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$$
$$\boxed{\delta \approx 0.0597 \text{ mm} = 59.7 \text{ μm}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – Pattern: Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Define the modulus of rigidity. A steel specimen has $E = 200$ GPa and $\nu = 0.3$. Find its modulus of rigidity and bulk modulus. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Prove that the shear stresses on two perpendicular planes of an elemental cube are complementary and equal. Show with a neat sketch. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Modulus of Rigidity and Bulk Modulus:**

*Definition:* The **Modulus of Rigidity ($G$)** is the ratio of shear stress to shear strain within the elastic limit, representing a material's resistance to shape distortion.
$$G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma}$$

*Given:* $E = 200$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$

**Step 1: Calculate $G$**
$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{200}{2(1+0.3)} = \frac{200}{2.6} = 76.92 \text{ GPa}$$
$$\boxed{G \approx 76.92 \text{ GPa}}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Bulk Modulus $K$**
$$K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)} = \frac{200}{3(1 - 0.6)} = \frac{200}{1.2} = 166.67 \text{ GPa}$$
$$\boxed{K \approx 166.67 \text{ GPa}}$$

---

**(b) Proof of Complementary Shear Stress Theorem:**

*Consider* a small elemental cube of sides $dx$, $dy$, and $dz$ (thickness $dz$ is into the page).

*Step 1:* Apply shear stress $\tau_{xy}$ on the face perpendicular to the $x$-axis. This produces a force $F_1 = \tau_{xy} \cdot dy \cdot dz$ on the top face.

*Step 2:* An equal and opposite force $F_1$ acts on the bottom face. These two forces form a **couple** with moment:
$$M_1 = F_1 \cdot dx = \tau_{xy} \cdot dx \cdot dy \cdot dz$$

*Step 3:* For rotational equilibrium, this couple must be balanced by a couple from the shear stresses $\tau_{yx}$ on the faces perpendicular to the $y$-axis. The opposing couple is:
$$M_2 = \tau_{yx} \cdot dx \cdot dz \cdot dy$$

*Step 4:* Setting $\Sigma M = 0$:
$$\tau_{xy} \cdot dx \cdot dy \cdot dz = \tau_{yx} \cdot dx \cdot dy \cdot dz$$
$$\boxed{\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}}$$

*Sketch:* (Elemental cube with four shear stresses, all equal, acting tangentially — two pointing toward the front edge, two pointing away on the opposite edge, or vice versa, satisfying rotational equilibrium.)

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Forgetting to convert mm² to m² (or GPa to Pa) before substituting into formulas. Always convert **all quantities to consistent SI units** *before* plugging values in.
>
> 2. **Single Shear vs. Double Shear Confusion:** A bolt in *single shear* has one shear plane; in *double shear* (e.g., a lap joint with a middle plate) it has two planes, halving the shear stress. Read the problem statement carefully.
>
> 3. **Sign Error in Complementary Shear Direction:** Students often draw the arrows of complementary shears *both* pointing inward or both outward. The correct configuration is such that on the **common edge**, both arrows either point *toward* it or *away* from it.
>
> 4. **Hooke's Law Application:** Hooke's law in shear ($\tau = G\gamma$) is valid **only within the elastic limit**. Beyond yield, $G$ is no longer a constant.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Sketch in Complementary Shear Proof:** Examiners *expect* a neat free-body diagram of the element. Skipping it costs at least 1–2 marks even if the math is perfect.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✍️

- ✅ **Shear stress** = tangential force per unit area: $\tau = V/A$ → units: **Pa** or **MPa**.
- ✅ **Shear strain** $\gamma$ is an **angular** quantity, measured in **radians** (dimensionless).
- ✅ **Hooke's Law in Shear:** $\tau = G\gamma$ — valid within the **proportional/elastic limit**.
- ✅ **Complementary shear theorem:** $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$ — essential for principal stress analysis.
- ✅ **Master relation:** $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ — the most-tested formula in shear questions.
- ✅ **Bulk modulus relation:** $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ — often paired with $G$ and $E$.
- ✅ **Strain energy density in shear:** $U = \dfrac{\tau^{2}}{2G}$ — used in impact and energy problems.
- ✅ **Pure shear** = principal stresses of $\pm\tau$ on 45° planes.
- ✅ **For typical steel:** $E \approx 200$ GPa, $G \approx 77$ GPa, $\nu \approx 0.3$, $K \approx 167$ GPa.
- ✅ **Conversion reminders:** $1 \text{ GPa} = 10^{9} \text{ Pa}$, $1 \text{ mm}^{2} = 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{2}$.
- ✅ **Always draw a free-body diagram** of the sheared element for complementary shear problems.
- ✅ **Single vs. double shear** must be identified *first* in bolted/riveted joint problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bending and Bearing Stress - Linear and Shear Strains. Hooke's Law - Stress-Strain Diagrams - Concepts of Isotropy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT402 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bending and Bearing Stress - Linear and Shear Strains. Hooke's Law - Stress-Strain Diagrams - Concepts of Isotropy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bending Stress (Flexural Stress)

### Formal Definition
**Bending Stress** (also called **Flexural Stress**) is the normal stress induced in a structural element when it is subjected to a moment (bending moment) that causes the element to bend. It acts parallel to the longitudinal axis of the member and varies linearly with distance from the neutral axis.

Mathematically, it is given by the **Flexure Formula**:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

where:
- $M$ = Bending Moment (N·m)
- $y$ = Distance from the Neutral Axis (m)
- $I$ = Second Moment of Area (Moment of Inertia) about the neutral axis (m⁴)
- $\sigma_b$ = Bending Stress (Pa or N/m²)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Bending stress is fundamentally derived from the assumption that plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the longitudinal axis after bending (**Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory**).

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine you place a wooden ruler on a table with one end sticking out and press down on the free end. The top fibers of the ruler get **compressed** (squeezed together) and the bottom fibers get **stretched** (pulled apart). The ruler bends. The internal stress that resists this bending — pushing back on the compressed top and pulling back on the stretched bottom — is the **bending stress**. It's nature's way of saying, "I won't let you deform me for free!"

---

## 1.2 Bearing Stress

### Formal Definition
**Bearing Stress** is the contact pressure generated between two bodies pressed against each other. It is the compressive stress that acts on the surface of contact when a load is transmitted from one member to another (e.g., a bolt bearing on a plate, a rivet bearing on a connecting plate, or a column resting on a foundation).

The standard formula is:

$$\sigma_{br} = \frac{P}{A_b}$$

where:
- $P$ = Applied compressive load (N)
- $A_b$ = Projected (nominal) bearing area (m²)
- $\sigma_{br}$ = Bearing Stress (Pa or N/m²)

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a heavy steel column standing on a concrete base. The column is pressing down on the concrete. The force is not concentrated at a single point but spreads over the **cross-sectional area** of the column in contact with the base. This distributed compressive pressure at the interface is bearing stress. If the contact area is too small, the concrete will **crush** (a classic bearing failure).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Bearing stress is essentially a **compressive contact stress**. It is not a "new" type of stress — it is a normal stress arising from compressive loads over contact areas (such as bolt holes, pin joints, and supports).

---

## 1.3 Linear Strain (Normal / Longitudinal Strain)

### Formal Definition
**Linear Strain** ($\varepsilon$) is the ratio of the change in length (deformation) of a body to its original length, due to the application of an axial (normal) load.

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0}$$

where:
- $\Delta L$ = Change in length (m)
- $L_0$ = Original length (m)
- $L_f$ = Final length (m)
- $\varepsilon$ = Linear strain (dimensionless, often expressed in mm/m or microstrain $\mu\varepsilon$)

> **Sign Convention:**
> - **Tensile strain** (elongation) → **Positive (+ve)**
> - **Compressive strain** (shortening) → **Negative (−ve)**

### Conceptual Analogy
Stretch a rubber band by 2 cm. If its original length was 20 cm, the linear strain is $2/20 = 0.1$ (or 10%). Strain is simply a measure of "how much something stretched, normalized by its original size" — so that a 1 cm stretch on a 1 m bar is correctly compared to a 1 cm stretch on a 10 m bar.

---

## 1.4 Shear Strain (Angular Strain)

### Formal Definition
**Shear Strain** ($\gamma$) is the change in angle (in radians) between two lines that were originally mutually perpendicular in an undeformed body, when the body is subjected to shear stress.

$$\gamma = \frac{\Delta x}{L} = \tan(\phi) \approx \phi \;\; (\text{for small deformations, in radians})$$

where:
- $\Delta x$ = Lateral displacement of the top face relative to the bottom face (m)
- $L$ = Perpendicular distance between the faces (m)
- $\phi$ = Angular distortion (radians)
- $\gamma$ = Shear strain (dimensionless, in radians)

> **Unit:** Although dimensionless, shear strain is expressed in **radians** since it represents an angle.

### Conceptual Analogy
Take a deck of playing cards and slide the top card sideways while holding the bottom card fixed. The originally rectangular side-view of the deck is now a **parallelogram**. The angle by which the rectangle has "tilted" is the shear strain. Shear strain is deformation by **angular distortion**, not by stretching or compressing.

---

## 1.5 Hooke's Law

### Formal Definition
**Hooke's Law** states that, within the **elastic limit**, the stress in a body is **directly proportional** to the strain producing it.

For normal stress and linear strain:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

For shear stress and shear strain:

$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma$$

where:
- $E$ = **Young's Modulus** (Modulus of Elasticity) — units: **Pa** or **N/m²**
- $G$ = **Modulus of Rigidity** (Shear Modulus) — units: **Pa** or **N/m²**

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a spring: if you pull it with twice the force, it stretches twice as far (within its elastic range). Hooke's Law is the material's "personality" — $E$ tells you how **stiff** a material is. Steel has a high $E$ (~200 GPa) — it doesn't stretch much. Rubber has a low $E$ (~0.01 GPa) — it stretches a lot.

> [!NOTE]
> Hooke's Law is **only valid in the linear elastic region** of the stress-strain diagram. Once the yield point is crossed, the proportionality breaks down.

---

## 1.6 Stress-Strain Diagram

### Formal Definition
A **Stress-Strain Diagram** is a graphical representation of the relationship between axial stress ($\sigma$) and axial strain ($\varepsilon$) for a material subjected to a uniaxial tensile (or compressive) test, typically up to failure. It is the most fundamental characterization of a material's mechanical behavior.

The diagram reveals several critical points and regions:
- **Proportional Limit (A)** — End of the linear region
- **Elastic Limit (B)** — Maximum stress up to which the body returns to its original shape on unloading
- **Yield Point (C)** — Stress at which material begins to deform plastically without any increase in load
- **Ultimate Stress Point (D)** — Maximum stress the material can withstand
- **Fracture / Rupture Point (E)** — Point at which the material breaks

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine pulling a toffee (candy). Initially, you feel strong resistance — small stretch, big pull (elastic region). Then, suddenly it gives way a little and stretches a lot with the same pull (yielding). It continues to stretch as you pull (plastic region) until finally, **snap!** — it breaks. The toffee's stress-strain curve would have told you exactly where each of these events happens.

---

## 1.7 Concept of Isotropy

### Formal Definition
A material is said to be **Isotropic** when its mechanical properties (such as Young's Modulus, Poisson's Ratio, Shear Modulus) are **the same in all directions** at a given point.

In contrast, an **Anisotropic** material has properties that **vary with direction** (e.g., wood, fiber-reinforced composites).

### Conceptual Analogy
A lump of steel is isotropic: a small cube cut from it behaves identically no matter how you orient it. A piece of wood is anisotropic: it's much stiffer along the grain than across it. A sheet of plywood with cross-laminated plies is also anisotropic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Most metals (steel, aluminum, copper) at room temperature can be treated as isotropic for engineering calculations, even though they are polycrystalline at the microscopic level. Materials like wood, composites, and single crystals are clearly anisotropic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theory of Bending Stress — Underlying Assumptions

The flexure formula $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ is derived under the following **assumptions** (must be memorized for KTU):

1. The beam material is **homogeneous** and **isotropic**.
2. The beam has a **constant cross-section** (prismatic) along its length.
3. The beam is initially **straight** and unstressed.
4. The applied loading is **pure bending** (constant bending moment, no shear force, no axial force, no torsion).
5. **Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the longitudinal axis** after bending (Euler-Bernoulli hypothesis — shear deformation is neglected).
6. The material obeys **Hooke's Law** (linear elastic behavior).
7. The stress does not exceed the **proportional limit**.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently tests these assumptions. A "Yes/No" or "True/False" question on these can fetch 3 marks easily.

### Section Modulus ($Z$)
For design convenience, we define:

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}}$$

where $y_{max}$ is the distance from the neutral axis to the **extreme fiber** (topmost or bottommost point of the cross-section). The maximum bending stress then becomes:

$$\sigma_{b,max} = \frac{M}{Z}$$

Units of $Z$: **m³** (or mm³ in practical design).

### Real-World Utility
- Designing **girders and beams** in bridges and buildings.
- Sizing **shafts** under combined bending and torsion.
- Designing **railway sleepers**, **cantilever balconies**, and **aircraft wings**.

---

## 2.2 Theory of Bearing Stress — Analysis

Bearing stress analysis follows two key steps:
1. Identify the **bearing surface** — the area over which the load is transmitted.
2. Apply $\sigma_{br} = P / A_b$ to compute the bearing pressure.

### Types of Bearing Surfaces
- **Bolt/Rivet in a hole** — projected area = (diameter of bolt) × (thickness of plate).
- **Pin in a knuckle joint** — projected area = (diameter of pin) × (thickness of the forked plate).
- **Column on a footing** — full cross-section of the column is the bearing area.

### Real-World Utility
- Sizing **anchor bolts** in foundations.
- Designing **riveted/bolted connections** in steel trusses.
- Checking the **crushing of concrete pads** under steel stanchions.

---

## 2.3 Hooke's Law — Poisson's Ratio and the Three Elastic Moduli

For a complete 3D elastic characterization, three elastic constants are needed:

| Constant | Symbol | Formula | Physical Meaning |
|----------|--------|---------|------------------|
| Young's Modulus (Modulus of Elasticity) | $E$ | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | Stiffness under axial loading |
| Shear Modulus (Modulus of Rigidity) | $G$ | $G = \dfrac{\tau}{\gamma}$ | Stiffness under shear loading |
| Bulk Modulus | $K$ | $K = \dfrac{-P}{\Delta V / V}$ | Resistance to uniform hydrostatic compression |

### Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$ or $\mu$)
When a bar is stretched in one direction, it contracts in the transverse direction. The ratio of lateral strain to axial strain is **Poisson's Ratio**:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{axial}}$$

> **Range of $\nu$ for most engineering materials:** $0 < \nu < 0.5$
> - Steel: $\nu \approx 0.30$
> - Concrete: $\nu \approx 0.20$
> - Rubber: $\nu \approx 0.49$ (nearly incompressible)
> - Cork: $\nu \approx 0$ (nearly zero lateral contraction)

### Relationship Between the Three Moduli (CRITICAL FOR KTU)
For an **isotropic** linear elastic material, only **two** of the three are independent:

$$E = 2G(1 + \nu) = \frac{9KG}{3K + G} = \frac{3K(1 - 2\nu)}{}$$

Therefore:
$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This relation $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ is one of the **most tested formulas** in KTU university exams. Memorize it!

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Linear Strain | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | dimensionless | +ve in tension, −ve in compression |
| 2 | Shear Strain | $\gamma = \dfrac{\Delta x}{L} \approx \phi$ | radians | Small angle approximation |
| 3 | Hooke's Law (Normal) | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Pa | Valid in elastic region only |
| 4 | Hooke's Law (Shear) | $\tau = G \cdot \gamma$ | Pa | Valid in elastic region only |
| 5 | Bending Stress | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Pa | $y$ from neutral axis |
| 6 | Max Bending Stress | $\sigma_{b,max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Pa | $Z = I / y_{max}$ |
| 7 | Bearing Stress | $\sigma_{br} = \dfrac{P}{A_b}$ | Pa | $A_b$ = projected contact area |
| 8 | Poisson's Ratio | $\nu = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_{lat}}{\varepsilon_{ax}}$ | dimensionless | Range: $0 < \nu < 0.5$ |
| 9 | Relation $E$–$G$–$\nu$ | $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ | — | Isotropic material |
| 10 | Relation $E$–$K$–$\nu$ | $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ | — | Isotropic material |
| 11 | Volumetric Strain (Hydrostatic) | $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z}{3K}$ | dimensionless | Sum of normal strains |
| 12 | Shear Strain from Pure Shear | $\gamma_{xy} = \dfrac{\tau_{xy}}{G}$ | radians | — |

---

## 2.5 Stress-Strain Diagram — Detailed Zones (For Ductile Material — Mild Steel)

The stress-strain curve of **mild steel** is the **most important** diagram in the entire Strength of Materials course. KTU asks for the labeled diagram almost every year.

**Zones (in sequence):**

1. **Linear Elastic Zone (O to A):** Hooke's Law holds; $\sigma \propto \varepsilon$. Slope = $E$.
2. **Non-Linear Elastic Zone (A to B):** Slight non-linearity, but unloading returns to original shape.
3. **Perfectly Plastic / Yield Plateau (B to C):** Material yields with little or no stress increase. **Upper Yield Point (B)** and **Lower Yield Point** are often marked.
4. **Strain Hardening Zone (C to D):** Stress rises again as the material work-hardens.
5. **Ultimate Stress Point (D):** Maximum stress the material can carry.
6. **Necking Zone (D to E):** Cross-section reduces locally (forms a "neck"); stress decreases (in engineering curve).
7. **Fracture Point (E):** Material breaks.

> **Engineering Stress vs. True Stress:**
> - **Engineering Stress** = Load / Original Area (used in standard diagrams)
> - **True Stress** = Load / Instantaneous Area (continues to rise even at necking)

### Brittle Material (e.g., Cast Iron, Concrete)
- No yield point.
- Curve is nearly linear up to fracture.
- Failure strain is very small.
- Compressive strength $\gg$ Tensile strength.

### Real-World Utility
- **Material selection** for design: ductile materials (steel) for shock loads; brittle materials (cast iron) for compressive structures (machine beds).
- Defining the **allowable working stress** using a factor of safety: $\sigma_{allow} = \sigma_{yield} / FoS$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bending Stress Formula $\sigma_b = M y / I$

### Starting Assumptions
Consider a beam of length $L$, rectangular cross-section, subjected to a pure bending moment $M$ at its ends. Initially straight, of homogeneous isotropic material, obeying Hooke's Law.

### Step 1 — Geometry of Deformation
A fiber at distance $y$ from the neutral axis (N.A.) undergoes a change in length $\Delta L$ when the beam bends. If the radius of curvature of the neutral axis is $\rho$, the geometry gives:

$$\text{Original length of fiber} = (\rho + y) \cdot d\theta$$

$$\text{Change in length} = (\rho + y) \cdot d\theta - \rho \cdot d\theta = y \cdot d\theta$$

Thus, the **linear strain** in the fiber is:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{y \cdot d\theta}{\rho \cdot d\theta} = \frac{y}{\rho}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Hooke's Law
Within the elastic limit, $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \frac{y}{\rho} = \frac{E \cdot y}{\rho}$$

### Step 3 — Internal Moment Equilibrium
The total internal moment due to the stress distribution must equal the externally applied moment $M$:

$$M = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \cdot dA = \int_A \left( \frac{E \cdot y}{\rho} \right) \cdot y \cdot dA = \frac{E}{\rho} \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

Recognize that $\displaystyle I = \int_A y^2 \, dA$ is the **Second Moment of Area** (Moment of Inertia) about the neutral axis. So:

$$M = \frac{E \cdot I}{\rho}$$

### Step 4 — Combine to Get the Flexure Formula
From Step 2: $\dfrac{1}{\rho} = \dfrac{\sigma}{E \cdot y}$. Substitute into Step 3:

$$M = \frac{E \cdot I \cdot \sigma}{E \cdot y} = \frac{I \cdot \sigma}{y}$$

Solving for $\sigma$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_b = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}}$$

This is the **flexure formula**, also called the **bending equation**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Relation $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$

### Step 1 — Consider a Square Element Under Pure Shear
A small square element of side $a$ in a 2D stress state is subjected to complementary shear stresses $\tau$ on all four faces. The element distorts into a rhombus with shear strain $\gamma$.

### Step 2 — Resolve Pure Shear into Biaxial Tension-Compression (Mohr's Circle Analogy)
Pure shear $\tau$ on horizontal and vertical planes is statically equivalent to:
- A **tensile stress** $\sigma = +\tau$ acting on a plane at 45° to the original faces.
- A **compressive stress** $\sigma = -\tau$ acting perpendicular to the tensile one.

This is a fundamental result derived from stress transformation equations (you will study this in detail in Module 2).

### Step 3 — Apply Hooke's Law in Both Directions
Let the 45° direction be $x'$ and the perpendicular direction be $y'$.

**Strain in the $x'$ direction** (tension):
$$\varepsilon_{x'} = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_{x'} - \nu \sigma_{y'}\right] = \frac{1}{E}[\tau - \nu(-\tau)] = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Strain in the $y'$ direction** (compression):
$$\varepsilon_{y'} = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_{y'} - \nu \sigma_{x'}\right] = \frac{1}{E}[-\tau - \nu \tau] = -\frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

### Step 4 — Relate to Shear Strain
The pure shear distortion causes the originally right angle to change by $\gamma$. Geometrically, the angular change equals the **difference** between the tensile and compressive strains along the diagonals:

$$\gamma = \varepsilon_{x'} - \varepsilon_{y'} = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E} - \left(-\frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}\right) = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

### Step 5 — Use Hooke's Law in Shear
By definition, $\tau = G \cdot \gamma$, so $\gamma = \dfrac{\tau}{G}$. Substituting:

$$\frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

Cancelling $\tau$ (assuming $\tau \neq 0$):

$$\frac{1}{G} = \frac{2(1+\nu)}{E}$$

Solving for $E$:

$$\boxed{E = 2G(1 + \nu)}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: How to Use Hooke's Law in 3D

For a general 3D state of stress ($\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z$) with no shear components:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$$

**Volumetric strain** (change in volume per unit volume) is:

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = \frac{1-2\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)$$

For **uniaxial stress** ($\sigma_y = \sigma_z = 0$):

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\sigma_x}{E}, \quad \varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = -\nu \frac{\sigma_x}{E}$$

This explains why a rubber band gets **thinner** as you stretch it — the lateral contractions are a direct consequence of $\nu > 0$.

---

## 3.4 Sample Numerical: Hooke's Law + Lateral Strain

**Problem:** A steel rod of diameter 20 mm and length 1.5 m is subjected to an axial tensile load of 60 kN. Given $E = 200$ GPa and $\nu = 0.30$, find:
1. The axial stress
2. The axial strain
3. The change in length
4. The change in diameter

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional Area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.020)^2 = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Axial Stress:**

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{60 \times 10^3}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.9099 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} = 190.99 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Axial Strain (Hooke's Law):**

$$\varepsilon_{ax} = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{1.9099 \times 10^8}{200 \times 10^9} = 9.549 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 — Change in Length:**

$$\Delta L = \varepsilon_{ax} \cdot L_0 = 9.549 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.5 = 1.432 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.432 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Lateral Strain:**

$$\varepsilon_{lat} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{ax} = -0.30 \times 9.549 \times 10^{-4} = -2.865 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 6 — Change in Diameter:**

$$\Delta d = \varepsilon_{lat} \cdot d_0 = -2.865 \times 10^{-4} \times 20 = -5.73 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm} = -0.00573 \text{ mm}$$

> The negative sign indicates the diameter **decreases** (contraction), as expected for a tensile load.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Stress-Strain Diagram for Ductile Material (Mild Steel)

The following Mermaid block renders a detailed **functional block architecture** of the stress-strain curve, showing all the key points and zones. This is a KTU-favorite diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["O: Origin - No Load, No Deformation"]:::start
    B["A: Proportional Limit - Hookes Law holds, slope equals E"]:::elastic
    C["B: Elastic Limit - Material returns to original shape on unloading"]:::elastic
    D["C: Upper Yield Point - Onset of plastic deformation"]:::yield
    E["C-prime: Lower Yield Point - Stress drops slightly, deformation continues"]:::yield
    F["D: Strain Hardening Zone - Stress rises with continued deformation"]:::plastic
    G["E: Ultimate Stress Point - Maximum stress material can carry"]:::ultimate
    H["F: Necking Zone - Cross section reduces locally, load drops"]:::necking
    I["G: Fracture Point - Material breaks and separates"]:::fracture

    A -->|"Linear Elastic Zone"| B
    B -->|"Non Linear Elastic"| C
    C -->|"Yielding Begins"| D
    D -->|"Stress Drops"| E
    E -->|"Plastic Flow"| F
    F -->|"Work Hardening"| G
    H -->|"Final Rupture"| I
    G -->|"Necking Starts"| H

    classDef start fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef elastic fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef yield fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef plastic fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ultimate fill:#ce93d8,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef necking fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fracture fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Bearing Stress — Schematic of a Riveted Joint

This block-flow illustrates how bearing stress is transmitted in a typical riveted lap joint (a classic KTU question).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Applied Tensile Load P"]:::load
    PL1["Top Plate"]:::plate
    RV["Rivet Shank - Diameter d, Thickness t"]:::rivet
    HOLE["Hole Diameter equals d in both plates"]:::hole
    PL2["Bottom Plate"]:::plate
    P2["Reaction Load P"]:::load

    P1 --> PL1
    PL1 -->|"Compressive Contact on Hole Wall"| RV
    RV -->|"Compressive Contact"| HOLE
    HOLE --> PL2
    PL2 --> P2

    BR["Bearing Stress: sigma_br = P divided by A_b"]:::formula
    A_b["A_b = d multiplied by t"]:::formula
    BR --> A_b

    classDef load fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef plate fill:#d7ccc8,stroke:#3e2723,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef rivet fill:#90a4ae,stroke:#263238,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef hole fill:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000,stroke-dasharray:5
    classDef formula fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Concept Map: Stress, Strain, and Elastic Constants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EXT["External Load Applied"]:::ext

    subgraph STR["Stress Types"]
        S1["Normal Stress sigma = P over A"]:::stress
        S2["Shear Stress tau = V over A"]:::stress
        S3["Bearing Stress sigma_br = P over A_b"]:::stress
        S4["Bending Stress sigma_b = M y over I"]:::stress
    end

    subgraph STR2["Strain Types"]
        ST1["Linear Strain epsilon = dL over L"]:::strain
        ST2["Shear Strain gamma = dx over L in radians"]:::strain
        ST3["Volumetric Strain ev = dV over V"]:::strain
    end

    subgraph HKM["Hookes Law Links"]
        H1["E = sigma divided by epsilon"]:::hk
        H2["G = tau divided by gamma"]:::hk
        H3["K = -P divided by ev"]:::hk
        H4["E equals 2G times 1 plus nu"]:::hk
    end

    MAT["Material Behavior"]:::mat
    ISO["Isotropic: Properties SAME in all directions"]:::iso
    ANI["Anisotropic: Properties VARY with direction"]:::iso

    EXT --> STR
    EXT --> STR2

    S2 --- H2
    ST1 --- H1
    ST2 --- H2
```
