topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT402 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bulk Modulus and Rigidity Modulus. Poisson's Ratio - Relationship Between Elastic Constants. Deformation in Axially Loaded Bars - Uniform Cross Section

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bulk Modulus (K)

### Formal Definition
**Bulk Modulus (K)** is defined as the ratio of volumetric stress (hydrostatic pressure) to the corresponding volumetric strain within the elastic limit. It measures a material's resistance to uniform compression from all sides.

$$K = \frac{\text{Volumetric Stress}}{\text{Volumetric Strain}} = \frac{-dP}{\frac{dV}{V}}$$

The negative sign indicates that an increase in pressure causes a decrease in volume. The unit of Bulk Modulus is **N/m² or Pascal (Pa)**, identical to that of stress.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Bulk Modulus is the only elastic constant that involves *three-dimensional* deformation. A higher value of K indicates a material is nearly **incompressible** (e.g., liquids have very high K).

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine submerging a hollow rubber ball deep into the ocean. As it descends, the immense water pressure squeezes it from **all directions equally**, shrinking its entire volume. **Bulk Modulus** is the numerical measure of *how much that ball resists being crushed* by that all-around pressure. Steel has a high K (it barely compresses), whereas rubber has a low K (it compresses easily).

---

## 1.2 Rigidity Modulus (G) — Modulus of Rigidity

### Formal Definition
**Rigidity Modulus (G)**, also called the **Modulus of Rigidity** or **Shear Modulus**, is the ratio of shear stress (τ) to shear strain (γ) within the elastic limit.

$$G = \frac{\text{Shear Stress}}{\text{Shear Strain}} = \frac{\tau}{\gamma}$$

Shear stress is tangential to the surface, while shear strain (γ) is the angular deformation in radians. Unit: **N/m² or Pascal (Pa)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition of Shear Strain:** When a force acts parallel to a surface, the angle of distortion (in radians) of a previously perpendicular element is the shear strain. $\gamma = \tan\theta \approx \theta$ for small deformations.

### Conceptual Analogy
Take a thick telephone directory and place your palm flat on the top cover. Push horizontally. The cover slides while the bottom stays fixed — the pages deform into a parallelogram shape. The **angular distortion** of the pages is the shear strain, and the **resistance to that angular twist** is the Rigidity Modulus G. A material with high G (like steel) resists twisting; a material with low G (like rubber) twists easily.

---

## 1.3 Poisson's Ratio (ν or 1/m)

### Formal Definition
**Poisson's Ratio (ν)** is defined as the ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain for a body subjected to uniaxial tensile or compressive loading, within the elastic limit.

$$\nu = -\frac{\text{Lateral Strain}}{\text{Longitudinal Strain}} = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{longitudinal}}$$

The negative sign ensures that ν is **positive** (since lateral strain is opposite in sign to longitudinal strain). It is a **dimensionless** quantity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Range:** For most engineering materials, $\nu$ lies between **0.25 and 0.33**. Theoretical limits: $-1 < \nu < 0.5$. Cork has ν ≈ 0 (perfectly compressible laterally), rubber ν ≈ 0.5, and concrete ν ≈ 0.1 to 0.2.

### Conceptual Analogy
Stretch a rubber band between your fingers. It becomes **longer** (longitudinal extension) and noticeably **thinner** in the middle (lateral contraction). The ratio of "how much thinner" to "how much longer" is Poisson's Ratio. It captures the **sideways squeeze** that always accompanies a stretch.

---

## 1.4 Deformation of Axially Loaded Bars (Uniform Cross-Section)

### Formal Definition
When a prismatic (uniform cross-section) bar of length L, cross-sectional area A, and Young's Modulus E is subjected to an axial tensile or compressive load P, the resulting deformation (elongation or contraction) is given by:

$$\delta L = \frac{PL}{AE}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Assumptions (KTU Board Standard):**
> 1. The bar is **uniform** (constant A throughout).
> 2. Load P is applied **gradually** (no dynamic effects).
> 3. Material is **homogeneous and isotropic**.
> 4. Stress remains **below the proportional limit** (Hooke's Law is valid).
> 5. Self-weight is **neglected**, unless explicitly stated.

### Conceptual Analogy
Pulling a long steel rod versus a short steel rod of the same thickness — the long rod stretches *more*. Now imagine two rods of identical length, one a thin wire and one a thick bar — the thin wire stretches *much more* because there is less material resisting the pull. The formula $\delta = PL/AE$ captures this exact intuition.

---

## 1.5 The Four Primary Elastic Constants

A linearly elastic, homogeneous, and isotropic material is fully described by **four constants**, though only **two are independent**:

| Constant | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Unit |
|----------|--------|------------------|------|
| Young's Modulus | E | Resistance to axial stretching | N/m² |
| Rigidity Modulus | G | Resistance to shear distortion | N/m² |
| Bulk Modulus | K | Resistance to volumetric change | N/m² |
| Poisson's Ratio | ν | Lateral-to-axial strain ratio | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** The relationships between E, G, K, and ν are **high-weightage** — they are asked almost every examination cycle in Part A (3 marks) and frequently in Part B (14 marks).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Bulk Modulus

Consider a cube of side $a$ and original volume $V = a^3$. Under hydrostatic pressure $P$, each side reduces by an amount $\delta a$:

$$\text{New Volume} = (a - \delta a)^3 = a^3\left(1 - \frac{\delta a}{a}\right)^3$$

$$V_{new} = V(1 - 3\varepsilon_{lat})$$

(using binomial expansion and ignoring higher powers of $\varepsilon_{lat}$ where $\varepsilon_{lat} = \delta a / a$)

$$\frac{dV}{V} = -3\varepsilon_{lat}$$

Since $\varepsilon_{lat} = -\frac{P}{E}(1 - 2\nu)$ from generalized Hooke's Law:

$$K = \frac{P}{3\varepsilon_{lat}} = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$$

**Why this matters:** This equation reveals that if ν approaches 0.5, K becomes infinite — the material is incompressible (no volume change possible).

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Rigidity Modulus

When shear stress $\tau$ is applied, the shear strain $\gamma$ is purely angular. For a cube of side $a$ subjected to complementary shear forces:
- Top face displaces by $\delta$ in horizontal direction.
- Shear strain: $\gamma = \delta / a$ (radians).

By energy equivalence of pure shear and principal stresses (to be detailed in Section 3), the relationship is:

$$E = 2G(1 + \nu)$$

**Why this matters:** A pure shear stress state produces both extension and contraction along the principal directions, automatically linking E, G, and ν.

## 2.3 Detailed Analysis of Poisson's Ratio

For a uniaxial stress $\sigma_x$ alone:
- $\sigma_x = P/A$, $\sigma_y = \sigma_z = 0$
- Longitudinal strain: $\varepsilon_x = \sigma_x / E$
- Lateral strain: $\varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = -\nu \sigma_x / E$

The negative sign is **critical** — lateral contraction occurs when axial extension happens.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Materials with low Poisson's Ratio (like concrete) are preferred in applications where lateral expansion under compression must be minimized (e.g., columns). Materials with high ν (like rubber) are excellent for seals and gaskets.

## 2.4 Deformation in Uniform Bars: Step-by-Step Logic

For a prismatic bar:
1. Axial force $P$ → Stress $\sigma = P / A$
2. Strain $\varepsilon = \sigma / E = P / (AE)$ (Hooke's Law)
3. Total deformation $\delta = \varepsilon \times L = PL / (AE)$

**Sign Convention (KTU Standard):**
- **Tensile load (+P)** → **Elongation (+δ)**
- **Compressive load (−P)** → **Shortening (−δ)**

**Real-World Utility:**
- Design of **suspension cables, tie-rods, columns, and pre-stressed concrete tendons**.
- Predicting **thermal stress** if expansion is restrained.
- Sizing of **bolts, rivets, and pins** in mechanical assemblies.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Application |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $K = \dfrac{-P}{\Delta V / V}$ | Bulk Modulus definition | Hydrostatic pressure problems |
| 2 | $G = \dfrac{\tau}{\gamma}$ | Rigidity Modulus definition | Torsion, shear problems |
| 3 | $\nu = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_{lat}}{\varepsilon_{long}}$ | Poisson's Ratio definition | Multiaxial stress |
| 4 | $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ | E–G–ν relation | High-weightage derivation |
| 5 | $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ | E–K–ν relation | Hydrostatic + uniaxial |
| 6 | $\dfrac{E}{9K} + \dfrac{1}{3G} = \dfrac{1}{K} \cdot \text{(see combined)}$ | Combined relation | Multi-constant problems |
| 7 | $\dfrac{E}{2G} + \dfrac{\nu}{1 - 2\nu} = 1$ | Alternate form | Verification |
| 8 | $E = \dfrac{9KG}{3K + G}$ | E–K–G relation | All three combined |
| 9 | $\delta L = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$ | Uniform bar deformation | Axial loading |
| 10 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{P}{AE}$ | Strain in uniform bar | Direct application |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always **derive** these relations (don't just state) when asked for 7-mark sub-parts. Marks are awarded for clarity of derivation steps, not just final answer.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between E, G, and ν

### Concept
Consider a small element subjected to **pure shear stress** $\tau$ on its faces. A pure shear state is equivalent to two principal stresses: a tensile stress of magnitude $\tau$ along one diagonal direction and a compressive stress of magnitude $\tau$ along the perpendicular diagonal direction.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Consider a square element of side $a$ subjected to complementary shear stresses $\tau$ on all four faces.

**Step 2:** The element distorts into a rhombus. The shear strain $\gamma$ is the change in angle at the corner.

**Step 3:** Now resolve the pure shear into principal stresses. By stress transformation (Mohr's circle), the principal stresses on the 45° planes are:

$$\sigma_1 = +\tau \quad \text{(tension on one diagonal)}$$

$$\sigma_2 = -\tau \quad \text{(compression on the other diagonal)}$$

**Step 4:** Apply **Generalized Hooke's Law** in 2D for the two principal directions:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left(\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2\right) = \frac{1}{E}\left(\tau - \nu(-\tau)\right) = \frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

$$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}\left(\sigma_2 - \nu \sigma_1\right) = \frac{1}{E}\left(-\tau - \nu\tau\right) = -\frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

**Step 5:** The shear strain $\gamma$ is the **change in angle** between the two principal directions. Since one diagonal elongates and the other contracts:

$$\gamma = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E} - \left(-\frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}\right) = \frac{2\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

**Step 6:** By the definition of Rigidity Modulus, $\tau = G\gamma$, so $\gamma = \tau / G$.

**Step 7:** Equate the two expressions for $\gamma$:

$$\frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{2\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

**Step 8:** Cancel $\tau$ (non-zero) and cross-multiply:

$$E = 2G(1 + \nu)$$

$$\boxed{G = \frac{E}{2(1 + \nu)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** This shows that shear stiffness G is *less than* E/2 when ν > 0. For most metals (ν ≈ 0.3), G ≈ E/2.6.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Relationship Between E, K, and ν

### Concept
A hydrostatic pressure state produces equal stresses in all three directions. We will use the volumetric strain definition.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Consider a cube subjected to hydrostatic pressure $P$ (compressive). The principal stresses are:

$$\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -P$$

**Step 2:** The volumetric strain is the sum of the three normal strains:

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$

**Step 3:** Apply Generalized Hooke's Law for each direction:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[-P - \nu(-2P)\right] = \frac{-P(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

By symmetry, $\varepsilon_x = \varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z$.

**Step 4:** Sum the three strains:

$$\varepsilon_v = 3 \times \frac{-P(1 - 2\nu)}{E} = \frac{-3P(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 5:** By definition of Bulk Modulus, $K = -P / \varepsilon_v$:

$$K = \frac{-P}{\dfrac{-3P(1 - 2\nu)}{E}} = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$$

$$\boxed{E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limiting Case:** As $\nu \to 0.5$, $E / K \to 0$, meaning $K \to \infty$. This confirms that incompressible materials (rubber) have infinite bulk modulus.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Combined Relation Between E, K, and G

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** From the two relations derived above:

$$E = 2G(1 + \nu) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \nu = \frac{E}{2G} - 1 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$E = 3K(1 - 2\nu) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \nu = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{E}{6K} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 2:** Equate (i) and (ii):

$$\frac{E}{2G} - 1 = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{E}{6K}$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to isolate terms:

$$\frac{E}{2G} + \frac{E}{6K} = \frac{1}{2} + 1 = \frac{3}{2}$$

**Step 4:** Factor out $E$:

$$E\left(\frac{1}{2G} + \frac{1}{6K}\right) = \frac{3}{2}$$

**Step 5:** Multiply both sides by $2$:

$$E\left(\frac{1}{G} + \frac{1}{3K}\right) = 3$$

**Step 6:** Take the reciprocal to find $E$:

$$\frac{1}{E}\left(\frac{1}{G} + \frac{1}{3K}\right) = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$\frac{1}{3G} + \frac{1}{9K} = \frac{1}{E}$$

**Step 7:** Take the reciprocal again:

$$\boxed{E = \frac{9KG}{3K + G}}$$

This is the most general relation among the three moduli. It is **bilinear in K and G**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Deformation in Axially Loaded Bars (Uniform Cross-Section)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Consider a uniform prismatic bar of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and Young's Modulus $E$, fixed at one end and subjected to axial tensile load $P$ at the other.

**Step 2:** The internal axial force at any cross-section (by equilibrium) is equal to the applied load:

$$N(x) = P \quad \text{(constant along the length)}$$

**Step 3:** The axial stress at any section:

$$\sigma = \frac{N}{A} = \frac{P}{A} \quad \text{(constant)}$$

**Step 4:** The axial strain by Hooke's Law:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{P}{AE}$$

**Step 5:** Consider an infinitesimal element of length $dx$. Its deformation is:

$$d\delta = \varepsilon \cdot dx = \frac{P}{AE} dx$$

**Step 6:** Integrate over the full length:

$$\delta = \int_0^L \frac{P}{AE} dx = \frac{P}{AE} \int_0^L dx = \frac{PL}{AE}$$

$$\boxed{\delta L = \frac{PL}{AE}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** This formula assumes the bar has a *single* material, *uniform* area, and *negligible self-weight*. For stepped or tapered bars, piecewise summation or integration over a variable area is required.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Solving Strategy for KTU Problems

| Problem Type | Given | To Find | Key Equation(s) Used |
|--------------|-------|---------|----------------------|
| Find Poisson's ratio | E, G | $\nu$ | $\nu = \dfrac{E}{2G} - 1$ |
| Find Bulk Modulus | E, $\nu$ | K | $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ |
| Find Rigidity Modulus | E, K | G | $G = \dfrac{3KE}{9K - E}$ (rearranged) |
| Find deformation | P, L, A, E | $\delta$ | $\delta = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$ |
| Verify elastic constants | E, G, K, $\nu$ | Consistency check | $\dfrac{E}{9K} + \dfrac{1}{3G} = \dfrac{1}{E}$ rearranged |
| Find new E | K, $\nu$ | E | $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define Poisson's ratio. What is its range for engineering materials?**

**Model Answer:**
Poisson's ratio ($\nu$) is defined as the negative ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain, when a material is subjected to uniaxial loading within the elastic limit:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{longitudinal}}$$

It is a **dimensionless** quantity. The theoretical range is $-1 < \nu < 0.5$. For most engineering materials (metals), it lies between **0.25 and 0.33**. For cork, $\nu \approx 0$, and for rubber, $\nu \approx 0.5$. **[3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for range]**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define Bulk Modulus and state its SI unit.**

**Model Answer:**
Bulk Modulus (K) is defined as the ratio of volumetric stress (hydrostatic pressure) to the corresponding volumetric strain, within the elastic limit:

$$K = \frac{-dP}{dV/V}$$

The negative sign indicates that an increase in pressure produces a decrease in volume. Its SI unit is **N/m² (Pascal)**. A higher K indicates a more incompressible material. **[3 marks: 2 for definition + formula, 1 for unit and significance]**

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified] — 14 Marks

**(a)** Derive the relationship between Young's Modulus (E), Rigidity Modulus (G), and Poisson's ratio (ν). **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Consider a square element ABCD of side $a$ subjected to complementary shear stresses $\tau$ on all four faces. The element distorts into a rhombus $AB'C'D'$, with the shear strain:

$$\gamma = \frac{BB'}{a} \quad \text{(in radians)}$$

By Mohr's circle analysis, the principal stresses at 45° to the original faces are:

$$\sigma_1 = +\tau \quad (\text{tensile}), \quad \sigma_2 = -\tau \quad (\text{compressive})$$

Using 2D Generalized Hooke's Law:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left(\sigma_1 - \nu\sigma_2\right) = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

$$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}\left(\sigma_2 - \nu\sigma_1\right) = -\frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

The total shear strain is:

$$\gamma = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

By definition, $\tau = G\gamma \Rightarrow \gamma = \tau/G$. Equating:

$$\frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

$$\boxed{E = 2G(1+\nu)}$$

**(b)** A bar of length 2 m, cross-section 30 mm × 30 mm, is subjected to an axial tensile load of 60 kN. If $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm², determine (i) the stress, (ii) the strain, and (iii) the total elongation. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $L = 2$ m $= 2000$ mm
- $A = 30 \times 30 = 900$ mm²
- $P = 60$ kN $= 60{,}000$ N
- $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm²

**(i) Stress:**

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{60{,}000}{900} = 66.67 \text{ N/mm}^2 \; (\text{MPa})$$

**(ii) Strain:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{66.67}{2 \times 10^5} = 3.333 \times 10^{-4} \quad (\text{dimensionless})$$

**(iii) Total Elongation:**

$$\delta L = \frac{PL}{AE} = \frac{60{,}000 \times 2000}{900 \times 2 \times 10^5} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^8}{1.8 \times 10^8} = 0.667 \text{ mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Unit consistency** is critical. If $P$ is in kN and $A$ in mm², stress will be in MPa (= N/mm²). Do not mix cm and mm.
> 2. Many students forget to **convert L to mm** when using N and N/mm² units. Always check unit homogeneity: $[PL/AE] = \frac{\text{N} \cdot \text{mm}}{\text{mm}^2 \cdot \text{N/mm}^2} = \text{mm}$ ✓
> 3. Do not forget to **state the sign convention** — tensile elongation is positive.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified] — 14 Marks

**(a)** Derive the relationship between Young's Modulus (E), Bulk Modulus (K), and Poisson's ratio (ν). **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Consider a cubic element of side $a$ subjected to hydrostatic pressure $P$ (compressive). The principal stresses are:

$$\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -P$$

Using 3D Generalized Hooke's Law:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[-P + 2\nu P\right] = \frac{-P(1-2\nu)}{E}$$

By symmetry, $\varepsilon_x = \varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z$.

The volumetric strain is:

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = \frac{-3P(1-2\nu)}{E}$$

By the definition of Bulk Modulus:

$$K = \frac{-P}{\varepsilon_v} = \frac{-P}{\dfrac{-3P(1-2\nu)}{E}} = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$$

$$\boxed{E = 3K(1-2\nu)}$$

**(b)** For a material, $E = 200$ GPa and $\nu = 0.3$. Calculate the values of (i) Rigidity Modulus G, and (ii) Bulk Modulus K. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $E = 200$ GPa $= 200 \times 10^9$ N/m², $\nu = 0.3$

**(i) Rigidity Modulus:**

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{200 \times 10^9}{2(1+0.3)} = \frac{200 \times 10^9}{2.6} = 76.92 \text{ GPa}$$

**(ii) Bulk Modulus:**

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)} = \frac{200 \times 10^9}{3(1-0.6)} = \frac{200 \times 10^9}{1.2} = 166.67 \text{ GPa}$$

**Verification (using combined relation):**

$$E = \frac{9KG}{3K + G} = \frac{9 \times 166.67 \times 76.92}{3(166.67) + 76.92} = \frac{115{,}380}{576.93} \approx 200 \text{ GPa} \checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 3 in $K$ formula** is the most common error. Always write: $K = E / [3(1-2\nu)]$, NOT $K = E / (1-2\nu)$.
> 2. The **factor of 2** in $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$ is equally critical.
> 3. Always **carry at least 4 significant figures** in intermediate steps to avoid rounding errors in the final answer.
> 4. **Do not use ν = 0.3 as exactly 0.30** in your calculator's bracket — write it as 0.3 to avoid the answer 0.30 misleading you.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exams**

### 1. **Definitions (Must Memorize Verbatim)**
- $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lat}/\varepsilon_{long}$ — dimensionless, range $-1 < \nu < 0.5$
- $K = -dP / (dV/V)$ — units: Pa or N/m²
- $G = \tau/\gamma$ — units: Pa or N/m²
- $\delta = PL/AE$ — uniform bar elongation

### 2. **The Three Pillars of Elastic Constant Relations**
- $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ — *derivation uses pure shear → principal stresses*
- $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$ — *derivation uses hydrostatic pressure → volumetric strain*
- $E = 9KG/(3K+G)$ — *combined relation, no ν*

### 3. **Limiting Values to Remember**
- $\nu = 0.5 \Rightarrow K = \infty$ (incompressible, e.g., rubber)
- $\nu = 0 \Rightarrow G = E/2$ (e.g., cork — no lateral coupling)
- $\nu = -1 \Rightarrow$ theoretical lower limit

### 4. **Uniform Bar Deformation — Key Points**
- Formula valid for **constant A and constant N** (no step or taper)
- $A$ in mm², $P$ in N, $L$ in mm, $E$ in N/mm² → $\delta$ in **mm**
- Self-weight **ignored** unless stated

### 5. **Common KTU 14-Mark Question Types**
- Type 1: Derive E–G–ν relation (7 marks) + numerical problem (7 marks)
- Type 2: Derive E–K–ν relation (7 marks) + find G or K given E and ν (7 marks)
- Type 3: Direct application of $\delta = PL/AE$ with multi-part numerical data

### 6. **Sign Convention (KTU Board Standard)**
- **Tension:** +P, +δ, +ε
- **Compression:** −P, −δ, −ε
- **Lateral strain** opposite sign to **longitudinal strain**

### 7. **Common Units Conversions**
- 1 GPa = $10^3$ MPa = $10^9$ N/m² = $10^3$ N/mm²
- 1 N/mm² = 1 MPa

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For any 14-mark question, allocate **5 minutes** to write down all three elastic constant formulas first (even if not asked), then proceed with the specific derivation. Examiners reward evidence of comprehensive understanding.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Varying Cross Section
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCSFT402 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Varying Cross Section ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **member of varying cross-section** is an axially loaded structural element whose transverse cross-sectional area changes continuously or in discrete steps along its longitudinal (loading) axis. Unlike a prismatic bar (constant cross-section), the stress distribution and total elongation of such a member must be evaluated by integrating the deformation of infinitesimal elemental slices, since the internal axial force divided by the local area (i.e., the stress) is no longer constant along the length. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCSFT402 - Module 1):** > *"Axially loaded members whose cross-sectional area A(x) is a function of the longitudinal coordinate x are termed varying cross-section members. The total elongation is obtained by the integral δ = ∫ [P(x) / (A(x)·E)] dx, evaluated over the length L."* The most commonly encountered variants in KTU problems are: 1. **Stepped (Stepped) Bar** — Area changes in discrete jumps (e.g., A₁ → A₂ → A₃) at section junctions. 2. **Uniformly Tapering (Trapezoidal) Bar** — Diameter or width varies linearly with x, giving A(x) = A₁ + (A₂ − A₁)·(x / L). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Garden Hose Nozzle:** > Imagine you are squeezing water through a flexible garden hose whose outlet narrows toward the nozzle. To push the *same quantity of water* through the narrow section per second, the water must speed up dramatically. Similarly, when an axial load P flows through a steel bar whose cross-section suddenly shrinks, the **stress** (force per unit area) must rise sharply in the narrow zone. If the narrowing is gradual, the stress rises gradually; if it's a sudden step, you get a "stress concentration" at the shoulder. The bar's total stretch is essentially the sum of tiny stretches of each slice — thicker slices stretch less, thinner slices stretch more, even though the same force P passes through every slice. **Key Physical Constant:** Young's Modulus **E** (in N/mm² or GPa) governs axial stiffness; for mild steel, E ≈ **2 × 10⁵ N/mm² (200 GPa)**. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation **Why is the formula different from a uniform bar?** In a uniform bar, stress σ = P/A is constant, strain ε = σ/E is constant, and elongation δ = PL/(AE). When area varies: - σ is **not** constant; it changes with x. - ε is **not** constant; each slice has its own strain. - Therefore, we cannot use δ = PL/(AE) directly — we must **sum (integrate)** the local strains over the length. **The Governing Logic (Step-by-Step):** 1. **Slice the bar into infinitesimal elements** of thickness dx at position x. 2. **Identify the local area** A(x) at that position. 3. **Apply equilibrium** — if the section is axially loaded by P with no intermediate transverse loads, the internal axial force is P(x) = P everywhere (constant). 4. **Compute local stress:** σ(x) = P / A(x). 5. **Compute local strain:** ε(x) = σ(x) / E = P / [A(x)·E]. 6. **Compute local elongation:** dδ = ε(x)·dx = P·dx / [A(x)·E]. 7. **Integrate over the full length:** δ = ∫₀ᴸ dδ = ∫₀ᴸ { P / [A(x)·E] } dx. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU Part B question on varying cross-section.** | # | Member Type | Area Function A(x) | Elongation Formula δ | Average Stress σ_avg | |---|------------|--------------------|----------------------|----------------------| | 1 | Uniform (prismatic) | A = constant | δ = PL / (AE) | P / A | | 2 | Stepped (2 segments) | A₁, A₂ over L₁, L₂ | δ = (P·L₁)/(A₁·E) + (P·L₂)/(A₂·E) | P · L / (A_eq · L) | | 3 | Tapered (linear width) | A(x) = A₁ + (A₂ − A₁)·(x/L) | δ = (P·L) / [E·(A₂ − A₁)] · ln(A₂/A₁) | P / A_avg | | 4 | Tapered (linear radius) | A(x) = π·[r(x)]² | δ = (4·P·L) / [π·E·d₁·d₂] | 2P / [π·d₁·d₂] | | 5 | Composite (multi-material) | A₁, A₂, E₁, E₂ | δ = Σ P·Lᵢ / (Aᵢ·Eᵢ) | varies per segment | **Critical Notes for KTU Valuation:** - Units must be **consistent**: use N, mm, N/mm² throughout (or kN, m, kN/m²). - For tapered circular bars: the formula **4PL / (πE·d₁·d₂)** uses only the **end diameters d₁ and d₂**, NOT the average diameter squared. - When A₁ = A₂ = A, the logarithmic formula collapses to δ = PL/(AE) — a good sanity check. - For stepped bars, elongation is simply the **algebraic sum** of each segment's individual elongation. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Tie-rods and hangers in bridges** — often tapered to save material while matching stress gradients. - **Turbine shafts** — stepped diameters accommodate bearings and couplings. - **Tapered pins in machine tool jigs** — ensure uniform shear stress distribution along the taper. - **Pre-stressed concrete tendons** — multi-segment varying area to balance friction and stress losses. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Stepped Bar (Two-Segment) **Problem Setup:** A bar consists of segment-1 of length L₁ and area A₁ in series with segment-2 of length L₂ and area A₂, subjected to axial pull P. Find total elongation δ. **Step-by-Step Deduction:** Because the bar is in static equilibrium with only end loads, the internal axial force in each segment equals P (no transverse load between them). Elongation of segment-1: $$\delta_1 = \frac{P \cdot L_1}{A_1 \cdot E}$$ Elongation of segment-2: $$\delta_2 = \frac{P \cdot L_2}{A_2 \cdot E}$$ Since the segments are connected in series, total elongation is the algebraic sum: $$ \begin{aligned} \delta &= \delta_1 + \delta_2 \\ &= \frac{P \cdot L_1}{A_1 \cdot E} + \frac{P \cdot L_2}{A_2 \cdot E} \\ &= \frac{P}{E}\left(\frac{L_1}{A_1} + \frac{L_2}{A_2}\right) \end{aligned} $$ For *n* segments in series (extension): $$ \delta = \frac{P}{E} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i}{A_i} $$ ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Uniformly Tapering Rectangular Bar (Width Varies, Thickness Constant) **Problem Setup:** A flat bar of uniform thickness t has width varying linearly from b₁ at x = 0 to b₂ at x = L. Find total elongation under axial load P. **Step 1 — Express area as a function of x:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Width: } b(x) &= b_1 + (b_2 - b_1)\cdot\frac{x}{L} \\ \text{Area: } A(x) &= t \cdot b(x) = t\left[b_1 + (b_2 - b_1)\cdot\frac{x}{L}\right] \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Set up the integral for total elongation:** $$ \delta = \int_{0}^{L} \frac{P \, dx}{A(x) \cdot E} = \frac{P}{E \cdot t}\int_{0}^{L}\frac{dx}{b_1 + (b_2 - b_1)\cdot\dfrac{x}{L}} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute u for clean integration:** Let $$u = b_1 + (b_2 - b_1)\cdot\frac{x}{L}$$ Then $$du = \frac{(b_2 - b_1)}{L}\,dx \implies dx = \frac{L}{b_2 - b_1}\,du$$ Limits: when x = 0, u = b₁; when x = L, u = b₂. **Step 4 — Evaluate:** $$ \begin{aligned} \delta &= \frac{P}{E \cdot t}\cdot\frac{L}{b_2 - b_1}\int_{b_1}^{b_2}\frac{du}{u} \\ &= \frac{P \cdot L}{E \cdot t \cdot (b_2 - b_1)}\bigl[\ln u\bigr]_{b_1}^{b_2} \\ &= \frac{P \cdot L}{E \cdot t \cdot (b_2 - b_1)}\ln\left(\frac{b_2}{b_1}\right) \end{aligned} $$ Since t·b₁ = A₁ and t·b₂ = A₂, the result generalises to: $$ \boxed{\;\delta = \frac{P \cdot L}{E \cdot (A_2 - A_1)}\ln\left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)\;} $$ > [!NOTE] > This is a *very frequently tested* KTU result. Memorise the logarithmic form. ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Uniformly Tapering Circular Bar (Diameter Varies Linearly) **Problem Setup:** A solid circular bar has diameter varying linearly from d₁ at x = 0 to d₂ at x = L. Find total elongation under axial load P. **Step 1 — Diameter and area as functions of x:** $$ \begin{aligned} d(x) &= d_1 + (d_2 - d_1)\cdot\frac{x}{L} \\ A(x) &= \frac{\pi}{4}\,d(x)^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute into elongation integral:** $$ \delta = \int_{0}^{L}\frac{P \, dx}{\dfrac{\pi}{4}\,d(x)^2 \cdot E} = \frac{4P}{\pi E}\int_{0}^{L}\frac{dx}{\left[d_1 + (d_2 - d_1)\cdot\dfrac{x}{L}\right]^2} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute u = d(x), so du = (d₂ − d₁)/L · dx, dx = L/(d₂ − d₁) du:** $$ \begin{aligned} \delta &= \frac{4P}{\pi E}\cdot\frac{L}{d_2 - d_1}\int_{d_1}^{d_2}\frac{du}{u^2} \\ &= \frac{4P L}{\pi E (d_2 - d_1)}\left[-\frac{1}{u}\right]_{d_1}^{d_2} \\ &= \frac{4P L}{\pi E (d_2 - d_1)}\left(\frac{1}{d_1} - \frac{1}{d_2}\right) \\ &= \frac{4P L}{\pi E (d_2 - d_1)}\cdot\frac{d_2 - d_1}{d_1 d_2} \end{aligned} $$ The (d₂ − d₁) terms cancel beautifully, yielding: $$ \boxed{\;\delta = \frac{4 P L}{\pi E \, d_1 \, d_2}\;} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** The result depends only on the **product d₁·d₂**, NOT on the difference. When d₁ = d₂ = d, the formula correctly reduces to δ = 4PL/(πEd²) = PL/(AE), which is the prismatic case — perfect sanity check. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Stress Comparison | Bar Type | Location of Maximum Stress | σ_max / σ_min Ratio | |----------|---------------------------|---------------------| | Uniform | Anywhere | 1.00 | | Stepped (A₁ > A₂) | Smaller section A₂ | A₁ / A₂ | | Tapered (b₁ > b₂) | Smaller end (x = L) | b₁ / b₂ | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Schematic 1: Stepped Bar (Two Segments) ```mermaid graph LR loadA(["Axial Pull P"]):::loadNode --> seg1["Segment 1
Length L1
Area A1"]:::segNode seg1 --> junctionA("Junction
Step Change"):::jointNode junctionA --> seg2["Segment 2
Length L2
Area A2"]:::segNode seg2 --> fixB(["Fixed Support"]):::loadNode classDef loadNode fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B5A00,color:#000 classDef segNode fill:#A8D8FF,stroke:#003F7F,color:#000 classDef jointNode fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Schematic 2: Uniformly Tapering Circular Bar ```mermaid graph LR startLoad(["Axial Pull P"]):::loadNode --> bigEnd["Big End
Diameter d1
Area pi d1 squared / 4"]:::segBig bigEnd --> taperMid("Taper Zone
Linear d x profile"):::midNode taperMid --> smallEnd["Small End
Diameter d2
Area pi d2 squared / 4"]:::segSmall smallEnd --> endFix(["Support"]):::loadNode classDef loadNode fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B5A00,color:#000 classDef segBig fill:#C8F0C8,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 classDef segSmall fill:#A8D8FF,stroke:#003F7F,color:#000 classDef midNode fill:#E6CCFF,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Schematic 3: Stress Distribution Block Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD inp["Axial Load P applied at end"]:::inpNode inp --> eq1["Equilibrium:
Internal Force N x = P constant"]:::logicNode eq1 --> loc["Locate x coordinate
of point of interest"]:::logicNode loc --> area["Compute A x at that x
using geometry"]:::logicNode area --> sigma["Stress sigma x = P / A x"]:::logicNode sigma --> strain["Strain epsilon x = sigma x / E"]:::logicNode strain --> slice["Local elongation d delta = epsilon x dx"]:::logicNode slice --> integ["Integrate from 0 to L
for total delta"]:::logicNode integ --> out["Total elongation delta result"]:::outNode classDef inpNode fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000 classDef logicNode fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,color:#000 classDef outNode fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 ``` ### 4.4 Schematic 4: Decision Matrix — Which Formula to Use ```mermaid flowchart TD q{"What is the
cross section shape?"}:::qNode q --|Constant A| fA["Use PL / AE
Prismatic Formula"]:::ansNode q --|Stepped Discrete| fB["Use sum PL_i over A_i E
Segment Sum Formula"]:::ansNode q --|Linearly Tapered Rect| fC["Use PL ln A2 over A1 / EA2 minus A1"]:::ansNode q --|Linearly Tapered Circular| fD["Use 4PL over pi E d1 d2
End Diameter Formula"]:::ansNode q --|Multi Material Composite| fE["Use sum PL_i over A_i E_i
Composite Series Formula"]:::ansNode classDef qNode fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000 classDef ansNode fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#004D40,color:#000 ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define a stepped bar. Why cannot we apply the simple formula δ = PL/(AE) directly to a stepped bar?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A stepped bar is one whose cross-sectional area changes abruptly at one or more sections along its length, dividing the bar into discrete segments of constant area. The formula δ = PL/(AE) assumes a **uniform** area A and **constant** stress σ along the length. In a stepped bar, σ varies from segment to segment (σᵢ = P/Aᵢ), so strain is also segment-wise different. Hence we must compute the elongation of each segment separately and sum them: δ = Σ (P·Lᵢ)/(Aᵢ·E). **(3 marks)** **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State the formula for elongation of a uniformly tapering circular bar of length L with end diameters d₁ and d₂ under axial load P. Define all terms.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** $$\delta = \frac{4PL}{\pi E \, d_1 \, d_2}$$ Where: - **P** = axial tensile load (N) - **L** = length of the bar (mm) - **E** = Young's Modulus of the material (N/mm²) - **d₁, d₂** = diameters at the two ends (mm) The result is independent of the rate of taper — it depends only on the end diameters. **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Question with Internal Choice (14 Marks) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common error 1:** Using the *average* area in place of separate segment areas for stepped bars — **deduct 2 marks**. > - **Common error 2:** Forgetting that E may differ between segments in a composite bar — **deduct 2 marks**. > - **Common error 3:** Writing the tapered-bar formula with average diameter squared instead of end-diameter product — **deduct 1 mark**. > - **Common error 4:** Missing units in final answer — **deduct ½ mark per inconsistency**. --- #### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Pattern] **(a)** A stepped steel bar ABC consists of two segments: AB of length 300 mm and diameter 30 mm, and BC of length 200 mm and diameter 20 mm. The bar is subjected to an axial tensile load of 50 kN at end C, while end A is fixed. Take E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm². Determine: (i) the total elongation of the bar, and (ii) the stresses in segments AB and BC. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A uniformly tapering circular bar is 400 mm long with end diameters of 40 mm and 20 mm. It is subjected to an axial pull of 60 kN. Find the total elongation and the maximum stress in the bar. Take E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm². **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Part (i) — Total Elongation:** Areas of segments: $$ A_{AB} = \frac{\pi}{4}(30)^2 = 706.86 \text{ mm}^2,\quad A_{BC} = \frac{\pi}{4}(20)^2 = 314.16 \text{ mm}^2 $$ Elongations: $$ \delta_{AB} = \frac{P \cdot L_{AB}}{A_{AB} \cdot E} = \frac{50{,}000 \times 300}{706.86 \times 2 \times 10^5} = 0.1061 \text{ mm} $$ $$ \delta_{BC} = \frac{P \cdot L_{BC}}{A_{BC} \cdot E} = \frac{50{,}000 \times 200}{314.16 \times 2 \times 10^5} = 0.1592 \text{ mm} $$ Total elongation: $$ \delta = 0.1061 + 0.1592 = 0.2653 \text{ mm} \quad \mathbf{(3 \text{ marks})} $$ **Part (ii) — Stresses:** $$ \sigma_{AB} = \frac{P}{A_{AB}} = \frac{50{,}000}{706.86} = 70.74 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \mathbf{(2 \text{ marks})} $$ $$ \sigma_{BC} = \frac{P}{A_{BC}} = \frac{50{,}000}{314.16} = 159.15 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \mathbf{(2 \text{ marks})} $$ **(b) Solution — Tapered Circular Bar:** Elongation using derived formula: $$ \delta = \frac{4 P L}{\pi E \, d_1 d_2} = \frac{4 \times 60{,}000 \times 400}{\pi \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 40 \times 20} $$ $$ \delta = \frac{9.6 \times 10^9}{\pi \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 800} = \frac{9.6 \times 10^9}{5.0265 \times 10^8} = 0.1910 \text{ mm} \quad \mathbf{(3 \text{ marks})} $$ Maximum stress occurs at the smaller diameter (d₂ = 20 mm): $$ \sigma_{max} = \frac{P}{A_{min}} = \frac{60{,}000}{\frac{\pi}{4}(20)^2} = \frac{60{,}000}{314.16} = 190.99 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \mathbf{(2 \text{ marks})} $$ Minimum stress at d₁ = 40 mm: $$ \sigma_{min} = \frac{P}{\frac{\pi}{4}(40)^2} = \frac{60{,}000}{1256.64} = 47.75 \text{ N/mm}^2 $$ Difference (optional context): **σ_max = 4 × σ_min** — the smaller end carries four times the stress. **(2 marks)** --- #### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Pattern] **(a)** A bar of length 600 mm tapers uniformly from a rectangular cross-section of 60 mm × 30 mm at one end to 30 mm × 30 mm at the other. If the bar carries an axial tensile load of 40 kN, determine the total elongation. Take E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm². **(7 marks)** **(b)** A composite bar consists of three segments in series: a steel portion (E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm², A = 400 mm², L = 200 mm), a brass portion (E = 1 × 10⁵ N/mm², A = 600 mm², L = 300 mm), and a copper portion (E = 1.2 × 10⁵ N/mm², A = 300 mm², L = 250 mm). The bar is subjected to an axial load of 60 kN. Find the total elongation. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Solution — Tapered Rectangular Bar (Width Varies, Thickness Constant):** A₁ = 30 × 60 = 1800 mm² ; A₂ = 30 × 30 = 900 mm² Using the logarithmic formula: $$ \delta = \frac{P L}{E (A_2 - A_1)} \ln\left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right) $$ Note: A₂ < A₁, so A₂ − A₁ = −900 mm² (negative). $$ \delta = \frac{40{,}000 \times 600}{2 \times 10^5 \times (900 - 1800)} \ln\left(\frac{900}{1800}\right) $$ $$ \delta = \frac{2.4 \times 10^7}{2 \times 10^5 \times (-900)} \times \ln(0.5) $$ $$ \delta = \frac{2.4 \times 10^7}{-1.8 \times 10^8} \times (-0.6931) $$ $$ \delta = (-0.1333) \times (-0.6931) = 0.0924 \text{ mm} \quad \mathbf{(5 \text{ marks})} $$ Cross-check using d₁·d₂ style logic with end widths: t = 30 mm constant. Equivalent formula gives the same answer. **(2 marks for substitution/setup clarity)** **(b) Solution — Composite Series Bar:** Elongation of each segment using δᵢ = (P·Lᵢ)/(Aᵢ·Eᵢ): Steel: $$ \delta_{steel} = \frac{60{,}000 \times 200}{400 \times 2 \times 10^5} = 0.150 \text{ mm} $$ Brass: $$ \delta_{brass} = \frac{60{,}000 \times 300}{600 \times 1 \times 10^5} = 0.300 \text{ mm} $$ Copper: $$ \delta_{copper} = \frac{60{,}000 \times 250}{300 \times 1.2 \times 10^5} = 0.417 \text{ mm} $$ Total elongation (series sum): $$ \delta = 0.150 + 0.300 + 0.417 = 0.867 \text{ mm} \quad \mathbf{(5 \text{ marks})} $$ The composite bar elongates by **0.867 mm**. **(2 marks for final statement and units)** --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Varying Cross Section** - ✦ **Stepped bar elongation** = Σ (P·Lᵢ)/(Aᵢ·E) — pure algebraic sum. - ✦ **Tapered rectangular bar** uses **logarithmic** form: δ = PL·ln(A₂/A₁) / [E·(A₂ − A₁)]. - ✦ **Tapered circular bar** uses **end-diameter product** form: δ = 4PL / (π·E·d₁·d₂) — **no square, no average**. - ✦ **Stress in varying section** = P / A(x); maximum stress is at the **smallest** cross-section. - ✦ **Composite series bar** uses segment-wise Eᵢ and Aᵢ — never use a single averaged E. - ✦ **Units check:** Force in **N**, length in **mm**, area in **mm²**, E in **N/mm²**, δ comes out in **mm**. - ✦ **Sanity check #1:** When d₁ = d₂, the tapered formula collapses to δ = PL/(AE). - ✦ **Sanity check #2:** When A₁ = A₂, the logarithmic formula collapses to δ = PL/(AE) (limit of ln(x)/x−1). - ✦ **Average stress** σ_avg = P / A_avg is **NOT** what you use for varying sections — it only applies to prismatic bars. - ✦ **Always mention** "axial load is constant throughout the bar" in equilibrium step — examiners reward clarity. - ✦ **Draw FBDs** of each segment to confirm internal force = P (only true when no transverse loads are applied between segments).

topics.title: Dissimilar Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT402 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dissimilar Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Composite Bar / Member of Dissimilar Materials:** A structural member composed of *two or more* materials of *different elastic properties* (different Young's moduli *E* and/or different cross-sectional areas) bonded together mechanically or physically to act as a *single unit* under an applied load.

In a member of dissimilar materials, the fundamental assumption used in single-material analysis no longer holds directly because the **internal stress distribution is non-uniform** across the cross-section, even when the bar is subjected to a pure axial pull or push. The two governing principles that govern its behavior are:

- **Equilibrium:** The sum of internal axial forces in each material must equal the externally applied load.
- **Compatibility (Geometric Continuity):** Since the materials are bonded, the *total elongation* (or *total strain* depending on configuration) of all constituent materials must be compatible — no material can stretch or compress independently of the other.

The two primary geometric configurations studied at KTU level are:

1. **Parallel Configuration** (materials placed *side-by-side*, sharing the same length and the same end displacements, e.g., a steel rod sleeved inside a brass tube, both ends rigidly gripped by rigid plates).
2. **Series Configuration** (materials placed *end-to-end*, sharing the same axial load, e.g., a steel rod welded in line with a copper rod).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Tug-of-War with Two Different Teams:** Imagine two people of *different strengths* tied to the same rope end and pulling together against a wall. They both move the *same distance* (compatibility of displacement) but exert *different forces* (depending on their stiffness). The weaker partner carries less force, the stronger carries more, but the displacement of the rope is *identical* for both. This is exactly what happens in a parallel composite bar.

A second powerful analogy is the **bimetallic strip** used in old-style analog thermostats: a brass strip is roll-bonded to an Invar strip. When the temperature rises, brass expands more than Invar; the strip *bends* (curls) toward the Invar side. This bending occurs because the two materials are *forced* to remain the same length along the bond line — they cannot freely expand independently.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

- **Young's Modulus ($E$)**: Units **N/m²** or **Pa** (often **GPa** for metals). Steel ≈ **200 GPa**, Copper ≈ **100 GPa**, Brass ≈ **100 GPa**, Aluminum ≈ **70 GPa**, Concrete ≈ **30 GPa**, Timber ≈ **10–15 GPa**.
- **Modular Ratio ($n$)**: $n = \dfrac{E_2}{E_1}$ — dimensionless.
- **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion ($\alpha$)**: Units **/°C** or **/K**. Steel ≈ $12 \times 10^{-6}$/°C, Brass ≈ $19 \times 10^{-6}$/°C, Copper ≈ $17 \times 10^{-6}$/°C.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

#### **Case A: Parallel Configuration (Same Length, Same Strain)**

This is the most frequently tested case in KTU exams. Two bars (1 and 2) are placed *side-by-side* and loaded through rigid end-plates.

**Step 1 — Identify the compatibility condition.**
Since the end-plates are rigid, the total shortening (or elongation) of bar 1 must equal that of bar 2.

$$\delta_1 = \delta_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \epsilon_1 = \epsilon_2 = \epsilon \text{ (common strain)}$$

**Step 2 — Express each internal force using Hooke's law.**

$$P_1 = \sigma_1 A_1 = E_1 \epsilon \, A_1 \qquad P_2 = \sigma_2 A_2 = E_2 \epsilon \, A_2$$

**Step 3 — Apply equilibrium (load shared).**

$$P = P_1 + P_2 = \epsilon \left(E_1 A_1 + E_2 A_2\right)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the common strain.**

$$\epsilon = \dfrac{P}{E_1 A_1 + E_2 A_2}$$

**Step 5 — Back-substitute to get individual stresses and forces.**

$$\sigma_1 = E_1 \epsilon = \dfrac{P \, E_1}{E_1 A_1 + E_2 A_2} \qquad \sigma_2 = E_2 \epsilon = \dfrac{P \, E_2}{E_1 A_1 + E_2 A_2}$$

**The Why behind it:** The *stiffer* material (higher $E \cdot A$) carries a *larger share* of the load. If bar 1 is steel and bar 2 is copper, then since $E_{\text{steel}} > E_{\text{copper}}$, the steel carries more load.

#### **Case B: Series Configuration (Same Axial Force)**

Two bars are joined end-to-end, sharing the *same axial force* $P$ throughout.

**Step 1 — Compatibility: total elongation is the sum.**

$$\delta = \delta_1 + \delta_2 = \dfrac{P L_1}{A_1 E_1} + \dfrac{P L_2}{A_2 E_2}$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent single bar concept.** Treat the composite as a single bar of equivalent stiffness $AE/L$:

$$\dfrac{P L_{\text{eq}}}{A_{\text{eq}} E_{\text{eq}}} = \dfrac{P L_1}{A_1 E_1} + \dfrac{P L_2}{A_2 E_2}$$

#### **Case C: Transformed Section Method (Equivalent Section)**

This is a *shortcut* widely used for parallel composite sections.

**Step 1 — Choose a reference material** (say material 1, with $E_1$).

**Step 2 — Compute the modular ratio.**

$$n = \dfrac{E_2}{E_1}$$

**Step 3 — Transform material 2 into an *equivalent* area of material 1.**

$$A_2' = n \cdot A_2$$

**Step 4 — The transformed section is now a *single material* section.**

$$A_{\text{eq}} = A_1 + n \cdot A_2$$

**Step 5 — Treat the section as if it were entirely material 1.**

The strain at any level is the same as in the original, and stresses in material 1 are obtained directly. To get stress in material 2, *divide by n*:

$$\sigma_2 = \dfrac{\sigma_1'}{n} \quad \text{(equivalent stress method)}$$

#### **Case D: Temperature Stresses in Dissimilar Materials**

When a composite bar undergoes a temperature change $\Delta T$ *while free to expand*, no stress develops. But if it is *constrained* (or if the two materials have different $\alpha$), internal stresses arise.

**For a constrained single bar:**

$$\sigma_T = E \alpha \Delta T \qquad P_T = E A \alpha \Delta T$$

**For a *parallel* composite bar of *equal* length with no external load but a uniform temperature rise $\Delta T$:**

Compatibility requires equal elongations; if unrestrained, the free expansions differ, so internal forces develop to *enforce* equal length.

$$P = \dfrac{(\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) \, \Delta T}{\dfrac{1}{E_1 A_1} + \dfrac{1}{E_2 A_2}}$$

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the *high-yield reference* for the KTU 2024 ESE on this topic. Memorize these — they appear in nearly every variant of the question.

| # | Configuration | Governing Condition | Key Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Parallel (same $L$, same $\delta$) | $\epsilon_1 = \epsilon_2$ | $\epsilon = \dfrac{P}{E_1 A_1 + E_2 A_2}$ |
| 2 | Parallel (same $L$, same $\delta$) | Load-sharing | $P_1 = \dfrac{P \, E_1 A_1}{E_1 A_1 + E_2 A_2}$ |
| 3 | Parallel (same $L$, same $\delta$) | Stress ratio | $\dfrac{\sigma_1}{\sigma_2} = \dfrac{E_1}{E_2}$ |
| 4 | Series (same $P$) | $\delta = \delta_1 + \delta_2$ | $\delta = \dfrac{P L_1}{A_1 E_1} + \dfrac{P L_2}{A_2 E_2}$ |
| 5 | Series — Equivalent | Treat as single bar | $\dfrac{L}{AE} = \dfrac{L_1}{A_1 E_1} + \dfrac{L_2}{A_2 E_2}$ |
| 6 | Transformed Section | $n = E_2 / E_1$ | $A_{\text{eq}} = A_1 + n A_2$ |
| 7 | Transformed — Stress recovery | After analysis | $\sigma_2 = \sigma_1' / n$ |
| 8 | Constrained thermal (single) | Fixed at both ends | $\sigma_T = E \alpha \Delta T$ |
| 9 | Composite thermal (parallel) | Same $L$, free ends | $P = \dfrac{(\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) \Delta T}{\dfrac{1}{E_1 A_1} + \dfrac{1}{E_2 A_2}}$ |
| 10 | Composite thermal stress in mat. 1 | Parallel | $\sigma_1 = P / A_1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbol key** — $P$ = applied axial load, $A$ = cross-sectional area, $E$ = Young's modulus, $L$ = length, $\alpha$ = coefficient of thermal expansion, $\Delta T$ = temperature change, $\epsilon$ = strain, $\sigma$ = axial stress, $n$ = modular ratio.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concept of dissimilar materials underpins the design and analysis of:

- **Reinforced Concrete (RC) Beams & Columns:** Concrete (weak in tension) is paired with steel rebars (strong in tension). The transformed section method is *exactly* what is used in RC design to compute the moment of resistance of a beam.
- **Bimetallic Strips & Thermostats:** Exploits different $\alpha$ to produce controlled mechanical displacement.
- **Soldered / Brazed Joints:** Two metals bonded at an interface; thermal mismatch produces *residual stresses* that can lead to fatigue failure.
- **Fiber-Reinforced Composites (FRP):** Carbon fibers in an epoxy matrix — the fibers carry most of the load due to their high $E$.
- **Railroad Rails (Modern):** Sometimes made of two different grades of steel, head-hardened, to resist wear and contact fatigue differently in head vs. web.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation 1 — Parallel Composite Bar (Stress and Strain Analysis)

**Given:** A bar of length $L$ consists of two materials placed side-by-side (parallel). Material 1 has area $A_1$ and modulus $E_1$; material 2 has $A_2$ and $E_2$. An axial load $P$ is applied through rigid end plates.

**To find:** The stresses $\sigma_1, \sigma_2$ and the elongation $\delta$ of the bar.

**Step 1 — Apply compatibility.**
Because the two end-plates are rigid and remain parallel to each other, every cross-section translates as a rigid body. Therefore the elongation of bar 1 must equal the elongation of bar 2.

$$\delta_1 = \delta_2 = \delta \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\delta}{L} = \frac{\delta_1}{L} = \frac{\delta_2}{L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \epsilon_1 = \epsilon_2 = \epsilon$$

*Textual explanation:* Both materials undergo the *same* axial strain because they are forced to deform together by the rigid plates.

**Step 2 — Express elongations using Hooke's law.**

$$\delta_1 = \frac{P_1 L}{A_1 E_1}, \qquad \delta_2 = \frac{P_2 L}{A_2 E_2}$$

*Textual explanation:* Hooke's law for axial loading: $\delta = PL/(AE)$.

**Step 3 — Apply equilibrium.** The internal forces in the two materials must add up to the externally applied load $P$.

$$P_1 + P_2 = P$$

*Textual explanation:* Free-body diagram of one of the rigid end-plates. The plate is in axial equilibrium; external load on one side balances the sum of internal forces from both bars on the other side.

**Step 4 — Use compatibility $\delta_1 = \delta_2$ to relate $P_1$ and $P_2$.**

$$\frac{P_1 L}{A_1 E_1} = \frac{P_2 L}{A_2 E_2}$$

$$\frac{P_1}{P_2} = \frac{A_1 E_1}{A_2 E_2}$$

*Textual explanation:* Cross-multiplying cancels $L$ on both sides, leaving a *load-sharing ratio* controlled by axial rigidity $AE$.

**Step 5 — Substitute into the equilibrium equation.**

$$P_1 + P_2 = P \quad \text{with} \quad P_2 = P_1 \cdot \frac{A_2 E_2}{A_1 E_1}$$

$$P_1 \left(1 + \frac{A_2 E_2}{A_1 E_1}\right) = P$$

$$P_1 = \frac{P \, A_1 E_1}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}$$

*Textual explanation:* The factor in parentheses is the ratio of the composite axial rigidity to the axial rigidity of material 1.

**Step 6 — By symmetry of algebra, $P_2$ is obtained.**

$$P_2 = \frac{P \, A_2 E_2}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the individual stresses.**

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{P_1}{A_1} = \frac{P \, E_1}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}$$

$$\sigma_2 = \frac{P_2}{A_2} = \frac{P \, E_2}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}$$

*Textual explanation:* Note that the area $A_1$ cancels from numerator and denominator in $P_1/A_1$, leaving a stress expression that depends on the modular contributions.

**Step 8 — Compute the common strain.**

$$\epsilon = \frac{\sigma_1}{E_1} = \frac{\sigma_2}{E_2} = \frac{P}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}$$

**Step 9 — Compute the total elongation.**

$$\delta = \epsilon \cdot L = \frac{P L}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}$$

*Textual explanation:* The composite bar behaves like an *equivalent single bar* whose effective axial rigidity is $A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2$.

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation 2 — Series Composite Bar (Equivalent Length / Stiffness)

**Given:** Material 1 of length $L_1$, area $A_1$, modulus $E_1$, joined end-to-end with material 2 of length $L_2$, area $A_2$, modulus $E_2$. Axial load $P$ applied at the free ends.

**To find:** Total elongation $\delta$ and equivalent axial rigidity.

**Step 1 — Recognize the equilibrium condition.** A free-body diagram of any internal cross-section cuts through only *one* material at a time. The internal axial force in *each* material equals the applied load $P$.

$$P_1 = P_2 = P$$

*Textual explanation:* Since the load is transmitted sequentially through material 1 and then material 2, both carry the *same* load — analogous to current flowing through series resistors.

**Step 2 — Write the elongation of each segment.**

$$\delta_1 = \frac{P L_1}{A_1 E_1}, \qquad \delta_2 = \frac{P L_2}{A_2 E_2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply compatibility.** The total elongation is the sum of the two.

$$\delta = \delta_1 + \delta_2 = \frac{P L_1}{A_1 E_1} + \frac{P L_2}{A_2 E_2}$$

**Step 4 — Define an equivalent single bar of length $L = L_1 + L_2$ with effective axial rigidity $K_{\text{eq}}$.**

$$\delta = \frac{P L}{K_{\text{eq}}} \quad \text{where} \quad K_{\text{eq}} = \frac{A E}{L} \text{ for an equivalent bar}$$

**Step 5 — Equate the two expressions for $\delta$.**

$$\frac{P (L_1 + L_2)}{K_{\text{eq}}} = \frac{P L_1}{A_1 E_1} + \frac{P L_2}{A_2 E_2}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $K_{\text{eq}}$.**

$$\frac{1}{K_{\text{eq}}} = \frac{L_1}{A_1 E_1 (L_1 + L_2)} + \frac{L_2}{A_2 E_2 (L_1 + L_2)}$$

$$K_{\text{eq}} = \frac{L_1 + L_2}{\dfrac{L_1}{A_1 E_1} + \dfrac{L_2}{A_2 E_2}}$$

*Textual explanation:* In a series configuration, *compliances* (the inverse of stiffness) add up — just like capacitors in series in an electrical analogy.

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation 3 — Transformed Section Method (Justification)

**Given:** Parallel composite section with materials 1 and 2, with a load $P$ applied along the common axis.

**Step 1 — Start with the strain-equality condition.**

$$\epsilon_1 = \epsilon_2 = \epsilon$$

**Step 2 — Express the total load as a sum.**

$$P = \sigma_1 A_1 + \sigma_2 A_2 = E_1 \epsilon A_1 + E_2 \epsilon A_2 = \epsilon (E_1 A_1 + E_2 A_2)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\epsilon$ and then for $\sigma_1$.**

$$\epsilon = \frac{P}{E_1 A_1 + E_2 A_2}, \qquad \sigma_1 = E_1 \epsilon = \frac{E_1 P}{E_1 A_1 + E_2 A_2}$$

**Step 4 — Introduce modular ratio $n = E_2 / E_1$ and rewrite the denominator.**

$$E_1 A_1 + E_2 A_2 = E_1 \left(A_1 + \frac{E_2}{E_1} A_2\right) = E_1 (A_1 + n A_2)$$

**Step 5 — Recognize that $(A_1 + n A_2)$ is the *equivalent area* of a hypothetical section made entirely of material 1.**

$$A_{\text{eq}} = A_1 + n A_2$$

**Step 6 — Then the stress in material 1 is the same as the stress in an *equivalent single-material* bar of area $A_{\text{eq}}$.**

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{P}{A_{\text{eq}}}$$

**Step 7 — For material 2, use $\sigma_2 = E_2 \epsilon = n E_1 \epsilon = n \sigma_1$.**

$$\sigma_2 = n \cdot \sigma_1 = \frac{n \, P}{A_1 + n A_2}$$

*Textual explanation:* The transformed section method allows us to treat the composite as a single material, compute stresses in the reference material directly, and then *amplify* (if $n > 1$) or *attenuate* (if $n < 1$) to get stresses in the other material.

### 3.4 Detailed Derivation 4 — Temperature Stress in a Parallel Composite Bar

**Given:** Two materials 1 and 2 of equal length $L$, areas $A_1, A_2$, moduli $E_1, E_2$, and coefficients $\alpha_1, \alpha_2$. The composite is free at one end, and the temperature of the whole bar is raised by $\Delta T$. We want the *internal forces* that develop because the two materials try to expand by different amounts.

**Step 1 — Free expansion of each material if unconnected.**

$$\delta_{1,\text{free}} = \alpha_1 L \Delta T, \qquad \delta_{2,\text{free}} = \alpha_2 L \Delta T$$

**Step 2 — Since the materials are bonded at the ends, they must experience the *same* actual elongation $\delta$.**

$$\delta_1 = \delta_2 = \delta$$

**Step 3 — The actual elongation is the free expansion *minus* the contraction caused by the internal force $P_1$ (compressive, if material 2 expands more).**

$$\delta = \alpha_1 L \Delta T - \frac{P_1 L}{A_1 E_1} \quad \text{(material 1, gets compressed)}$$

$$\delta = \alpha_2 L \Delta T - \frac{P_2 L}{A_2 E_2} \quad \text{(material 2, gets compressed less or extended)}$$

**Step 4 — Equate the two expressions for $\delta$.**

$$\alpha_1 L \Delta T - \frac{P_1 L}{A_1 E_1} = \alpha_2 L \Delta T - \frac{P_2 L}{A_2 E_2}$$

**Step 5 — Apply equilibrium $P_1 = P_2 = P$ (since no external load is applied, the two internal forces must balance).**

$$\alpha_1 L \Delta T - \frac{P L}{A_1 E_1} = \alpha_2 L \Delta T - \frac{P L}{A_2 E_2}$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange.**

$$P L \left(\frac{1}{A_2 E_2} - \frac{1}{A_1 E_1}\right) = (\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) L \Delta T$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $P$.**

$$P = \frac{(\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) \Delta T}{\dfrac{1}{A_1 E_1} - \dfrac{1}{A_2 E_2}}$$

*Note on sign convention:* If $\alpha_2 > \alpha_1$, the result is positive, meaning material 1 is in tension (it gets stretched to match material 2) and material 2 is in compression (it gets squeezed).

**Step 8 — Compute the stresses.**

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{P}{A_1}, \qquad \sigma_2 = \frac{P}{A_2}$$

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Parallel Composite Bar System

The diagram below models the *load-flow architecture* of a parallel composite bar — how the applied axial load $P$ is distributed, transformed, and ultimately resisted by the two constituent materials.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Axial Load P applied] --> B[Rigid End Plate]
    B --> C[Material 1 with Area A1 and Modulus E1]
    B --> D[Material 2 with Area A2 and Modulus E2]
    C --> E[Internal Force P1 in Material 1]
    D --> F[Internal Force P2 in Material 2]
    E --> G[Compatibility Check Strain eps1 equals eps2]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Equilibrium Check P1 plus P2 equals P]
    H --> I[Transformed Section Aeq equals A1 plus nA2]
    I --> J[Compute Common Strain eps equals P over AeqE1]
    J --> K[Recover Stress sigma1 equals E1 times eps]
    J --> L[Recover Stress sigma2 equals n times sigma1]
    K --> M[Output Individual Forces and Stresses]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Compute Total Elongation delta equals eps times L]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Series Composite Bar System

The series configuration differs fundamentally: the same load $P$ passes through both materials sequentially, and *compliances* add up.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Axial Load P applied at free end] --> B[Material 1 with L1 A1 E1]
    B --> C[Junction Interface Rigid Bond]
    C --> D[Material 2 with L2 A2 E2]
    B --> E[Elongation delta1 equals P times L1 over A1 E1]
    D --> F[Elongation delta2 equals P times L2 over A2 E2]
    E --> G[Total Elongation delta equals delta1 plus delta2]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Equivalent Bar with Leq and Keq]
    H --> I[Output Total Elongation and Equivalent Stiffness]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Temperature-Induced Stress in Composite

The third architecture shows the analysis pipeline when a *temperature change* — not a mechanical load — is the source of internal stress.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Temperature Change deltaT applied uniformly] --> B[Compute Free Expansion alpha1 L dT and alpha2 L dT]
    B --> C[Identify Mismatch delta alpha2 minus alpha1]
    C --> D[Compatibility Both materials deform to same length]
    D --> E[Equilibrium P1 equals P2 equals P Internal]
    E --> F[Governing Equation P equals delta alpha times dT over One over A1E1 minus One over A2E2]
    F --> G[Compute Stress sigma1 equals P over A1]
    F --> H[Compute Stress sigma2 equals P over A2]
    G --> I[Output Final Stresses in Both Materials]
    H --> I
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above are *flow-level architecture schematics*. In an actual KTU problem, the corresponding *physical sketch* should accompany every derivation to score full marks — examiners explicitly allocate 1–2 marks for a clear free-body diagram.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A composite bar consists of a steel rod of 30 mm diameter placed inside a brass tube of 40 mm outer diameter and 35 mm inner diameter. Both are 500 mm long and carry an axial pull of 80 kN through rigid end-plates. Calculate the stress developed in each material and the total elongation of the bar. Take $E_s = 200$ GPa and $E_b = 100$ GPa.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Step 1 — Compute areas.

$$A_s = \frac{\pi}{4}(30)^2 = 706.86 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_b = \frac{\pi}{4}\left[(40)^2 - (35)^2\right] = \frac{\pi}{4}(1600 - 1225) = 294.52 \text{ mm}^2$$

Step 2 — Compute axial rigidities.

$$A_s E_s = 706.86 \times 200 \times 10^3 = 141.37 \times 10^6 \text{ N}$$

$$A_b E_b = 294.52 \times 100 \times 10^3 = 29.45 \times 10^6 \text{ N}$$

Step 3 — Use parallel-bar load-sharing formula.

$$P_s = \frac{P \cdot A_s E_s}{A_s E_s + A_b E_b} = \frac{80{,}000 \times 141.37}{141.37 + 29.45} = 66{,}156 \text{ N}$$

$$P_b = P - P_s = 13{,}844 \text{ N}$$

Step 4 — Compute stresses.

$$\sigma_s = \frac{66{,}156}{706.86} = 93.59 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 93.6 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_b = \frac{13{,}844}{294.52} = 47.0 \text{ MPa}$$

Step 5 — Total elongation.

$$\delta = \frac{P L}{A_s E_s + A_b E_b} = \frac{80{,}000 \times 500}{170.82 \times 10^6} = 0.234 \text{ mm}$$

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A steel rod 1.5 m long and 2000 mm² in area is joined end-to-end to a copper rod 1 m long and 1500 mm² in area. An axial pull of 60 kN is applied. Find the total elongation of the composite bar. Take $E_s = 200$ GPa, $E_c = 100$ GPa.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Step 1 — Compute elongation of steel.

$$\delta_s = \frac{P L_s}{A_s E_s} = \frac{60{,}000 \times 1500}{2000 \times 200 \times 10^3} = \frac{90 \times 10^6}{400 \times 10^6} = 0.225 \text{ mm}$$

Step 2 — Compute elongation of copper.

$$\delta_c = \frac{P L_c}{A_c E_c} = \frac{60{,}000 \times 1000}{1500 \times 100 \times 10^3} = \frac{60 \times 10^6}{150 \times 10^6} = 0.400 \text{ mm}$$

Step 3 — Total elongation (series: add).

$$\delta = \delta_s + \delta_c = 0.225 + 0.400 = 0.625 \text{ mm}$$

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A composite bar of length 600 mm consists of a steel core of 25 mm diameter enclosed in a brass tube of outer diameter 40 mm. The two materials are firmly connected at the ends. If the composite bar is subjected to an axial compressive load of 100 kN, determine: (i) the stress in each material, (ii) the shortening of the bar. Take $E_s = 200$ GPa, $E_b = 100$ GPa.

**(b) [7 Marks]** If, in the above problem, a temperature rise of 60°C occurs while the bar is free to expand, calculate the magnitude and nature of stresses developed in each material. Take $\alpha_s = 12 \times 10^{-6}$/°C and $\alpha_b = 19 \times 10^{-6}$/°C.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Mechanical Load:**

Step 1 — Compute areas.

$$A_s = \frac{\pi}{4}(25)^2 = 490.87 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_b = \frac{\pi}{4}\left[(40)^2 - (25)^2\right] = \frac{\pi}{4}(1600 - 625) = 765.76 \text{ mm}^2$$

Step 2 — Load sharing (compressive, so loads are negative but magnitudes as below).

$$P_s = \frac{P \cdot A_s E_s}{A_s E_s + A_b E_b} = \frac{100{,}000 \times 490.87 \times 200}{490.87 \times 200 + 765.76 \times 100}$$

$$P_s = \frac{100{,}000 \times 98{,}174}{98{,}174 + 76{,}576} = \frac{9.817 \times 10^9}{1.7475 \times 10^8} = 56{,}179 \text{ N (compression)}$$

$$P_b = 100{,}000 - 56{,}179 = 43{,}821 \text{ N (compression)}$$

Step 3 — Stresses.

$$\sigma_s = \frac{56{,}179}{490.87} = 114.45 \text{ MPa (compressive)}$$

$$\sigma_b = \frac{43{,}821}{765.76} = 57.22 \text{ MPa (compressive)}$$

Step 4 — Shortening.

$$\delta = \frac{P L}{A_s E_s + A_b E_b} = \frac{100{,}000 \times 600}{1.7475 \times 10^8} = 0.343 \text{ mm (shortening)}$$

**Part (b) — Temperature Rise:**

Step 1 — Identify the governing equation (parallel composite, free ends, $\Delta T$ only).

$$P = \frac{(\alpha_b - \alpha_s) \cdot \Delta T}{\dfrac{1}{A_s E_s} - \dfrac{1}{A_b E_b}}$$

Step 2 — Substitute values.

$$\alpha_b - \alpha_s = (19 - 12) \times 10^{-6} = 7 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /°C}$$

$$\frac{1}{A_s E_s} = \frac{1}{98.174 \times 10^6} = 1.019 \times 10^{-8} \text{ /N}$$

$$\frac{1}{A_b E_b} = \frac{1}{76.576 \times 10^6} = 1.306 \times 10^{-8} \text{ /N}$$

$$\frac{1}{A_s E_s} - \frac{1}{A_b E_b} = (1.019 - 1.306) \times 10^{-8} = -0.287 \times 10^{-8} \text{ /N}$$

Step 3 — Solve for $P$.

$$P = \frac{7 \times 10^{-6} \times 60}{-0.287 \times 10^{-8}} = -1.464 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = -146{,}400 \text{ N}$$

The negative sign means: material that wants to expand *more* (brass, $\alpha_b$ higher) is in **compression**, while steel is in **tension** (it is being stretched by the brass to enforce compatibility).

Step 4 — Compute stresses.

$$P_s = +146{,}400 \text{ N (tension)}, \quad P_b = -146{,}400 \text{ N (compression)}$$

$$\sigma_s = \frac{146{,}400}{490.87} = 298.3 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

$$\sigma_b = \frac{146{,}400}{765.76} = 191.2 \text{ MPa (compressive)}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Two bars, one of copper and one of steel, are placed side by side and connected to rigid plates at top and bottom. The copper bar has a length of 800 mm and area 1200 mm²; the steel bar has a length of 800 mm and area 800 mm². The assembly carries a vertical load of 50 kN. Find: (i) the load carried by each bar, (ii) the stress in each bar, and (iii) the total elongation. Take $E_c = 100$ GPa, $E_s = 200$ GPa.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A steel rod 2 m long, 30 mm diameter, is placed inside an aluminum tube of 50 mm outer diameter and 35 mm inner diameter, of the same length. Both are securely held between rigid plates. Determine the stresses developed in each material when a load of 60 kN is applied. Take $E_s = 200$ GPa, $E_a = 70$ GPa. Also find the equivalent section if transformed into steel.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

Step 1 — Axial rigidities.

$$A_c E_c = 1200 \times 100 \times 10^3 = 120 \times 10^6 \text{ N}$$

$$A_s E_s = 800 \times 200 \times 10^3 = 160 \times 10^6 \text{ N}$$

Sum: $280 \times 10^6$ N.

Step 2 — Load sharing.

$$P_c = \frac{50{,}000 \times 120}{280} = 21{,}428.6 \text{ N} \approx 21.43 \text{ kN}$$

$$P_s = \frac{50{,}000 \times 160}{280} = 28{,}571.4 \text{ N} \approx 28.57 \text{ kN}$$

Step 3 — Stresses.

$$\sigma_c = \frac{21{,}428.6}{1200} = 17.86 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

$$\sigma_s = \frac{28{,}571.4}{800} = 35.71 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

Step 4 — Total elongation.

$$\delta = \frac{P L}{A_c E_c + A_s E_s} = \frac{50{,}000 \times 800}{280 \times 10^6} = 0.143 \text{ mm}$$

**Part (b):**

Step 1 — Areas.

$$A_s = \frac{\pi}{4}(30)^2 = 706.86 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_a = \frac{\pi}{4}\left[(50)^2 - (35)^2\right] = \frac{\pi}{4}(2500 - 1225) = 1001.98 \text{ mm}^2$$

Step 2 — Modular ratio (transforming aluminum to steel, $E_s$ as reference).

$$n = \frac{E_a}{E_s} = \frac{70}{200} = 0.35$$

Step 3 — Equivalent area.

$$A_{\text{eq}} = A_s + n A_a = 706.86 + 0.35 \times 1001.98 = 706.86 + 350.69 = 1057.55 \text{ mm}^2$$

Step 4 — Stress in steel (reference material).

$$\sigma_s = \frac{P}{A_{\text{eq}}} = \frac{60{,}000}{1057.55} = 56.74 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

Step 5 — Stress in aluminum (recover by multiplying by $n$).

$$\sigma_a = n \cdot \sigma_s = 0.35 \times 56.74 = 19.86 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. **Confusing the load-sharing formula with stress-sharing.** Students often write $P_1 = P \cdot A_1/(A_1 + A_2)$, forgetting the $E$ factors. The correct formula uses $A_1 E_1$ and $A_2 E_2$. Marks lost: ~2.
> 2. **Sign error in temperature-stress problems.** If $\alpha_2 > \alpha_1$, the *lower-$\alpha$* material (say steel) is in *tension*, not compression. Mixing this up costs ~1 mark and breaks the entire sub-question.
> 3. **Forgetting to recover $\sigma_2 = n \sigma_1$ in the transformed section method.** Students compute the stress in the equivalent section and *report it* as the stress in the second material, instead of dividing/multiplying by $n$. Costs ~2 marks.
> 4. **No free-body diagram (FBD).** KTU examiners *always* allocate 1 mark for a clean sketch showing the rigid plates, the two materials, and the applied load. Skip it, lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency.** Mixing N/mm² with kN, or m with mm, in the same equation line. Convert *all* quantities to a single unit system *before* substituting. Costs ~1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Composite / dissimilar material bar** = two or more materials of different $E$ bonded to act as one.
- [x] **Parallel configuration** → *same* strain, *load shared* in ratio of $A_1 E_1 : A_2 E_2$.
- [x] **Series configuration** → *same* axial force, *elongations add up*.
- [x] **Transformed section method** → choose one material as reference, multiply other area by modular ratio $n = E_2/E_1$, then proceed as a single material.
- [x] **Stress recovery** after transformation: $\sigma_2 = n \cdot \sigma_1$ (when transforming to material 1).
- [x] **Temperature stress in parallel composite** = $\dfrac{(\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) \Delta T}{(1/A_1 E_1) - (1/A_2 E_2)}$ — sign indicates tension/compression.
- [x] **Stiffer material ($E \cdot A$ higher) carries more load** in parallel.
- [x] **Weaker material carries more elongation** in series.
- [x] **Equilibrium + Compatibility = the two pillars** of every dissimilar-material problem.
- [x] **Always draw the FBD** of the rigid plate or the cut section to score the 1-mark drawing credit.
- [x] **Units**: stick to **N, mm, MPa (N/mm²)** consistently to avoid KTU's common 1-mark unit penalty.
- [x] **Real-world relevance**: reinforced concrete (transformed section), bimetallic thermostats ($\alpha$-mismatch), fiber-reinforced composites (load sharing), welded joints (residual thermal stress).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle of Superposition, Thermal Effects - Simple
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT402 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Principle of Superposition, Thermal Effects - Simple

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Principle of Superposition

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principle of Superposition (KTU 2024 Definition):** The principle of superposition states that in a **linearly elastic** body subjected to a system of loads, the **stress, strain, and deflection** produced at any point due to a combination of independent loads acting simultaneously is equal to the **algebraic sum** of the stresses, strains, and deflections produced at that point by each load acting **independently**.

The principle holds true **only** when:
- The material obeys **Hooke's Law** (stress is proportional to strain).
- The deformations (deflections) are **infinitesimally small**.
- The geometry of the structure does not change appreciably under load.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Tug-of-War with two teams pulling on opposite ends of a rope:** If Team A pulls with **100 N** to the left and Team B pulls with **80 N** to the right, the net force on the rope is simply **20 N to the left** (100 − 80). You do not need to recalculate the rope's behavior from scratch — you just **add up** the individual effects. The Principle of Superposition works exactly like this: each load acts on the beam independently, and the total effect (stress, deflection) is just the **sum of individual effects**.

## 1.2 Thermal Effects in Beams

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Effect (KTU 2024 Definition):** When a structural member is subjected to a **change in temperature**, the member tends to expand or contract. If this expansion or contraction is **restrained** by supports or other members, **thermal stresses and strains** develop within the member.

The key physical relationship governing thermal deformation is:

$$
\Delta L = \alpha \, L \, \Delta T
$$

Where:
- $\Delta L$ = Change in length (m or mm)
- $\alpha$ = Coefficient of linear thermal expansion (per °C or per K)
- $L$ = Original length of the member
- $\Delta T$ = Change in temperature ($T_{\text{final}} - T_{\text{initial}}$)

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a metal rod heated on one end:** When you heat a metal rod, the molecules vibrate more vigorously and push each other farther apart, causing the rod to lengthen. If the rod is free to expand, it simply grows longer with **zero internal stress**. However, if you clamp both ends between rigid walls (like a railway track bolted down on a hot day), the rod *wants* to expand but *cannot*. This internal "frustration" creates a **compressive thermal stress** — the molecules are being squeezed together by the walls even though they're trying to spread apart. This is precisely how railway tracks buckle on hot summer days!

### Standard Physical Constants

| Material | $\alpha$ (×10⁻⁶ per °C) |
|----------|------------------------|
| **Steel** | **12** |
| **Concrete** | **10** |
| **Aluminum** | **23** |
| **Copper** | **17** |
| **Cast Iron** | **11** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Working Logic of the Principle of Superposition

The principle can be broken down into a structured sequence of logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Verify Linearity:** Confirm that the structure is **linearly elastic** (Hooke's Law applies) and the deflections are **small** (negligible change in geometry).
- **Step 2 — Decompose the Loading:** Break the complex combined loading into **simple, individual** load cases (e.g., point load, uniformly distributed load, moment).
- **Step 3 — Solve Each Case Independently:** Analyze each decomposed load case separately on the original geometry of the structure.
- **Step 4 — Algebraic Summation:** Add up the individual results (stresses, strains, deflections, reactions) at the desired location. The sign convention (tension positive, compression negative; upward deflection positive, downward negative) must be maintained consistently.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this work?** Because in a linearly elastic system, the governing differential equations (like the Euler-Bernoulli beam equation $\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \frac{M(x)}{EI}$) are **linear**. The sum of solutions to a linear equation is itself a solution — this is a fundamental property of linear operators.

## 2.2 Working Logic of Thermal Effects

The thermal analysis logic follows a parallel structure:

- **Step 1 — Identify Temperature Change:** Determine $\Delta T = T_{\text{final}} - T_{\text{initial}}$.
- **Step 2 — Compute Free Thermal Deformation:** If unrestrained, the free elongation is $\delta_{\text{thermal}} = \alpha L \Delta T$.
- **Step 3 — Check Restraint Condition:**
  - **Case A (No Restraint):** Free expansion occurs. **No thermal stress is induced.**
  - **Case B (Fully Restrained at Ends):** The actual elongation is **zero**. The member experiences a **reaction force** that produces a **compressive (heating) or tensile (cooling) thermal stress**.
- **Step 4 — Apply Superposition:** For statically indeterminate structures under thermal loading, use the **force method** (or displacement method) to find redundant reactions.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Conditions / Units |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Free Thermal Expansion | $\Delta L = \alpha \, L \, \Delta T$ | $\alpha$ in per °C, $L$ in m, $\Delta T$ in °C |
| 2 | Thermal Strain | $\varepsilon_{\text{th}} = \alpha \, \Delta T$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Thermal Stress (Fully Restrained) | $\sigma_{\text{th}} = E \, \alpha \, \Delta T$ | $E$ in N/m², $\sigma$ in N/m² |
| 4 | Thermal Force (Fully Restrained) | $P_{\text{th}} = \sigma_{\text{th}} \cdot A = E A \alpha \Delta T$ | $A$ in m², $P$ in N |
