| 5 | Superposition for Deflection | $y_{\text{total}} = y_1 + y_2 + y_3 + \ldots$ | $y$ in mm |
| 6 | Superposition for Bending Stress | $\sigma_{\text{total}} = \sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3 + \ldots$ | Sign convention must be consistent |
| 7 | Compatibility for Thermal in Indeterminate Beam | $\delta_{\text{thermal}} + \delta_{\text{reaction}} = 0$ | For fully restrained: actual length change = 0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention Pitfall:** For thermal effects, a **rise** in temperature ($\Delta T > 0$) causes **expansion** (positive $\Delta L$). If this expansion is restrained, the resulting stress is **compressive** (negative by KTU convention). Conversely, a **drop** in temperature causes **contraction** and **tensile** thermal stress if restrained.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Design of expansion joints in bridges, railway tracks, pipelines, and concrete dams to accommodate thermal movement.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Design of bimetallic strips in thermostats, thermal compensation in precision instruments, and boiler tubes.
- **Aerospace Engineering:** Thermal stress analysis of aircraft wings (which experience extreme temperature swings between ground and cruise altitude) and spacecraft components.
- **KTU Context:** Most KTU numerical problems on this topic fall into three categories: (i) finding **free thermal expansion**, (ii) finding **thermal stress in fully restrained bars**, and (iii) solving **statically indeterminate beams** using superposition with thermal loads.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Thermal Stress in a Fully Restrained Bar

**Problem Setup:** Consider a uniform bar of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, Young's modulus $E$, and coefficient of thermal expansion $\alpha$, fixed rigidly between two immovable walls. The temperature of the bar is raised by $\Delta T$.

**Step 1: Free Thermal Elongation (Imaginary Unrestrained Case)**
Imagine the walls are removed temporarily. The bar expands freely by:

$$
\delta_{\text{free}} = \alpha \, L \, \Delta T
$$

**Step 2: Compatibility Condition (Real Restrained Case)**
Since the walls are rigid, the actual elongation of the bar must be **zero**:

$$
\delta_{\text{actual}} = 0
$$

**Step 3: Superposition — Apply a Compressive Force**
To enforce the compatibility condition, imagine applying an axial compressive force $P$ to push the bar back to its original length. The shortening produced by this force is:

$$
\delta_{\text{force}} = \frac{P \, L}{A \, E}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the Compatibility Equation**
The net change in length must equal zero. Hence, the shortening due to the reaction force must exactly cancel the free thermal expansion:

$$
\delta_{\text{free}} - \delta_{\text{force}} = 0
$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$
\alpha \, L \, \Delta T - \frac{P \, L}{A \, E} = 0
$$

**Step 5: Solve for the Reaction Force $P$**
Rearranging the equation to isolate $P$:

$$
\frac{P \, L}{A \, E} = \alpha \, L \, \Delta T
$$

Cancel $L$ from both sides (since $L \neq 0$):

$$
P = E \, A \, \alpha \, \Delta T
$$

**Step 6: Derive the Thermal Stress**
Dividing the force by the cross-sectional area:

$$
\sigma_{\text{th}} = \frac{P}{A} = E \, \alpha \, \Delta T
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The thermal stress is **independent of the length** of the bar. This is a counterintuitive but very important result — whether the bar is 1 m or 100 m long, the thermal stress depends only on the material properties ($E$, $\alpha$) and the temperature change ($\Delta T$).

## 3.2 Derivation: Superposition Applied to a Simply Supported Beam with Multiple Loads

**Problem Setup:** A simply supported beam of length $L$, flexural rigidity $EI$, carries a central point load $W$ and a uniformly distributed load $w$ over its entire span.

**Step 1: Identify the Independent Load Cases**
- **Case 1:** Central point load $W$ alone.
- **Case 2:** Uniformly distributed load $w$ alone.

**Step 2: Standard Result — Central Deflection Due to Point Load $W$ at Midspan**

$$
\delta_1 = \frac{W \, L^3}{48 \, EI}
$$

**Step 3: Standard Result — Central Deflection Due to UDL $w$**

$$
\delta_2 = \frac{5 \, w \, L^4}{384 \, EI}
$$

**Step 4: Apply Superposition**
The total central deflection is the algebraic sum:

$$
\delta_{\text{total}} = \delta_1 + \delta_2
$$

$$
\delta_{\text{total}} = \frac{W \, L^3}{48 \, EI} + \frac{5 \, w \, L^4}{384 \, EI}
$$

Factorizing for a clean final form:

$$
\delta_{\text{total}} = \frac{L^3}{384 \, EI} \left( 8 \, W + 5 \, w \, L \right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** Because the differential equation governing beam deflection $EI \frac{d^4y}{dx^4} = w(x)$ is **linear** in $w(x)$, the solution for two load cases can simply be added. This is the mathematical foundation of superposition.

## 3.3 Derivation: Statically Indeterminate Beam with Thermal Loading Using Superposition

**Problem Setup:** A fixed-fixed beam of length $L$ is subjected to a uniform temperature rise $\Delta T$ across its depth. Find the redundant end moments.

**Step 1: Identify Redundants**
For a fixed-fixed beam, there are **6 reaction components** but only **3 equilibrium equations** are available. Hence, the beam is **3 degrees statically indeterminate**. To simplify, assume axial restraint only and focus on the bending effect due to a temperature gradient.

**Step 2: Apply Compatibility**
For a fixed-fixed beam heated uniformly:

$$
\delta_{\text{thermal}} + \delta_{\text{reaction}} = 0
$$

**Step 3: Compute Thermal Free Deflection**
If both ends were simply supported, the free thermal bowing curvature would be:

$$
\frac{1}{R} = \frac{\alpha \, \Delta T}{d}
$$

Where $d$ is the depth of the beam.

**Step 4: Solve for End Moments**
By applying the principle of superposition in the force method:

$$
M = \frac{E I \, \alpha \, \Delta T}{d}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Note:** For Module 1 "Simple" thermal effects, KTU typically tests **only axially restrained members** (trusses and bars), not thermal gradients across beam depth. Be prepared for both, but expect the simpler case in the first internal assessment.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Superposition Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Given Loading on Elastic Body] --> B{Is Material Linearly Elastic?}
    B -- NO --> B1[STOP: Superposition NOT Applicable]
    B -- YES --> C{Are Deflections Small?}
    C -- NO --> C1[STOP: Geometric Nonlinearity]
    C -- YES --> D[Decompose Loading into Simple Independent Cases]
    D --> E1[Case 1: Point Load Only]
    D --> E2[Case 2: UDL Only]
    D --> E3[Case 3: Moment Only]
    E1 --> F[Solve Each Case on Original Geometry]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Algebraically Sum Individual Results]
    G --> H[APPLY SIGN CONVENTION CONSISTENTLY]
    H --> I[Final Answer: Stress, Strain, Deflection]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Thermal Effect Analysis Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INPUT: Length L, Area A, E, alpha, dT] --> B[Compute Free Thermal Strain]
    B --> B1[eps_th = alpha times dT]
    B1 --> C{Is Member Restrained?}
    C -- NO --> D[Free Expansion: sigma_th = 0]
    C -- YES --> E[Compute Reaction Force P]
    E --> E1[P = E times A times alpha times dT]
    E1 --> F[Compute Thermal Stress]
    F --> F1[sigma_th = E times alpha times dT]
    F1 --> G[Compute Deflection if Needed]
    G --> H[OUTPUT: sigma_th, P, delta]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Force Method for Thermal Indeterminate Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Statically Indeterminate Beam Subjected to Thermal Load dT] --> B[Identify Primary Structure by Releasing Redundant]
    B --> C[Compute Free Thermal Displacement delta_T0 at Released Location]
    C --> D[Compute Flexibility Coefficient f11 due to Unit Redundant Force]
    D --> E[Apply Compatibility Equation: delta_T0 plus f11 times X1 equals 0]
    E --> F[Solve for Redundant X1]
    F --> G[Apply Superposition: Final Reactions equals Original plus Redundant Effects]
    G --> H[OUTPUT: Final Bending Moment, Shear Force, Thermal Stress Diagram]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Schematic: Beam Cross-Section Under Temperature Gradient

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Top Fiber: Temperature T1 Hotter] --> B[Top Fiber Tries to Expand More]
    C[Bottom Fiber: Temperature T2 Cooler] --> D[Bottom Fiber Tries to Expand Less]
    B --> E[Internal Curvature Develops]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Beam Bows: Concave Toward Hot Side]
    F --> G[Fixed Supports Restrain the Bowing]
    G --> H[Thermal Bending Stress Induced]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each node represents a logical state or computational step in the engineering analysis. The arrows show the direction of information flow. This is the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** representation recommended for thermal stress analysis problems where the physical geometry would be difficult to render directly.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

> **State the Principle of Superposition as applied to the analysis of beams subjected to bending.**

### Model Answer

The Principle of Superposition states that in a **linearly elastic** body where the deflections are small, the **stress, strain, and deflection** at any point caused by a system of loads acting simultaneously is equal to the **algebraic sum** of the stresses, strains, and deflections caused by each load acting **independently**. The essential conditions are: (i) the material must obey Hooke's Law, (ii) the deformations must be negligible compared to the original dimensions, and (iii) the principle does not apply where one load significantly affects the geometry or internal forces caused by another load.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

> **A steel rod of length 2 m is heated from 20 °C to 80 °C. Calculate the free thermal expansion. Take $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ per °C.**

### Model Answer

Given:
- $L = 2$ m
- $\Delta T = 80 - 20 = 60$ °C
- $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ per °C

Using the formula $\Delta L = \alpha \, L \, \Delta T$:

$$
\Delta L = 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times 60
$$

$$
\Delta L = 1440 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta L = 1.44 \, \text{mm}}
$$

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**

> **(a)** A steel bar of length 1.5 m and cross-sectional area 600 mm² is fixed between two rigid walls. The bar is heated through a temperature rise of 40 °C. Determine: **(i)** the thermal stress developed, and **(ii)** the reaction force exerted by the walls. Take $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm² and $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ per °C. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A simply supported beam of span 4 m carries a central point load of 30 kN and a uniformly distributed load of 10 kN/m over the entire span. Using the principle of superposition, calculate the maximum deflection. Take $EI = 8 \times 10^3$ kN·m². **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Thermal Stress in Restrained Bar

**Given:**
- $L = 1.5$ m = 1500 mm
- $A = 600$ mm²
- $\Delta T = 40$ °C
- $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm²
- $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ per °C

**Step 1: Compute Free Thermal Expansion**

$$
\Delta L_{\text{free}} = \alpha \, L \, \Delta T
$$

$$
\Delta L_{\text{free}} = 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 1500 \times 40
$$

$$
\Delta L_{\text{free}} = 0.72 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Compatibility Condition**
Since the walls are rigid:

$$
\delta_{\text{thermal}} - \delta_{\text{reaction}} = 0
$$

$$
\alpha \, L \, \Delta T - \frac{P \, L}{A \, E} = 0
$$

**Step 3: Solve for Reaction Force $P$**

$$
P = E \, A \, \alpha \, \Delta T
$$

$$
P = 2 \times 10^5 \times 600 \times 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 40
$$

$$
P = 57600 \, \text{N} = 57.6 \, \text{kN}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Thermal Stress**

$$
\sigma_{\text{th}} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{57600}{600}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{\text{th}} = 96 \, \text{N/mm}^2 = 96 \, \text{MPa (compressive)}}
$$

#### Part (b) — Superposition for Simply Supported Beam

**Given:**
- $L = 4$ m
- Central point load $W = 30$ kN
- UDL $w = 10$ kN/m
- $EI = 8 \times 10^3$ kN·m²

**Step 1: Deflection Due to Central Point Load**

$$
\delta_1 = \frac{W \, L^3}{48 \, EI}
$$

$$
\delta_1 = \frac{30 \times 4^3}{48 \times 8000}
$$

$$
\delta_1 = \frac{30 \times 64}{384000} = \frac{1920}{384000}
$$

$$
\delta_1 = 0.005 \, \text{m} = 5 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step 2: Deflection Due to UDL**

$$
\delta_2 = \frac{5 \, w \, L^4}{384 \, EI}
$$

$$
\delta_2 = \frac{5 \times 10 \times 4^4}{384 \times 8000}
$$

$$
\delta_2 = \frac{5 \times 10 \times 256}{3072000} = \frac{12800}{3072000}
$$

$$
\delta_2 = 0.004167 \, \text{m} \approx 4.167 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Superposition**

$$
\delta_{\text{total}} = \delta_1 + \delta_2
$$

$$
\delta_{\text{total}} = 5 + 4.167
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta_{\text{total}} = 9.167 \, \text{mm (downward)}}
$$

---

### Question B — Option B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

> **(a)** Explain the principle of superposition with two essential conditions. A simply supported beam of span 6 m carries a point load of 40 kN at 2 m from the left support. Using Macaulay's method and the principle of superposition, find the deflection under the load. Take $EI = 12 \times 10^3$ kN·m². **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A copper pipe of length 5 m is heated from 30 °C to 130 °C. If the pipe is **(i)** free to expand and **(ii)** rigidly clamped at both ends, find the free expansion and the thermal stress. Take $E_{\text{copper}} = 1 \times 10^5$ N/mm² and $\alpha = 17 \times 10^{-6}$ per °C. Area = 500 mm². **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Superposition and Deflection at Eccentric Point Load

**Principle:** Superposition applies when the structure is linearly elastic with small deflections. The two essential conditions are: **(1)** the material must obey Hooke's Law (stress is linearly proportional to strain), and **(2)** the deflections must be small enough that the change in geometry of the structure does not appreciably affect the internal forces.

**Given:**
- $L = 6$ m, $W = 40$ kN at $a = 2$ m from left, so $b = 4$ m
- $EI = 12 \times 10^3$ kN·m²

**Standard Formula:** Deflection under an eccentric point load on a simply supported beam:

$$
\delta = \frac{W \, a^2 \, b^2}{3 \, EI \, L}
$$

**Step 1: Substitute Values**

$$
\delta = \frac{40 \times 2^2 \times 4^2}{3 \times 12000 \times 6}
$$

$$
\delta = \frac{40 \times 4 \times 16}{216000}
$$

$$
\delta = \frac{2560}{216000}
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta = 0.01185 \, \text{m} = 11.85 \, \text{mm (downward)}}
$$

#### Part (b) — Copper Pipe: Free vs Restrained Thermal Analysis

**Given:**
- $L = 5$ m = 5000 mm
- $\Delta T = 130 - 30 = 100$ °C
- $\alpha = 17 \times 10^{-6}$ per °C
- $E = 1 \times 10^5$ N/mm²
- $A = 500$ mm²

**Case (i) — Free to Expand:**

$$
\Delta L_{\text{free}} = \alpha \, L \, \Delta T
$$

$$
\Delta L_{\text{free}} = 17 \times 10^{-6} \times 5000 \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta L_{\text{free}} = 8.5 \, \text{mm}}
$$

No thermal stress is induced because the pipe is free to expand: $\sigma_{\text{th}} = 0$.

**Case (ii) — Rigidly Clamped:**

The actual elongation is zero (compatibility), so the reaction force compresses the pipe back by 8.5 mm.

$$
P = \frac{E \, A \, \Delta L_{\text{free}}}{L}
$$

$$
P = \frac{1 \times 10^5 \times 500 \times 8.5}{5000}
$$

$$
P = 85000 \, \text{N} = 85 \, \text{kN (compressive)}
$$

Thermal stress:

$$
\sigma_{\text{th}} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{85000}{500}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{\text{th}} = 170 \, \text{N/mm}^2 = 170 \, \text{MPa (compressive)}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Losing Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Blunder:** Students frequently write thermal stress as "positive" for heating without noting it is **compressive**. KTU explicitly tests sign awareness. Always state: **heating + restrained = compressive stress**.
> 2. **Forgetting the "Free Expansion First" Step:** When solving restrained bar problems, students jump directly to $P = EA\alpha\Delta T$ without showing the **compatibility equation**. This costs at least 1–2 marks in part (a) of a 7-mark sub-question.
> 3. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing mm and m in the same calculation is the #1 error. Always **convert to a single unit system** before substituting into the formula.
> 4. **Applying Superposition to Nonlinear Problems:** Superposition is **invalid** for problems involving plastic deformation, large deflections, or instability (buckling). Students occasionally misapply it to such cases, losing full marks.
> 5. **Macaulay's Method — Missing $x > a$ vs $x < a$ Brackets:** When using Macaulay's method, the singularity function $\langle x - a \rangle^n$ must be written with proper Macaulay brackets. Forgetting the brackets costs 2–3 marks easily.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- ✅ The Principle of Superposition is **valid only** for **linearly elastic** materials with **small deflections**.
- ✅ Free thermal expansion: $\Delta L = \alpha L \Delta T$. **No stress** develops if the member is unrestrained.
- ✅ Restrained thermal stress: $\sigma_{\text{th}} = E \alpha \Delta T$. **Independent of length** — only depends on material ($E$, $\alpha$) and $\Delta T$.
- ✅ Heating + restraint = **compressive** stress. Cooling + restraint = **tensile** stress.
- ✅ Superposition formula for deflection: $\delta_{\text{total}} = \delta_1 + \delta_2 + \ldots$
- ✅ The three pillars of KTU Module 1 superposition: **standard formula table must be memorized** (point load, UDL, moment).
- ✅ For indeterminate thermal problems, use **Force Method** with $\delta_{\text{thermal}} + \delta_{\text{reaction}} = 0$.
- ✅ Standard thermal expansion coefficients: Steel = $12 \times 10^{-6}$/°C, Concrete = $10 \times 10^{-6}$/°C, Copper = $17 \times 10^{-6}$/°C, Aluminum = $23 \times 10^{-6}$/°C.
- ✅ Always write **compatibility equation explicitly** before solving for redundant forces.
- ✅ KTU typical question pattern: **Part (a) = pure thermal bar problem**, **Part (b) = superposition on simply supported beam**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Composite Bars
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT402 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Composite Bars

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition

A **Composite Bar** (also termed a **compound bar**, **composite member**, or **bi-metallic bar**) is a structural member consisting of **two or more distinct engineering materials** rigidly bonded together along their longitudinal axis, such that the assembly behaves as a **single integral unit** when subjected to external mechanical loading and/or thermal effects.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim from Syllabus):** *"A member composed of two or more different materials, firmly connected together to act as a single unit, is called a composite bar."*

The materials are usually **metals** (e.g., steel and brass, copper and aluminum) or **metal–alloy combinations**, but the framework extends conceptually to **reinforced concrete** and **fiber-reinforced polymers (FRP)**. The fundamental analytical requirement is that the **strain at the interface** between the constituent materials must be **equal** (compatibility), while the **load is shared** in proportion to the **axial stiffness** of each component (equilibrium).

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The Tug-of-War Logic

Imagine **two people of different strengths** pulling a heavy sack tied to a single rope. Both pull on the same rope (so the rope stretches by the same amount), but the **stronger person bears more load** than the weaker one. Neither can pull independently of the other — they are mechanically coupled.

| Scenario | Real-World Equivalent | Mathematical Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Two people on one rope | Two materials in a composite bar | Equal elongation → **Compatibility Equation** |
| Stronger person pulls harder | Stiffer material carries more load | $P \propto AE$ → **Equilibrium Equation** |
| Rope is common | Bar is common | Strain $\varepsilon$ is the same across both |
| Rope has a limit | Material has yield stress | Stress $\sigma$ differs by $E$ ratio |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants Used Throughout This Module:**
> - Young's Modulus of Steel: $E_s = \mathbf{2 \times 10^5 \; N/mm^2}$ (most common)
> - Young's Modulus of Copper: $E_c = \mathbf{1 \times 10^5 \; N/mm^2}$
> - Young's Modulus of Brass: $E_b = \mathbf{1 \times 10^5 \; N/mm^2}$
> - Young's Modulus of Aluminum: $E_a = \mathbf{0.7 \times 10^5 \; N/mm^2}$
> - Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (Steel): $\alpha_s = \mathbf{12 \times 10^{-6} / ^\circ C}$
> - Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (Copper): $\alpha_c = \mathbf{18 \times 10^{-6} / ^\circ C}$
> - Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (Brass): $\alpha_b = \mathbf{19 \times 10^{-6} / ^\circ C}$

## 1.3 Why Composite Bars Are Used in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Motivation:** No single material possesses an optimal combination of **high strength**, **light weight**, **corrosion resistance**, and **thermal stability**. Composite bars allow engineers to **combine complementary properties** — e.g., a high-strength steel core surrounded by a corrosion-resistant copper sheath in overhead transmission cables, or a steel-reinforced concrete column in civil construction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physics — The Two Governing Conditions

Every composite bar problem in the KTU syllabus reduces to solving **two simultaneous equations** derived from two fundamental laws of mechanics:

### Condition 1 — Compatibility (Geometric Condition)
> The total elongation of **each** constituent material must be **equal** because the materials are rigidly bonded at the interface.

$$\delta_1 = \delta_2 = \delta_3 = \ldots = \delta_n$$

This is the **kinematic constraint** — without it, the bond between materials would fracture.

### Condition 2 — Equilibrium (Static Condition)
> The sum of internal forces developed in **all** constituent materials must equal the **applied external load** (or reaction force).

$$P_{\text{ext}} = P_1 + P_2 + P_3 + \ldots + P_n$$

This is the **Newtonian force balance** — the assembly is in static equilibrium.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Flow for Solving Composite Bar Problems

For a two-material composite bar (Material 1 and Material 2) of equal length $L$ under axial load $P$:

1. **Apply Compatibility:** $\delta_1 = \delta_2 \Rightarrow \dfrac{P_1 L_1}{A_1 E_1} = \dfrac{P_2 L_2}{A_2 E_2}$
2. **Apply Equilibrium:** $P_1 + P_2 = P$ (where $P$ is the externally applied load)
3. **Solve the two simultaneous equations** to obtain $P_1$ and $P_2$.
4. **Compute stresses** using $\sigma_1 = P_1/A_1$ and $\sigma_2 = P_2/A_2$.
5. **Compute total elongation** using $\delta = P_1 L_1 / (A_1 E_1)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Students frequently confuse the **"same stress"** assumption (valid only when $A_1 = A_2$ and $E_1 = E_2$) with the **"same strain"** assumption (always valid for bonded composite bars). The KTU answer key will mark you down for assuming equal stress.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Elongation of single bar | $\delta = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$ | mm | Hooke's Law application |
| 2 | Axial stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | N/mm² | Internal force per unit area |
| 3 | Axial strain | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\sigma}{E} = \dfrac{\delta}{L}$ | dimensionless | Same for both materials |
| 4 | Compatibility equation | $\dfrac{P_1 L_1}{A_1 E_1} = \dfrac{P_2 L_2}{A_2 E_2}$ | — | Core governing equation |
| 5 | Equilibrium equation | $P_1 + P_2 = P_{\text{applied}}$ | N | Newton's third law |
| 6 | Load sharing ratio | $\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \dfrac{A_1 E_1}{A_2 E_2}$ | — | Derived from (4) |
| 7 | Stress ratio | $\dfrac{\sigma_1}{\sigma_2} = \dfrac{E_1}{E_2}$ | — | Independent of area |
| 8 | Equivalent stiffness (parallel) | $A_{\text{eq}} E_{\text{eq}} = A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2$ | N | For two parallel materials |
| 9 | Equivalent area (single material) | $A_{\text{eq}} = A_1 + \dfrac{E_2}{E_1} A_2$ | mm² | Transform Section Method |
| 10 | Equivalent length (single material) | $L_{\text{eq}} = \dfrac{L_1 + L_2 \cdot (E_2/E_1)(A_2/A_1)}{1}$ | mm | Series arrangement |
| 11 | Free thermal elongation | $\delta_T = \alpha L \Delta T$ | mm | For unrestrained bar |
| 12 | Total deformation | $\delta = \delta_{\text{mech}} + \delta_{\text{thermal}}$ | mm | Superposition principle |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Applications of Composite Bar Theory:**
> - **Bimetallic strips** in thermostats and circuit breakers (differential thermal expansion triggers switching)
> - **Steel-reinforced concrete** columns and beams in civil structures
> - **Overhead electrical conductors** (steel core for tensile strength, aluminum strands for conductivity)
> - **Railroad rails** in extreme climates (composite material analysis for thermal stress)
> - **Aircraft fuselage skins** (aluminum–lithium composite layers)
> - **Bi-metallic thermometers** in process industries

The governing equations derived here are **universal** — they apply unchanged to any bonded structural assembly, regardless of scale, from micro-electro-mechanical systems (MEMS) to kilometer-long suspension bridge cables.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Compatibility and Load-Sharing Relation

**Statement to Prove:** When two materials of equal length $L$ are bonded and loaded axially, the load shared by each material is proportional to the product $AE$ of that material.

**Starting Point:** Hooke's Law for axial deformation of a uniform bar.

$$\delta = \frac{PL}{AE}$$

**For Material 1** (area $A_1$, modulus $E_1$, internal load $P_1$, length $L_1$):

$$\delta_1 = \frac{P_1 L_1}{A_1 E_1}$$

**For Material 2** (area $A_2$, modulus $E_2$, internal load $P_2$, length $L_2$):

$$\delta_2 = \frac{P_2 L_2}{A_2 E_2}$$

**Applying Compatibility (equal elongations since bonded):**

$$\delta_1 = \delta_2$$

$$\frac{P_1 L_1}{A_1 E_1} = \frac{P_2 L_2}{A_2 E_2}$$

**Assuming equal lengths** $L_1 = L_2 = L$ (the most common KTU problem setup):

$$\frac{P_1}{A_1 E_1} = \frac{P_2}{A_2 E_2}$$

**Rearranging for the load-sharing ratio:**

$$\boxed{\frac{P_1}{P_2} = \frac{A_1 E_1}{A_2 E_2}}$$

**Equilibrium (Newton's first law):**

$$P_1 + P_2 = P$$

**Solving the two equations simultaneously:**

$$P_1 = P \cdot \frac{A_1 E_1}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}$$

$$P_2 = P \cdot \frac{A_2 E_2}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** The material with the **larger $AE$ product** carries a **larger share** of the load. This product $AE$ is called the **axial rigidity** of the bar.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stress Ratio in a Composite Bar

**Statement to Prove:** The stress in each material is in direct proportion to its Young's modulus.

**From the load-sharing relation:**

$$P_1 = \frac{A_1 E_1}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2} \cdot P$$

**The stress in Material 1:**

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{P_1}{A_1} = \frac{E_1}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2} \cdot P$$

**The stress in Material 2:**

$$\sigma_2 = \frac{P_2}{A_2} = \frac{E_2}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2} \cdot P$$

**Taking the ratio:**

$$\frac{\sigma_1}{\sigma_2} = \frac{E_1 / (A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2)}{E_2 / (A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2)} = \frac{E_1}{E_2}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\sigma_1}{\sigma_2} = \frac{E_1}{E_2}}$$

> **Critical Insight:** The stress ratio is **independent of cross-sectional area**. It depends **solely on the Young's modulus ratio**. This is a high-frequency KTU exam result.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Total Elongation of a Composite Bar

**Using Material 1's parameters:**

$$\delta = \delta_1 = \frac{P_1 L_1}{A_1 E_1} = \frac{P L}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}$$

**Cross-check using Material 2's parameters:**

$$\delta = \delta_2 = \frac{P_2 L_2}{A_2 E_2} = \frac{P L}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}$$

Both give the **same result** (consistency check ✓).

$$\boxed{\delta = \frac{P L}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}}$$

This is the **parallel-connection** formula for springs (with stiffness $k = AE/L$), confirming that composite bars behave as **springs in parallel**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Composite Bar with Series Arrangement of Two Materials

In the **series case**, the **two materials are connected end-to-end** (not side-by-side), and the **same load $P$** passes through both. The total elongation is the **sum of individual elongations**.

**Given:** Material 1 of length $L_1$, area $A_1$, modulus $E_1$ in series with Material 2 of length $L_2$, area $A_2$, modulus $E_2$, both carrying load $P$.

**Elongation of Material 1:**

$$\delta_1 = \frac{P L_1}{A_1 E_1}$$

**Elongation of Material 2:**

$$\delta_2 = \frac{P L_2}{A_2 E_2}$$

**Total Elongation (compatibility, additive):**

$$\delta = \delta_1 + \delta_2 = P \left( \frac{L_1}{A_1 E_1} + \frac{L_2}{A_2 E_2} \right)$$

**Equivalent Length of a uniform bar of Material 1 having the same total elongation:**

$$\frac{P L_{\text{eq}}}{A_1 E_1} = P \left( \frac{L_1}{A_1 E_1} + \frac{L_2}{A_2 E_2} \right)$$

$$\boxed{L_{\text{eq}} = L_1 + L_2 \cdot \frac{A_1 E_1}{A_2 E_2}}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Thermal Stresses in a Composite Bar

**Problem Setup:** A composite bar (two materials bonded) is subjected to a temperature rise $\Delta T$. The bar is **restrained** at both ends (no free expansion allowed).

**Free (unrestrained) thermal expansion** of the bar (both materials expand equally since bonded):

$$\delta_{\text{thermal}} = \alpha_{\text{eff}} \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$$

where $\alpha_{\text{eff}}$ is an **effective** coefficient of thermal expansion for the composite.

**For un-restrained free expansion** (no external force, no stress — but internal force exists if materials have different $\alpha$):

Actually, the **complete thermal-stress problem** is more involved. The textbook approach is:

**Step 1 — Assume the bar expands freely.** Both materials try to expand by $\alpha_1 L \Delta T$ and $\alpha_2 L \Delta T$ respectively.

**Step 2 — Compatibility constraint** (they must expand by the same amount since bonded). This requires an **internal force** $P$ in the bar.

**Step 3 — Total elongation = Mechanical elongation + Free thermal elongation**

$$\delta = \frac{P L}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2} + \alpha_{\text{avg}} L \Delta T$$

**If the bar is fully constrained** (ends fixed, $\delta = 0$):

$$0 = \frac{P L}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2} + \alpha_{\text{avg}} L \Delta T$$

$$P = -(A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2) \alpha_{\text{avg}} \Delta T$$

> **Note on Sign Convention:** A positive $\Delta T$ with rigid constraints produces a **compressive** internal force (the bar pushes against the walls). The stress in each material is:

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{E_1 \alpha_{\text{avg}} \Delta T \cdot A_1 E_1}{(A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2) A_1} = \text{compressive}$$

---

## 3.6 Worked Example — KTU-Style Numerical

**Problem:** A composite bar consists of a steel rod of 30 mm diameter enclosed in a brass tube of 50 mm outer diameter and 30 mm inner diameter. The composite bar carries an axial tensile load of **80 kN**. Find:
**(a)** Stresses in steel and brass
**(b)** Extension of the composite bar over a length of **200 mm**

Take: $E_s = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$, $E_b = 1 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$.

**Given Data:**
- Steel: $d_s = 30 \text{ mm}$, $A_s = \frac{\pi}{4}(30)^2 = 706.86 \text{ mm}^2$
- Brass: $D_o = 50 \text{ mm}$, $D_i = 30 \text{ mm}$, $A_b = \frac{\pi}{4}(50^2 - 30^2) = 1256.64 \text{ mm}^2$
- $L = 200 \text{ mm}$, $P = 80{,}000 \text{ N}$

**Step 1 — Calculate Axial Rigidities:**

$$A_s E_s = 706.86 \times 2 \times 10^5 = 141.37 \times 10^6 \text{ N}$$

$$A_b E_b = 1256.64 \times 1 \times 10^5 = 125.66 \times 10^6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Total Axial Rigidity:**

$$AE_{\text{total}} = (141.37 + 125.66) \times 10^6 = 267.03 \times 10^6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Load Shared by Each Material:**

$$P_s = P \cdot \frac{A_s E_s}{A_s E_s + A_b E_b} = 80{,}000 \times \frac{141.37}{267.03} = 42{,}359 \text{ N}$$

$$P_b = P \cdot \frac{A_b E_b}{A_s E_s + A_b E_b} = 80{,}000 \times \frac{125.66}{267.03} = 37{,}641 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Stresses:**

$$\sigma_s = \frac{P_s}{A_s} = \frac{42{,}359}{706.86} = 59.92 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 60 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_b = \frac{P_b}{A_b} = \frac{37{,}641}{1256.64} = 29.95 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 30 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Verify Stress Ratio:**

$$\frac{\sigma_s}{\sigma_b} = \frac{60}{30} = 2 = \frac{E_s}{E_b} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{1 \times 10^5} \;\; \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Total Elongation:**

$$\delta = \frac{P L}{A_s E_s + A_b E_b} = \frac{80{,}000 \times 200}{267.03 \times 10^6} = 0.0599 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (a) $\sigma_s = 60 \text{ MPa}$ (tensile), $\sigma_b = 30 \text{ MPa}$ (tensile)
- (b) $\delta = 0.0599 \text{ mm} \approx 0.06 \text{ mm}$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Composite Bar Load-Transfer System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **load-transfer mechanism** inside a two-material composite bar subjected to axial tension. The diagram maps the flow of force, strain compatibility, and stress distribution.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inputA["APPLIED EXTERNAL LOAD P (Newtons)"]:::inputNode
    splitPoint{"LOAD DISTRIBUTION JUNCTION - STATIC EQUILIBRIUM"}:::junctionNode
    matBlock1["MATERIAL 1 BLOCK - Cross Section A1, Modulus E1"]:::material1Node
    matBlock2["MATERIAL 2 BLOCK - Cross Section A2, Modulus E2"]:::material2Node
    forceShare1["INTERNAL LOAD P1 = P times AE1 over AE_total"]:::forceNode
    forceShare2["INTERNAL LOAD P2 = P times AE2 over AE_total"]:::forceNode
    stressBlock1["STRESS sigma1 = P1 divided by A1"]:::stressNode
    stressBlock2["STRESS sigma2 = P2 divided by A2"]:::stressNode
    strainEq["COMPATIBILITY EQUALITY delta1 = delta2"]:::compatNode
    elongOut["TOTAL ELONGATION delta = PL over AE_total"]:::outputNode
    ratioCheck["STRESS RATIO CHECK sigma1 over sigma2 = E1 over E2"]:::verifyNode

    inputA --> splitPoint
    splitPoint --> forceShare1 --> matBlock1
    splitPoint --> forceShare2 --> matBlock2
    matBlock1 --> stressBlock1
    matBlock2 --> stressBlock2
    matBlock1 --> strainEq
    matBlock2 --> strainEq
    strainEq --> elongOut
    stressBlock1 --> ratioCheck
    stressBlock2 --> ratioCheck

    classDef inputNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef junctionNode fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef material1Node fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef material2Node fill:#2E8B57,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef forceNode fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stressNode fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef compatNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef verifyNode fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Schematic Description — Physical Composite Bar Geometry

The diagram below represents a **concentric composite bar** (steel core + brass tube) as seen in cross-section. This is the most commonly tested configuration in KTU exams.

```
+----------------------------------------------------+
|                  CONCENTRIC COMPOSITE BAR          |
|                  (Cross-Section View)              |
+----------------------------------------------------+

                  <---------- 50 mm ---------->
                  |                            |
                  |    +-----------------+     |
                  |    |                 |     |  ^
                  |    |   BRASS TUBE     |     |  |
                  |    |   Outer 50 mm    |     |  50 mm
                  |    |   Inner 30 mm    |     |  |
                  |    |                 |     |  v
                  |    |   +---------+   |     |
                  |    |   |  STEEL  |   |     |  ^
                  |    |   |  CORE   |   |     |  |
                  |    |   |  30 mm  |   |     |  30 mm
                  |    |   |  Dia    |   |     |  |
                  |    |   +---------+   |     |  v
                  |    +-----------------+     |
                  |                            |
                  +----------------------------+

         Area of Steel :  A_s = pi/4 x 30^2
         Area of Brass :  A_b = pi/4 x (50^2 - 30^2)
         Total Area    :  A_tot = A_s + A_b
```

## 4.3 Free-Body Concept — Force Split at the Junction

```
        APPLIED LOAD                      REACTION AT FIXED END
        P (downward)                      P (upward)
            |                                 ^
            v                                 |
    +---------------+                 +---------------+
    |  FREE BODY 1  |                 |  FREE BODY 2  |
    |   (Steel)     |                 |   (Brass)     |
    |               |                 |               |
    |   <---P1      |                 |   <---P2      |
    |               |                 |               |
    |   A1, E1      |                 |   A2, E2      |
    +---------------+                 +---------------+
            \                               /
             \                             /
              \                           /
               \                         /
                +---------|-------------+
                          |
                  BONDED INTERFACE
                  (Same elongation enforced)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The total external load $P$ enters at the top, splits at the bonded interface into $P_1$ (carried by steel) and $P_2$ (carried by brass), and recombines at the bottom support. The constraint that both materials must elongate by the **same amount** is enforced by the **bonded interface** in the middle.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: Define a composite bar. State the two fundamental conditions that govern its analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A composite bar is a structural member made of two or more different materials rigidly joined together along their length so that the assembly behaves as a single unit under load.

The two governing conditions are:

**1. Compatibility Condition (Geometric):** Since the materials are bonded, the elongation of each material must be equal, i.e., $\delta_1 = \delta_2 = \delta_3 = \ldots$

**2. Equilibrium Condition (Static):** The algebraic sum of the internal forces developed in each material must equal the applied external load, i.e., $P = P_1 + P_2 + P_3 + \ldots$

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Q: In a composite bar of two materials, the stresses in the two materials are always in the ratio of their Young's moduli. Justify this statement.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a composite bar, the compatibility condition gives:

$$\frac{P_1 L_1}{A_1 E_1} = \frac{P_2 L_2}{A_2 E_2}$$

Assuming equal lengths $L_1 = L_2$:

$$P_1 A_2 E_2 = P_2 A_1 E_1$$

Now, $\sigma_1 = P_1/A_1$ and $\sigma_2 = P_2/A_2$. Therefore:

$$\sigma_1 A_2 E_2 = \sigma_2 A_1 E_1$$

But by compatibility $\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_2$, i.e., $\sigma_1 / E_1 = \sigma_2 / E_2$, which simplifies to:

$$\boxed{\frac{\sigma_1}{\sigma_2} = \frac{E_1}{E_2}}$$

Hence, stresses are in the ratio of Young's moduli, **independent of cross-sectional area**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Step-marker penalty:** Examiners award partial credit for each clearly labeled step (Free Body Diagram → Compatibility → Equilibrium → Numerical Computation → Final Answer). Skipping the FBD stage costs 2 marks.
> - **Unit inconsistency is the #1 mistake.** A stress value of "60" without "N/mm²" or "MPa" gets a **0.5-mark deduction** per occurrence.
> - **Don't assume equal stress in a composite bar.** Equal stress holds only when both materials have the same Young's modulus. The KTU key will mark you down for this.
> - **Show the load ratio derivation explicitly** — writing only the final answers $P_1$ and $P_2$ without showing the governing equations loses 3–4 marks.
> - **Always verify your final answer** using the stress ratio check $\sigma_1/\sigma_2 = E_1/E_2$ — it earns a "verification" mark.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

A composite bar consists of a steel rod of **20 mm diameter** firmly attached to a brass tube of **30 mm outer diameter** and **20 mm inner diameter**. The bar is **2 m** long and carries an axial tensile load of **50 kN** at its free end. Calculate:

**(a)** The stresses developed in the steel and brass sections. (7 marks)

**(b)** The total elongation of the bar. (7 marks)

**Given:** $E_s = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$, $E_b = 1 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$

#### Solution:

**Given Data:**

$$A_s = \frac{\pi}{4}(20)^2 = 314.16 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_b = \frac{\pi}{4}(30^2 - 20^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(900 - 400) = 392.70 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$L = 2000 \text{ mm}, \quad P = 50{,}000 \text{ N}$$

#### Part (a) — Stress Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Apply Compatibility:**

$$\frac{P_s L}{A_s E_s} = \frac{P_b L}{A_b E_b}$$

$$\frac{P_s}{A_s E_s} = \frac{P_b}{A_b E_b}$$

$$P_s = P_b \cdot \frac{A_s E_s}{A_b E_b}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Equilibrium:**

$$P_s + P_b = 50{,}000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Axial Rigidities:**

$$A_s E_s = 314.16 \times 2 \times 10^5 = 6.283 \times 10^7 \text{ N}$$

$$A_b E_b = 392.70 \times 1 \times 10^5 = 3.927 \times 10^7 \text{ N}$$

$$A_s E_s + A_b E_b = 10.21 \times 10^7 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $P_s$ and $P_b$:**

$$P_s = 50{,}000 \times \frac{6.283 \times 10^7}{10.21 \times 10^7} = 50{,}000 \times 0.6154 = 30{,}769 \text{ N} \approx 30.77 \text{ kN}$$

$$P_b = 50{,}000 - 30{,}769 = 19{,}231 \text{ N} \approx 19.23 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Stresses:**

$$\sigma_s = \frac{P_s}{A_s} = \frac{30{,}769}{314.16} = 97.94 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 98 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

$$\sigma_b = \frac{P_b}{A_b} = \frac{19{,}231}{392.70} = 48.97 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 49 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

**Verification:** $\sigma_s / \sigma_b = 98/49 = 2 = E_s / E_b$ ✓

#### Part (b) — Total Elongation (7 marks)

**Step 6 — Calculate Elongation of Steel Section:**

$$\delta_s = \frac{P_s L}{A_s E_s} = \frac{30{,}769 \times 2000}{6.283 \times 10^7} = \frac{6.154 \times 10^7}{6.283 \times 10^7} = 0.9795 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 7 — Calculate Elongation of Brass Section (cross-check):**

$$\delta_b = \frac{P_b L}{A_b E_b} = \frac{19{,}231 \times 2000}{3.927 \times 10^7} = \frac{3.846 \times 10^7}{3.927 \times 10^7} = 0.9795 \text{ mm} \;\; \checkmark$$

**Step 8 — Total Elongation (using shortcut formula):**

$$\delta = \frac{P L}{A_s E_s + A_b E_b} = \frac{50{,}000 \times 2000}{10.21 \times 10^7} = \frac{10^8}{10.21 \times 10^7} = 0.9795 \text{ mm}$$

#### **Final Answers:**
- (a) $\sigma_s = 98 \text{ MPa}$, $\sigma_b = 49 \text{ MPa}$ (both tensile)
- (b) $\delta = 0.98 \text{ mm}$

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2022] — Alternative Choice

A **3-meter long** composite bar is made of copper and steel sections placed end-to-end (series arrangement). The copper section has a length of **1.5 m**, area of **1500 mm²**, and Young's modulus $1 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$. The steel section has a length of **1.5 m**, area of **1000 mm²**, and Young's modulus $2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$. If the bar is subjected to an axial tensile load of **60 kN**, determine:

**(a)** The stresses in the copper and steel sections. (7 marks)

**(b)** The total elongation of the composite bar. (7 marks)

#### Solution:

**Given Data:**

| Section | Length (mm) | Area (mm²) | Modulus (N/mm²) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Copper | $L_c = 1500$ | $A_c = 1500$ | $E_c = 1 \times 10^5$ |
| Steel | $L_s = 1500$ | $A_s = 1000$ | $E_s = 2 \times 10^5$ |

**Applied load:** $P = 60{,}000 \text{ N}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Series vs. Parallel Distinction:** In a **series** arrangement, the **same load $P$** passes through both materials (no load sharing). In a **parallel** arrangement, the load is **shared** between materials. This problem tests the **series** configuration.

#### Part (a) — Stress Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify that this is a series arrangement, so $P_c = P_s = P = 60{,}000$ N.**

**Step 2 — Stress in Copper Section:**

$$\sigma_c = \frac{P}{A_c} = \frac{60{,}000}{1500} = 40 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 40 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

**Step 3 — Stress in Steel Section:**

$$\sigma_s = \frac{P}{A_s} = \frac{60{,}000}{1000} = 60 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 60 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

> **Note:** In a series arrangement, stresses are determined purely by $P$ and $A$ — **Young's modulus does not affect the stress distribution in series**, only the deformation.

#### Part (b) — Total Elongation (7 marks)

**Step 4 — Elongation of Copper Section:**

$$\delta_c = \frac{P L_c}{A_c E_c} = \frac{60{,}000 \times 1500}{1500 \times 1 \times 10^5} = \frac{9 \times 10^7}{1.5 \times 10^8} = 0.6 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Elongation of Steel Section:**

$$\delta_s = \frac{P L_s}{A_s E_s} = \frac{60{,}000 \times 1500}{1000 \times 2 \times 10^5} = \frac{9 \times 10^7}{2 \times 10^8} = 0.45 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Total Elongation (compatibility — additive in series):**

$$\delta = \delta_c + \delta_s = 0.6 + 0.45 = 1.05 \text{ mm}$$

#### **Final Answers:**
- (a) $\sigma_c = 40 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$, $\sigma_s = 60 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$
- (b) $\delta = 1.05 \text{ mm}$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Frequency KTU Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Definition:** Composite bar = bonded assembly of 2+ materials behaving as a single unit.
- **Two governing equations — always:** (1) **Compatibility:** $\delta_1 = \delta_2$ (2) **Equilibrium:** $P_1 + P_2 = P$.
- **Parallel (concentric) arrangement** → load shared, strain same.
- **Series (end-to-end) arrangement** → load same, deformation additive.
- **Load-sharing formula:** $P_1 = P \cdot \dfrac{A_1 E_1}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}$ (parallel case).
- **Stress ratio identity:** $\dfrac{\sigma_1}{\sigma_2} = \dfrac{E_1}{E_2}$ — **independent of area**, true for parallel arrangement.
- **Total elongation (parallel):** $\delta = \dfrac{PL}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}$ — analogous to springs in parallel.
- **Total elongation (series):** $\delta = PL_1/(A_1 E_1) + PL_2/(A_2 E_2)$ — analogous to springs in series.
- **Strain is the same in parallel composite bars** (most-tested fact).
- **Material with higher $AE$ carries more load** in a parallel composite bar.
- **Thermal stress in restrained composite bar:** produces compressive internal force when $\Delta T > 0$.
- **Always write units:** N/mm² or MPa for stress, mm for elongation, N for force.
- **Verify with the ratio check** $\sigma_1 / \sigma_2 = E_1 / E_2$ to catch arithmetic errors.
- **Common Young's modulus values to memorize:** $E_{steel} = 2 \times 10^5$, $E_{brass} = E_{copper} = 1 \times 10^5$, $E_{aluminum} = 0.7 \times 10^5$ (all in N/mm²).
- **Always draw a Free Body Diagram** before writing equations — earns 2 marks outright.
- **Two arrangements tested:** (i) materials **side-by-side** (parallel — load shared) (ii) materials **end-to-end** (series — load common).
- **Equivalent area (transform section):** $A_{eq} = A_1 + A_2 (E_2/E_1)$ — useful for finding elongation as if the bar were a single material.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shafts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT402 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Shafts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shaft:** A shaft is a rotating machine element, usually of circular cross-section, which is used to transmit power and torque from one rotating element to another. It is a structural member that is subjected primarily to **torsion**, and sometimes to combined **bending and torsion**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Axle:** An axle is a stationary member (does not rotate) used to support a rotating body (like a wheel). It is subjected primarily to **bending moment** only and does NOT transmit torque. This is the fundamental distinction between a **shaft** and an **axle**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Spindle:** A spindle is a short shaft that may or may not be used to transmit power. It is often associated with machine tool spindles where accuracy and stiffness are critical.

## 1.2 Classification of Shafts

| Type | Description | Loading |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **Line Shaft / Counter Shaft** | Supports multiple pulleys, used in workshops/factories. Long, slender, supported on bearings. | Transmit power between machines; subject to combined torsion, bending, and axial loads. |
| **Transmission Shaft (Line Shaft)** | Carries power from one machine to another via pulleys/gears. | Torsion + Bending. |
| **Machine Shaft (Stub Shaft)** | Integral part of the machine; one end attached to the body of the machine. | Carries torque, bending moments, and sometimes axial thrust. |
| **Axle** | Does not transmit torque; supports rotating wheels/rollers. | Pure bending (no torsion). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Exam Line:** A **shaft** is a rotating member; an **axle** is a stationary member. A shaft is subjected to torsion (and often bending), while an axle is subjected to bending only.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Drill Chuck Shaft:** Imagine the **chuck of a handheld power drill**. When you press the trigger, the motor generates a *twisting force* (torque) that travels through a long, thin metal rod (the shaft) to the chuck, which then rotates the drill bit. That long rod is twisting along its length — it is experiencing **torsion**. If you also push the drill forward (axial force) and bend it slightly sideways (bending moment), then the shaft experiences **combined bending and torsion** — exactly what happens in real machine components like gear shafts in a gearbox or the drive shaft of an automobile.

## 1.4 Standard Material Properties (Bolded)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 standard design problems, the following material properties are commonly assumed unless otherwise specified:
> - **Allowable Shear Stress (τ_allow) for steel shafts:** $\tau_{allow} = 40 \text{ to } 60 \text{ MPa}$ (typically **$40 \text{ MPa}$** for commercial steel and **$60 \text{ MPa}$** for alloy steel).
> - **Modulus of Rigidity (G) for steel:** $G = 80 \text{ GPa}$ (i.e., $80 \times 10^3 \text{ MPa}$).
> - **Standard ASME Code values** for combined shock and fatigue factors ($K_b$ and $K_t$).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Basic Theory of Pure Torsion

When a circular shaft of radius $r$ and length $L$ is subjected to a torque $T$ at one end (with the other end fixed):

1. **Plane sections remain plane and rotate as rigid discs** — the cross-section twists about the longitudinal axis by an angle $\theta$.
2. **The shear strain** at any point at radius $\rho$ is $\gamma = \rho \theta / L$ (proportional to the distance from the axis).
3. **The shear stress** is $\tau = G \gamma = G \rho \theta / L$ (linear distribution; zero at the center, maximum at the outer surface).
4. The total torque resisted by the cross-section is obtained by integrating the elemental shear forces around the area.

## 2.2 The Torsion Equation (The Single Most Important Equation)

$$\boxed{\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G \theta}{L}}$$

Where:
- $T$ = Applied Torque (N·m)
- $J$ = Polar Moment of Inertia of the cross-section about the longitudinal axis (m⁴ or mm⁴)
- $\tau$ = Shear stress at the outer surface (Pa or N/mm² = MPa)
- $r$ = Outer radius of the shaft (m or mm)
- $G$ = Modulus of Rigidity (Pa or MPa)
- $\theta$ = Angle of twist (radians)
- $L$ = Length of the shaft (m or mm)

## 2.3 Polar Moment of Inertia (J) and Polar Section Modulus (Z_p)

> [!NOTE]
> **Polar Section Modulus** is defined as $Z_p = J / r$ and is used to relate torque directly to the maximum shear stress: $\tau = T / Z_p$.

| Shaft Type | Polar Moment of Inertia ($J$) | Polar Section Modulus ($Z_p$) | Max Shear Stress ($\tau_{max}$) |
|------------|-------------------------------|------------------------------|---------------------------------|
| **Solid shaft** (diameter $d$) | $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$ | $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{16}$ | $\tau = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$ |
| **Hollow shaft** (outer $d_o$, inner $d_i$, $k = d_i / d_o$) | $J = \dfrac{\pi (d_o^4 - d_i^4)}{32} = \dfrac{\pi d_o^4 (1 - k^4)}{32}$ | $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi (d_o^4 - d_i^4)}{16 d_o} = \dfrac{\pi d_o^3 (1 - k^4)}{16}$ | $\tau = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d_o^3 (1 - k^4)}$ |

## 2.4 Power Transmission

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The power transmitted by a rotating shaft is given by the fundamental relation:
> $$P = 2\pi N T \quad \text{(if } N \text{ in rev/s)}$$ $$P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60} \quad \text{(if } N \text{ in rpm)}$$ Where $P$ is in **watts**, $N$ in **rpm**, and $T$ in **N·m**.

**Standard KTU trick:** $T (\text{N·m}) = \dfrac{60 P (\text{W})}{2\pi N (\text{rpm})}$ or $T (\text{N·m}) = \dfrac{9550 \times P (\text{kW})}{N (\text{rpm})}$ — a formula you should **memorize**.

## 2.5 Design Based on Strength and Rigidity

> [!NOTE]
> **Design based on Strength:** The shaft diameter is determined so that the maximum shear stress does not exceed the allowable shear stress. $\tau_{max} \le \tau_{allow}$.
>
> **Design based on Rigidity (Stiffness):** The shaft diameter is determined so that the angle of twist per unit length does not exceed the allowable twist. $\theta / L \le \theta_{allow}$ (typically $0.25°$ per metre or $0.5°$ per metre for line shafts).

## 2.6 The ASME Code for Combined Bending and Torsion (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ASME Code** is the **American Society of Mechanical Engineers** code for shaft design. It uses two equivalent moments to handle the **combined bending and torsion** problem:
> - **Equivalent Twisting Moment (Te):** Combines the bending and torque into a single equivalent torque.
> - **Equivalent Bending Moment (Me):** Combines the bending and torque into a single equivalent bending moment.

### Two Methodologies under the ASME Code:

**Method 1: Equivalent Twisting Moment (Te) Approach**

$$T_e = \sqrt{(K_b \cdot M)^2 + (K_t \cdot T)^2}$$

$$\boxed{d^3 = \dfrac{16}{\pi \tau_{max}} \cdot \sqrt{(K_b \cdot M)^2 + (K_t \cdot T)^2}}$$

**Method 2: Equivalent Bending Moment (Me) Approach**

$$M_e = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[K_b \cdot M + \sqrt{(K_b \cdot M)^2 + (K_t \cdot T)^2}\right]$$

$$\boxed{d^3 = \dfrac{32}{\pi \sigma_b} \cdot M_e = \dfrac{16}{\pi \sigma_b}\left[K_b M + \sqrt{(K_b M)^2 + (K_t T)^2}\right]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Pure Torsion Check:** When the shaft is subjected to torque only ($M = 0$), both methods reduce to $d^3 = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi \tau_{max}}$ — consistent with the simple torsion formula.
>
> **Pure Bending Check:** When there is bending only ($T = 0$), $T_e = K_b M$ and $M_e = K_b M$, giving $d^3 = \dfrac{16 K_b M}{\pi \tau_{max}}$ for a solid shaft. This uses the shear stress formula for a solid shaft with shear stress $\tau = (16/3) \sigma_b$, giving $\tau_{max} = 0.5 \sigma_b$. Many textbooks use $\tau = 0.5 \sigma_b$ for this reason, although the more conservative relation is $\tau_{max} = 0.577 \sigma_b$ (maximum shear stress theory) or $\tau_{max} = 0.62 \sigma_b$ for circular shafts.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $T/J = \tau/r = G\theta/L$ | Master torsion equation |
| 2 | $\tau = 16T / (\pi d^3)$ | Max shear stress in a **solid** shaft |
| 3 | $\tau = 16T / [\pi d_o^3 (1-k^4)]$ | Max shear stress in a **hollow** shaft ($k = d_i/d_o$) |
| 4 | $T (\text{N·m}) = 9550 \times P (\text{kW}) / N (\text{rpm})$ | Convert power to torque (very high-yield) |
| 5 | $\theta / L = T / (J G)$ | Rigidity-based design (twist per unit length) |
| 6 | $T_e = \sqrt{(K_b M)^2 + (K_t T)^2}$ | ASME Equivalent Twisting Moment |
| 7 | $M_e = \tfrac{1}{2}[K_b M + \sqrt{(K_b M)^2 + (K_t T)^2}]$ | ASME Equivalent Bending Moment |
| 8 | $d^3 = 16 T_e / (\pi \tau_{max})$ | Shaft diameter (strength) |
| 9 | $d^4 = 32 T \cdot 180 / (\pi^2 G N \cdot \theta_{allow})$ for twist in degrees per metre (line shaft) | Rigidity design with twist in °/m |
| 10 | $J = \pi d^4 / 32$ (solid), $J = \pi(d_o^4 - d_i^4)/32$ (hollow) | Polar moment of inertia |

### Standard ASME Code Load Factors (For Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU reference values for combined shock and fatigue factors:**
>
> | Loading Condition | $K_b$ (Bending) | $K_t$ (Torsion) |
> |---|---|---|
> | Gradually applied load (e.g., electric motor, turbine) | 1.5 | 1.0 |
> | Suddenly applied load with minor shock (e.g., belt drives, chain drives) | 1.5 to 2.0 | 1.0 to 1.5 |
> | Suddenly applied load with heavy shock (e.g., reciprocating engines, crushers) | 2.0 to 3.0 | 1.5 to 3.0 |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Shaft design is the **backbone of all rotating machinery** — every gearbox, automobile drive-train, turbine, electric motor, pump, and CNC spindle contains a shaft. Under-designed shafts lead to torsional failure (sudden fracture) or excessive angular deflection (loss of synchronization). The ASME code unifies combined loading into a single design equation, which is the *industry standard* taught in KTU and used by mechanical engineers worldwide.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Torsion Equation $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G \theta}{L}$

**Assumptions (Standard Saint-Venant's Torsion Theory):**
1. The shaft material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law.
2. Cross-sections plane before twisting remain plane and undistorted after twisting.
3. Radial lines in the cross-section remain radial and straight.
4. The shaft is loaded only by pure torques at its ends.

### Step 1: Consider a small element at radius $\rho$ from the center

A line originally on the surface of the shaft of length $L$ becomes a helix after twisting by an angle $\theta$ (radians) at the free end. The **shear strain** at radius $\rho$ is the deformation angle:

$$\gamma = \dfrac{\text{arc length}}{\text{length}} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \theta}{L}$$

### Step 2: Apply Hooke's Law in Shear

The shear stress at the same point is:

$$\tau_\rho = G \cdot \gamma = \dfrac{G \rho \theta}{L}$$

This shows that **shear stress varies linearly** with $\rho$: zero at the center and maximum at the outer surface.

### Step 3: Compute the Elemental Resisting Torque

Consider an elementary ring of radius $\rho$ and thickness $d\rho$. The elementary area is:

$$dA = 2\pi \rho \, d\rho$$

The elemental shear force on this ring is:

$$dF = \tau_\rho \cdot dA = \dfrac{G \rho \theta}{L} \cdot 2\pi \rho \, d\rho = \dfrac{2\pi G \theta}{L} \cdot \rho^2 \, d\rho$$

The elemental resisting torque about the axis is $dT = \rho \cdot dF$:

$$dT = \dfrac{2\pi G \theta}{L} \cdot \rho^3 \, d\rho$$

### Step 4: Integrate Over the Cross-Section

The total torque resisted by the cross-section is the sum of elemental torques:

$$T = \int dT = \dfrac{2\pi G \theta}{L} \int_0^r \rho^3 \, d\rho$$

$$T = \dfrac{2\pi G \theta}{L} \cdot \dfrac{r^4}{4} = \dfrac{\pi G \theta r^4}{2L}$$

### Step 5: Recognize the Polar Moment of Inertia

For a solid circular section, the polar moment of inertia is:

$$J = \int_0^r \rho^2 \, dA = \int_0^r \rho^2 \cdot 2\pi \rho \, d\rho = 2\pi \cdot \dfrac{r^4}{4} = \dfrac{\pi r^4}{2} = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$$

### Step 6: Combine the Results

From Step 4: $T = \dfrac{G \theta}{L} \cdot \dfrac{\pi r^4}{2} = \dfrac{G \theta J}{L}$.

Rearranging:

$$\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{G \theta}{L}$$

From Step 2: $\tau_\rho = \dfrac{G \rho \theta}{L}$. At $\rho = r$: $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{G r \theta}{L}$.

Dividing the two:

$$\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau_{max}}{r} = \dfrac{G \theta}{L}$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G \theta}{L}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the master equation** from which ALL other shaft design formulas in KTU are derived. Memorize it!

---

## 3.2 Derivation of $\tau = 16T/(\pi d^3)$ for a Solid Shaft

From the master equation:

$$\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau_{max}}{r}$$

Substitute $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$ and $r = d/2$:

$$\dfrac{T}{\pi d^4 / 32} = \dfrac{\tau_{max}}{d/2}$$

$$\dfrac{32 T}{\pi d^4} = \dfrac{2 \tau_{max}}{d}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$\tau_{max} \cdot \pi d^3 = 32 T \quad \text{(after multiplying both sides by } \pi d^4 \cdot d / 2\text{)}$$

$$\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$$

$$\boxed{\tau = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^3} \quad \text{(solid shaft)}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the ASME Equivalent Twisting Moment (Te)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ASME code uses the **maximum shear stress theory** (also called the **distortion energy theory** in some texts, but here specifically the **Gerber-Coulomb theory of combined stresses** simplified for shaft design).

For a shaft under combined bending moment $M$ and torque $T$, the principal stresses are:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{32 M}{\pi d^3} \pm \dfrac{16 \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}}{\pi d^3}$$

The maximum shear stress in the shaft is therefore:

$$\tau_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2} \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 4 \tau_t^2}$$

Substituting $\sigma_b = 32 M / (\pi d^3)$ and $\tau_t = 16 T / (\pi d^3)$:

$$\tau_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2} \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{32 M}{\pi d^3}\right)^2 + 4 \left(\dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^3}\right)^2}$$

$$\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16}{\pi d^3} \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$$

Therefore, equating $\tau_{max} \le \tau_{allow}$:

$$\dfrac{16}{\pi d^3} \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} \le \tau_{allow}$$

$$d^3 \ge \dfrac{16}{\pi \tau_{allow}} \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$$

Introducing the load factors $K_b$ (for bending) and $K_t$ (for torsion):

$$\boxed{d^3 = \dfrac{16}{\pi \tau_{allow}} \sqrt{(K_b M)^2 + (K_t T)^2}}$$

This is the **ASME equivalent twisting moment formula** with:

$$T_e = \sqrt{(K_b M)^2 + (K_t T)^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note (KTU Special):** The equivalent twisting moment and equivalent bending moment methods both give the same physical design, but the twisting moment method uses $\tau_{max} = \tau_{allow}$ while the bending moment method uses $\sigma_b = \sigma_{allow}$, with the conversion $\tau_{allow} = 0.5 \sigma_{allow}$ for the bending moment method. Many KTU problems specify one or the other explicitly — read the question carefully!

---

## 3.4 Rigidity-Based Design of a Line Shaft (Twist in Degrees per Metre)

In a long line shaft, the **angle of twist per unit length** is limited (typically to $0.5°$/m or $1°$/m for line shafts, and $2.5°$/m to $5°$/m for machine shafts). The angle of twist over the entire length $L$ is:

$$\theta = \dfrac{T L}{J G} \quad \text{(radians)}$$

Converting to degrees:

$$\theta \text{ (degrees)} = \dfrac{180}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{T L}{J G}$$

For a **solid shaft**, $J = \pi d^4 / 32$:

$$\theta = \dfrac{180}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{32 T L}{\pi d^4 G} = \dfrac{5760 \, T L}{\pi^2 d^4 G}$$

> **Important** — The angle of twist per metre ($\theta_L = \theta / L$, in degrees/m) is therefore:

$$\theta_L = \dfrac{5760 \, T}{\pi^2 d^4 G}$$

Solving for $d$:

$$d^4 = \dfrac{5760 \, T}{\pi^2 G \cdot \theta_L}$$

$$d = \left[\dfrac{5760 \, T}{\pi^2 G \cdot \theta_L}\right]^{1/4}$$

Or in convenient units (d in mm, T in N·m, G in MPa, $\theta_L$ in degrees/m, multiply numerator by $10^6$ conversion since G is in N/mm²):

$$\boxed{d^4 = \dfrac{5760 \times 10^6 \times T}{\pi^2 \times G \times \theta_L} \quad \text{with } d \text{ in mm}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **standard line-shaft design formula** you must remember. Many KTU problems give the maximum power, the RPM, and the allowable twist per metre, and ask you to design the diameter. The trick is to first convert $P$ and $N$ to $T$ using $T = 9550 P / N$, then plug into the formula above.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Standard Pattern)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A solid circular shaft of diameter $60 \text{ mm}$ transmits $7.5 \text{ kW}$ at $200 \text{ rpm}$. Find the maximum shear stress developed in the shaft. Take $G = 80 \text{ GPa}$. Also find the angle of twist over a length of $2 \text{ m}$.

**Solution — Step 1: Compute the Torque**

$$T = \dfrac{9550 \times P}{N} = \dfrac{9550 \times 7.5}{200} = \dfrac{71625}{200} = 358.125 \text{ N·m}$$

$$T = 358.125 \times 10^3 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Solution — Step 2: Compute the Maximum Shear Stress**

Using $\tau = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$:

$$\tau = \dfrac{16 \times 358125}{\pi \times (60)^3} = \dfrac{5730000}{\pi \times 216000} = \dfrac{5730000}{678584} \approx 8.44 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{\tau \approx 8.44 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Solution — Step 3: Compute the Polar Moment of Inertia**

$$J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32} = \dfrac{\pi \times (60)^4}{32} = \dfrac{\pi \times 12960000}{32} = 1.272 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Solution — Step 4: Compute the Angle of Twist**

$$\theta = \dfrac{T L}{J G} = \dfrac{358125 \times 2000}{1.272 \times 10^6 \times 80 \times 10^3}$$

$$\theta = \dfrac{7.1625 \times 10^8}{1.0176 \times 10^{11}} = 7.04 \times 10^{-3} \text{ radians}$$

Converting to degrees:

$$\theta = 7.04 \times 10^{-3} \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} = 0.403°$$

$$\boxed{\theta \approx 0.403° \text{ over 2 m, or } 0.20°/\text{m}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hierarchical Concept Map of Shaft Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Shaft Loading Analysis"]:::root --> B["Identify Type of Loading"]
    B --> C1["Pure Torsion Only"]
    B --> C2["Combined Bending and Torsion"]

    C1 --> D1["Use Master Torsion Equation"]
    D1 --> D1a["T / J = tau / r = G theta / L"]
    D1a --> D1b["Design for Strength: tau less than or equal to tau_allow"]
    D1a --> D1c["Design for Rigidity: theta per L less than theta_allow"]

    C2 --> D2["Apply ASME Code Factors Kb and Kt"]
    D2 --> D2a["Compute Equivalent Twisting Moment Te"]
    D2 --> D2b["Compute Equivalent Bending Moment Me"]
    D2a --> D2c["Strength Design: d cubed = 16 Te / pi tau_allow"]
    D2b --> D2d["Bending Design: d cubed = 32 Me / pi sigma_b"]

    D1b --> E["Compute Shaft Diameter d"]
    D1c --> E
    D2c --> E
    D2d --> E
    E --> F1["Solid Shaft: J = pi d^4 / 32"]
    E --> F2["Hollow Shaft: J = pi do^4 minus di^4 divided by 32"]

    classDef root fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#8B5A00,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#A8D5BA,stroke:#1F6F3F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class A root
    class D1a,D1b,D1c,D2a,D2b,D2c,D2d,F1,F2 core
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Power Transmission Flow in a Shaft System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["Input Power P kW"]:::input
    N["Shaft Speed N rpm"]:::input
    T["Torque T N.m by formula T equals 9550 P divided by N"]:::process
    SHAFT["Circular Shaft diameter d"]:::shaft
    STRESS["Shear Stress tau equals 16 T over pi d cubed"]:::stress
    TWIST["Angle of Twist theta equals T L over J G"]:::twist
    OUT["Output: Power to Driven Machine"]:::output

    P --> T
    N --> T
    T --> SHAFT
    SHAFT --> STRESS
    SHAFT --> TWIST
    SHAFT --> OUT

    classDef input fill:#FFD3B5,stroke:#A0420A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#B5D8FF,stroke:#0A42A0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef shaft fill:#FFB5E0,stroke:#A00A60,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef stress fill:#D5B5FF,stroke:#5C0AA0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef twist fill:#B5FFD5,stroke:#0AA05C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#8B5A00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class P,N input
    class T process
    class SHAFT shaft
    class STRESS stress
    class TWIST twist
    class OUT output
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: ASME Code Design Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Given: M, T, Kb, Kt, tau_allow"]:::start
    M1["Path A: Equivalent Twisting Moment Method"]:::pathA
    M2["Path B: Equivalent Bending Moment Method"]:::pathB

    S --> M1
    S --> M2

    M1 --> A1["Compute Te = sqrt of Kb M squared plus Kt T squared"]
    A1 --> A2["Compute d cubed = 16 Te over pi tau_allow"]
    A2 --> A3["Solve for d"]

    M2 --> B1["Compute Me = 0.5 times Kb M plus sqrt of Kb M squared plus Kt T squared"]
    B1 --> B2["Compute d cubed = 32 Me over pi sigma_b"]
    B2 --> B3["Solve for d"]

    A3 --> RESULT["Final Shaft Diameter d"]
    B3 --> RESULT

    classDef start fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#8B5A00,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef pathA fill:#A8D5BA,stroke:#1F6F3F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class M1,A1,A2,A3 pathA
    class M2,B1,B2,B3 pathB
    class RESULT start
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Decision Logic for Strength vs. Rigidity Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q["Is the design governed by twist limit or stress limit?"]:::question
    A["Strength-Based: tau_max less than tau_allow"]:::strength
    B["Rigidity-Based: theta per L less than theta_allow"]:::rigidity
    C["Strength Formula: d cubed = 16 T over pi tau_allow"]:::form1
    D["Rigidity Formula: d to the power 4 = 5760 times 10^6 times T divided by pi squared times G times theta_L"]:::form2
    E["Choose LARGER of d_strength and d_rigidity"]:::final
    F["Final Safe Shaft Diameter d_max"]:::result

    Q --> A
    Q --> B
    A --> C
    B --> D
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F

    classDef question fill:#FFD3B5,stroke:#A0420A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef strength fill:#A8D5BA,stroke:#1F6F3F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```
