# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> Define: **(a)** Shaft, **(b)** Axle. Distinguish between them with a clear example for each.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **(a) Shaft:** A shaft is a rotating machine element of circular cross-section, used to transmit **torque (and hence power)** from one component to another. It is primarily subjected to **torsion** along with possible **bending** and **axial loads**.
>
> **(b) Axle:** An axle is a stationary (non-rotating) machine element used to support rotating members such as wheels or pulleys. It is subjected to **bending moment only** and does **not** transmit any torque.
>
> **Distinguishing Example:**
> - **Shaft example:** The drive shaft of a car (rotates and transmits power from the engine to the rear wheels).
> - **Axle example:** The fixed axle of a railway wagon (does not rotate with the wheel; the wheels rotate independently on bearings mounted on the axle).
>
> **Key Distinction:** A shaft **rotates** and transmits **torque**; an axle is **stationary** and transmits only **bending**.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> State the **Torsion Equation** for a circular shaft. Explain each term in the equation with standard SI units.

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Torsion Equation is:
> $$\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G \theta}{L}$$
>
> Where:
> - $T$ = **Twisting moment / Torque** applied at the free end, in **N·m** (or N·mm).
> - $J$ = **Polar moment of inertia** of the cross-section about the longitudinal axis, in **m⁴** (or mm⁴).
> - $\tau$ = **Shear stress** at the outer surface of the shaft, in **Pa** (or N/mm² = MPa).
> - $r$ = **Radius of the shaft**, in **m** (or mm).
> - $G$ = **Modulus of rigidity** of the shaft material, in **Pa** (or MPa).
> - $\theta$ = **Angle of twist** at the free end, in **radians**.
> - $L$ = **Length of the shaft**, in **m** (or mm).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination pattern**, Module 2 carries **one full 14-mark question**, typically with internal choice. The question may have two sub-parts of **7 marks each**, or one part (a) for 7 marks and part (b) for 7 marks.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the **torsion equation** $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G \theta}{L}$ for a solid circular shaft. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hollow circular shaft of outer diameter $80 \text{ mm}$ and inner diameter $50 \text{ mm}$ transmits $60 \text{ kW}$ at $250 \text{ rpm}$. Find the **maximum shear stress** in the shaft and the **angle of twist per metre length**. Take $G = 80 \text{ GPa}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — Torsion Equation Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumptions (Saint-Venant's Theory of Pure Torsion):**
> 1. The shaft material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and elastic** (obeys Hooke's law).
> 2. Cross-sections of the shaft, which are **plane and circular** before loading, **remain plane and circular** after loading.
> 3. **Radial lines** in the cross-section **remain straight and radial** after deformation (i.e., the cross-section rotates as a rigid disc).
> 4. The shaft is subjected to **pure torque only** at the ends (no bending, no axial load).
> 5. The shaft has a **uniform circular cross-section** along its length.

**Derivation:**

Consider a solid circular shaft of radius $r$ and length $L$ fixed at one end and twisted at the other by a torque $T$, causing an angle of twist $\theta$.

**Step 1 — Shear Strain at Radius $\rho$:**

A line of length $L$ on the surface becomes a helix, with the free end rotating through angle $\theta$. The arc length of this deformation at radius $\rho$ is $\rho \cdot \theta$. Therefore:

$$\gamma = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \theta}{L}$$

**Step 2 — Shear Stress (Hooke's Law):**

$$\tau_\rho = G \cdot \gamma = \dfrac{G \rho \theta}{L}$$

**Step 3 — Elemental Area and Force:**

Consider an elementary ring of radius $\rho$ and thickness $d\rho$:

$$dA = 2\pi \rho \, d\rho$$

The elemental shear force:

$$dF = \tau_\rho \cdot dA = \dfrac{G \rho \theta}{L} \cdot 2\pi \rho \, d\rho = \dfrac{2\pi G \theta}{L} \cdot \rho^2 \, d\rho$$

**Step 4 — Elemental Torque:**

$$dT = \rho \cdot dF = \dfrac{2\pi G \theta}{L} \cdot \rho^3 \, d\rho$$

**Step 5 — Integration:**

$$T = \int_0^r dT = \dfrac{2\pi G \theta}{L} \int_0^r \rho^3 \, d\rho = \dfrac{2\pi G \theta}{L} \cdot \dfrac{r^4}{4} = \dfrac{\pi G \theta r^4}{2L}$$

**Step 6 — Polar Moment of Inertia:**

$$J = \int_0^r \rho^2 \, dA = \int_0^r 2\pi \rho^3 \, d\rho = \dfrac{\pi r^4}{2} = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$$

**Step 7 — Final Equation:**

Substituting $J = \pi r^4 / 2$ into Step 5: $T = (G \theta / L) \cdot J$

From Step 2 at $\rho = r$: $\tau_{max} = G r \theta / L$

Rearranging both:

$$\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G \theta}{L} \quad \blacksquare$$

#### Model Solution for (b) — Hollow Shaft Numerical

**Given:**
- $d_o = 80 \text{ mm}$, $d_i = 50 \text{ mm}$
- $P = 60 \text{ kW}$, $N = 250 \text{ rpm}$
- $G = 80 \text{ GPa} = 80 \times 10^3 \text{ N/mm}^2$

**Step 1 — Compute the Torque:**

$$T = \dfrac{9550 \times 60}{250} = \dfrac{573000}{250} = 2292 \text{ N·m}$$

$$T = 2292 \times 10^3 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Polar Moment of Inertia:**

$$J = \dfrac{\pi (d_o^4 - d_i^4)}{32} = \dfrac{\pi (80^4 - 50^4)}{32}$$

Computing the powers:
- $80^4 = 40960000$
- $50^4 = 6250000$
- $d_o^4 - d_i^4 = 40960000 - 6250000 = 34710000$

$$J = \dfrac{\pi \times 34710000}{32} = \dfrac{1.0903 \times 10^8}{32} = 3.407 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Shear Stress:**

$$\tau_{max} = \dfrac{T \cdot r}{J} = \dfrac{2292 \times 10^3 \times 40}{3.407 \times 10^6}$$

$$\tau_{max} = \dfrac{9.168 \times 10^7}{3.407 \times 10^6} \approx 26.91 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{max} \approx 26.91 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 4 — Angle of Twist per Metre:**

$$\dfrac{\theta}{L} = \dfrac{T}{J \cdot G} = \dfrac{2292 \times 10^3}{3.407 \times 10^6 \times 80 \times 10^3}$$

$$\dfrac{\theta}{L} = \dfrac{2.292 \times 10^6}{2.726 \times 10^{11}} = 8.41 \times 10^{-6} \text{ rad/mm}$$

Converting to radians per metre (multiply by 1000):

$$\dfrac{\theta}{L} = 8.41 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad/m}$$

Converting to degrees per metre:

$$\dfrac{\theta}{L} = 8.41 \times 10^{-3} \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} \approx 0.482 \text{ °/m}$$

$$\boxed{\theta/L \approx 0.482°/\text{m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **well within the standard $0.5°$/m limit** for line shafts, so the design is acceptable from a rigidity perspective.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **ASME code** for the design of a shaft subjected to combined bending and torsion. Define the terms **equivalent twisting moment (Te)** and **equivalent bending moment (Me)** and write the corresponding design equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shaft supported on bearings carries a **pulley at the centre**. The pulley is driven by a belt with a tight-side tension of $3600 \text{ N}$ and a slack-side tension of $1200 \text{ N}$. The pulley diameter is $600 \text{ mm}$, the shaft diameter is unknown, and the bending moment at the pulley location is $1500 \text{ N·m}$. Use the **ASME equivalent twisting moment method** with $K_b = 1.5$ and $K_t = 1.0$, and allowable shear stress $\tau = 60 \text{ MPa}$. Design the shaft diameter. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — ASME Code

> [!NOTE]
> When a shaft carries pulleys or gears, it experiences **combined bending and torsion**. Direct application of the simple torsion equation is not valid. The **ASME code** introduces two equivalent moments:
>
> **1. Equivalent Twisting Moment (Te):** The torque that, acting alone, would produce the same maximum shear stress as the combined bending and torsion.
> $$T_e = \sqrt{(K_b M)^2 + (K_t T)^2}$$
>
> **2. Equivalent Bending Moment (Me):** The bending moment that, acting alone, would produce the same maximum normal stress as the combined loading.
> $$M_e = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[K_b M + \sqrt{(K_b M)^2 + (K_t T)^2}\right]$$
>
> Where:
> - $K_b$ = Combined shock and fatigue factor for bending (typical values: 1.5 to 3.0).
> - $K_t$ = Combined shock and fatigue factor for torsion (typical values: 1.0 to 3.0).
> - $M$ = Maximum bending moment on the shaft.
> - $T$ = Torque transmitted by the shaft.
>
> **Design Equations:**
>
> **Using Te:**
> $$d^3 = \dfrac{16}{\pi \tau_{allow}} \cdot T_e = \dfrac{16}{\pi \tau_{allow}} \sqrt{(K_b M)^2 + (K_t T)^2}$$
>
> **Using Me:**
> $$d^3 = \dfrac{32}{\pi \sigma_b} \cdot M_e = \dfrac{16}{\pi \sigma_b}\left[K_b M + \sqrt{(K_b M)^2 + (K_t T)^2}\right]$$
>
> The **Te method** uses $\tau_{max}$ (based on **Maximum Shear Stress Theory / Coulomb-Gerber Theory**).
> The **Me method** uses $\sigma_b$ (based on **Maximum Normal Stress Theory**), with $\sigma_b = 2 \tau$ to be consistent.
>
> **Physical Interpretation:** The ASME code recognizes that for ductile materials, the failure is governed by the **maximum shear stress** (a slight modification of the maximum distortion energy theory). The formula $\tau_{max} = (16/\pi d^3) \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$ emerges from this principle.

#### Model Solution for (b) — Numerical

**Given:**
- Tight side tension: $T_1 = 3600 \text{ N}$
- Slack side tension: $T_2 = 1200 \text{ N}$
- Pulley diameter: $D = 600 \text{ mm}$, radius $R = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.3 \text{ m}$
- Bending moment at pulley: $M = 1500 \text{ N·m}$
- $K_b = 1.5$, $K_t = 1.0$, $\tau = 60 \text{ MPa}$

**Step 1 — Compute the Belt-Driven Net Torque:**

Net torque transmitted by the pulley is the net tangential force times the pulley radius:

$$T_{shaft} = (T_1 - T_2) \times R = (3600 - 1200) \times 0.3 = 2400 \times 0.3 = 720 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Equivalent Twisting Moment (Te):**

$$T_e = \sqrt{(K_b M)^2 + (K_t T)^2}$$

$$T_e = \sqrt{(1.5 \times 1500)^2 + (1.0 \times 720)^2}$$

$$T_e = \sqrt{(2250)^2 + (720)^2} = \sqrt{5062500 + 518400}$$

$$T_e = \sqrt{5580900} \approx 2362.4 \text{ N·m}$$

$$T_e \approx 2362.4 \times 10^3 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Shaft Diameter using ASME Te formula:**

$$d^3 = \dfrac{16}{\pi \tau_{allow}} \cdot T_e = \dfrac{16}{\pi \times 60} \times 2362.4 \times 10^3$$

$$d^3 = \dfrac{16 \times 2362.4 \times 10^3}{60 \pi} = \dfrac{37.798 \times 10^6}{188.496}$$

$$d^3 = 2.005 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$d = (2.005 \times 10^5)^{1/3} = (200500)^{1/3}$$

$$d \approx 58.7 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 60 \text{ mm (next standard size)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Final Selection:** In KTU exams, the answer $d \approx 58.7 \text{ mm}$ is acceptable. In practice, the next standard size ($60 \text{ mm}$) is selected. Also, KTU expects students to write down the design equation first, then substitute the values step by step — this is the key to full marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
>
> 1. **Units Mismatch:** Mixing N and mm (which gives N/mm² = MPa correctly) with m (which gives Pa). KTU examiners *love* checking units. Always state: $T$ in N·mm, $d$ in mm, $J$ in mm⁴, $G$ in N/mm² (MPa). NEVER use metres for the diameter in the strength formula.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Load Factors $K_b$ and $K_t$:** A very common error. If the problem says "suddenly applied load" or specifies ASME factors, you **MUST** include them. Forgetting them is a guaranteed loss of 2-3 marks.
>
> 3. **Confusing $M$ and $T$ in the Formula:** Students often mix the bending moment and torque symbols in the equivalent moment formula. **Memorize:** $T_e$ uses $\sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$ (with $K_b$ on $M$ and $K_t$ on $T$); $M_e$ uses the same expression but is half the sum of $K_b M$ and the square root.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to Convert $P$ and $N$ to $T$:** The formula $T = 9550 P/N$ is the FIRST step in most problems. Skipping this step is the most common reason for wrong answers.
>
> 5. **Hollow vs Solid:** For a hollow shaft, the factor $(1 - k^4)$ (where $k = d_i/d_o$) appears in BOTH $J$ and $\tau$. Forgetting it gives a wrong (smaller) shear stress, which is unsafe.
>
> 6. **Standardize $\theta$ in Radians or Degrees Consistently:** $T/J = G\theta/L$ uses $\theta$ in **radians**. If the answer is required in degrees, multiply by $180/\pi$.
>
> 7. **Reading the Problem Carefully:** If a problem says "design the shaft" but gives a twist limit AND a stress limit, you must compute **both diameters** and take the **larger**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Shafts (Module 2)**
>
> - **Shaft vs Axle:** Shaft rotates and transmits torque; Axle is stationary and carries bending only.
> - **Master Torsion Equation:** $T/J = \tau/r = G\theta/L$ — MEMORIZE THIS VERBATIM.
> - **Solid shaft:** $J = \pi d^4 / 32$, $\tau = 16T/(\pi d^3)$, $\theta = 32TL/(\pi d^4 G)$.
> - **Hollow shaft:** $J = \pi(d_o^4 - d_i^4)/32$, $\tau = 16T/[\pi d_o^3(1-k^4)]$, where $k = d_i/d_o$.
> - **Power → Torque:** $T (\text{N·m}) = 9550 \times P (\text{kW}) / N (\text{rpm})$. **MUST memorize.**
> - **Torque → Power:** $P (\text{kW}) = 2\pi N T / (60 \times 1000) = NT/9550$.
> - **Strength Design (solid):** $d^3 = 16T/(\pi \tau_{allow})$.
> - **Rigidity Design (line shaft, solid, twist in °/m):** $d^4 = 5760 \times 10^6 \times T / (\pi^2 G \theta_L)$.
> - **ASME Equivalent Twisting Moment:** $T_e = \sqrt{(K_b M)^2 + (K_t T)^2}$, design eq.: $d^3 = 16 T_e / (\pi \tau_{allow})$.
> - **ASME Equivalent Bending Moment:** $M_e = 0.5 \left[K_b M + \sqrt{(K_b M)^2 + (K_t T)^2}\right]$, design eq.: $d^3 = 32 M_e / (\pi \sigma_b)$.
> - **Load Factors:** Gradually loaded ($K_b=1.5, K_t=1.0$); Minor shock ($K_b=1.5\text{-}2.0, K_t=1.0\text{-}1.5$); Heavy shock ($K_b=2.0\text{-}3.0, K_t=1.5\text{-}3.0$).
> - **Always** state units in the final answer. Always state which design method (Strength / Rigidity / ASME) you are using. Always convert $P$ and $N$ to $T$ first.
> - **Pure Torsion Check (ASME):** When $M = 0$, $T_e = K_t T$ and $M_e = 0.5 K_t T$ — both reduce to the simple torsion equation with a factor of $K_t$ on $T$.
> - **Pure Bending Check (ASME):** When $T = 0$, $T_e = K_b M$ and $M_e = K_b M$ — equivalent to a bending-only design.
> - **Units throughout:** Use **N·mm, mm, N/mm² (MPa)** for consistency in design problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torsion Theory of Elastic Circular Bars
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT402 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Torsion Theory of Elastic Circular Bars

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Torsion of Elastic Circular Bars** is the branch of solid mechanics that studies the behavior of cylindrical shafts of **circular cross-section** subjected to *twisting moments* (torques) about their longitudinal axis, while the material remains within its **elastic limit** (i.e., stress is proportional to strain as per **Hooke's Law**).

When a torque **T** is applied at the free end of a circular shaft fixed at the other end, every cross-section rotates by a small angle relative to the fixed end. This angular displacement is called the **Angle of Twist ($\theta$)**.

The theory was first rigorously developed by **Coulomb (1784)** and forms the backbone for the design of:
- **Drive shafts** in automobiles
- **Axles** of railway wagons
- **Propeller shafts** of ships and aircraft
- **Drill rods** in boring machines
- **Turbo-generator rotor shafts**

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Wringing a Wet Towel 🌀**
> 
> Imagine you are holding a wet towel with both hands, palms down, and you twist it in opposite directions to squeeze out the water. The fibers of the towel — which were originally straight and parallel to the towel's length — now form a **helical (spiral) pattern**. This is *exactly* what happens to the longitudinal fibers inside a circular shaft under torsion.
> 
> - The **fibers at the center** of the shaft barely twist (like the central core of the towel).
> - The **fibers at the outer surface** twist the most (like the outermost strands of the towel).
> - Because these outer fibers deform more, they carry the **maximum shear stress**.

**Key Takeaway:** Torsion produces *shear strain* (not bending), and the stress varies **linearly** from zero at the center to a maximum at the outer surface.

---

## 1.3 Fundamental Assumptions of the Theory

For the classical theory of torsion of **circular (solid and hollow) bars** to be valid, the following assumptions are mandatory:

1. The shaft material is **homogeneous**, **isotropic**, and obeys **Hooke's Law** (linear elastic).
2. The cross-section of the shaft is **circular** and remains **circular** (plane) and **undistorted** after twisting.
3. Cross-sections **rotate as a rigid disc** about the longitudinal axis (i.e., they do not warp).
4. The radius of the shaft remains **constant** (no elongation of the longitudinal axis).
5. The twist is **uniform** along the length (for pure torsion).
6. **Saint-Venant's Principle** applies: at sections far from the point of load application and from the fixed end, the stress distribution is uniform.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-section remains plane** is the most critical assumption. This holds for **circular cross-sections only**. For non-circular sections (rectangle, I-section), the cross-section **warps**, and a different theory (e.g., membrane analogy) must be used.

---

## 1.4 Key Physical Quantities (Standard SI Units)

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Torque / Twisting Moment** | $T$ | N·m | Moment applied about longitudinal axis |
| **Length of Shaft** | $L$ | m | Distance between fixed end and load point |
| **Radius of Shaft** | $R$ | m | Outer radius of circular cross-section |
| **Diameter of Shaft** | $d$ | m | Outer diameter |
| **Shear Stress at radius $r$** | $\tau$ | Pa (N/m²) | Tangential stress on cross-section |
| **Angle of Twist** | $\theta$ | rad | Total angular rotation of free end |
| **Polar Moment of Inertia** | $J$ | m⁴ | Torsional geometric property of cross-section |
| **Modulus of Rigidity** | $G$ | Pa (N/m²) | Material property (≈ 0.4 × E for steel) |
| **Polar Section Modulus** | $Z_p$ | m³ | $J/R$ — used in strength design |

> [!WARNING]
> Always work in **consistent units**. If $T$ is in N·mm and $d$ is in mm, then $J$ must be in mm⁴ and stress in MPa (N/mm²). Mixing m and mm is the most common source of numerical error in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Torsion Equations (The Holy Trinity ⭐)

The entire theory of torsion of circular bars is summarized in **one powerful relation**, often called the **Universal Torsion Equation**:

$$
\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\,\theta}{L}
$$

This equation has **three distinct physical meanings**:

### 2.1.1 Geometric Meaning — $\dfrac{\theta}{L} = \dfrac{\phi}{L}$ (Twist per unit length)
The **rate of twist** $\left(\dfrac{\theta}{L}\right)$ is constant for a uniform shaft. The angle $\theta$ is proportional to $L$.

### 2.1.2 Strength Meaning — $\dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{T}{J}$ (Shear stress distribution)
Shear stress varies **linearly** with radial distance $r$.
- At the **center** ($r = 0$): $\tau = 0$
- At the **outer surface** ($r = R$): $\tau = \tau_{\max} = \dfrac{T \cdot R}{J} = \dfrac{T}{Z_p}$

### 2.1.3 Stiffness Meaning — $\dfrac{\theta}{L} = \dfrac{T}{GJ}$ (Shaft stiffness)
The angle of twist depends on the **rigidity** $GJ$ — a larger $GJ$ means a stiffer shaft.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Development

**Step 1 — Consider a shaft element of length $L$, radius $R$ subjected to torque $T$ at the free end.**

**Step 2 — A radial line $OP$ on the cross-section at the free end rotates by angle $\theta$ to position $OP'$, while the fixed end's radial line $OQ$ remains stationary.**

**Step 3 — A longitudinal fiber on the outer surface (initially parallel to the axis) tilts by a small angle $\gamma$ (the shear strain).**

$$
\gamma = \frac{R \cdot \theta}{L} \quad \text{(arc length ÷ radius)}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Hooke's Law in shear:**

$$
\tau = G \cdot \gamma = G \cdot \frac{R \theta}{L}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{\tau}{R} = \frac{G \theta}{L}
$$

**Step 5 — By static equilibrium of the cross-section, the moment of the shear stress about the centroid equals the applied torque $T$:**

$$
T = \int_A \tau \cdot r \, dA = \int_A \frac{T}{J} \cdot r^2 \, dA
$$

This yields the **Polar Moment of Inertia** $J$.

---

## 2.3 Polar Moment of Inertia — $J$

| Section Type | $J$ (Polar Moment of Inertia) | Polar Section Modulus $Z_p = J/R$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Solid Circular** (diameter $d$) | $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$ | $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{16}$ |
| **Hollow Circular** (outer $D$, inner $d$) | $J = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$ | $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{16 D}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on notation:** Some textbooks use $d_o$ and $d_i$ for outer and inner diameters. The KTU 2024 syllabus uses uppercase $D$ for outer and lowercase $d$ for inner — be consistent in your answer scripts.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table. Every KTU question is a rearrangement or substitution of one of these formulas.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Universal Torsion Eq.** | $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ | Master relation |
| 2 | **Max Shear Stress** | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{16\,T}{\pi\,d^3}$ (solid) | Strength of shaft |
| 3 | **Max Shear Stress (Hollow)** | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{16\,T\,D}{\pi\,(D^4 - d^4)}$ | Strength of hollow shaft |
| 4 | **Angle of Twist** | $\theta = \dfrac{T L}{G J}$ | Stiffness of shaft |
| 5 | **Polar Moment (Solid)** | $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$ | Geometric property |
| 6 | **Polar Moment (Hollow)** | $J = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$ | Geometric property |
| 7 | **Polar Modulus (Solid)** | $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{16}$ | Direct stress calc. |
| 8 | **Polar Modulus (Hollow)** | $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{16 D}$ | Direct stress calc. |
| 9 | **Strain Energy (Pure Torsion)** | $U = \dfrac{T^2 L}{2 G J} = \dfrac{1}{2} T \theta$ | Energy stored |
| 10 | **Power Transmitted** | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ (Watts) | Shaft design |
| 11 | **Twist per unit length** | $\theta/L = \dfrac{T}{G J}$ | Local twist rate |
| 12 | **Stiffness (Shaft Rigidity)** | $k_t = \dfrac{G J}{L}$ | Spring constant analogy |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit conversion for Power Equation:** If $N$ is in **rpm** and $T$ is in **N·m**, the power $P$ is in **Watts**. For **kW**, divide by 1000. For horsepower, divide Watts by 745.7. This is a frequent 2-mark trap in KTU exams.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Field | Component | Reason Torsion Theory is Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Automobile** | Drive shaft, axle | Transmits engine torque to wheels |
| **Marine** | Propeller shaft | Transmits engine power to propeller |
| **Aerospace** | Helicopter rotor shaft | Carries huge centrifugal + torque loads |
| **Civil** | Building under earthquake | Twisting of vertical columns |
| **Mechanical** | Lathe spindle, drill bit | Cutting torque transmission |
| **Power** | Turbine shaft, generator rotor | Continuous rotation under torque |

---

## 2.6 Why Hollow Shafts are Preferred Over Solid Shafts 🚗

For the **same material and same weight**, a hollow shaft transmits more torque because:
- Material near the **center** of a solid shaft carries **very little stress** (since $\tau \propto r$).
- Removing the center material (making it hollow) reduces weight without losing much strength.
- **Strength-to-weight ratio** is significantly higher.
- Used in: **aircraft propeller shafts, bicycle frames, automobile drive shafts**.

**Strength efficiency ratio:**

$$
\frac{\tau_{\text{hollow}}}{\tau_{\text{solid}}} = \frac{16 D/\pi(D^4 - d^4)}{16/\pi d_s^3}
$$
where $d_s$ is the equivalent solid diameter for the same weight.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** KTU 2024 Scheme ESE questions frequently ask for the **derivation of the torsion equation** for 7 marks. Practice the derivation below word-by-word. Skipping any step costs 1 mark.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the Universal Torsion Equation $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$

### Setup

Consider a **solid circular shaft** of radius $R$, length $L$, fixed at one end and subjected to a torque $T$ at the free end.

**Before loading:** Two reference lines are drawn on the surface:
- A **longitudinal line** $AB$ (parallel to the axis).
- A **transverse line** $CD$ (on the cross-section, perpendicular to $AB$).

**After loading:** Line $AB$ shifts to $AB'$, and line $CD$ rotates by angle $\theta$ to $CD'$.

---

### Step 1: Shear Strain at the Outer Surface

The point $B$ on the outer surface moves to $B'$ such that $BB' = R\theta$ (arc length on a circle of radius $R$ subtending angle $\theta$).

The original length of the longitudinal fiber $AB$ is approximately $L$ (since shear strain is small).

The **shear strain** $\gamma$ at the outer surface is:

$$
\gamma = \tan \gamma = \frac{BB'}{AB} = \frac{R \theta}{L}
$$

$$
\boxed{\gamma = \frac{R \theta}{L}}
$$

*Reason:* For small angles (elastic torsion), $\tan \gamma \approx \gamma$ in radians.

---

### Step 2: Apply Hooke's Law in Shear

The shear stress $\tau$ at the outer surface is related to shear strain $\gamma$ by the **Modulus of Rigidity** $G$:

$$
\tau = G \cdot \gamma = G \cdot \frac{R \theta}{L}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\boxed{\frac{\tau}{R} = \frac{G \theta}{L}}
$$

*Reason:* This is the second part of the universal equation. It connects stress at radius $R$ to the total twist $\theta$.

---

### Step 3: Generalization to Any Radius $r$

By the assumption of **plane sections remaining plane** and **undistorted**, any inner fiber at radius $r$ will shear by strain:

$$
\gamma_r = \frac{r \theta}{L}
$$

So the shear stress at radius $r$ is:

$$
\tau_r = G \gamma_r = \frac{G r \theta}{L}
$$

Dividing by $r$:

$$
\boxed{\frac{\tau_r}{r} = \frac{G \theta}{L}}
$$

*Reason:* This proves shear stress is **linearly proportional** to radius. At $r = 0$ (center), $\tau = 0$. At $r = R$ (surface), $\tau = \tau_{\max}$.

---

### Step 4: Static Equilibrium of the Cross-Section

Consider an elemental ring of radius $r$, width $dr$, on the cross-section. Its area is:

$$
dA = 2 \pi r \, dr
$$

The shear stress $\tau_r$ on this element acts **tangentially** (perpendicular to the radius). The elemental force is:

$$
dF = \tau_r \cdot dA = \frac{G r \theta}{L} \cdot (2 \pi r \, dr)
$$

The elemental moment about the axis is:

$$
dT = r \cdot dF = \frac{2 \pi G \theta}{L} \cdot r^3 \, dr
$$

Total torque on the cross-section:

$$
T = \int_0^R \frac{2 \pi G \theta}{L} \cdot r^3 \, dr = \frac{2 \pi G \theta}{L} \cdot \int_0^R r^3 \, dr
$$

$$
T = \frac{2 \pi G \theta}{L} \cdot \left[ \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_0^R = \frac{2 \pi G \theta}{L} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4}
$$

$$
T = \frac{\pi G \theta R^4}{2 L}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\boxed{\frac{T}{\pi R^4/2} = \frac{G \theta}{L}}
$$

---

### Step 5: Define the Polar Moment of Inertia

We define the **Polar Moment of Inertia** $J$ as:

$$
J = \int_A r^2 \, dA = \int_0^R r^2 (2 \pi r \, dr) = 2 \pi \int_0^R r^3 \, dr = \frac{\pi R^4}{2}
$$

In terms of diameter $d$ (where $R = d/2$):

$$
J = \frac{\pi (d/2)^4}{2} = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\boxed{\frac{T}{J} = \frac{G \theta}{L}}
$$

---

### Step 6: Combine the Three Ratios

From Step 2: $\dfrac{\tau}{R} = \dfrac{G \theta}{L}$
From Step 5: $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{G \theta}{L}$

Both equal $\dfrac{G \theta}{L}$, and since $\tau$ varies linearly with $r$, we can replace $R$ with general $r$:

$$
\boxed{\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \theta}{L}}
$$

**This is the Universal Torsion Equation.** ∎ (Q.E.D.)

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Strain Energy in Torsion

Consider a circular shaft of length $L$ subjected to a gradually applied torque $T$ at the free end. The angle of twist at any intermediate stage (when torque is $T'$) is:

$$
\theta' = \frac{T' L}{G J}
$$

The elemental work done by the torque as it increases by $dT'$ is:

$$
dU = \frac{1}{2} T' \cdot d\theta' = \frac{1}{2} T' \cdot \frac{L}{G J} \cdot dT'
$$

Total strain energy:

$$
U = \int_0^T \frac{L}{2 G J} \cdot T' \, dT' = \frac{L}{2 G J} \cdot \frac{T^2}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{U = \frac{T^2 L}{2 G J} = \frac{1}{2} T \theta}
$$

*Reason:* The expression $\frac{1}{2} T \theta$ is the **area under the torque-twist curve** (analogous to $\frac{1}{2} F \delta$ in axial loading).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Power Transmitted by a Shaft

If the shaft rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute (rpm), the angular velocity in rad/s is:

$$
\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60}
$$

The **power transmitted** is the rate of doing work:

$$
P = T \cdot \omega = T \cdot \frac{2 \pi N}{60}
$$

$$
\boxed{P = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} \text{ Watts}}
$$

For power in **kW**:

$$
P_{kW} = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{\pi N T}{30000}
$$

---

## 3.4 Composite Shafts (Shafts in Series/Parallel)

### Series Connection (Stepped Shaft)
For two segments in series with different $J$ and $L$ but **same torque** $T$:

$$
\theta_{\text{total}} = \theta_1 + \theta_2 = \frac{T L_1}{G_1 J_1} + \frac{T L_2}{G_2 J_2}
$$

### Parallel Connection (Compound Shaft)
When two shafts are connected in parallel (e.g., two concentric tubes), the **angle of twist is the same** in both, and the **torques add up**:

$$
T = T_1 + T_2 \quad \text{and} \quad \theta_1 = \theta_2 = \theta
$$

From the torsion equation: $T_1 = \dfrac{G_1 J_1 \theta}{L_1}$ and $T_2 = \dfrac{G_2 J_2 \theta}{L_2}$

Dividing:

$$
\boxed{\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{G_1 J_1 / L_1}{G_2 J_2 / L_2}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU 2024 ESE:** Always draw a free-body diagram showing internal torques in each segment. The **method of sections** (cutting and summing torques from one end) is the standard approach.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define: (a) Angle of twist, (b) Modulus of rigidity, (c) Polar moment of inertia. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Angle of Twist ($\theta$):** It is the angle in radians through which a cross-section of a shaft rotates relative to a fixed reference cross-section when a torque is applied. For a uniform shaft, $\theta = \dfrac{TL}{GJ}$. **Unit: radian (rad).**

**(b) Modulus of Rigidity ($G$):** It is the slope of the shear stress–shear strain curve in the elastic region, representing the material's resistance to shear deformation. Mathematically, $G = \dfrac{\tau}{\gamma}$. **Unit: N/m² (Pa).** For steel, $G \approx 80$ GPa; for aluminium, $G \approx 27$ GPa.

**(c) Polar Moment of Inertia ($J$):** It is a geometrical property of the cross-section that measures its ability to resist torsion. It is the second moment of area about the longitudinal axis (polar axis). For a solid circular section of diameter $d$:

$$
J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}
$$

**Unit: m⁴ (or mm⁴).**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State the assumptions made in the theory of torsion of circular shafts. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The theory of torsion of circular shafts is based on the following key assumptions:

1. The shaft material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's Law** (linear elastic).
2. The shaft has a **uniform circular cross-section** throughout its length.
3. **Cross-sections remain plane, circular, and undistorted** after twisting (no warping).
4. The **radius of the shaft remains constant** during twisting (no longitudinal elongation).
5. The **twist is uniform** along the length of the shaft.
6. **Saint-Venant's principle** is valid: stress distribution is uniform at sections sufficiently far from points of load application or geometric discontinuities.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme, ESE Part B questions carry **14 marks each** and offer an **internal choice** between two questions. Both options are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the torsion equation $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ for a solid circular shaft, stating clearly the assumptions involved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A solid circular shaft of diameter **80 mm** transmits **150 kW** of power at **200 rpm**. If the maximum permissible shear stress in the material is **60 MPa**, determine:
- (i) The torque transmitted.
- (ii) Whether the shaft is safe under the given loading.
- (iii) The angle of twist per metre length, given $G = 80$ GPa. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Part A(a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Assumptions (2 marks)**
- Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and elastic.
- Cross-section is circular and remains plane and undistorted.
- Radius remains constant; twist is uniform.
- Hooke's law in shear holds: $\tau = G\gamma$.

**Step 2: Geometry of Deformation (2 marks)**
Consider a shaft of radius $R$ and length $L$ fixed at one end. Under torque $T$, the free end rotates by angle $\theta$. A surface fiber of length $L$ tilts such that the shear strain at radius $R$ is:

$$
\gamma = \frac{R\theta}{L}
$$

**Step 3: Hooke's Law (1 mark)**

$$
\tau = G\gamma = \frac{GR\theta}{L} \Rightarrow \frac{\tau}{R} = \frac{G\theta}{L}
$$

**Step 4: Equilibrium (1 mark)**
The total torque equals the moment of shear stress distribution:

$$
T = \int_0^R \frac{Tr}{J} \cdot r \cdot (2\pi r\, dr) \Rightarrow J = \frac{\pi R^4}{2} = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}
$$

**Step 5: Combine (1 mark)**

$$
\boxed{\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}}
$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Part A(b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Diameter $d = 80$ mm = $0.08$ m
- Power $P = 150$ kW = $150{,}000$ W
- Speed $N = 200$ rpm
- Allowable shear stress $\tau_{\text{allow}} = 60$ MPa = $60$ N/mm²
- $G = 80$ GPa = $80{,}000$ N/mm² = $80 \times 10^3$ MPa

**Step 1: Calculate Torque (2 marks)**

$$
P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}
$$

$$
150{,}000 = \frac{2\pi \times 200 \times T}{60}
$$

$$
T = \frac{150{,}000 \times 60}{2\pi \times 200} = \frac{9 \times 10^6}{1256.64}
$$

$$
\boxed{T = 7161.97 \text{ N·m} = 7.162 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Maximum Shear Stress (2 marks)**

$$
\tau_{\max} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 7.162 \times 10^6}{\pi \times (80)^3}
$$

$$
\tau_{\max} = \frac{1.1459 \times 10^8}{\pi \times 512{,}000} = \frac{1.1459 \times 10^8}{1.6085 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_{\max} = 71.24 \text{ MPa}}
$$

**Step 3: Check Safety (1 mark)**

Since $\tau_{\max} = 71.24$ MPa $> \tau_{\text{allow}} = 60$ MPa,

**❌ The shaft is NOT safe.** It must be redesigned with a larger diameter.

**Step 4: Angle of Twist per metre (2 marks)**

Polar moment of inertia:

$$
J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times (80)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 4.096 \times 10^7}{32} = 4.021 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4
$$

For $L = 1$ m = $1000$ mm:

$$
\frac{\theta}{L} = \frac{T}{G J} = \frac{7.162 \times 10^6}{80{,}000 \times 4.021 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{\theta}{L} = 2.226 \times 10^{-5} \text{ rad/m} = 0.02226 \text{ rad/m}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A hollow circular shaft of **outer diameter 100 mm** and **inner diameter 60 mm** is subjected to a torque of **20 kN·m**. Calculate:
- (i) The maximum shear stress induced in the shaft.
- (ii) The minimum shear stress (at the inner surface).
- (iii) The angle of twist over a length of **3 m**, given $G = 80$ GPa. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two solid circular shafts of the same length and same material are connected end-to-end. Shaft 1 has diameter **50 mm** and Shaft 2 has diameter **75 mm**. A torque of **5 kN·m** is applied at one end. Determine the angle of twist of the free end relative to the fixed end, and the maximum shear stress in each shaft. Take $L = 2$ m, $G = 80$ GPa. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Part B(a) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $D = 100$ mm, $d = 60$ mm, $T = 20$ kN·m = $20 \times 10^6$ N·mm, $L = 3$ m = $3000$ mm, $G = 80$ GPa

**Step 1: Polar Moment of Inertia (1 mark)**

$$
J = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32} = \frac{\pi \times (100^4 - 60^4)}{32}
$$

$$
J = \frac{\pi \times (10^8 - 1.296 \times 10^7)}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 8.704 \times 10^7}{32}
$$

$$
\boxed{J = 8.546 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4}
$$

**Step 2: Maximum Shear Stress at $r = D/2$ (2 marks)**

$$
\tau_{\max} = \frac{T \cdot (D/2)}{J} = \frac{20 \times 10^6 \times 50}{8.546 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_{\max} = 117.0 \text{ MPa}}
$$

**Step 3: Minimum Shear Stress at $r = d/2$ (2 marks)**

$$
\tau_{\min} = \frac{T \cdot (d/2)}{J} = \frac{20 \times 10^6 \times 30}{8.546 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_{\min} = 70.2 \text{ MPa}}
$$

**Step 4: Angle of Twist (2 marks)**

$$
\theta = \frac{T L}{G J} = \frac{20 \times 10^6 \times 3000}{80{,}000 \times 8.546 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta = 0.0878 \text{ rad} \approx 5.03°}
$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Part B(b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Same material, same length $L = 2$ m
- Shaft 1: $d_1 = 50$ mm, Shaft 2: $d_2 = 75$ mm
- Same torque throughout (series connection): $T = 5$ kN·m = $5 \times 10^6$ N·mm
- $G = 80$ GPa

**Step 1: Polar Moments (1 mark)**

$$
J_1 = \frac{\pi (50)^4}{32} = 6.136 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^4
$$

$$
J_2 = \frac{\pi (75)^4}{32} = 3.108 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4
$$

**Step 2: Angle of Twist in Each Shaft (2 marks)**

$$
\theta_1 = \frac{T L}{G J_1} = \frac{5 \times 10^6 \times 2000}{80{,}000 \times 6.136 \times 10^5}
$$

$$
\theta_1 = \frac{10^{10}}{4.909 \times 10^{10}} = 0.2037 \text{ rad}
$$

$$
\theta_2 = \frac{T L}{G J_2} = \frac{5 \times 10^6 \times 2000}{80{,}000 \times 3.108 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\theta_2 = \frac{10^{10}}{2.486 \times 10^{11}} = 0.0402 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 3: Total Angle of Twist (1 mark)**

$$
\boxed{\theta_{\text{total}} = \theta_1 + \theta_2 = 0.2037 + 0.0402 = 0.2439 \text{ rad} \approx 13.97°}
$$

**Step 4: Maximum Shear Stress in Shaft 1 (1.5 marks)**

$$
\tau_{1,\max} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d_1^3} = \frac{16 \times 5 \times 10^6}{\pi \times (50)^3} = \frac{8 \times 10^7}{3.927 \times 10^5}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_{1,\max} = 203.7 \text{ MPa}}
$$

**Step 5: Maximum Shear Stress in Shaft 2 (1.5 marks)**

$$
\tau_{2,\max} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d_2^3} = \frac{16 \times 5 \times 10^6}{\pi \times (75)^3} = \frac{8 \times 10^7}{1.326 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_{2,\max} = 60.3 \text{ MPa}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The smaller shaft (50 mm) experiences a much higher stress (203.7 MPa) than the larger shaft (60.3 MPa). This confirms that **the smaller diameter governs the design** in a stepped shaft under uniform torque.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost 2–4 Marks Each in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (most common, –2 marks):** Mixing mm with m. *If you compute $J$ in m⁴ but $T$ in N·mm, your answer will be off by a factor of $10^{12}$. Pick ONE system and stick to it.*
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the Inner Diameter in Hollow Shafts (–2 marks):** Using $J = \pi d^4/32$ for a hollow shaft gives wrong answers. Use $J = \pi(D^4 - d^4)/32$.
> 
> 3. **Confusing Polar ($J$) with Area Moment ($I$) (–2 marks):** $I = \pi d^4/64$ (bending), $J = \pi d^4/32$ (torsion). They differ by a factor of 2.
> 
> 4. **Power-RPM-Torque Formula Error (–1 to –2 marks):** $P = 2\pi NT/60$ gives **Watts** if $T$ is in N·m. If $T$ is in N·mm, you must convert $T$ to N·m or use $P = 2\pi NT/(60 \times 10^6)$.
> 
> 5. **Wrong Sign in Angle of Twist (–1 mark):** Direction of twist is determined by **Right-Hand Rule** with respect to torque vector. Show it clearly in diagrams.
> 
> 6. **Skipping Assumptions in Derivation (–1 to –2 marks):** A derivation question in KTU 2024 ESE explicitly asks to *state assumptions*. Don't omit them.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Save this section for last-minute revision before the KTU ESE.**

### Core Concepts ✅
- [x] Torsion = **twisting** of a shaft about its longitudinal axis.
- [x] The theory applies only to **circular cross-sections** (solid and hollow).
- [x] Cross-sections **remain plane and undistorted** — the most important assumption.
- [x] Shear stress varies **linearly** with radius $r$ (zero at center, max at surface).

### Master Formula ⭐
- [x] **Universal Torsion Equation:** $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$

### Geometric Properties of Cross-Section 📐
- [x] Solid: $J = \pi d^4/32$ and $Z_p = \pi d^3/16$
- [x] Hollow: $J = \pi(D^4 - d^4)/32$ and $Z_p = \pi(D^4 - d^4)/(16D)$

### Strength and Stiffness 💪
- [x] **Strength criterion:** $\tau_{\max} = 16T/(\pi d^3) \leq \tau_{\text{allow}}$
- [x] **Stiffness criterion:** $\theta = TL/(GJ) \leq \theta_{\text{allow}}$

### Power Transmission ⚙️
- [x] $P = 2\pi NT/60$ (Watts) — remember unit conversion!

### Energy and Rigidity 🔋
- [x] Strain energy: $U = T^2 L / (2GJ) = \frac{1}{2} T\theta$
- [x] Shaft stiffness: $k_t = GJ/L$ (analogy to spring constant $k$)

### Compound Shafts 🔗
- [x] **Series:** Same torque, angles add up.
- [x] **Parallel:** Same angle, torques share based on $GJ/L$ ratio.

### Why Hollow Shafts? 🚗
- [x] Same weight, higher strength — material at center is underutilized in solid shafts.

### Examiner's Red Flags 🚩
- [x] **Never** confuse $I$ (second moment) with $J$ (polar second moment).
- [x] **Always** use consistent units throughout the calculation.
- [x] **Always** show free-body diagram and method of sections for multi-segment shafts.
- [x] **Always** state assumptions in derivation questions.

### High-Yield Derivations for KTU ESE 📚
- [x] Derivation of universal torsion equation (most frequent — 7 marks).
- [x] Derivation of strain energy in torsion (frequent — 5 marks).
- [x] Derivation of power equation (sometimes asked — 3 marks).

### Typical KTU Numerical Pattern 🔢
1. Power and rpm given → Find torque.
2. Torque and diameter given → Find max shear stress.
3. Shear stress and power given → Find required diameter.
4. Multiple segments → Use method of sections to find internal torques, then apply torsion equation to each segment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Mantra for Torsion:** *"Stress is linear in radius, twist is linear in length, and the universal equation connects torque, geometry, material, and deformation in a single line."*

---

**End of Module 2 — Torsion Theory of Elastic Circular Bars Notes** 🎯
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assumptions and Limitations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT402 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Assumptions and Limitations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In **Strength of Materials (PCSFT402) – Module 2**, the term *"Assumptions and Limitations"* refers to the set of idealised physical, geometrical, and mechanical conditions that are imposed on a material (typically a structural member) so that the governing equations of **Simple Stress and Simple Strain** can be derived, solved, and applied in a closed analytical form. These assumptions form the *axiomatic foundation* of the classical theory of elasticity used at the undergraduate level and define the *domain of validity* within which the mathematical predictions of the theory remain physically meaningful.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> The student must be able to **list, justify, and critically evaluate** the assumptions used in the derivation of stress–strain relations, Hooke's Law, and the elastic constants ($E$, $G$, $K$, $\nu$). The limitations explain *why real engineering materials deviate* from these idealised predictions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are predicting the trajectory of a cricket ball thrown from the boundary. In Physics I, you assume:

- No air drag (vacuum)
- Constant gravity
- Point mass
- Rigid body (no deformation)

These *assumptions* simplify the math enormously. But the real ball *does* experience drag, gravity varies with altitude, the ball has a finite size, and it slightly deforms on impact. The "limitations" are the *real-world gaps* between your model and reality. The same logic applies in **Strength of Materials**: the *assumptions* are the simplifying axioms, and the *limitations* are the scenarios where they break down.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used in This Module:**
> - **Young's Modulus ($E$):** expressed in **$\text{N/mm}^2$** or **GPa** ($\text{1 GPa} = 10^3 \text{ MPa} = 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$)
> - **Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$):** dimensionless
> - **Shear Modulus ($G$):** in **$\text{N/mm}^2$** or **GPa**
> - **Bulk Modulus ($K$):** in **$\text{N/mm}^2$** or **GPa**

### 1.3 The Role of Assumptions in Module 2

The theory of **Simple Stresses and Strains** (Module 2) is built on a *cascade* of assumptions, each enabling a specific mathematical operation. Removing even one assumption transforms the problem from a simple one-dimensional ODE into a complex partial differential equation (PDE) requiring the full machinery of **Theory of Elasticity**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Master List of Assumptions in Simple Stress–Strain Theory

The classical theory used in KTU Module 2 rests on the following **six fundamental assumptions**. Each is justified below with its engineering motivation.

#### Assumption 1 – Homogeneity
- **Statement:** The material has *uniform composition* and *identical mechanical properties* at every point in its volume.
- **Why:** Allows us to define a single set of elastic constants ($E$, $G$, $K$, $\nu$) for the entire body.
- **Real-world counterexample:** Concrete is a *composite* (cement + aggregate + voids) and is not strictly homogeneous at the micro-scale.

#### Assumption 2 – Isotropy
- **Statement:** Mechanical properties (especially $E$) are *identical in all directions* at any point.
- **Why:** Ensures that the stress–strain relation $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ is direction-independent.
- **Real-world counterexample:** Wood, fibre-reinforced composites, and rolled steel sheets are *anisotropic*.

#### Assumption 3 – Continuity
- **Statement:** The material is a *continuous medium* — there are no voids or discontinuities.
- **Why:** Permits the use of differential calculus ($\frac{d\sigma}{dx}$, $\frac{d\varepsilon}{dy}$) and stress functions.
- **Real-world counterexample:** Foams, porous castings, and cracked bodies violate continuity.

#### Assumption 4 – Perfect Elasticity
- **Statement:** The material obeys **Hooke's Law** within the elastic range; the body returns to its original shape *completely* upon unloading.
- **Why:** Gives a *linear* relationship between stress and strain, enabling superposition.
- **Real-world counterexample:** Mild steel exhibits a *yield point*; polymers often show *viscoelastic* (time-dependent) behaviour.

#### Assumption 5 – Small Deformations (Infinitesimal Strain)
- **Statement:** Deformations are *very small* compared to the original dimensions of the body, so the change in geometry can be ignored when writing equilibrium equations.
- **Why:** Linearises the strain–displacement relations: $\varepsilon = \frac{du}{dx}$ (not $\frac{du}{dx + u}$).
- **Real-world counterexample:** Thin rubber membranes, large-deflection beams, and inflatable structures.

#### Assumption 6 – Plane Sections Remain Plane (Bernoulli's Hypothesis)
- **Statement:** Cross-sections that are flat and perpendicular to the axis *before loading* remain flat and perpendicular to the deformed axis *after loading* (no warping).
- **Why:** Justifies the assumption of a *linear strain distribution* across the section, leading to the flexure formula $\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}$.
- **Real-world counterexample:** Short, deep beams where *shear deformation* causes warping.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Unit / Dimension |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | Direct (Normal) Stress | $\text{N/mm}^2$ or MPa |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\delta L}{L}$ | Linear Strain | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\sigma = E \, \varepsilon$ | **Hooke's Law** (within elastic limit) | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |
| 4 | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | Young's Modulus (Modulus of Elasticity) | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |
| 5 | $G = \dfrac{\tau}{\gamma}$ | Shear Modulus (Modulus of Rigidity) | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |
| 6 | $K = \dfrac{\sigma_{v}}{\Delta V / V}$ | Bulk Modulus | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |
| 7 | $\nu = -\dfrac{\text{lateral strain}}{\text{longitudinal strain}}$ | Poisson's Ratio | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ | Relation between $E$, $G$, $\nu$ | — |
| 9 | $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ | Relation between $E$, $K$, $\nu$ | — |
| 10 | $\dfrac{E}{9K} + \dfrac{1}{G} = \dfrac{3}{E}$ | Combined identity (verifying relations) | — |
| 11 | $\text{Volumetric strain} \, \varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$ | Sum of normal strains (for hydrostatic pressure) | Dimensionless |
| 12 | $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$ | Bulk modulus in terms of $E$ and $\nu$ | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The three elastic-constant relations ($E = 2G(1+\nu)$, $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$, and $G = \frac{3KE}{9K+E}$) are asked in *almost every* University Exam Module 2 paper. Memorise them along with the **standard value of $\nu$ for steel ($\approx 0.3$)** and **cast iron ($\approx 0.25$)**.

### 2.3 Limitations — When the Theory Fails

The above assumptions give a beautifully simple theory, but the following **limitations** must always be declared in your exam answer:

| # | Limitation | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| L1 | Stress concentration near holes, notches, and sharp corners is ignored. | Local stresses can be 2–3× the nominal $\frac{P}{A}$ value (St. Venant's principle partially addresses this). |
| L2 | Temperature effects (thermal stresses) are not included in the basic equations. | Requires $\sigma_{th} = E \alpha \Delta T$ to be superposed. |
| L3 | Dynamic / impact loading is excluded. | Stress-wave propagation theory must be used. |
| L4 | Creep and fatigue (time-dependent failure) are not predicted. | High-temperature and cyclic applications need specialised design codes. |
| L5 | Plastic deformation beyond the elastic limit is not modelled. | Requires plastic analysis (yield criteria like Tresca, von Mises). |
| L6 | Behaviour at very high strain rates (e.g., crash, blast) is not captured. | Requires viscoelastic / viscoplastic constitutive models. |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

This set of assumptions and limitations is the **design language of every day-to-day structural analysis**: from designing a steel I-beam in a building to checking the deflection of a simple cantilever, civil and mechanical engineers *always* operate within this framework. Recognising its boundaries is what separates a *technician* from a *design engineer* — for example, knowing that the simple theory under-predicts stresses near a weld toe (L1) leads to the use of **fillet radii** and **fatigue-rated details** in bridges, pressure vessels, and aircraft structures.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Relationship $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ from First Principles

This derivation is a **KTU favourite** (asked frequently in Parts A and B). We derive the relation between Young's Modulus, Shear Modulus, and Poisson's Ratio from the *assumptions of isotropy, homogeneity, and small deformations*.

#### Step 1 — Set Up the Stress State
Consider a *cube* of side $a$ made of homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic material. Apply a **pure shear stress $\tau$** on its faces parallel to the $x$–$y$ plane.

By the property of *complementary shear*, equal shear stresses $\tau$ act on the four lateral faces of the element.

#### Step 2 — Rotate the Element by 45°

We now examine the *principal stresses* on a plane oriented at $\theta = 45°$ to the original axes. For a general 2-D state, the normal stress on a plane whose normal makes angle $\theta$ with the $x$-axis is:

$$
\sigma_{\theta} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta
$$

For our *pure shear* case, $\sigma_x = 0$, $\sigma_y = 0$, and $\tau_{xy} = \tau$. Substituting $\theta = 45°$ (so that $\cos 90° = 0$ and $\sin 90° = 1$):

$$
\sigma_{45} = \frac{0 + 0}{2} + \frac{0 - 0}{2}\cos 90° + \tau \sin 90°
$$

$$
\sigma_{45} = 0 + 0 + \tau \cdot 1 = \tau
$$

The perpendicular direction (at $\theta = -45°$) gives:

$$
\sigma_{-45} = 0 + 0 + \tau \sin(-90°) = -\tau
$$

#### Step 3 — Compute the Principal Strains

By *Assumption 4* (Hooke's Law for isotropic materials), the strain in the direction of $\sigma_{45} = \tau$ is:

$$
\varepsilon_{45} = \frac{1}{E}\bigl(\sigma_{45} - \nu \, \sigma_{-45}\bigr)
$$

Substituting the principal stresses:

$$
\varepsilon_{45} = \frac{1}{E}\bigl(\tau - \nu(-\tau)\bigr) = \frac{1}{E}\bigl(\tau + \nu \tau\bigr) = \frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}
$$

#### Step 4 — Compute the Engineering Shear Strain

The **engineering shear strain** $\gamma$ is the *change in angle* between two originally perpendicular lines. The diagonal along $45°$ stretches, and the diagonal along $-45°$ compresses. The total change in the right angle equals the *sum* of these two principal strains:

$$
\gamma = \varepsilon_{45} - \varepsilon_{-45} = 2\,\varepsilon_{45}
$$

(The factor of 2 arises because the perpendicular diagonal undergoes equal and opposite strain, contributing the same magnitude to the angle change.)

Substituting:

$$
\gamma = 2 \cdot \frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E} = \frac{2\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}
$$

#### Step 5 — Identify the Shear Modulus

By *definition* of the Shear Modulus, $\tau = G \gamma$, so:

$$
\gamma = \frac{\tau}{G}
$$

#### Step 6 — Equate the Two Expressions for $\gamma$

$$
\frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{2\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}
$$

Cancelling $\tau$ (non-zero) and cross-multiplying:

$$
E = 2G(1 + \nu)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,E = 2G(1 + \nu)\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This is *not* an empirical fit — it is a **direct consequence** of the assumptions of isotropy, homogeneity, linearity, and small deformation. If any of these fail (e.g., wood, which is anisotropic), this relation no longer holds.

### 3.2 Derivation of $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ from Hydrostatic Pressure

#### Step 1 — Apply Uniform Hydrostatic Pressure $p$
Let the cube be subjected to equal compressive stresses on all six faces: $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$.

#### Step 2 — Compute the Volumetric Strain
The strain in any one direction, using *Hooke's Law for isotropic materials*:

$$
\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\bigl(\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\bigr) = \frac{1}{E}\bigl(-p - \nu(-p - p)\bigr) = \frac{1}{E}\bigl(-p + 2\nu p\bigr) = \frac{-p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}
$$

#### Step 3 — Sum the Three Normal Strains
By definition of volumetric strain (small deformations):

$$
\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = 3\varepsilon_x
$$

$$
\varepsilon_v = \frac{-3p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}
$$

#### Step 4 — Apply the Definition of Bulk Modulus
By definition, $p = -K \, \varepsilon_v$ (the negative sign because $p$ is compressive). So:

$$
\varepsilon_v = -\frac{p}{K}
$$

#### Step 5 — Equate and Solve

$$
-\frac{p}{K} = \frac{-3p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}
$$

Cancelling $-p$:

$$
\frac{1}{K} = \frac{3(1 - 2\nu)}{E}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)\,}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cross-Check

| Identity | Derivation Origin | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|
| $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ | Pure shear (45° rotation) | Convert between $E$ and $G$ |
| $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$ | Hydrostatic pressure | Convert between $E$ and $K$ |
| $G = \dfrac{3KE}{9K+E}$ | Eliminate $E$ from above two | Direct formula for $G$ |
| $-1 < \nu < 0.5$ | Thermodynamic stability of materials | Bound check in numerical problems |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical — A KTU-Class Problem

**Problem:** A steel bar of length **2 m** and cross-section **40 mm × 40 mm** is subjected to an axial tensile load of **120 kN**. Given $E = 2.0 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$ and $\nu = 0.3$, find the (i) stress, (ii) longitudinal strain, (iii) lateral strain, and (iv) change in volume. Discuss *which assumptions* are involved.

**Solution:**

**Step (i) — Stress**
Cross-sectional area:
$$
A = 40 \times 40 = 1600 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Stress:
$$
\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{120 \times 10^3}{1600} = 75 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step (ii) — Longitudinal Strain** *(Assumption: Hooke's Law valid)*
$$
\varepsilon_{long} = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{75}{2.0 \times 10^5} = 3.75 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step (iii) — Lateral Strain** *(Assumption: Isotropy — same $\nu$ in all lateral directions)*
$$
\varepsilon_{lat} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{long} = -0.3 \times 3.75 \times 10^{-4} = -1.125 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step (iv) — Change in Volume** *(Assumption: Small deformations — volumetric strain is the sum of linear strains)*

Volumetric strain:
$$
\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_{long} + 2\varepsilon_{lat} = 3.75 \times 10^{-4} + 2(-1.125 \times 10^{-4}) = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}
$$

Original volume:
$$
V_0 = 2000 \times 1600 = 3.2 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3
$$

Change in volume:
$$
\Delta V = \varepsilon_v \cdot V_0 = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 3.2 \times 10^6 = 480 \text{ mm}^3
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discussion of Assumptions Used:**
> 1. **Homogeneity + Isotropy** — same $E$ and $\nu$ at every point and in every direction.
> 2. **Linearity / Hooke's Law** — $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ is valid (75 MPa is well below the yield strength of mild steel $\approx 250$ MPa).
> 3. **Small deformations** — strain is of order $10^{-4}$, so geometric non-linearity is negligible.
> 4. **Continuity** — the bar is a solid, void-free prism.
> 5. **Uniform stress distribution** — load is applied through the centroid (St. Venant's principle ensures uniformity away from the ends).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flowchart — The Assumption Cascade in Module 2

This Mermaid block shows *how* each assumption in Module 2 supports a specific mathematical step, and *where* the theory fails when any one is removed.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Solid Body Under Load] --> B{Assumption 1\nHomogeneity}
    B -- Holds --> C{Assumption 2\nIsotropy}
    B -- Fails --> Bfail[Composite materials\nWood, Concrete]
    C -- Holds --> D{Assumption 3\nContinuity}
    C -- Fails --> Cfail[Anisotropic plates\nFibre composites]
    D -- Holds --> E{Assumption 4\nPerfect Elasticity}
    D -- Fails --> Dfail[Porous / cracked bodies]
    E -- Holds --> F{Assumption 5\nSmall Deformations}
    E -- Fails --> Efail[Plastics, Yielded steel]
    F -- Holds --> G{Assumption 6\nPlane Sections Remain Plane}
    F -- Fails --> Ffail[Rubber membranes\nLarge deflection beams]
    G -- Holds --> H[Valid Simple Stress Theory\nsigma = P/A, sigma = E epsilon]
    G -- Fails --> Gfail[Deep beams\nShear deformation dominant]
    H --> I[KTU Module 2 Result\nE = 2G 1 plus nu\nE = 3K 1 minus 2nu]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — The Elastic Constants and Their Interrelation

The following Mermaid block visualises the **logical dependency** between the four elastic constants ($E$, $G$, $K$, $\nu$) that is the heart of Module 2.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[Young's Modulus E] --- R1[Poisson's Ratio nu]
    R1 --- G1[Shear Modulus G]
    E1 --- K1[Bulk Modulus K]
    R1 --- K1
    G1 --- K1
    E1 -.derived relation.-> G1
    E1 -.derived relation.-> K1
    G1 -.derived relation.-> K1
```

**Reading the diagram:** The arrows show that *all four* elastic constants of an isotropic, homogeneous, linearly elastic material are **not independent**. Knowing any two of them (e.g., $E$ and $\nu$) fixes the other two ($G$ and $K$) through the derived relations.

### 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — Where the Theory Sits in Engineering Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Real Engineering Problem] --> S2[Identify Material]
    S1 --> S3[Identify Loading]
    S2 --> Q1{Material Isotropic\nHomogeneous Elastic?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> A1[Apply Module 2 Assumptions]
    Q1 -- No --> A2[Switch to Advanced Theory\nAnisotropic / Composite]
    S3 --> Q2{Static or Dynamic?\nSmall or Large Strain?}
    Q2 -- Static Small --> A1
    Q2 -- Dynamic or Large --> A3[Use FEM\nTime-dependent analysis]
    A1 --> R1[Compute E G K nu relations]
    A1 --> R2[Compute sigma, epsilon, delta]
    A3 --> R3[Validate using Software\nANSYS, ABAQUS]
    R1 --> F1[Final Design Check]
    R2 --> F1
    R3 --> F1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The simple theory taught in Module 2 is the *first-pass design check*. For critical or non-standard applications (composites, impact, high temperature), the engineer must consciously step *outside* these assumptions and use the appropriate advanced theory — a fact that the KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests in the **"limitations"** part of the question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State any **six** fundamental assumptions made in the theory of simple stresses and strains. Mention the engineering importance of assuming the material to be homogeneous and isotropic.*

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

1. **Homogeneity** — the material has uniform composition and identical mechanical properties at every point.
2. **Isotropy** — mechanical properties are the same in all directions.
3. **Continuity** — the material is a continuous, void-free medium.
4. **Perfect Elasticity** — the material obeys Hooke's Law and fully recovers its original shape on unloading.
5. **Small (Infinitesimal) Deformations** — strains are small enough that change in geometry can be neglected in equilibrium equations.
6. **Plane Sections Remain Plane** (Bernoulli's hypothesis) — cross-sections do not warp under axial load.

**Engineering Importance:**
- Treating the material as **homogeneous** allows a *single set* of elastic constants ($E$, $G$, $K$, $\nu$) to represent the entire body, which is essential for closed-form design formulas.
- Treating it as **isotropic** allows the *same* formulas to be applied in any direction, removing the need for directional testing of every component (e.g., a steel shaft can be analysed identically along its length or across its diameter).
- Together, they reduce a complex 3-D boundary value problem to a *1-D* strength-of-materials problem — the foundation of everyday design.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List the **limitations** of the simple stress–strain theory. Why is it unsafe to use $\sigma = P/A$ near a hole or a sharp corner?*

**Model Answer:**

**Limitations (3 marks total):**

1. It cannot predict **stress concentrations** near geometric discontinuities (holes, notches, fillets).
2. It excludes **thermal, dynamic, and time-dependent** effects (creep, fatigue).
3. It does not model **plastic deformation** beyond the elastic limit.
4. It assumes **small deformations**, so it fails for rubber-like or membrane structures.
5. It assumes **isotropy**, so it cannot be applied to composites or wood.
6. The basic $\sigma = P/A$ formula assumes **uniform stress distribution** across the section.

**Why $\sigma = P/A$ is unsafe near a hole or notch:**
The simple formula gives the *average* stress across the *gross* cross-section. However, near a circular hole in a plate under tension, classical theory (Kirsch, 1898) and experiments show that the *peak* stress occurs at the edge of the hole and is approximately **3 times** the nominal $\frac{P}{A}$ value. This is called the **stress concentration factor (SCF or $K_t$)**. If the engineer blindly uses $\sigma = P/A$, the local stress at the hole edge may exceed the yield strength, causing crack initiation and sudden failure. Hence, design codes (IS 800, ASME) mandate generous fillet radii and pre-machined holes with reinforcement.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. *Always* state the **assumption name** *and* its **mathematical consequence** (e.g., "Isotropy implies $E_x = E_y = E_z$"). Stating only the name fetches 1 mark; the mathematical link fetches the remaining 2.
> 2. In derivation questions, **never skip the 45° rotation step** — most students lose 2–3 marks by jumping from shear stress directly to $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ without showing the rotated-element analysis.
> 3. When asked for "limitations", give **at least four** distinct ones. A single-sentence answer like "theory is approximate" gets 0 marks — be specific (stress concentration, temperature, dynamic loading, anisotropy, etc.).
> 4. *Always* write the units in box-style final answers (e.g., $\sigma = 75 \text{ N/mm}^2$, not just 75).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** State and explain the assumptions made in the derivation of the relations between the elastic constants $E$, $G$, $K$, and $\nu$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A solid cylindrical bar of diameter **30 mm** and length **1.5 m** is subjected to an axial pull of **60 kN**. If $E = 2.1 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$ and $\nu = 0.3$, determine the (i) stress, (ii) longitudinal strain, (iii) lateral strain, (iv) change in diameter, and (v) change in volume. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The relations $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ and $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ are derived under the following assumptions:

| # | Assumption | Role in the Derivation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Homogeneity** | A single set of elastic constants applies at every point of the cube. |
| 2 | **Isotropy** | $E$ and $\nu$ are the same in every direction; shear acts identically on all faces. |
| 3 | **Continuity** | Strain and stress fields are differentiable (no voids). |
| 4 | **Perfect Elasticity (Hooke's Law)** | The stress–strain law $\varepsilon = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma - \nu(\sigma_{perp1} + \sigma_{perp2})]$ is valid. |
| 5 | **Small Deformations** | Engineering shear strain $\gamma = 2\varepsilon_{45}$ is valid (not $\tan\gamma$); volumetric strain is the sum of linear strains. |
| 6 | **Plane Sections Remain Plane** | Used in pure-shear derivation to argue no warping on the 45° rotated element. |

**Engineering significance of each (1 mark each, 5 marks for the table + 2 marks for an introductory paragraph):**

*Introductory paragraph (2 marks):* These assumptions together transform a complex 3-D boundary value problem into a tractable 1-D problem. The *validity range* of the resulting relations is limited to *isotropic, homogeneous engineering materials* (metals, alloys) loaded *quasi-statically* and *elastically*. They fail for wood, composites, polymers, and at elevated temperatures or high strain rates.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- Diameter $d = 30 \text{ mm}$
- Length $L = 1.5 \text{ m} = 1500 \text{ mm}$
- Axial load $P = 60 \text{ kN} = 60\,000 \text{ N}$
- $E = 2.1 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$
- $\nu = 0.3$

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional Area**

$$
A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 30^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 900}{4} = 706.86 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — (i) Axial Stress**

$$
\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{60\,000}{706.86} = 84.88 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 84.9 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — (ii) Longitudinal Strain**

$$
\varepsilon_{long} = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{84.88}{2.1 \times 10^5} = 4.042 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 4 — (iii) Lateral Strain**

$$
\varepsilon_{lat} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{long} = -0.3 \times 4.042 \times 10^{-4} = -1.2126 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 5 — (iv) Change in Diameter**

$$
\Delta d = \varepsilon_{lat} \cdot d = -1.2126 \times 10^{-4} \times 30 = -3.638 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm}
$$

(Negative sign indicates *contraction*.)

**Step 6 — (v) Change in Volume**

Original volume:
$$
V_0 = A \times L = 706.86 \times 1500 = 1.0603 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3
$$

Volumetric strain:
$$
\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_{long} + 2\varepsilon_{lat} = 4.042 \times 10^{-4} + 2(-1.2126 \times 10^{-4}) = 1.617 \times 10^{-4}
$$

Change in volume:
$$
\Delta V = \varepsilon_v \cdot V_0 = 1.617 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.0603 \times 10^6 = 171.45 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Final Answer Box:**
$$
\boxed{\sigma = 84.9 \text{ MPa}, \quad \varepsilon_{long} = 4.042 \times 10^{-4}, \quad \varepsilon_{lat} = -1.213 \times 10^{-4}, \quad \Delta d = -0.00364 \text{ mm}, \quad \Delta V = 171.45 \text{ mm}^3}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the relation $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ starting from a pure shear stress element of side $a$. Show the 45° rotation step clearly. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **limitations** of the simple stress theory. Explain why the formula $\sigma = P/A$ is inadequate for (i) a stepped bar with a sharp fillet, (ii) a plate with a central circular hole, and (iii) a member subjected to impact loading. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

*Refer to the detailed derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes.* Stepwise marks are:

| Step | Action | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Draw the cube under pure shear $\tau$; mention complementary shear. | 1 |
| 2 | State the principal stress formulas. | 1 |
| 3 | Apply $\theta = 45°$ and $\theta = -45°$; derive $\sigma_{45} = +\tau$, $\sigma_{-45} = -\tau$. | 2 |
| 4 | Use Hooke's law for isotropic material to get $\varepsilon_{45} = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$. | 1 |
| 5 | Equate $\gamma = 2\varepsilon_{45} = \frac{\tau}{G}$ and simplify to $E = 2G(1+\nu)$. | 2 |

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks** *(2 marks per case + 1 mark for the summary)*

**General Limitations (briefly mention — 1 mark):**
The simple theory assumes a homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic, small-strain, statically loaded, continuous body. It ignores stress concentrations, temperature, dynamic effects, plasticity, and anisotropy.

**Case (i) — Stepped Bar with Sharp Fillet:** *(2 marks)*
At a sudden change in cross-section, the flow of stress lines is *disturbed* and *crowds* near the inner corner. The local stress at the fillet is far higher than the nominal $\frac{P}{A}$ value. The **stress concentration factor** $K_t = \frac{\sigma_{max}}{\sigma_{nom}}$ can be 1.5 to 3.0 depending on the fillet radius. The simple theory predicts a uniform stress distribution (St. Venant's principle is *not* valid at the discontinuity) and therefore *under-estimates* the maximum stress, leading to unsafe design.

**Case (ii) — Plate with a Central Circular Hole:** *(2 marks)*
This is the classic **Kirsch problem (1898)**. The nominal stress is $\sigma_{nom} = \frac{P}{A_{net}}$ or $\frac{P}{A_{gross}}$ depending on the convention. The actual *peak* stress occurs at the edge of the hole, perpendicular to the load, and is:

$$
\sigma_{max} = 3\,\sigma_{nom}
$$

That is, the stress is **three times** the average. The simple $\sigma = P/A$ formula assumes the load is distributed uniformly across the entire section and ignores the *disruption* caused by the hole. A design based on this formula would severely *under-predict* the failure risk.

**Case (iii) — Member under Impact Loading:** *(2 marks)*
The simple theory assumes *quasi-static* loading, where the load is applied slowly enough for inertial effects to be negligible. Under impact (e.g., a hammer blow), the load is applied in milliseconds, and **stress waves** propagate through the member at the speed of sound in the material ($c = \sqrt{E/\rho}$). The stress distribution is *not* uniform; it is governed by wave equations. Furthermore, the dynamic stress can be 10–100× the equivalent static stress. Hence $\sigma = P/A$ (with the impact load treated statically) is grossly unsafe, and impact factors or full dynamic analysis (using strain energy and conservation of energy) must be employed.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision**

- [x] The **six core assumptions** are: **H**omogeneity, **I**sotropy, **C**ontinuity, **P**erfect Elasticity (Hooke's Law), **S**mall Deformations, **P**lane Sections Remain Plane. *(Mnemonic: "HIC-PSP" — pronounced "hiccups".)*
- [x] Hooke's Law is the cornerstone: $\sigma = E\varepsilon$, valid *only* within the proportional limit.
- [x] The **four elastic constants** are $E$, $G$, $K$, $\nu$. Of these, **only two are independent** for isotropic materials.
- [x] The **must-memorise identities** are:
  - $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$
  - $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$
  - $G = \frac{3KE}{9K + E}$
  - $-1 < \nu < 0.5$ (thermodynamic bounds)
- [x] **Standard value of $\nu$ for steel ≈ 0.3**, for **cast iron ≈ 0.25**, for **concrete ≈ 0.15 to 0.20**, for **rubber ≈ 0.45 to 0.49**.
- [x] The **45° rotation trick** is the key to deriving $E = 2G(1+\nu)$. Don't forget the factor of **2** between engineering shear strain $\gamma$ and principal tensile strain $\varepsilon_{45}$.
- [x] The **bulk modulus $K$** relates to *volumetric* change under *hydrostatic* pressure; the **Young's modulus $E$** relates to *linear* deformation under *uniaxial* load.
- [x] **Limitations** to always mention: stress concentration, temperature, dynamic/impact loading, plastic behaviour, anisotropy, large deformations, creep, and fatigue.
- [x] **St. Venant's Principle** says stress distribution becomes uniform *far* from the point of load application — but not near it.
- [x] The simple $\sigma = P/A$ formula is valid only for *prismatic* bars loaded through the *centroid* with *uniform* stress distribution.
- [x] For **KTU 2024 answer-writing**: always *state the assumption*, *show the formula it enables*, and *give one engineering example* where it fails. This 3-part structure fetches full marks in 7-mark questions.
- [x] **Units to use:** $E$, $G$, $K$ in $\text{N/mm}^2$ (MPa) or GPa; $\nu$ is dimensionless; stresses in MPa; strains as pure decimals (e.g., $4.04 \times 10^{-4}$).
- [x] **Final answer boxing** is mandatory in KTU scripts; use a single-line `\boxed{}` for the final numerical answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polar Modulus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT402 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Polar Modulus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Polar Modulus — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

**Polar Modulus** (also called the **Polar Section Modulus** or **Torsion Modulus**), denoted by the symbol $Z_p$ or simply $Z$, is defined as the **ratio of the polar moment of inertia ($J$) of a cross-section about its longitudinal axis to the radius ($R$) of the shaft**.

$$Z_p = \frac{J}{R}$$

It is a **purely geometric property** of the cross-section (units: $mm^3$ or $m^3$) that quantifies how efficiently a shaft transmits **torsional (twisting) loads** without exceeding the permissible shear stress. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PCSFT402 (Strength of Materials), Polar Modulus is the foundational geometric parameter used to size circular shafts subjected to pure torsion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Polar Modulus is the rotational/torsional analogue of the *Section Modulus* ($Z = I/y$) used in bending. Just as $Z$ converts bending stress into bending moment, $Z_p$ converts **shear stress into torque**.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are twisting a wet towel to wring out water.

- A **thin towel** (small radius) is very easy to twist — but it twists too much, and fibers break easily. *(Low Polar Modulus)*
- A **thick rolled-up towel** (large radius) resists twisting firmly, and the water is wrung out without the towel tearing. *(High Polar Modulus)*

Now think of the fibers in the towel as the cross-section of a shaft. The further the material lies from the **centerline (axis of rotation)**, the more it contributes to resisting the twist. The Polar Modulus mathematically captures *how far* and *how much* material is placed at a distance from the axis — it is essentially a measure of the **twisting strength per unit stress**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Intuition:**
> - A **solid shaft** has a higher Polar Modulus per unit weight than an equivalent-strength hollow shaft only for very small diameters. Beyond a certain size, hollow shafts are more efficient.
> - **Drive shafts** in automobiles, **propeller shafts** in ships, and **drill rods** in mining are all designed using the Polar Modulus concept.
> - The Polar Modulus assumes **linear elastic behavior** (Hooke's Law in shear: $\tau = G\gamma$) — it is **not valid** beyond the elastic limit.

## Standard Geometric Constants (Bolded)

- **Diameter of shaft = $D$** (outer diameter for hollow sections)
- **Inner diameter (for hollow shafts) = $d$**
- **Radius = $R = D/2$**
- **Inner radius = $r = d/2$**
- **Polar Moment of Inertia, $J$ = $\dfrac{\pi D^4}{32}$** (solid shaft)
- **Polar Moment of Inertia, $J$ = $\dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$** (hollow shaft)
- **Shear Modulus = $G$** (material property, e.g., **$G = 80$ GPa for structural steel**)
- **Allowable Shear Stress = $\tau_{allow}$**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Polar Modulus — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Operational Logic Behind Polar Modulus

The Polar Modulus emerges directly from the **Torsion Equation** for a circular shaft. Let us break down the logic step by step:

### Step 1 — Start with the Torsion Equation (Clapeyron's Formulation)

For a circular shaft under pure torsion, the shear stress at any radius $r$ from the center is **linearly proportional** to the distance from the axis:

$$\frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{T}{J} = \frac{G\theta}{L}$$

where:
- $\tau$ = shear stress at radius $r$
- $T$ = applied torque
- $J$ = polar moment of inertia of the cross-section
- $G$ = shear modulus of rigidity
- $\theta$ = angle of twist (in radians)
- $L$ = length of the shaft

### Step 2 — Identify the Maximum Stress Location

Since $\tau$ is maximum when $r$ is maximum (i.e., at the **outer surface**, where $r = R$):

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot R}{J}$$

### Step 3 — Rearrange to Isolate Torque

$$T = \tau_{max} \cdot \frac{J}{R}$$

### Step 4 — Define Polar Modulus

The factor $\dfrac{J}{R}$ converts the **maximum allowable shear stress** into the **maximum safe torque**. By definition:

$$\boxed{Z_p = \frac{J}{R}}$$

So the design equation becomes:

$$T_{max} = \tau_{allow} \cdot Z_p$$

> [!NOTE]
