> **Why "Polar"?** The word *polar* arises because we are integrating the area elements with respect to the **radial distance from a pole (the axis of rotation)** — this is exactly how the polar moment of inertia $J = \int r^2 \, dA$ is computed in polar coordinates.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Applicable Section |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $Z_p = \dfrac{J}{R}$ | Fundamental definition of Polar Modulus | All circular sections |
| 2 | $T_{max} = \tau_{max} \cdot Z_p$ | Torque capacity from max shear stress | All shafts |
| 3 | $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi D^3}{16}$ | Polar Modulus for **solid circular** shaft | Solid shaft |
| 4 | $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{16 D}$ | Polar Modulus for **hollow circular** shaft | Hollow shaft |
| 5 | $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi D^3}{16}\left(1 - k^4\right)$ where $k = d/D$ | Compact form for hollow shaft | Hollow shaft |
| 6 | $J = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{32}$ | Polar moment of inertia, solid | Solid shaft |
| 7 | $J = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$ | Polar moment of inertia, hollow | Hollow shaft |
| 8 | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{T}{Z_p}$ | Maximum shear stress from torque | All shafts |
| 9 | $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ | Torsion equation (full form) | Stiffness analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Note:** Polar Modulus $Z_p$ has units of $mm^3$ (or $m^3$). When using SI consistently: $T$ in N·mm, $\tau$ in N/mm² (= MPa), $Z_p$ in mm³. KTU examiners typically use **N, mm, MPa** — avoid mixing units.

## Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Polar Modulus |
|------------------|------------------------------|
| **Mechanical Design** | Sizing drive shafts, gear shafts, and axles |
| **Automotive** | Propeller shafts in rear-wheel-drive vehicles |
| **Aerospace** | Helicopter rotor drive shafts (often hollow for low weight) |
| **Civil Engineering** | Torsion in reinforced concrete beams (analogous concept) |
| **Marine** | Ship propeller shafts, where hollow sections reduce weight while maintaining $Z_p$ |
| **Power Transmission** | Generator and turbine shafts operating at high rpm |

The Polar Modulus is the **first parameter calculated** when designing a shaft for torque transmission. The design process follows this KTU-standard sequence:

1. Calculate the **applied torque** ($T = 9550 \cdot P/N$ for power transmission).
2. Select the **allowable shear stress** based on material.
3. Compute the **required Polar Modulus** ($Z_p = T/\tau_{allow}$).
4. Solve for **shaft diameter** $D$ from the $Z_p$ formula.
5. Check **deflection/twist angle** using the torsion equation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Polar Modulus — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Polar Modulus for a Solid Circular Shaft

### Starting Point: Definition of Polar Moment of Inertia

The polar moment of inertia $J$ for a solid circular cross-section of diameter $D$ (radius $R = D/2$) about its centroidal axis is **derived by integrating** $r^2 \, dA$ over the entire cross-sectional area.

For an annular elemental ring of radius $r$, thickness $dr$:
- Area of element: $dA = 2\pi r \, dr$
- Contribution to $J$: $dJ = r^2 \cdot dA = 2\pi r^3 \, dr$

Integrating from the center ($r = 0$) to the outer surface ($r = R$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
J &= \int_0^{R} 2\pi r^3 \, dr \\
  &= 2\pi \left[\frac{r^4}{4}\right]_0^{R} \\
  &= 2\pi \cdot \frac{R^4}{4} \\
  &= \frac{\pi R^4}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Substitute $R = D/2$ to Get Standard Form

$$
\begin{aligned}
J &= \frac{\pi}{2} \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^4 \\
  &= \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \frac{D^4}{16} \\
  &= \frac{\pi D^4}{32}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the standard textbook formula for $J$ of a solid shaft. Note: this is **double** the area moment of inertia $I$ about any diameter (since $J = I_x + I_y = 2I$ for circular sections by symmetry, and $I = \pi D^4/64$).

### Apply the Definition of Polar Modulus

By definition, $Z_p = J / R$. Substituting $J = \pi D^4 / 32$ and $R = D/2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_p &= \frac{J}{R} = \frac{\pi D^4 / 32}{D/2} \\
    &= \frac{\pi D^4}{32} \cdot \frac{2}{D} \\
    &= \frac{\pi D^4 \cdot 2}{32 \cdot D} \\
    &= \frac{2 \pi D^4}{32 D} \\
    &= \frac{\pi D^3}{16}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Result for Solid Shaft

$$\boxed{Z_p = \frac{\pi D^3}{16}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cubic dependence on diameter:** Since $Z_p \propto D^3$, doubling the shaft diameter increases the torque capacity by **8 times**. This is why KTU problems often explore small diameter changes having dramatic effects.

## Derivation 2: Polar Modulus for a Hollow Circular Shaft

### Starting Point: Polar Moment of Inertia for Hollow Section

For a hollow circular cross-section with outer radius $R$ and inner radius $r$, the polar moment of inertia is computed by subtracting the inner "hole" contribution from the full disc:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J &= \int_r^{R} 2\pi \rho^3 \, d\rho \\
  &= 2\pi \left[\frac{\rho^4}{4}\right]_r^{R} \\
  &= \frac{\pi}{2}\left(R^4 - r^4\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Substitute $R = D/2$ and $r = d/2$

$$
\begin{aligned}
J &= \frac{\pi}{2}\left[\left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^4 - \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^4\right] \\
  &= \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \frac{D^4 - d^4}{16} \\
  &= \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Apply the Definition of Polar Modulus

Now $Z_p = J / R$ with $R = D/2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_p &= \frac{J}{R} = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)/32}{D/2} \\
    &= \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32} \cdot \frac{2}{D} \\
    &= \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{16 D}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Alternative Form Using the Ratio $k = d/D$

Let $k = d/D$ (diameter ratio). Then $d^4 = k^4 D^4$, and:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_p &= \frac{\pi (D^4 - k^4 D^4)}{16 D} \\
    &= \frac{\pi D^4 (1 - k^4)}{16 D} \\
    &= \frac{\pi D^3 (1 - k^4)}{16}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Result for Hollow Shaft

$$\boxed{Z_p = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{16 D} = \frac{\pi D^3 (1 - k^4)}{16}}$$

## Analytical Comparison Matrix: Solid vs. Hollow Shafts

| Property | Solid Shaft | Hollow Shaft | Remarks |
|----------|-------------|--------------|---------|
| $J$ | $\pi D^4 / 32$ | $\pi(D^4 - d^4)/32$ | Hollow always smaller for same $D$ |
| $Z_p$ | $\pi D^3 / 16$ | $\pi(D^4 - d^4)/(16 D)$ | Hollow reduced by factor $(1 - k^4)$ |
| Weight | Higher (full material) | Lower (less material) | Hollow is weight-efficient |
| Strength | Higher for same $D$ | Lower for same $D$ | Trade-off between weight and strength |
| Common $k$ ratio | N/A | $0.5$ to $0.8$ | Standard engineering range |
| Cost | Higher material cost | Lower material cost | Hollow preferred for large diameters |

## KTU-Style Worked Example (Demonstrating Usage)

> **Problem:** A solid circular shaft of diameter **60 mm** transmits a torque of **2 kN·m**. Find the maximum shear stress induced in the shaft.

**Step 1 — Identify Given Data:**
- $D = 60$ mm, so $R = 30$ mm
- $T = 2$ kN·m $= 2 \times 10^6$ N·mm

**Step 2 — Compute Polar Modulus:**

$$
Z_p = \frac{\pi D^3}{16} = \frac{\pi (60)^3}{16} = \frac{\pi \cdot 216000}{16} = 13500\pi \approx 42411.5 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Torsion Equation:**

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{T}{Z_p} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{42411.5} \approx 47.16 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 47.16 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Result:**

$$\boxed{\tau_{max} \approx 47.16 \text{ MPa}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Polar Modulus — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Mermaid Block Diagram: Torsion Analysis Workflow

The diagram below maps the **functional workflow** for solving any KTU-style torsion problem using Polar Modulus. It traces the logical flow from the applied load to the final design verification.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Applied Torque T] --> B[Step 1: Determine Cross-Section Geometry]
    B --> C{Is Shaft Solid or Hollow?}
    C -->|Solid| D[Use D and compute R = D/2]
    C -->|Hollow| E[Use D and d and compute R = D/2]
    D --> F[Step 2: Compute Polar Modulus Zp]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Solid: Zp equals pi D cubed over 16]
    F --> H[Hollow: Zp equals pi D cubed times 1 minus k fourth over 16]
    G --> I[Step 3: Compute Maximum Shear Stress]
    H --> I
    I --> J[tau max equals T divided by Zp]
    J --> K{Is tau max within Allowable Limit?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Step 4: Design is SAFE]
    K -->|No| M[Step 5: INCREASE Diameter and Recompute]
    M --> B
    L --> N[Step 6: Optional Check - Angle of Twist]
    N --> O[theta equals TL divided by GJ]
    O --> P[Verify theta within Permissible Limit]
    P --> Q[FINAL DESIGN VERIFIED]
```

## Mermaid Diagram: Conceptual Relationship Between Stress, Torque, and Geometry

This diagram illustrates how Polar Modulus acts as the **conversion factor** between shear stress and applied torque, analogous to how Section Modulus works in bending.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Applied Torque T] -->|Divided by| B[Polar Modulus Zp equals J over R]
    B --> C[Maximum Shear Stress tau max]
    D[Cross-Section Geometry] -->|Defines| E[Polar Moment of Inertia J]
    E -->|Combined with| F[Radius R]
    F --> B
    C --> G{Comparison with Allowable Stress}
    G -->|Less than Allowable| H[Safe Design]
    G -->|Greater than Allowable| I[Redesign Required]
```

## Mermaid Diagram: Solid vs. Hollow Shaft Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Circular Shaft Cross-Section] --> B{Solid or Hollow}
    B -->|Solid| C[Full Disc Geometry]
    B -->|Hollow| D[Annular Ring Geometry]
    C --> C1[Outer radius R equals D over 2]
    C --> C2[Inner radius equals 0]
    C --> C3[J equals pi D to the 4 over 32]
    C --> C4[Zp equals pi D cubed over 16]
    D --> D1[Outer radius R equals D over 2]
    D --> D2[Inner radius r equals d over 2]
    D --> D3[J equals pi times D to the 4 minus d to the 4 over 32]
    D --> D4[Zp equals pi D cubed times 1 minus k to the 4 over 16]
    C4 --> E[Apply Torsion Equation]
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Compute Maximum Shear Stress and Angle of Twist]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Note for Students:** The Polar Modulus $Z_p$ is the "geometric signature" of the cross-section under torsion. Any change in diameter or wall thickness (for hollow sections) changes $Z_p$ **cubicly** in $D$ — so even a 10% increase in diameter produces approximately a 33% increase in torque capacity. This is why KTU problems frequently test diameter optimization.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Polar Modulus — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define Polar Modulus. Write the expression for the Polar Modulus of a hollow circular shaft.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Polar Modulus is defined as the ratio of the polar moment of inertia ($J$) of a cross-section about its longitudinal centroidal axis to the radius ($R$) of the shaft:

$$Z_p = \frac{J}{R}$$

**Expression for hollow shaft (2 marks):** For a hollow circular shaft with outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d$:

$$Z_p = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{16 D}$$

Equivalently, using the diameter ratio $k = d/D$:

$$Z_p = \frac{\pi D^3}{16}(1 - k^4)$$

Units: $mm^3$ (in mm-based SI) or $m^3$.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State the torsion equation. How is the Polar Modulus related to the maximum torque transmitted by a shaft?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Torsion equation (2 marks):** For a circular shaft of length $L$, subjected to torque $T$ at one end (with the other end fixed), the shear stress $\tau$ at a radial distance $r$ from the center is given by:

$$\frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{T}{J} = \frac{G\theta}{L}$$

**Relation to maximum torque (1 mark):** At the outer surface ($r = R$), the shear stress is maximum:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot R}{J} \implies T_{max} = \tau_{max} \cdot \frac{J}{R} = \tau_{max} \cdot Z_p$$

Thus, Polar Modulus $Z_p$ is the geometric factor that converts the allowable shear stress into the maximum safe torque.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the Polar Modulus of a **solid circular shaft** of diameter $D$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A solid steel shaft of diameter **80 mm** is required to transmit **60 kW** at **200 rpm**. If the allowable shear stress is **45 MPa**, determine whether the shaft is safe. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the fundamental definition:

$$Z_p = \frac{J}{R}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Derive the polar moment of inertia $J$ for a solid circular cross-section. Consider an elemental ring of radius $r$ and thickness $dr$:
- Area: $dA = 2\pi r \, dr$
- Contribution: $dJ = r^2 \, dA = 2\pi r^3 \, dr$

Integrating from $0$ to $R$:

$$J = \int_0^{R} 2\pi r^3 \, dr = 2\pi \left[\frac{r^4}{4}\right]_0^{R} = \frac{\pi R^4}{2}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Substitute $R = D/2$:

$$J = \frac{\pi}{2}\left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^4 = \frac{\pi D^4}{32}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Apply $Z_p = J/R$:

$$Z_p = \frac{\pi D^4 / 32}{D/2} = \frac{\pi D^4}{32} \times \frac{2}{D} = \frac{\pi D^3}{16}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** State final result with units:

$$\boxed{Z_p = \frac{\pi D^3}{16} \;\; \text{(units: mm}^3\text{)}}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Calculate the applied torque using the power-torque-speed relationship:

$$T = \frac{9550 \times P}{N} = \frac{9550 \times 60}{200} = 2865 \text{ N·m}$$

Converting to N·mm: $T = 2865 \times 10^3 = 2.865 \times 10^6$ N·mm.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute the Polar Modulus of the shaft:

$$Z_p = \frac{\pi D^3}{16} = \frac{\pi (80)^3}{16} = \frac{\pi \times 512000}{16} = 32000\pi \approx 100530.96 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Calculate the maximum shear stress induced:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{T}{Z_p} = \frac{2.865 \times 10^6}{100530.96} \approx 28.50 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compare with allowable stress:

$$\tau_{max} = 28.50 \text{ MPa} < \tau_{allow} = 45 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Conclusion:

$$\boxed{\text{Since } \tau_{max} < \tau_{allow}, \text{ the shaft is SAFE.}}$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Derive the expression for the Polar Modulus of a **hollow circular shaft** with outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A hollow shaft with outer diameter **100 mm** and inner diameter **60 mm** transmits a torque of **10 kN·m**. Compare its torque capacity with that of a **solid shaft** of the same outer diameter and same material. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the definition:

$$Z_p = \frac{J}{R}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Derive the polar moment of inertia for an annular section. Integrate from inner radius $r = d/2$ to outer radius $R = D/2$:

$$J = \int_{d/2}^{D/2} 2\pi \rho^3 \, d\rho = 2\pi \left[\frac{\rho^4}{4}\right]_{d/2}^{D/2} = \frac{\pi}{2}\left[\left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^4 - \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^4\right]$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Simplify:

$$J = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Apply $Z_p = J/R$ with $R = D/2$:

$$Z_p = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)/32}{D/2} = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{16 D}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Alternative form with $k = d/D$:

$$Z_p = \frac{\pi D^3}{16}(1 - k^4)$$

Final answer:

$$\boxed{Z_p = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{16 D}}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute the Polar Modulus of the **hollow shaft**:

$$Z_{p,\text{hollow}} = \frac{\pi (100^4 - 60^4)}{16 \times 100} = \frac{\pi (10^8 - 1.296 \times 10^7)}{1600} = \frac{\pi (8.704 \times 10^7)}{1600}$$

$$Z_{p,\text{hollow}} = \frac{\pi \times 8.704 \times 10^7}{1600} \approx 170,890.66 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute the Polar Modulus of the **solid shaft** (same $D = 100$ mm):

$$Z_{p,\text{solid}} = \frac{\pi D^3}{16} = \frac{\pi (100)^3}{16} = \frac{\pi \times 10^6}{16} = 196349.54 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Calculate the torque capacity of the hollow shaft (using $\tau_{allow}$):

For a specific allowable shear stress, $T = \tau \cdot Z_p$. The ratio of torque capacities equals the ratio of polar moduli:

$$\frac{T_{\text{hollow}}}{T_{\text{solid}}} = \frac{Z_{p,\text{hollow}}}{Z_{p,\text{solid}}} = \frac{170890.66}{196349.54} \approx 0.8704$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Verify by computing actual torques for an assumed $\tau_{allow} = 45$ MPa:

- Hollow: $T_{\text{hollow}} = 45 \times 170890.66 \times 10^{-3} = 7690.08$ N·m
- Solid: $T_{\text{solid}} = 45 \times 196349.54 \times 10^{-3} = 8835.73$ N·m

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Check the given applied torque of 10 kN·m against capacities:

$$\tau_{\text{hollow, actual}} = \frac{10 \times 10^6}{170890.66} \approx 58.51 \text{ MPa} \;\; (\text{Unsafe for } \tau_{allow} = 45 \text{ MPa})$$

$$\tau_{\text{solid, actual}} = \frac{10 \times 10^6}{196349.54} \approx 50.93 \text{ MPa} \;\; (\text{Unsafe for } \tau_{allow} = 45 \text{ MPa})$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Conclusion:

Both shafts are unsafe for the given torque at $\tau_{allow} = 45$ MPa. The **hollow shaft transmits approximately 87%** of the torque capacity of the solid shaft of the same outer diameter — confirming that the hollow shaft is **less strong but lighter**. The percentage reduction in torque capacity:

$$\text{Reduction} = (1 - 0.8704) \times 100\% = 12.96\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{Hollow shaft transmits } 87.04\% \text{ of solid shaft torque capacity for the same outer diameter.}}$$

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in KTU Valuations:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (loses 1–2 marks instantly):** Mixing kN·m with MPa·mm³ — KTU examiners deduct heavily for unit mismatch. Always convert torque to **N·mm** when $Z_p$ is in **mm³** and $\tau$ is in **N/mm² (MPa)**.
> 2. **Confusing Polar Modulus with Polar Moment of Inertia:** Students often write $Z_p = J$ without dividing by $R$. Both are denoted with similar symbols; $Z_p$ has units of **mm³** while $J$ has units of **mm⁴** — this is a fatal dimensional error.
> 3. **Forgetting the inner radius in hollow shafts:** Using the solid shaft formula $Z_p = \pi D^3/16$ instead of $\pi D^3 (1 - k^4)/16$ for hollow sections. The $(1 - k^4)$ factor is **mandatory**.
> 4. **Not stating the basic definition:** When asked to "define" Polar Modulus, students often jump to formulas. Always begin with the **ratio definition** $Z_p = J/R$ first.
> 5. **Skipping the integration step in derivations:** KTU expects the derivation of $J$ via integration $\int r^2 \, dA$ for full marks on derivation questions — do not just state $J = \pi D^4/32$ as a starting point.
> 6. **Incorrect radius selection:** For a hollow shaft, $R = D/2$ (outer radius), **never** $r = d/2$ — this single mistake cascades through the entire solution.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Polar Modulus $Z_p = J/R$** — the torsional counterpart of Section Modulus $Z = I/y$ in bending.
- **Solid shaft:** $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi D^3}{16}$
- **Hollow shaft:** $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{16 D} = \dfrac{\pi D^3}{16}(1 - k^4)$, where $k = d/D$
- **Design equation:** $T_{max} = \tau_{max} \cdot Z_p$ (analogous to $M = \sigma \cdot Z$)
- **Units:** $Z_p$ in $mm^3$, $T$ in N·mm, $\tau$ in MPa — never mix systems.
- **Cubic dependence:** Doubling $D$ increases torque capacity **8 times** ($2^3$).
- **Hollow shafts are weight-efficient** but transmit less torque than solid shafts of the same outer diameter; their $Z_p$ is reduced by the factor $(1 - k^4)$.
- **Derivation of $J$** is required for full marks: integrate $r^2 \, dA$ using an elemental annular ring $dA = 2\pi r \, dr$.
- **Power–torque relation** (commonly tested): $T = \dfrac{9550 \cdot P \text{ (kW)}}{N \text{ (rpm)}}$ in N·m, or $T = \dfrac{60 P}{2\pi N}$ in SI base units.
- **Assumptions:** Material is **homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic**; cross-section remains **plane** after twisting; **circular** cross-section only (Polar Modulus is not directly applicable to non-circular sections in the same form).
- **Order of problem-solving:** Identify geometry → Compute $Z_p$ → Compute $\tau_{max}$ → Compare with $\tau_{allow}$ → Conclude on safety.
- **KTU favourite trick:** Problems often provide the torque and diameter and ask for $\tau_{max}$, OR provide $\tau_{allow}$ and torque and ask for **minimum diameter** — practice both directions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torsional Rigidity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT402 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Torsional Rigidity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔧 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 📘 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **Torsional Rigidity** is defined as the product of the **Modulus of Rigidity (G)** and the **Polar Moment of Inertia (J)** of the cross-section of a shaft. It is denoted by the symbol **GJ** and represents the inherent capacity of a shaft to resist angular (twisting) deformation when subjected to a torque.

Mathematically, the Torsional Rigidity is expressed as:

$$
T_R = G \cdot J
$$

Where:
- **G** = Modulus of Rigidity (also called Shear Modulus), measured in **N/m²** or **Pa**.
- **J** = Polar Moment of Inertia of the cross-section about its longitudinal axis, measured in **m⁴** or **mm⁴**.
- The unit of Torsional Rigidity is **N·m²** (Newton-meter squared).

In the broader context of the Torsion Equation, the relationship is:

$$
\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \cdot \theta}{L}
$$

Where **GJ** acts as the proportionality constant linking the applied torque **T** to the resulting angle of twist **θ**.

---

## 🌟 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

Imagine you are holding a **steel rod** at one end and twisting the other end with your hand. A thin rod will twist a lot (low rigidity), while a thick rod of the same material will barely twist (high rigidity). Now, if you take two rods of the **same diameter** but one is made of **steel** and the other of **rubber**, the steel rod will resist twisting far more than the rubber rod.

This intuitive observation reveals the **two fundamental ingredients** of torsional rigidity:
1. **Geometric Property (J)** — How the material is *distributed* around the axis (shape and size).
2. **Material Property (G)** — How the material itself *resists* shear deformation (atomic bonding stiffness).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Torsional Rigidity is essentially the "stiffness" of a shaft in the rotational sense. Higher GJ means the shaft twists less for a given torque — a critically desirable property in drive shafts, axles, and turbine rotors.

---

## 🧭 Physical Significance

- **GJ** is the slope of the torque vs. angle of twist curve in the linear elastic region.
- It determines how much torque is required to produce a **unit angle of twist** over a **unit length** of shaft.
- In power transmission systems, a shaft with high torsional rigidity ensures **minimal angular deformation** and **precise synchronization** between driver and driven components.

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this:** A shaft's resistance to bending is governed by **EI** (Flexural Rigidity), while its resistance to twisting is governed by **GJ** (Torsional Rigidity). These two are analogous in rotational vs. translational mechanics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🔬 Theoretical Foundation: The Torsion Equation

The torsion equation is the cornerstone of all torsional analysis. It is derived from the assumption that a circular shaft, when twisted, remains plane and its cross-sections rotate as rigid discs relative to each other.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Torsion:

1. **Shear Stress Assumption:** When a torque **T** is applied at the free end of a shaft of radius **r**, a shear stress **τ** develops at any point on the cross-section at radial distance **ρ** from the center.
2. **Linear Distribution:** The shear stress varies **linearly** from zero at the center to a maximum at the outer surface: **τ ∝ ρ**.
3. **Equilibrium:** The total torque **T** must equal the integral of shear stress × area element × radius over the entire cross-section.
4. **Polar Moment of Inertia (J):** This integral simplifies to **J**, the polar second moment of area.
5. **Hooke's Law in Shear:** The shear strain γ equals τ/G, and geometrically, γ = ρθ/L.
6. **Final Equation:** Combining the above yields the torsion equation.

### Derivation Summary:

$$
\tau = \frac{T \cdot \rho}{J} \quad \text{...(a)} \qquad \theta = \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J} \quad \text{...(b)}
$$

---

## 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all essential formulas** for Torsional Rigidity problems expected in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations.

| **#** | **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Remarks** |
|:-----:|:--------------|:------------|:---------:|:------------|
| 1 | Torsional Rigidity | $T_R = G \cdot J$ | N·m² | The primary quantity of interest |
| 2 | Torsion Equation | $T / J = \tau / r = G\theta / L$ | — | Master equation for all torsion problems |
| 3 | Max Shear Stress | $\tau_{max} = 16T / (\pi d^3)$ | Pa (N/m²) | For **solid** circular shaft |
| 4 | Polar Moment (Solid) | $J = \pi d^4 / 32$ | m⁴ or mm⁴ | Solid circular cross-section |
| 5 | Polar Moment (Hollow) | $J = \pi (D^4 - d^4) / 32$ | m⁴ or mm⁴ | Hollow circular cross-section |
| 6 | Angle of Twist | $\theta = TL / (GJ)$ | radians | Total twist over length L |
| 7 | Twist per unit length | $\theta / L = T / (GJ)$ | rad/m | Angular deformation rate |
| 8 | Max Shear Stress (Hollow) | $\tau_{max} = 16TD / [\pi (D^4 - d^4)]$ | Pa | Evaluated at outer radius D/2 |
| 9 | Power Transmitted | $P = 2\pi N T / 60$ | Watts | N in rpm, T in N·m |
| 10 | Strain Energy in Torsion | $U = T^2 L / (2GJ)$ | Joules | Elastic strain energy stored |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Hack:** Always remember that $\tau_{max}$ occurs at the **outer surface** of the shaft. In a hollow shaft, the inner surface is **NOT** where max stress occurs (assuming solid material, no stress on the inner bore).

---

## 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Application** | **Role of Torsional Rigidity** |
|:----------------|:-------------------------------|
| **Automotive Drive Shafts** | Ensures smooth power transfer from engine to wheels without excessive twist |
| **Turbo-generator Rotors** | Maintains precise alignment of blades under high-speed rotation |
| **Drill Pipes (Oil Rigs)** | Withstands combined torsion, bending, and axial loads during drilling |
| **Helicopter Rotor Hubs** | Resists cyclic torsional fatigue from main rotor reactions |
| **Marine Propeller Shafts** | Transmits engine power to propellers across long distances with minimal twist |
| **Lathe Spindles & CNC Machines** | Provides rigidity for accurate machining under varying cutting torques |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** In design, engineers often **prefer hollow circular shafts** over solid ones because they provide a higher J-to-weight ratio, giving superior torsional rigidity with less material.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🔍 Derivation 1: Torsion Equation (Full Derivation)

Consider a **solid circular shaft** of diameter **d**, length **L**, fixed at one end and subjected to a torque **T** at the free end.

### Step 1: Geometric Strain Relationship

When the shaft twists, a point on the outer surface (at radius **r = d/2**) moves through an arc length on the surface. The shear strain **γ** at radius **r** is:

$$
\gamma = \frac{\text{arc length}}{\text{longitudinal length}} = \frac{r \cdot \theta}{L}
$$

**Logic:** As the shaft rotates by angle θ, the point at radius r sweeps an arc of length rθ. The shear strain is the tangent of this angle (small angle approximation), giving the above.

### Step 2: Hooke's Law in Shear

By Hooke's Law, shear stress is related to shear strain through the Modulus of Rigidity:

$$
\tau = G \cdot \gamma = G \cdot \frac{r \cdot \theta}{L}
$$

**Logic:** Within the elastic limit, shear stress is directly proportional to shear strain. The constant of proportionality is G.

### Step 3: Equilibrium of Torque

The total torque **T** is obtained by integrating the moment of shear stress over the entire cross-sectional area:

$$
T = \int_A \tau \cdot r \, dA
$$

**Logic:** Each elemental area dA at radius r experiences shear stress τ, which contributes a moment τ·r·dA to the total torque.

### Step 4: Substitution

Substituting the expression for τ from Step 2:

$$
T = \int_A \left( G \cdot \frac{r \cdot \theta}{L} \right) \cdot r \, dA = \frac{G \cdot \theta}{L} \int_A r^2 \, dA
$$

### Step 5: Recognizing the Polar Moment of Inertia

The integral $\int_A r^2 \, dA$ is, by definition, the **Polar Moment of Inertia (J)**:

$$
J = \int_A r^2 \, dA
$$

### Step 6: Final Torsion Equation

Therefore:

$$
T = \frac{G \cdot J \cdot \theta}{L}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\boxed{\frac{T}{J} = \frac{G \cdot \theta}{L} = \frac{\tau}{r}}
$$

This is the **Torsion Equation**, and **GJ** is the **Torsional Rigidity**.

---

## 🔍 Derivation 2: Polar Moment of Inertia for Solid Circular Cross-Section

For a solid circular cross-section of diameter **d** (radius **R = d/2**):

### Step 1: Set Up the Integral

Consider an elemental ring of radius **r** and thickness **dr**. The area of this ring is:

$$
dA = 2\pi r \, dr
$$

**Logic:** Circumference of the ring × thickness = 2πr × dr.

### Step 2: Write J as the Integral

$$
J = \int_A r^2 \, dA = \int_0^R r^2 \cdot (2\pi r) \, dr = 2\pi \int_0^R r^3 \, dr
$$

### Step 3: Perform the Integration

$$
J = 2\pi \left[ \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_0^R = 2\pi \cdot \frac{R^4}{4} = \frac{\pi R^4}{2}
$$

### Step 4: Substitute R = d/2

$$
J = \frac{\pi}{2} \left( \frac{d}{2} \right)^4 = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{J_{solid} = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}}
$$

---

## 🔍 Derivation 3: Polar Moment of Inertia for Hollow Circular Cross-Section

For a hollow shaft with outer diameter **D** and inner diameter **d**:

### Step 1: Apply the Subtraction Principle

$$
J_{hollow} = J_{outer} - J_{inner}
$$

### Step 2: Write Both Polar Moments

$$
J_{hollow} = \frac{\pi D^4}{32} - \frac{\pi d^4}{32}
$$

### Step 3: Simplify

$$
\boxed{J_{hollow} = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}}
$$

---

## 🔍 Derivation 4: Maximum Shear Stress in a Solid Circular Shaft

From the torsion equation:

$$
\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau_{max}}{R}
$$

Solving for τ_max:

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot R}{J} = \frac{T \cdot (d/2)}{\pi d^4 / 32} = \frac{16T}{\pi d^3}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\tau_{max} = \frac{16T}{\pi d^3}}
$$

**Logic:** This is the most frequently used formula in KTU problems. The stress is inversely proportional to the **cube** of the diameter, which is why even small increases in shaft diameter dramatically reduce stress.

---

## 🔍 Derivation 5: Angle of Twist (θ) in Terms of Torsional Rigidity

From the torsion equation rearranged:

$$
\theta = \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J} = \frac{T \cdot L}{T_R}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\theta = \frac{T \cdot L}{GJ}}
$$

**Logic:** This shows that for a fixed torque, increasing either G or J reduces the angle of twist. Doubling the diameter reduces θ by a factor of **16** (since J ∝ d⁴).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 📊 Diagram 1: Torsion of a Circular Shaft — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied Torque T at Free End] --> B[Torque Transmitted Along Shaft Length L]
    B --> C[Shear Stress Distribution on Cross-Section]
    C --> D[Linear Variation: Zero at Center to Max at Outer Surface]
    D --> E[Internal Resistance: Modulus of Rigidity G]
    E --> F[Geometric Resistance: Polar Moment of Inertia J]
    F --> G[Torsional Rigidity GJ = G multiplied by J]
    G --> H[Resulting Angle of Twist theta = TL / GJ]
    H --> I[Twist is Inversely Proportional to GJ]
    I --> J[Higher GJ means Stiffer Shaft and Smaller Twist]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The flowchart traces how an applied external torque transforms into internal stresses and deformations, with **GJ** serving as the key "resistance multiplier" that governs the final angular twist.

---

## 📊 Diagram 2: Shear Stress Distribution on a Shaft Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cross-Section View of Shaft] --> B[Center r = 0]
    A --> C[Mid Radius r = R/2]
    A --> D[Outer Surface r = R]
    B --> E[Shear Stress = 0]
    C --> F[Shear Stress = tau at R/2]
    D --> G[Shear Stress = tau_max]
    E --> H[Linear Gradient]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[tau proportional to r from center to surface]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Concept:** Shear stress is **zero at the center** of the shaft and **maximum at the outer surface**. This is why material near the center contributes very little to torsional strength — a key reason why hollow shafts are efficient.

---

## 📊 Diagram 3: Solid vs. Hollow Shaft — Comparative Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cross-Section Type] --> B[Solid Shaft Diameter d]
    A --> C[Hollow Shaft Outer D Outer d Inner]
    B --> D[J_solid equals pi d to power 4 divided by 32]
    C --> E[J_hollow equals pi D to power 4 minus d to power 4 divided by 32]
    D --> F[Material at Center Contributes Little to J]
    E --> G[Material Near Mean Radius Contributes Most to J]
    F --> H[Hollow Shaft with Same Outer Diameter Has Higher J per kg]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Engineering Conclusion: Hollow Shafts are More Efficient for Torsion]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Design Implication:** For the same weight (or same outer diameter), a hollow shaft provides significantly higher torsional rigidity. This is why aircraft structures, drive shafts, and bicycle frames commonly use hollow tubes.

---

## 📊 Diagram 4: Factors Affecting Torsional Rigidity — Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Torsional Rigidity GJ] --> B[Material Property G]
    A --> C[Geometric Property J]
    B --> D[Steel: G approx 80 GPa]
    B --> E[Aluminum: G approx 27 GPa]
    B --> F[Brass: G approx 39 GPa]
    C --> G[Solid Circle: pi d to power 4 / 32]
    C --> H[Hollow Circle: pi D to power 4 minus d to power 4 / 32]
    C --> I[Higher Diameter means Much Higher J due to d to power 4]
    G --> J[For Same Material, Diameter Dominates Torsional Rigidity]
    H --> J
    I --> J
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Torsional Rigidity. State its units and the factors on which it depends.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Torsional Rigidity is the product of the **Modulus of Rigidity (G)** and the **Polar Moment of Inertia (J)** of the cross-section of a shaft. It is denoted by **GJ** and represents the resistance of a shaft to angular deformation under an applied torque.

**Units (0.5 Marks):**
The SI unit of Torsional Rigidity is **N·m²** (Newton-meter squared).

**Factors (0.5 Marks):**
It depends on:
1. **Modulus of Rigidity (G)** — a material property.
2. **Polar Moment of Inertia (J)** — a geometric property depending on shaft cross-section shape and size.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Torsional Rigidity $T_R = G \cdot J$** is analogous to **Flexural Rigidity EI** in bending. Both govern the deformation of structural members under their respective load types.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**A solid circular shaft of diameter 50 mm transmits a torque of 2 kN·m. Find the maximum shear stress developed in the shaft.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Given:**
- Diameter, $d = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- Torque, $T = 2 \text{ kN·m} = 2000 \text{ N·m}$

**Formula (1 Mark):**

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{16T}{\pi d^3}
$$

**Calculation (2 Marks):**

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{16 \times 2000}{\pi \times (0.05)^3}
$$

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{32000}{\pi \times 0.000125} = \frac{32000}{0.0003927}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_{max} \approx 81.49 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 81.49 \text{ MPa}}
$$

---

## 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** A solid steel shaft of diameter **60 mm** and length **2 m** is subjected to a torque of **5 kN·m**. Take **G = 80 GPa**. Find:
   1. The maximum shear stress in the shaft.
   2. The angle of twist in degrees.
   3. The torsional rigidity of the shaft.

**(b)** Compare the torsional rigidities of a solid shaft and a hollow shaft of the same material, weight, and length, when the hollow shaft has an inner-to-outer diameter ratio of **0.5**.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- $d = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.06 \text{ m}$
- $L = 2 \text{ m}$
- $T = 5 \text{ kN·m} = 5000 \text{ N·m}$
- $G = 80 \text{ GPa} = 80 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$

**Step 1: Polar Moment of Inertia (1 Mark):**

$$
J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times (0.06)^4}{32}
$$

$$
J = \frac{\pi \times 1.296 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = \frac{4.0715 \times 10^{-5}}{32}
$$

$$
\boxed{J = 1.2723 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4}
$$

**Step 2: Maximum Shear Stress (2 Marks):**

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{16T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 5000}{\pi \times (0.06)^3}
$$

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{80000}{\pi \times 2.16 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{80000}{6.786 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_{max} \approx 117.89 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \approx 117.89 \text{ MPa}}
$$

**Step 3: Angle of Twist in Radians (2 Marks):**

$$
\theta = \frac{TL}{GJ} = \frac{5000 \times 2}{80 \times 10^9 \times 1.2723 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\theta = \frac{10000}{101784} \approx 0.09825 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to Degrees (1 Mark):**

$$
\theta = 0.09825 \times \frac{180}{\pi} \approx 5.63^\circ
$$

**Step 5: Torsional Rigidity (1 Mark):**

$$
GJ = 80 \times 10^9 \times 1.2723 \times 10^{-6} = 101784 \text{ N·m}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{GJ \approx 101.78 \text{ kN·m}^2}
$$

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison: Solid vs. Hollow Shaft of Same Weight**

**Given:** Same material (same G), same weight (same cross-sectional area), same length.
- For hollow shaft: $d/D = 0.5$, i.e., $d = 0.5D$.

**Step 1: Relate Diameters from Equal Area Condition (2 Marks):**

Equal area (weight per unit length):

$$
\frac{\pi d_s^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}
$$

$$
d_s^2 = D^2 - d^2 = D^2 - (0.5D)^2 = D^2 - 0.25D^2 = 0.75D^2
$$

$$
d_s = D\sqrt{0.75} = 0.866D
$$

**Step 2: Polar Moment of Solid Shaft (1 Mark):**

$$
J_s = \frac{\pi d_s^4}{32} = \frac{\pi (0.866D)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 0.5625D^4}{32}
$$

$$
J_s = \frac{0.5625\pi D^4}{32}
$$

**Step 3: Polar Moment of Hollow Shaft (2 Marks):**

$$
J_h = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32} = \frac{\pi (D^4 - 0.0625D^4)}{32} = \frac{0.9375\pi D^4}{32}
$$

**Step 4: Ratio of Torsional Rigidities (2 Marks):**

Since the material (G) is the same, the ratio of torsional rigidities equals the ratio of J:

$$
\frac{(GJ)_h}{(GJ)_s} = \frac{J_h}{J_s} = \frac{0.9375}{0.5625} = \frac{5}{3} \approx 1.667
$$

**Conclusion:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Hollow shaft has } 66.7\% \text{ more torsional rigidity than a solid shaft of the same weight.}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Derive the torsion equation $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ for a solid circular shaft. Clearly state the assumptions involved.

**(b)** A hollow circular shaft has an outer diameter of **80 mm** and inner diameter of **50 mm**. If the shaft is **3 m** long and is subjected to a torque of **6 kN·m**, determine:
   1. The maximum shear stress.
   2. The angle of twist in degrees (Take G = 80 GPa).
   3. The strain energy stored in the shaft.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Assumptions in Torsion Theory (2 Marks):**
1. The shaft material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic**.
2. Cross-sections **plane and perpendicular to the axis** before loading remain plane and undistorted after twisting (no warping).
3. **Radial lines remain radial** and **cross-sections rotate as rigid discs** relative to each other.
4. The twist is **uniform** along the length.
5. **Saint-Venant's principle** holds: the disturbance near the point of application of torque is neglected.

**Derivation (5 Marks):**

Let a solid shaft of radius **R** and length **L** be fixed at one end and twisted by torque **T** at the free end through angle **θ**.

**Geometric Relation (1 Mark):** The shear strain at radius r is:

$$
\gamma = \frac{r\theta}{L}
$$

**Hooke's Law in Shear (1 Mark):**

$$
\tau = G\gamma = \frac{G r \theta}{L}
$$

**Equilibrium of Torque (1.5 Marks):**

$$
T = \int_A \tau \cdot r \, dA = \int_A \frac{G r^2 \theta}{L} \, dA = \frac{G\theta}{L} \int_A r^2 \, dA
$$

**Polar Moment of Inertia (0.5 Mark):**

$$
J = \int_A r^2 \, dA
$$

**Final Equation (1 Mark):**

$$
T = \frac{G J \theta}{L} \implies \frac{T}{J} = \frac{G\theta}{L} = \frac{\tau}{r}
$$

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $D = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$
- $d = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- $L = 3 \text{ m}$
- $T = 6 \text{ kN·m} = 6000 \text{ N·m}$
- $G = 80 \text{ GPa} = 80 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$

**Step 1: Polar Moment of Inertia (1.5 Marks):**

$$
J = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32} = \frac{\pi [(0.08)^4 - (0.05)^4]}{32}
$$

$$
J = \frac{\pi [4.096 \times 10^{-5} - 6.25 \times 10^{-6}]}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 3.471 \times 10^{-5}}{32}
$$

$$
\boxed{J = 3.407 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4}
$$

**Step 2: Maximum Shear Stress (1.5 Marks):**

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot (D/2)}{J} = \frac{6000 \times 0.04}{3.407 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{240}{3.407 \times 10^{-6}} = 70.44 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_{max} \approx 70.44 \text{ MPa}}
$$

**Step 3: Angle of Twist (2 Marks):**

$$
\theta = \frac{TL}{GJ} = \frac{6000 \times 3}{80 \times 10^9 \times 3.407 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\theta = \frac{18000}{272560} \approx 0.06604 \text{ rad}
$$

**Convert to Degrees (1 Mark):**

$$
\theta = 0.06604 \times \frac{180}{\pi} \approx 3.785^\circ
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta \approx 3.79^\circ}
$$

**Step 4: Strain Energy (1 Mark):**

$$
U = \frac{T^2 L}{2GJ} = \frac{(6000)^2 \times 3}{2 \times 80 \times 10^9 \times 3.407 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
U = \frac{1.08 \times 10^8}{545120} \approx 198.1 \text{ J}
$$

$$
\boxed{U \approx 198.1 \text{ Joules}}
$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (–1 to –2 marks):** Students often mix mm and m. If diameter is in mm, then either convert to m before using formulas, OR keep everything in mm (with τ in N/mm² = MPa, G in N/mm²).
> 2. **Forgetting to Convert Radians to Degrees (–1 mark):** When asked for the angle of twist "in degrees," always multiply the radian value by 180/π. Simply giving θ in radians is incomplete.
> 3. **Using the Wrong J Formula (–2 marks):** Confusing $J = \pi d^4/32$ (solid) with $J = \pi(D^4 - d^4)/32$ (hollow) is a critical error. Always identify the shaft type first.
> 4. **Neglecting G in Strain Energy Formula (–1 mark):** Forgetting to include the Modulus of Rigidity G in the denominator of $U = T^2L/(2GJ)$ is a common oversight.
> 5. **Not Stating Assumptions in Derivations (–1 to –2 marks):** For derivation questions, **always** write down the assumptions (homogeneous, isotropic, plane sections remain plane, etc.) before starting the derivation.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🎯 **Torsional Rigidity** = $GJ$ (Modulus of Rigidity × Polar Moment of Inertia). **Unit: N·m²**.
- 🎯 **Torsion Equation:** $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ — the master formula for all torsion problems.
- 🎯 **Polar Moment of Inertia:**
   - Solid shaft: $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$
   - Hollow shaft: $J = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$
- 🎯 **Maximum Shear Stress (Solid):** $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16T}{\pi d^3}$ — occurs at the outer surface.
- 🎯 **Angle of Twist:** $\theta = \dfrac{TL}{GJ}$ — inversely proportional to $GJ$.
- 🎯 **Strain Energy:** $U = \dfrac{T^2 L}{2GJ}$ — energy stored due to elastic twist.
- 🎯 **Power Transmitted:** $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ where $N$ is in **rpm** and $T$ in **N·m**.
- 🎯 **Hollow shafts are more efficient** for torsion (higher $J$ per unit weight) because material near the center contributes little to torsional strength.
- 🎯 **$J \propto d^4$** — doubling the diameter increases $J$ by **16 times** and reduces twist by 16 times.
- 🎯 **Assumptions:** Homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic material; plane sections remain plane; shaft is circular.
- 🎯 **Key Analogy:** $GJ$ (Torsional Rigidity) ↔ $EI$ (Flexural Rigidity).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shaft Design for Torsional Load. Beams - Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT402 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Shaft Design for Torsional Load. Beams - Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Shaft — Formal Definition

A **shaft** is a rotating machine element, usually of circular cross-section (solid or hollow), used to transmit power, torque, and rotational motion from one component to another (e.g., from an engine to a gearbox). When subjected to twisting moments about its longitudinal axis, the shaft experiences **torsional loading**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"A shaft is a rotating member, usually circular in cross-section, which transmits power and rotational motion. Shaft design under torsional load ensures the shaft operates below the maximum allowable shear stress and an acceptable angle of twist."*

**Two Primary Design Criteria for Shafts:**

| Criterion | Symbol | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|
| Strength Criterion (Shear Stress) | $\tau_{max}$ | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{T}{Z_p} \leq \tau_{allowable}$ |
| Stiffness Criterion (Angle of Twist) | $\theta$ | $\dfrac{T}{G J} = \dfrac{\theta}{L} \leq \theta_{allowable}$ per unit length |

## 1.2 Beam — Formal Definition

A **beam** is a structural member subjected predominantly to **transverse loads** (loads perpendicular to its longitudinal axis), producing internal bending moments and shear forces. Beams transfer loads from their application points to the supports.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"A beam is a flexural member, primarily designed to resist bending moments and shear forces arising from transverse loading. Classification of beams is based on support conditions, geometry, cross-section, and loading patterns."*

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogies

### Shaft Analogy — The Bicycle Wheel Spokes 🛞
Imagine a **bicycle wheel spoke** twisting when you turn the wrench to tighten the spoke nipple. The spoke resists the twist. Similarly, a shaft resists the rotational twist transmitted from a motor. The internal shear stress is analogous to the "stretching" of one side of the spoke while the opposite side is "compressed" (in terms of angular deformation).

### Beam Analogy — The Diving Board 🏊
A **diving board** cantilevered at one end with a person standing at the other end bends downward. The fibers on top stretch (tension), and the fibers below compress. This bending behavior is exactly how a beam behaves — the cross-section rotates about the **neutral axis** while top and bottom fibers experience opposite stresses.

## 1.4 Key Constants and Standard Metrics

| Physical Constant | Value | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Modulus of Rigidity (Steel)** | $G = 80 \text{ GPa}$ | Stiffness of shaft |
| **Modulus of Rigidity (Aluminum)** | $G = 27 \text{ GPa}$ | Light shaft applications |
| **Allowable Shear Stress (Mild Steel Shaft)** | $\tau_{allow} = 40 \text{ to } 55 \text{ MPa}$ | Power transmission shafts |
| **Allowable Twist per meter** | $\theta = 0.25°/\text{m}$ to $1°/\text{m}$ | Machine design standard |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theory of Torsion — Analytical Framework

When a circular shaft is subjected to a torque $T$ at its free end, the following assumptions hold (St. Venant's Torsion Theory for Circular Shafts):

1. **Cross-sections remain plane and circular** after twisting (no warping).
2. **Radial lines remain straight and radial** — cross-section rotates as a rigid disk.
3. **The angle of twist is proportional to the applied torque** (linear elastic behavior).
4. **Shear stress varies linearly** from zero at the center to maximum at the outer surface.

### The Universal Torsion Equation

The relationship connecting torque, shear stress, geometry, and deformation is:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{T}{J} \;=\; \dfrac{\tau}{r} \;=\; \dfrac{G \cdot \theta}{L}}$$

where every term has a clear physical meaning:

- $T$ → Applied **Torque** (N·m) — the external twisting moment.
- $J$ → **Polar Moment of Inertia** of the cross-section about its centroidal axis (m⁴) — geometric resistance.
- $\tau$ → **Shear stress** at radius $r$ from the center (Pa or N/m²).
- $r$ → Radial distance from the centerline (m).
- $G$ → **Modulus of Rigidity** of the material (Pa) — material stiffness.
- $\theta$ → **Angle of twist** in radians over the length $L$ (m).

## 2.2 Polar Moment of Inertia (J) and Polar Section Modulus ($Z_p$)

For a **solid circular shaft** of diameter $d$:

$$J_{solid} = \dfrac{\pi \, d^{4}}{32}$$

For a **hollow circular shaft** with outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d$:

$$J_{hollow} = \dfrac{\pi \, (D^{4} - d^{4})}{32}$$

The **Polar Section Modulus** $Z_p$ converts the torsion equation into a strength-check format:

$$Z_p = \dfrac{J}{r_{max}} \; \Rightarrow \; \boxed{\tau_{max} = \dfrac{T}{Z_p}}$$

- Solid shaft: $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi \, d^{3}}{16}$
- Hollow shaft: $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi \, (D^{4} - d^{4})}{16 \, D}$

## 2.3 Power Transmission Through Shafts

The torque transmitted by a shaft rotating at $N$ revolutions per minute while delivering power $P$ is:

$$\boxed{T = \dfrac{60 \, P}{2 \pi \, N} = \dfrac{9.55 \, P}{N}}$$

where $P$ is in **watts (W)** and $N$ is in **rpm**. The resulting torque $T$ is in N·m.

## 2.4 Beams — Multi-Dimensional Classification

### Classification 1: Based on Support Conditions

| Beam Type | Support Description | Reaction Components | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Simply Supported Beam** | Pinned at one end, roller at other | $R_A$ (vertical) and $R_B$ (vertical) only | Bridges, roof trusses |
| **Overhanging Beam** | Simply supported with one or both ends extending beyond supports | $R_A$, $R_B$, and $M$ possible | Balconies, ship hulls |
| **Cantilever Beam** | Fixed (built-in) at one end, free at other | $R$ and $M$ at fixed end | Diving boards, balconies, aircraft wings |
| **Fixed Beam (Propped Cantilever)** | Fixed at one end, simply supported at other | 3 reactions: $R_A$, $R_B$, $M_A$ | Continuous structures |
| **Fixed-Fixed (Built-in) Beam** | Fixed at both ends | 6 reactions (3 at each end) | Rigid frames, retaining walls |
| **Continuous Beam** | More than two supports, extends continuously | Multiple vertical reactions | Multi-span bridges, building floors |

### Classification 2: Based on Geometry

| Beam Type | Geometry Description |
|---|---|
| **Straight Beam** | Axis is a straight line (most common) |
| **Curved Beam** | Axis is curved (hooks, arches) |
| **Tapered Beam** | Cross-section varies along length |

### Classification 3: Based on Cross-Section

| Beam Type | Common Use |
|---|---|
| **Rectangular Beam** | Timber beams, concrete slabs |
| **I-Section Beam** | Steel girders, bridges (high $I$ with low material) |
| **T-Section Beam** | Reinforced concrete in T-beams |
| **Circular Beam** | Round bars, shafts used as beams |

### Classification 4: Based on Loading

| Beam Type | Loading Nature |
|---|---|
| **Concentrated (Point) Load Beam** | Single force at a point |
| **Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL)** | Load spread uniformly, $w$ (N/m) |
| **Uniformly Varying Load (UVL)** | Load varying linearly (triangular load) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Shaft Design:** Critical in automotive drivelines, turbine rotor shafts, propeller shafts, and machine tool spindles. Optimizing diameter balances **strength** (avoiding yield) and **stiffness** (avoiding excessive twist that misaligns gears).
- **Beam Classification:** Allows engineers to select appropriate analysis methods (e.g., cantilever analysis differs from simply supported analysis) and design supports with correct **stiffness** and **load-carrying** capacity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Both Solid AND Hollow Shafts?**
> Hollow shafts deliver a higher $J/A$ ratio per unit weight. For the **same torque capacity**, a hollow shaft uses 30–50% less material, making it the preferred choice in aerospace, automotive, and marine applications.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Torsion Equation $\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}$

**Geometric Setup:** Consider a solid circular shaft of radius $R$, length $L$, fixed at one end and subjected to torque $T$ at the free end.

**Step 1 — Define the Initial Geometry**
Before twisting, an element on the surface is identified by the radial line $OA$ of length $R$. A second line $OB$ (on the surface, at distance $L$ from $OA$) is parallel to $OA$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Torque**
After applying torque $T$, the free end rotates by angle $\theta$. The line $OB$ tilts by angle $\phi$ (twist in radians), and line $OA$ swings to $OA'$.

**Step 3 — Compute the Shear Strain ($\gamma$)**
The shear strain at the surface equals the arc length divided by the radius:

$$\gamma = \dfrac{\text{arc } BB'}{L} = \dfrac{R \, \theta}{L}$$

This is valid **only at the outer surface** (radius $R$). At a general radius $r$:

$$\gamma_r = \dfrac{r \, \theta}{L}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Hooke's Law in Shear**
For a linear elastic material, shear stress is proportional to shear strain:

$$\tau_r = G \cdot \gamma_r = G \cdot \dfrac{r \, \theta}{L}$$

**Step 5 — Convert Shear Stress at Radius $r$ to Shear Stress at Surface**
Dividing $\tau_r$ by $r$, and substituting the surface shear stress $\tau = G \cdot R \cdot \theta / L$ divided by $R$:

$$\dfrac{\tau_r}{r} = \dfrac{G \, \theta}{L}$$

This ratio is **constant** for any $r$ from 0 to $R$:

$$\dfrac{\tau_r}{r} = \dfrac{G \, \theta}{L}$$

**Step 6 — Equate to Torque via Static Equilibrium**
The total torque equals the integral of shear stress × area × radius over the cross-section:

$$T = \int_{A} \tau_r \cdot r \, dA = \int_{A} \left( \dfrac{G \, \theta}{L} \cdot r \right) r \, dA = \dfrac{G \, \theta}{L} \int_{A} r^{2} \, dA$$

By definition, $\int_A r^2 dA = J$ (Polar Moment of Inertia):

$$T = \dfrac{G \, \theta \, J}{L}$$

**Step 7 — Rearrange to the Universal Torsion Equation**
From Step 5: $\dfrac{G\theta}{L} = \dfrac{\tau_r}{r}$ and from Step 6: $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$. Combining:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{T}{J} \;=\; \dfrac{\tau}{r} \;=\; \dfrac{G \, \theta}{L}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this elegant?** All three ratios are constant throughout the shaft cross-section (for a given radius $r$). This means we can solve for **any one unknown** given the other two.

## 3.2 Derivation of Polar Moment of Inertia for Solid Circular Cross-Section

Consider a circular cross-section of radius $R$. Take a thin ring element at radius $r$ with thickness $dr$.

**Step 1 — Area of the Thin Ring**
The differential area of an annular ring at radius $r$ is:

$$dA = 2 \pi r \, dr$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Definition of $J$**
By definition, $J = \int_A r^2 \, dA$:

$$J = \int_{0}^{R} r^{2} \, (2 \pi r) \, dr = 2 \pi \int_{0}^{R} r^{3} \, dr$$

**Step 3 — Perform the Integration**

$$J = 2 \pi \left[ \dfrac{r^{4}}{4} \right]_{0}^{R} = 2 \pi \cdot \dfrac{R^{4}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \, R^{4}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Convert Radius to Diameter ($R = d/2$)**

$$J_{solid} = \dfrac{\pi \, (d/2)^{4}}{2} = \dfrac{\pi \, d^{4}}{32}$$

$$\boxed{J_{solid} = \dfrac{\pi \, d^{4}}{32}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Polar Section Modulus $Z_p$

**Step 1 — Definition**

$$Z_p = \dfrac{J}{R}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $J$ for Solid Shaft**

$$Z_{p,solid} = \dfrac{\pi \, d^{4}/32}{d/2} = \dfrac{\pi \, d^{4}}{32} \cdot \dfrac{2}{d} = \dfrac{\pi \, d^{3}}{16}$$

$$\boxed{Z_{p,solid} = \dfrac{\pi \, d^{3}}{16}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation of the Power-Torque Equation

**Step 1 — Define Work Done by Torque**
Work done per revolution = Force × Distance traveled in one revolution = $F \times 2\pi R$.

For torque $T = F \times R$, work per revolution = $T \times 2\pi$.

**Step 2 — Number of Revolutions per Second**
If the shaft rotates at $N$ rpm, then revolutions per second = $N/60$.

**Step 3 — Power = Work per Second**

$$P = (T \times 2\pi) \times \dfrac{N}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Torque**

$$T = \dfrac{60 \, P}{2 \pi \, N} = \dfrac{9.548 \, P}{N}$$

$$\boxed{T \, (\text{N·m}) = \dfrac{9.55 \, P \, (\text{W})}{N \, (\text{rpm})}}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Tool for Shaft Design

| Given | Find | Direct Formula |
|---|---|---|
| $T$, $d$ (solid) | $\tau_{max}$ | $\tau = \dfrac{16 \, T}{\pi \, d^{3}}$ |
| $\tau_{allow}$, $T$ | $d$ (solid) | $d = \left( \dfrac{16 \, T}{\pi \, \tau_{allow}} \right)^{1/3}$ |
| $T$, $L$, $G$, $d$ | $\theta$ | $\theta = \dfrac{32 \, T \, L}{\pi \, G \, d^{4}}$ (radians) |
| $P$ (W), $N$ (rpm) | $T$ | $T = \dfrac{9.55 \, P}{N}$ |
| $T$, $D$, $d$ (hollow) | $\tau_{max}$ | $\tau = \dfrac{16 \, T \, D}{\pi \, (D^{4} - d^{4})}$ |

## 3.6 SFD and BMD for Standard Beams (Foundation for Module 2)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Beams are formally analyzed using **Shear Force Diagrams (SFD)** and **Bending Moment Diagrams (BMD)**. The classification determines the **shape and values** of these diagrams.

| Beam Type | Loading | Maximum Bending Moment | Location |
|---|---|---|---|
| Simply Supported (length $L$, UDL $w$) | UDL | $wL^2/8$ | Mid-span |
| Simply Supported (length $L$, point load $P$ at center) | Point Load | $PL/4$ | Mid-span |
| Cantilever (length $L$, point load $P$ at free end) | Point Load | $PL$ | Fixed end |
| Cantilever (length $L$, UDL $w$) | UDL | $wL^2/2$ | Fixed end |

> [!NOTE]
> These values will be revisited in detail in **Module 3** (Bending Stress and Shear Stress in Beams). For Module 2, focus on **identifying** beam types and recognizing their **support and loading** conditions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Shaft Design Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Shaft Design Problem] --> B[Identify Power P and Speed N]
    B --> C[Compute Torque T = 9.55 P / N]
    C --> D{Choose Shaft Type}
    D -- Solid --> E[Solid: d to power 4]
    D -- Hollow --> F[Hollow: D and d ratio]
    E --> G[Apply Strength Criterion]
    F --> G
    G --> H[d = 16T divided by pi tau power 1/3]
    H --> I[Apply Stiffness Criterion]
    I --> J[theta per meter = TL / GJ less than allowable]
    J --> K[Compare d from Both Criteria]
    K --> L[Choose Larger d as Final Diameter]
    L --> M[Add Factor of Safety if Required]
    M --> N[FINAL: Recommended Shaft Diameter]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Beam Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[BEAMS] --> B[Based on Support]
    A --> C[Based on Geometry]
    A --> D[Based on Cross Section]
    A --> E[Based on Loading]

    B --> B1[Simply Supported]
    B --> B2[Cantilever]
    B --> B3[Overhanging]
    B --> B4[Fixed Fixed]
    B --> B5[Continuous]
    B --> B6[Propped Cantilever]

    C --> C1[Straight]
    C --> C2[Curved]
    C --> C3[Tapered]

    D --> D1[Rectangular]
    D --> D2[I Section]
    D --> D3[T Section]
    D --> D4[Channel Section]
    D --> D5[Circular]

    E --> E1[Point Load Beam]
    E --> E2[UDL Beam]
    E --> E3[UVL Beam]
    E --> E4[Couple / Moment Beam]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Torsion Element Internal Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Apply Torque T at Free End] --> B[Twist Angle theta over Length L]
    B --> C[Shear Strain gamma = r theta per L]
    C --> D[Shear Stress tau = G gamma at radius r]
    D --> E[Integrate over Cross Section]
    E --> F[T equals G theta J per L]
    F --> G[Solve for tau max at r equals R]
    G --> H[Tau max = T by Zp]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Schematic — Torsional Stress Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Solid Circular Shaft Cross Section] --> B[Center r equals 0]
    A --> C[Mid Radius r]
    A --> D[Outer Surface r equals R]
    B --> E[Tau equals 0]
    C --> F[Tau increases linearly with r]
    D --> G[Tau equals Tau max = 16 T by pi d cubed]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Q:** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define **polar section modulus** and write its expression for a solid circular shaft of diameter $d$.

**Model Answer:**
> The **polar section modulus** $Z_p$ is defined as the ratio of the polar moment of inertia $J$ to the radius $R$ of the shaft cross-section. It represents the geometric property that converts applied torque into maximum shear stress.
>
> $$Z_p = \dfrac{J}{R} = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}/32}{d/2} = \dfrac{\pi \, d^{3}}{16}$$
>
> It is used in the **strength design** equation: $\tau_{max} = T/Z_p$.

### Question 2
**Q:** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] List **four different classifications of beams** with one example each.

**Model Answer:**
>
> 1. **Simply Supported Beam** — Example: Bridge girder resting on two piers.
> 2. **Cantilever Beam** — Example: Balcony projecting from a building wall.
> 3. **Overhanging Beam** — Example: Chajja (sunshade) extending beyond a window.
> 4. **Fixed-Fixed Beam** — Example: Beam between two rigid columns in a portal frame.
>
> *(Other valid classifications accepted: Curved, Tapered, Continuous, I-section, T-section, UDL beam, UVL beam.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Shaft Design (Strength + Stiffness)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

A solid circular shaft transmits **75 kW at 200 rpm**. The maximum allowable shear stress is **55 MPa** and the allowable twist per meter length is **0.25°/m**. Take $G = 80$ GPa.

**(a)** Design the diameter of the shaft based on the **strength criterion**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Check the **stiffness criterion** and recommend the final diameter. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Strength-Based Design**

**Step 1 — Compute the Applied Torque**

$$T = \dfrac{9.55 \times P}{N} = \dfrac{9.55 \times 75{,}000}{200}$$

$$\boxed{T = 3581.25 \; \text{N·m} = 3.581 \times 10^{6} \; \text{N·mm}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Strength Equation**

$$\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16 \, T}{\pi \, d^{3}} \leq \tau_{allow}$$

Solving for $d$:

$$d^{3} \geq \dfrac{16 \, T}{\pi \, \tau_{allow}} = \dfrac{16 \times 3.581 \times 10^{6}}{\pi \times 55}$$

$$d^{3} \geq \dfrac{5.730 \times 10^{7}}{172.788} = 3.316 \times 10^{5} \; \text{mm}^{3}$$

$$\boxed{d \geq 69.27 \; \text{mm}}$$

**Part (b) — Stiffness-Based Design**

**Step 1 — Convert Allowable Twist to rad/m**

$$\theta_{allow} = 0.25° \times \dfrac{\pi}{180} = 4.363 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{rad/m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Stiffness Equation per Unit Length** (using $L = 1$ m = 1000 mm):

$$\dfrac{\theta}{L} = \dfrac{T}{G \, J} \leq \theta_{allow} \; \text{rad/m}$$

$$J_{solid} = \dfrac{\pi \, d^{4}}{32}$$

Solving:

$$d^{4} \geq \dfrac{32 \, T \times L}{\pi \times G \times \theta_{allow}}$$

Substituting $T = 3.581 \times 10^{6}$ N·mm, $L = 1000$ mm, $G = 80 \times 10^{3}$ N/mm²:

$$d^{4} \geq \dfrac{32 \times 3.581 \times 10^{6} \times 1000}{\pi \times 80 \times 10^{3} \times 4.363 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$d^{4} \geq \dfrac{1.146 \times 10^{11}}{1.097 \times 10^{3}} = 1.045 \times 10^{8} \; \text{mm}^{4}$$

$$\boxed{d \geq 56.93 \; \text{mm}}$$

**Step 3 — Compare and Recommend**

- Strength criterion: $d \geq 69.27$ mm
- Stiffness criterion: $d \geq 56.93$ mm

$$\boxed{\text{Final Shaft Diameter} = d_{standard} = 70 \; \text{mm}}$$

The strength criterion governs here.

---

### Question B — Beams Classification + Reactions

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Classify beams based on **support conditions**. Sketch and label each type with appropriate **support reactions**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A simply supported beam of span **6 m** carries a **uniformly distributed load (UDL) of 20 kN/m** over its entire length. Calculate the **support reactions**, draw the **Shear Force Diagram (SFD)** and **Bending Moment Diagram (BMD)**, and determine the **maximum bending moment**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Beam Classification by Support**

The 6 major types of beams based on support conditions are:

| # | Beam Type | Sketch (Description) | Reactions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Simply Supported** | Pinned + Roller, both at ends | $R_A$ (↑), $R_B$ (↑) |
| 2 | **Overhanging** | Simply supported with overhang(s) | $R_A$ (↑), $R_B$ (↑), $M$ possible |
| 3 | **Cantilever** | Fixed at one end, free at other | $R$ (↑), $M$ at fixed end |
| 4 | **Propped Cantilever** | Fixed at one end, roller/hinge at other | $R_A$ (↑), $R_B$ (↑), $M_A$ |
| 5 | **Fixed-Fixed** | Fixed at both ends | $R_A$, $M_A$, $R_B$, $M_B$ |
| 6 | **Continuous** | More than two supports | Multiple vertical reactions |

**Part (b) — UDL on Simply Supported Beam**

**Step 1 — Total Load**
Total UDL = $w \times L = 20 \times 6 = 120$ kN, acting at the center ($x = 3$ m).

**Step 2 — Support Reactions**
By symmetry:

$$R_A = R_B = \dfrac{120}{2} = 60 \; \text{kN} \; (\uparrow)$$

**Step 3 — Shear Force at Distance $x$ from A**
SF at section $x$ from left support:

$$V_x = R_A - w \cdot x = 60 - 20x$$

| $x$ (m) | $V_x$ (kN) |
|---|---|
| 0 | +60 |
| 3 | 0 |
| 6 | -60 |

**Step 4 — Bending Moment at Distance $x$ from A**

$$M_x = R_A \cdot x - \dfrac{w \cdot x^{2}}{2} = 60x - 10x^{2}$$

| $x$ (m) | $M_x$ (kN·m) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0 |
| 1 | 50 |
| 2 | 80 |
| 3 | **90** |
| 4 | 80 |
| 5 | 50 |
| 6 | 0 |

**Step 5 — Maximum Bending Moment**
At mid-span ($x = 3$ m), where SF = 0:

$$M_{max} = \dfrac{w L^{2}}{8} = \dfrac{20 \times 6^{2}}{8} = \boxed{90 \; \text{kN·m}}$$

**Step 6 — SFD Shape:** Linear from +60 kN at A to -60 kN at B, crossing zero at mid-span.

**Step 7 — BMD Shape:** Parabolic curve, zero at both supports, maximum 90 kN·m at mid-span.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion in Power Equation:** Students frequently substitute $P$ in **kW without converting to W** ($75 \text{ kW} = 75{,}000 \text{ W}$). This alone can cost 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to Convert Degrees to Radians** in the stiffness criterion: $0.25° \neq 0.25$ rad. Always multiply by $\pi/180$.
> 3. **Choosing the Smaller Diameter:** The final shaft diameter must be the **larger** of the two computed values (strength AND stiffness must both be satisfied).
> 4. **Beam Reactions for Cantilever:** A simply supported beam has **NO moment reaction** at the supports. Only the fixed beam (propped cantilever / fixed-fixed) has a moment reaction. Mixing these up is a frequent error.
> 5. **Sign Convention in SFD/BMD:** Use **upward force = positive SF** and **sagging moment = positive BMD**. Inconsistent sign convention loses marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### Shaft Design Essentials
- [x] Universal torsion equation: $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$
- [x] Polar moment of inertia for solid shaft: $J = \pi d^4 / 32$
- [x] Polar moment of inertia for hollow shaft: $J = \pi(D^4 - d^4)/32$
- [x] Polar section modulus for solid shaft: $Z_p = \pi d^3 / 16$
- [x] Power-torque relation: $T = 9.55 P / N$ (with $P$ in W, $N$ in rpm)
- [x] Maximum shear stress location: **outer surface** ($r = R$)
- [x] Strength criterion: $\tau_{max} = T / Z_p \leq \tau_{allow}$
- [x] Stiffness criterion: $\theta / L \leq \theta_{allow}$ (in **rad/m**)
- [x] St. Venant's assumptions: cross-sections remain plane, no warping for circular shafts
- [x] Always **choose the larger** diameter from strength vs. stiffness analysis
- [x] Hollow shafts save material: $J_{hollow} > J_{solid}$ for the **same outside diameter**

### Beam Classification Essentials
- [x] **Simply Supported** = pin + roller (2 vertical reactions, NO moment)
- [x] **Cantilever** = fixed at one end (vertical reaction + moment reaction at fixed end)
- [x] **Fixed Beam / Propped Cantilever** = 3 reactions (2 vertical + 1 moment)
- [x] **Fixed-Fixed Beam** = 4 reactions (2 vertical + 2 moments)
- [x] **Continuous Beam** = multiple supports, statically indeterminate
- [x] **Overhanging Beam** = simply supported with end(s) projecting beyond supports
- [x] UDL: Total load = $wL$, acts at center; $M_{max} = wL^2/8$ at mid-span
- [x] Point load (simply supported, at center): $M_{max} = PL/4$ at mid-span

### Common KTU Numerical Traps to Avoid
- [x] Convert **all units to SI** (N, m, Pa) before applying formulas
- [x] Convert **rpm to rad/s** when computing angular velocity $\omega = 2\pi N/60$
- [x] Convert **degrees to radians** for angle of twist calculations
- [x] Remember $1 \text{ GPa} = 10^3 \text{ MPa} = 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$
- [x] Watch for the term **"per meter length"** in stiffness problems — means use $L = 1$ m
- [x] Beam **SFD is linear** for point loads and **parabolic** for UDL/UVL
- [x] Beam **BMD is zero at free ends** and **zero at pin/roller supports** of simply supported beams

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diagrammatic Conventions for Supports and Loading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT402 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Diagrammatic Conventions for Supports and Loading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagrammatic Conventions for Supports and Loading** are the **standardized graphical symbols** prescribed by engineering drawing codes (such as IS 800, IS 875, and Eurocode 3) used universally in **Strength of Materials** and **Structural Analysis** to represent physical supports, applied forces, and moments on structural members (beams, frames, trusses) in free-body diagrams (FBDs).

These conventions ensure unambiguous communication between engineers, allowing every drawing to be interpreted identically regardless of geographic location. In KTU's Outcome-Based Education framework, this topic maps to **CO1** (Apply the fundamental concepts of solid mechanics to solve problems involving axial loading, bending, and shear) under the **BTL-2 (Comprehension)** cognitive level of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Language of Structures"

Imagine you are writing a letter to a friend in another country. Just as the letters and words in your language (English, Hindi, Malayalam) form a standard set of symbols that everyone understands, **diagrammatic conventions** form the **universal language of engineers**. When you draw a small triangle beneath a line, every structural engineer in the world immediately knows: *"Ah, this is a pin support — it prevents translation but allows rotation."*

Think of it like traffic signals:
- **Red light** = Stop (like a fixed support — no movement at all)
- **Yellow light** = Caution — partial movement allowed (like a roller — one direction restricted)
- **Green light** = Full freedom (like a free end — no restriction)

The beam is the road; supports are the traffic signals; loads are the vehicles. **Without standardized symbols, traffic (engineering intent) would lead to collisions (misinterpreted designs).**

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Standard Load Unit:** $\text{kN}$ (kilo-Newton) or $\text{N/mm}^2$ for stress. In SI, loads are expressed in **Newtons (N)**, while engineering practice favors **kN** or **MN**.
> - **Standard Length Unit:** **mm** for cross-sections, **m** for spans.
> - **Sign Convention (KTU 2024):** Downward forces are **negative**, upward forces are **positive**. Clockwise moments are typically **positive** for beams (varies by textbook; KTU follows **sagging positive** convention for bending moment).

### 1.4 Importance in KTU 2024 Scheme

This topic underpins **every subsequent module** — without correct identification of support types, calculating reactions, drawing SFDs (Shear Force Diagrams) and BMDs (Bending Moment Diagrams), and analyzing statically determinate/indeterminate structures becomes impossible. It is the **"grammar"** of structural mechanics.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Classification of Supports

A support is a **physical constraint** that restricts certain degrees of freedom (DOFs) of a structural member. A rigid body in a 2D plane has **3 DOFs**: horizontal translation, vertical translation, and rotation.

#### 2.1.1 Simple Support (Roller Support)

- **Symbol:** A small circle (roller) resting on a horizontal surface, with or without a triangle above.
- **Restraints:** Vertical translation only (1 reaction — perpendicular to rolling surface).
- **Freedom:** Horizontal movement and rotation are permitted.
- **Reaction:** Single force, $R_V$, perpendicular to the rolling surface (usually vertical).
- **Real-world example:** A roller bearing beneath a bridge girder, allowing thermal expansion.

#### 2.1.2 Hinged Support (Pin Support)

- **Symbol:** A triangle with two small circles at its base, attached to the ground.
- **Restraints:** Horizontal and vertical translation (2 reactions: $H_A$ and $V_A$).
- **Freedom:** Rotation is permitted.
- **Reaction:** Two mutually perpendicular force components.
- **Real-world example:** A door hinge, the pin of a swing.

#### 2.1.3 Fixed Support (Built-in / Clamped Support)

- **Symbol:** A vertical hatched wall with the beam rigidly connected to it.
- **Restraints:** All 3 DOFs — horizontal translation, vertical translation, and rotation (3 reactions: $H_A$, $V_A$, $M_A$).
- **Freedom:** None.
- **Reaction:** Two force components + one moment.
- **Real-world example:** A cantilever beam embedded in a wall, a flag pole in its concrete base.

#### 2.1.4 Internal Hinge (Internal Pin)

- **Symbol:** A small circle at a point along the beam axis.
- **Function:** Connects two members allowing free relative rotation; **transmits force but not moment** ($M = 0$ at the hinge).

### 2.2 Classification of Loads

Loads are external actions applied to a structure. They are classified by:

#### 2.2.1 By Distribution

| Load Type | Description | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **Point Load (Concentrated Load)** | Applied at a single point | Vertical arrow, magnitude in **kN** |
| **Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL)** | Constant intensity $w$ (kN/m) along a length | Rectangle with intensity value on top |
| **Uniformly Varying Load (UVL)** | Linearly varying from 0 to $w$ | Triangle (right or left) |
| **Trapezoidal Load** | Combination of UDL + UVL | Trapezoid |

#### 2.2.2 By Direction & Nature

- **Vertical loads:** Gravity (DL — Dead Load, LL — Live Load)
- **Horizontal loads:** Wind, seismic, hydrostatic thrust
- **Axial loads:** Along the member's longitudinal axis (tension/compression)
- **Moment (Couple):** Rotational effect without translation; shown as a curved arrow or a moment vector symbol

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All high-yield formulas for Module 2 supports and load conversions:**

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Equivalent point load from UDL | $W = w \cdot L$ | Total load = intensity $\times$ length |
| 2 | Centroid of UDL | $\bar{x} = L/2$ | Distance from either end |
| 3 | Equivalent load from UVL (triangular) | $W = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot w_0 \cdot L$ | Area of triangle |
| 4 | Centroid of UVL (from zero-intensity end) | $\bar{x} = L/3$ | From apex of triangle |
| 5 | Centroid of UVL (from max-intensity end) | $\bar{x} = 2L/3$ | From base of triangle |
| 6 | Trapezoidal load split | $W = W_{\text{UDL}} + W_{\text{UVL}}$ | Split rectangle + triangle |
| 7 | Resultant of general distributed load | $W = \int_{0}^{L} w(x)\, dx$ | Area under load diagram |
| 8 | Location of resultant | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\int_{0}^{L} x \cdot w(x)\, dx}{\int_{0}^{L} w(x)\, dx}$ | First moment of area / area |
| 9 | Support reactions (statically determinate beam) | $\Sigma F_x = 0,\ \Sigma F_y = 0,\ \Sigma M = 0$ | Three equilibrium equations |
| 10 | Degree of static indeterminacy | $D_s = R - (3 + C)$ | $R$ = reactions, $C$ = internal hinges |

> **Critical Note on Units:** Never mix units — if length is in **m**, intensity must be in **kN/m**, and force in **kN**. Convert to consistent SI before substitution.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **bridge design** (e.g., Kochi Metro, GIFT City structures), identifying each support type correctly is crucial: a **roller on one end** allows thermal expansion; a **pin on the other** allows rotation but prevents longitudinal drift. In **machine tool frames**, fixed supports absorb vibrations. In **offshore platforms**, support conventions dictate how wave loads are transferred to the seabed. **A single misinterpreted support symbol can lead to catastrophic structural failure** — making this topic non-negotiable in KTU's professional engineering curriculum.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Equivalent Point Load of a Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL)

**Problem Setup:** A beam of length $L$ carries a uniformly distributed load of intensity $w$ (kN/m) over its entire span.

**Step 1 — Conceptualize the load distribution.**

Consider an infinitesimal element of length $dx$ at distance $x$ from the left end. The load on this element is:

$$dW = w \, dx$$

where $w$ is the load intensity in kN/m.

**Step 2 — Integrate over the entire length.**

The total equivalent point load is the integral of $dW$ from $x = 0$ to $x = L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \int_{0}^{L} dW = \int_{0}^{L} w \, dx \\
&= w \int_{0}^{L} dx \\
&= w \cdot [x]_{0}^{L} \\
&= w \cdot (L - 0) \\
&= w \cdot L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the line of action (location of resultant).**

For symmetry of the UDL about the midspan, the centroid lies at:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \frac{\int_{0}^{L} x \cdot w \, dx}{\int_{0}^{L} w \, dx} \\
&= \frac{w \int_{0}^{L} x \, dx}{w \cdot L} \\
&= \frac{w \cdot \left[\tfrac{x^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{L}}{w \cdot L} \\
&= \frac{w \cdot \tfrac{L^2}{2}}{w \cdot L} \\
&= \frac{L}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A UDL of intensity $w$ over a length $L$ is equivalent to a concentrated load $W = wL$ acting at the **midpoint** ($L/2$ from either end).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Equivalent Point Load of a Triangular (UVL) Load

**Problem Setup:** A UVL varies linearly from 0 at the left end to $w_0$ (kN/m) at the right end, over a span $L$.

**Step 1 — Express intensity as a function of position.**

At any distance $x$ from the left end, by similar triangles:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{w(x)}{x} &= \frac{w_0}{L} \\
