\therefore \quad w(x) &= \frac{w_0 \cdot x}{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total load by integration.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \int_{0}^{L} w(x) \, dx = \int_{0}^{L} \frac{w_0 \cdot x}{L} \, dx \\
&= \frac{w_0}{L} \int_{0}^{L} x \, dx \\
&= \frac{w_0}{L} \cdot \left[\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{L} \\
&= \frac{w_0}{L} \cdot \frac{L^2}{2} \\
&= \frac{w_0 \cdot L}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

This confirms the geometric formula: **Area of triangle** = $\tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \text{base} \cdot \text{height} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot L \cdot w_0$.

**Step 3 — Locate the centroid from the left end (zero-intensity apex).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \frac{\int_{0}^{L} x \cdot w(x) \, dx}{\int_{0}^{L} w(x) \, dx} \\
&= \frac{\int_{0}^{L} x \cdot \tfrac{w_0 x}{L} \, dx}{w_0 L / 2} \\
&= \frac{\tfrac{w_0}{L} \int_{0}^{L} x^2 \, dx}{w_0 L / 2} \\
&= \frac{\tfrac{w_0}{L} \cdot \tfrac{L^3}{3}}{w_0 L / 2} \\
&= \frac{\tfrac{w_0 L^2}{3}}{\tfrac{w_0 L}{2}} \\
&= \frac{w_0 L^2}{3} \cdot \frac{2}{w_0 L} \\
&= \frac{2L}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret.**

The resultant of a triangular UVL is $\tfrac{1}{2} w_0 L$, located at $\tfrac{2L}{3}$ from the **apex (zero-intensity end)** — equivalently, $\tfrac{L}{3}$ from the **base (maximum-intensity end)**. This matches the standard centroid of a triangle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Centroid Rule (Triangular Load):** The resultant always acts through the **centroid of the load diagram**, located at $\tfrac{L}{3}$ from the larger end and $\tfrac{2L}{3}$ from the smaller end.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Equivalent of a Trapezoidal Load (UDL + UVL Superposition)

A trapezoidal load of intensity $w_1$ at the left, $w_2$ at the right ($w_2 > w_1$), over length $L$:

**Step 1 — Split the trapezoid into a rectangle (UDL) and a triangle (UVL).**

Rectangle: intensity $w_1$, length $L$ → $W_1 = w_1 \cdot L$, centroid at $L/2$.

Triangle: intensity $(w_2 - w_1)$, length $L$ → $W_2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(w_2 - w_1)L$, centroid at $\tfrac{2L}{3}$ from the left (smaller end).

**Step 2 — Total equivalent load.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{total}} &= W_1 + W_2 \\
&= w_1 L + \tfrac{1}{2}(w_2 - w_1)L \\
&= L \left[w_1 + \tfrac{w_2 - w_1}{2}\right] \\
&= L \cdot \frac{w_1 + w_2}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Locate the resultant.**

Using the **first moment of area** principle about the left end:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \frac{W_1 \cdot \tfrac{L}{2} + W_2 \cdot \tfrac{2L}{3}}{W_1 + W_2} \\
&= \frac{w_1 L \cdot \tfrac{L}{2} + \tfrac{1}{2}(w_2 - w_1)L \cdot \tfrac{2L}{3}}{\tfrac{L(w_1 + w_2)}{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

After algebraic simplification:

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{L(w_1 + 2w_2)}{3(w_1 + w_2)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This split-method is the **fastest** approach in KTU exam halls. Memorizing the integrated formula is unnecessary — splitting into UDL + UVL and applying centroids works universally for any trapezoid.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Support Classification & Reactions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STRUCTURAL SUPPORTS IN 2D] --> B[SIMPLE / ROLLER]
    A --> C[HINGED / PIN]
    A --> D[FIXED / CLAMPED]
    A --> E[INTERNAL HINGE]

    B --> B1[Symbol: Circle on line]
    B --> B2[Reactions: 1 vertical force Rv]
    B --> B3[DOF: Rotation free, horizontal free]

    C --> C1[Symbol: Triangle with pin]
    C --> C2[Reactions: H sub A and V sub A]
    C --> C3[DOF: Rotation free]

    D --> D1[Symbol: Hatched wall]
    D --> D2[Reactions: H sub A, V sub A, M sub A]
    D --> D3[DOF: None]

    E --> E1[Symbol: Small circle on beam axis]
    E --> E2[Transmits force, no moment]
    E --> E3[Used in compound beams]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#6a1b9a,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#ef6c00,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Load Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L[APPLIED LOADS] --> L1[POINT LOAD]
    L --> L2[DISTRIBUTED LOAD]
    L --> L3[MOMENT / COUPLE]

    L1 --> L1A[Concentrated Force in kN]
    L1 --> L1B[Single vertical or horizontal arrow]

    L2 --> L2A[UDL Uniform intensity w kN per m]
    L2 --> L2B[UVL Linearly varying intensity]
    L2 --> L2C[TRAPEZOIDAL UDL plus UVL]

    L3 --> L3A[Curved arrow symbol]
    L3 --> L3B[Value in kN m]
    L3 --> L3C[Clockwise or anticlockwise]

    L2A --> R1[Equivalent: W equals w times L, centroid L over 2]
    L2B --> R2[Equivalent: W equals half times w0 times L, centroid 2L over 3 from apex]
    L2C --> R3[Equivalent: W equals L times w1 plus w2 over 2]

    style L fill:#0d47a1,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style R1 fill:#4caf50,color:#fff
    style R2 fill:#4caf50,color:#fff
    style R3 fill:#4caf50,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Support-to-Reaction Mapping Table (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph S1[Beam with Pin and Roller]
        BEAM1[Simply Supported Beam] --> SUPP1[LEFT: PIN]
        BEAM1 --> SUPP2[RIGHT: ROLLER]
        SUPP1 --> REACT1[H sub A horizontal, V sub A vertical]
        SUPP2 --> REACT2[V sub B vertical only]
    end

    subgraph S2[Beam with Fixed and Free]
        BEAM2[Cantilever Beam] --> SUPP3[LEFT: FIXED]
        BEAM2 --> FREE[RIGHT: FREE END]
        SUPP3 --> REACT3[H, V, M all three reactions]
        FREE --> REACT4[No reaction, free to move]
    end

    subgraph S3[Beam with Two Fixes]
        BEAM3[FIXED-FIXED BEAM] --> SUPP4[LEFT: FIXED]
        BEAM3 --> SUPP5[RIGHT: FIXED]
        SUPP4 --> REACT5[H1, V1, M1]
        SUPP5 --> REACT6[H2, V2, M2]
        BEAM3 --> NOTE[Degree of indeterminacy equals 3]
    end

    style S1 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0
    style S2 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style S3 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    style NOTE fill:#ffeb3b,color:#000
```

### 4.4 ASCII Schematic: Typical Beam Loading Conventions

```
                    Concentrated Load
                          |
                          v  P = 20 kN
                          |
   _______________________|_______________________
  |                                               |
[A] — Pin Support                              [B] — Roller Support
  |_______________________________________________|
  |←————————————— L = 6 m ——————————————→|

        Distributed Load Intensity w = 10 kN/m
        _________________________________________
       |█████████████████████████████████████████|
       |_________________________________________|
        ←————————————— L = 6 m ——————————————→
                  Equivalent: W = 60 kN at 3 m


                Triangular UVL: 0 to 20 kN/m
                          /|
                        /  |
                      /    |
                    /      |  w0 = 20 kN/m
                  /        |
                /__________|
              <-  L = 4 m ->
         Equivalent: W = 40 kN at 8/3 m from left
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
Differentiate between a hinged support and a roller support with the help of neat sketches. Mention the number of reaction components in each case.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Hinged (Pin) Support | Roller Support |
|---|---|---|
| **Symbol** | Triangle with pin at base | Triangle with circle/roller below |
| **Reactions** | Two ($H_A$, $V_A$) | One (perpendicular to rolling surface) |
| **Translation resisted** | Horizontal + Vertical | Only one direction (usually vertical) |
| **Rotation** | Allowed | Allowed |
| **Use** | Bridge piers, trusses | Bridge decks, expansion joints |

A pin support resists forces in **both horizontal and vertical directions** and hence has **two reaction components**, whereas a roller resists force in **only one direction** (perpendicular to the surface of rolling) and hence has **one reaction component**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
A simply supported beam of span 6 m carries a uniformly distributed load of intensity 10 kN/m over its entire span. Find the equivalent concentrated load and its point of application.

**Model Answer:**

Equivalent point load:

$$
W = w \times L = 10 \times 6 = 60 \text{ kN}
$$

Point of application: at the centroid of UDL, i.e.,

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{L}{2} = \frac{6}{2} = 3 \text{ m from either support}
$$

The equivalent concentrated load is **60 kN acting at the midspan (3 m from each end)**.

---

### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the different types of supports used in structural analysis. State the number of reactions for each type. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A beam ABC of total length 10 m is simply supported at A and C. It carries a uniformly distributed load of 15 kN/m over the entire span, and a concentrated load of 30 kN at a point B located 4 m from A. Find the reactions at the supports. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution:

**(a) Types of Supports** — (As detailed in Section 2.1 above)
- **Roller support:** 1 reaction (vertical)
- **Hinged/Pin support:** 2 reactions ($H$, $V$)
- **Fixed support:** 3 reactions ($H$, $V$, $M$)

**(b) Reaction Calculation:**

Free-body diagram: Beam simply supported at A (pin) and C (roller), span = 10 m.

Taking moments about A (counterclockwise positive):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma M_A &= 0 \\
V_C \times 10 - (15 \times 10) \times 5 - 30 \times 4 &= 0 \\
V_C \times 10 &= 750 + 120 \\
V_C \times 10 &= 870 \\
V_C &= 87 \text{ kN} \ (\uparrow)
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum of vertical forces:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma F_y &= 0 \\
V_A + V_C &= 15 \times 10 + 30 \\
V_A + 87 &= 180 \\
V_A &= 93 \text{ kN} \ (\uparrow)
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum of horizontal forces:

$$
\Sigma F_x = 0 \implies H_A = 0
$$

**Final Answers:** $V_A = 93$ kN, $V_C = 87$ kN, $H_A = 0$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A trapezoidal load varies from 20 kN/m at the left end to 10 kN/m at the right end over a span of 8 m. Determine the magnitude and location of the equivalent concentrated load. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With neat sketches, explain the standard symbols used to represent (i) point load, (ii) uniformly distributed load, (iii) uniformly varying load, and (iv) a moment (couple). **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution:

**(a) Trapezoidal Equivalent Load:**

Split into Rectangle + Triangle:

- **Rectangle (UDL):** $w_1 = 10$ kN/m, $L = 8$ m
  $W_1 = 10 \times 8 = 80$ kN at $x_1 = 4$ m
- **Triangle (UVL):** base = 8 m, height = $(20-10) = 10$ kN/m
  $W_2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 8 \times 10 = 40$ kN at $x_2 = \tfrac{2L}{3} = \tfrac{16}{3} \approx 5.333$ m from left (apex of triangle is on the left)

Total load:

$$
W = 80 + 40 = 120 \text{ kN}
$$

Location of resultant:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \frac{W_1 \cdot x_1 + W_2 \cdot x_2}{W_1 + W_2} \\
&= \frac{80 \times 4 + 40 \times \tfrac{16}{3}}{120} \\
&= \frac{320 + \tfrac{640}{3}}{120} \\
&= \frac{\tfrac{960 + 640}{3}}{120} \\
&= \frac{1600}{360} \\
&= \frac{40}{9} \approx 4.44 \text{ m from left}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $W = 120$ kN acting at $\bar{x} \approx 4.44$ m from the left support.

**(b) Load Symbols** — (As shown in Section 4.4 ASCII schematic)
- **Point load:** vertical arrow with magnitude in kN
- **UDL:** rectangle with intensity $w$ (kN/m) on top
- **UVL:** right-angled triangle with intensity gradient marked
- **Moment:** curved arrow with magnitude in kN·m

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Sign convention errors:** Many students mix clockwise/counterclockwise signs while computing $\Sigma M = 0$. **Always stick to one convention** (CCW positive is KTU standard).
>
> 2. **Centroid location for triangular loads:** A triangle's centroid is at $L/3$ from the **base** (larger end), NOT from the apex. Mixing this up leads to **2–3 mark deduction**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to split trapezoidal loads:** If you attempt to compute moment directly with a wrong centroid, expect **full mark loss** on the (a) sub-part.
>
> 4. **Units mismatch:** Writing "10 kN/m × 6 m = 60" without units of kN, or mixing m and mm, costs **1 mark minimum**.
>
> 5. **Reaction direction:** Always draw assumed direction (↑ for vertical). If your final answer is negative, **mention it explicitly** — examiners penalize ambiguous arrows.

---

### 🔑 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- ✅ **Three DOFs in 2D:** Horizontal translation, vertical translation, rotation. Supports restrict these in combinations.
- ✅ **Roller → 1 reaction, Pin → 2 reactions, Fixed → 3 reactions.**
- ✅ **Equivalent point load of UDL** = $wL$, centroid at $L/2$.
- ✅ **Equivalent point load of UVL (triangular)** = $\tfrac{1}{2}w_0 L$, centroid at $\tfrac{L}{3}$ from the larger (max intensity) end.
- ✅ **Trapezoidal load** = always split into **rectangle (UDL) + triangle (UVL)** for fastest centroid calculation.
- ✅ **Internal hinge** transmits force but **NOT moment** ($M = 0$ at hinge location).
- ✅ **Sign convention:** Downward loads negative, upward reactions positive; clockwise moments positive (KTU standard).
- ✅ **Static determinacy rule:** A 2D beam is statically determinate when $R = 3$ (e.g., 1 pin + 1 roller); indeterminate when $R > 3$.
- ✅ **Always convert to consistent SI units** before calculation.
- ✅ **Sketches earn marks:** Neat, labeled diagrams with arrow directions and values are awarded marks even in purely numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differential Equations Between Load, Shear Force and Bending Moment - Shear Force and Bending Moment Diagrams of Cantilever and Simply Supported Beam with Point Load/UDL. Point of Inflection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCSFT402 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Differential Equations Between Load, Shear Force and Bending Moment - Shear Force and Bending Moment Diagrams of Cantilever and Simply Supported Beam with Point Load/UDL. Point of Inflection # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Shear Force (SF or V) **Formal Definition:** The algebraic sum of the vertical (transverse) forces acting on either side of a section of a beam is called the **Shear Force** at that section. It represents the internal resistance developed to balance the transverse external load. $$V(x) = \sum F_y \text{ (on one side of section)}$$ **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine holding a piece of paper horizontally with both hands and trying to slide one hand forward while the other stays back. The internal "sliding resistance" you feel inside the paper is exactly what shear force is in a beam — it is the internal force that resists the two parts of a beam from sliding past each other vertically. ## 1.2 Bending Moment (BM or M) **Formal Definition:** The algebraic sum of the moments of all the forces acting on either side of a section about that section is called the **Bending Moment** at that section. It is the internal moment developed to resist the rotational tendency caused by external loads. $$M(x) = \sum M \text{ (about the section, on one side)}$$ **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a diving board. When a diver stands at the free end, the board bends downward. The internal moment resisting this bending is the **bending moment**. The diver's weight creates a *hogging* (concave-down) moment, and the fixed support at the other end counters it. A simply supported beam with a central load, on the other hand, experiences *sagging* (concave-up) bending — like a hammock with a person in the middle. ## 1.3 Sign Convention (KTU Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Standard Sign Convention:** > - **Positive Shear Force:** The left part of the section tends to move **upward** relative to the right part (clockwise rotation tendency). > - **Positive Bending Moment:** The beam **sags** (concave upward) at the section — tension on the bottom fiber, compression on the top fiber. > - **Positive Load:** Downward distributed load is conventionally taken as **negative** in the differential equation (since it points opposite to the positive y-axis). | Quantity | Positive Sign | Negative Sign | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Shear Force (V) | Left side ↑, Right side ↓ | Left side ↓, Right side ↑ | | Bending Moment (M) | Sagging (smile shape) | Hogging (frown shape) | | Load (w) | Upward (rare in gravity loads) | Downward (gravity) | ## 1.4 Types of Loads on Beams 1. **Point Load (Concentrated Load):** A force acting at a single point. Unit: **kN** or **N**. 2. **Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL):** Load spread uniformly over a length. Unit: **kN/m** or **N/m**. 3. **Uniformly Varying Load (UVL):** Load varying linearly along the length. (Covered in detail in later modules.) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Fundamental Relationships Consider a small beam element of length **dx** subjected to a distributed load **w(x)** per unit length (positive upward), with shear force **V** and bending moment **M** on the left face, and **V + dV**, **M + dM** on the right face. **Step 1 — Force Equilibrium (Vertical):** The net upward force on the element must equal zero. $$V + w(x)\,dx - (V + dV) = 0$$ **Step 2 — Simplification:** Canceling **V** from both sides: $$w(x)\,dx = dV \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\dfrac{dV}{dx} = w(x)}$$ **Step 3 — Moment Equilibrium (about the right face, CCW positive):** Taking moments and neglecting the second-order term **w(x)·(dx)²/2** (infinitesimal): $$M + V \cdot dx - (M + dM) = 0$$ **Step 4 — Simplification:** $$V\,dx = dM \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V}$$ **Step 5 — Combining the two:** $$\boxed{\dfrac{d^2M}{dx^2} = w(x)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Memory Trick for Sign:** If the load **w** is taken as **positive upward**, the rate of change of shear is positive upward. Most KTU problems take **gravity loads as negative** in the y-direction, so the actual governing equation is: > $$\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w(x) \quad \text{and} \quad \dfrac{d^2M}{dx^2} = -w(x)$$ > where **w** is the magnitude of the downward distributed load. ## 2.2 Area-Moment Theorems (Critical for KTU Problems) | # | Theorem | Statement | Equation | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Load → SF** | Change in SF between two points equals the **(negative) area** under the load diagram between those points | $V_2 - V_1 = -\int_{x_1}^{x_2} w(x)\,dx$ | | 2 | **SF → BM** | Change in BM between two points equals the **area** under the SF diagram between those points | $M_2 - M_1 = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} V(x)\,dx$ | | 3 | **Max BM** | Maximum/Minimum BM occurs where **SF = 0 or changes sign** | $V = 0 \Rightarrow M_{max}$ | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | S.No. | Formula | Meaning | Units | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w$ | Rate of change of SF = negative load intensity | kN/m per m = kN/m | | 2 | $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$ | Slope of BMD = SF value | kN | | 3 | $\dfrac{d^2M}{dx^2} = -w$ | Curvature of BMD $\propto$ negative load | kN/m² | | 4 | $M_{max}$ (Cantilever, point load at end) | $P \cdot L$ | kN·m | | 5 | $M_{max}$ (SSB, point load at center) | $\dfrac{P \cdot L}{4}$ | kN·m | | 6 | $M_{max}$ (SSB, UDL over full span) | $\dfrac{w \cdot L^2}{8}$ | kN·m | | 7 | $M_{max}$ (Cantilever, UDL over full span) | $\dfrac{w \cdot L^2}{2}$ | kN·m | ## 2.4 Shape Characteristics of SFD & BMD | Loading Condition | SFD Shape | BMD Shape | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **No load** (between loads) | Horizontal line (constant) | Straight inclined line (linear) | | **Point Load** | Vertical jump (step) | Straight inclined line (kink) | | **UDL** | Straight inclined line | Parabola (2nd degree) | | **UVL** | Parabola | Cubic curve (3rd degree) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Quick Rule:** The SFD for a UDL is **linear**, and the BMD is **parabolic** (second-degree). Always identify these shapes correctly during sketching — KTU examiners deduct marks for incorrectly drawn curves. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Bridge Design:** Maximum bending moment determines girder depth and cross-section. - **Machine Tool Shafts:** SF and BM diagrams predict fatigue failure points. - **Aircraft Wings:** Bending moment envelope under gust loads is essential for structural sizing. - **Building Frames:** Inflection points locate where the column moment reverses, critical for joint design. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Rigorous Derivation of the Differential Equations **Setup:** Consider a beam element of length **dx** cut at distance **x** from the left support. The element is acted upon by: - Distributed load: $w(x)$ per unit length (downward, magnitude $w$) - Shear force on left face: $V$ - Bending moment on left face: $M$ - Shear force on right face: $V + dV$ - Bending moment on right face: $M + dM$ **Step 1 — Free Body Diagram of the Element:** ``` ↑ V ↑ V + dV | | +--------- dx -------------+ | | ↓ w·dx (resultant of UDL) ↓ | | M →(CCW) (M + dM) →(CCW) ``` **Step 2 — Apply Vertical Force Equilibrium:** Sum of all vertical forces on the element = 0. $$V - w \cdot dx - (V + dV) = 0$$ **Step 3 — Expand and Cancel Terms:** $$V - w \cdot dx - V - dV = 0$$ $$-w \cdot dx - dV = 0$$ $$dV = -w \cdot dx$$ **Step 4 — Divide both sides by dx:** $$\boxed{\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)} \quad \text{...(Equation 1)}$$ **Step 5 — Apply Moment Equilibrium (about the right face, CCW positive):** $$M + V \cdot dx - w \cdot dx \cdot \frac{dx}{2} - (M + dM) = 0$$ The term $w \cdot dx \cdot \frac{dx}{2}$ is of second order (very small) and is neglected. **Step 6 — Simplify:** $$M + V \cdot dx - M - dM = 0$$ $$V \cdot dx = dM$$ **Step 7 — Divide both sides by dx:** $$\boxed{\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V(x)} \quad \text{...(Equation 2)}$$ **Step 8 — Differentiate Equation 2 and Substitute Equation 1:** $$\dfrac{d^2M}{dx^2} = \dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$$ $$\boxed{\dfrac{d^2M}{dx^2} = -w(x)} \quad \text{...(Equation 3)}$$ These three equations are the **mathematical backbone** of all SFD/BMD analysis. --- ## 3.2 Case Study 1 — Cantilever Beam with Point Load at Free End **Problem Data:** - Length: $L = 4$ m - Point load: $P = 20$ kN at the free end (downward) - Fixed at left end (A), free at right end (B) **Step 1 — Support Reactions:** At the fixed end A, vertical reaction $R_A = 20$ kN (upward), Moment reaction $M_A = 80$ kN·m (CCW). **Step 2 — Section at distance x from free end (B):** Take the right-hand portion (from section to B). Only force is $P = 20$ kN downward at B. $$V(x) = -P = -20 \text{ kN (constant for all } x \in [0, L])$$ $$M(x) = -P \cdot x = -20x \text{ kN·m}$$ **Step 3 — Tabulate Values:** | x (m) | 0 (Free end B) | 2 (Mid) | 4 (Fixed end A) | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | V (kN) | $-20$ | $-20$ | $-20$ | | M (kN·m) | $0$ | $-40$ | $-80$ | **Step 4 — Sketch Description:** - **SFD:** A horizontal rectangle below the axis, drawn at $-20$ kN from $x = 0$ to $x = 4$ m. - **BMD:** A right triangle below the axis, with zero at the free end (B) and maximum magnitude $-80$ kN·m at the fixed end (A). > **Maximum Bending Moment:** $\vert M_{max} \vert = P \cdot L = 20 \times 4 = \mathbf{80 \text{ kN·m}}$ at the fixed end. --- ## 3.3 Case Study 2 — Cantilever Beam with UDL over Entire Span **Problem Data:** - Length: $L = 3$ m - UDL: $w = 10$ kN/m (downward) over the entire span - Fixed at A (left), free at B (right) **Step 1 — Reactions:** $R_A = wL = 30$ kN (upward) $M_A = \dfrac{wL^2}{2} = \dfrac{10 \times 9}{2} = 45$ kN·m (CCW) **Step 2 — Section at distance x from free end B:** Total downward load on the right portion = $w \cdot x$ $$V(x) = -w \cdot x = -10x \text{ kN}$$ $$M(x) = -(w \cdot x) \cdot \frac{x}{2} = -\frac{wx^2}{2} = -5x^2 \text{ kN·m}$$ **Step 3 — Tabulate Values:** | x (m) | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | V (kN) | $0$ | $-10$ | $-20$ | $-30$ | | M (kN·m) | $0$ | $-5$ | $-20$ | $-45$ | **Step 4 — Sketch Description:** - **SFD:** A right triangle below the axis, starting at 0 (free end) and reaching $-30$ kN at the fixed end. - **BMD:** A second-degree parabola below the axis, with zero slope at $x = 0$ (since V = 0 there) and maximum magnitude $-45$ kN·m at the fixed end. > **Maximum Bending Moment:** $\vert M_{max} \vert = \dfrac{wL^2}{2} = \dfrac{10 \times 9}{2} = \mathbf{45 \text{ kN·m}}$ --- ## 3.4 Case Study 3 — Simply Supported Beam with Central Point Load **Problem Data:** - Length: $L = 6$ m - Point load: $P = 24$ kN at the center (3 m from A) - Supports at A (pin) and B (roller) **Step 1 — Reactions (by symmetry):** $$R_A = R_B = \frac{P}{2} = 12 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 2 — Section at distance x from A (for $0 \le x \le 3$):** $$V(x) = R_A = +12 \text{ kN}$$ $$M(x) = R_A \cdot x = 12x \text{ kN·m}$$ **Step 3 — Section at distance x from A (for $3 \le x \le 6$):** $$V(x) = R_A - P = 12 - 24 = -12 \text{ kN}$$ $$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - P(x - 3) = 12x - 24(x-3) = 12(6-x) \text{ kN·m}$$ **Step 4 — Tabulate Values:** | x (m) | 0 | 1.5 | 3 (Center) | 4.5 | 6 | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | V (kN) | $+12$ | $+12$ | $+12/-12$ | $-12$ | $-12$ | | M (kN·m) | $0$ | $+18$ | $+36$ | $+18$ | $0$ | **Step 5 — Sketch Description:** - **SFD:** A rectangle above the axis from 0 to 3 m at +12 kN, then a rectangle below the axis from 3 to 6 m at -12 kN. - **BMD:** A triangle above the axis, peak at center = +36 kN·m, zero at both supports. > **Maximum Bending Moment:** $M_{max} = \dfrac{PL}{4} = \dfrac{24 \times 6}{4} = \mathbf{36 \text{ kN·m}}$ at the center. --- ## 3.5 Case Study 4 — Simply Supported Beam with UDL over Entire Span **Problem Data:** - Length: $L = 8$ m - UDL: $w = 5$ kN/m over the entire span - Supports at A and B **Step 1 — Reactions (by symmetry):** $$R_A = R_B = \frac{wL}{2} = \frac{5 \times 8}{2} = 20 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 2 — Section at distance x from A:** $$V(x) = R_A - w \cdot x = 20 - 5x \text{ kN}$$ $$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - w \cdot x \cdot \frac{x}{2} = 20x - 2.5x^2 \text{ kN·m}$$ **Step 3 — Find Location of Maximum BM:** Set $V(x) = 0$: $$20 - 5x = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = 4 \text{ m (center)}$$ **Step 4 — Maximum BM:** $$M_{max} = 20(4) - 2.5(4)^2 = 80 - 40 = 40 \text{ kN·m}$$ **Step 5 — Tabulate Values:** | x (m) | 0 | 2 | 4 (Center) | 6 | 8 | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | V (kN) | $+20$ | $+10$ | $0$ | $-10$ | $-20$ | | M (kN·m) | $0$ | $+30$ | $+40$ | $+30$ | $0$ | **Step 6 — Sketch Description:** - **SFD:** A straight inclined line, +20 kN at A, crossing zero at center, -20 kN at B. - **BMD:** A second-degree parabola (opening downward), zero at both ends, peak +40 kN·m at center. > **Verification using formula:** $M_{max} = \dfrac{wL^2}{8} = \dfrac{5 \times 64}{8} = \mathbf{40 \text{ kN·m}}$ ✓ --- ## 3.6 Point of Inflection (Contraflexure Point) **Formal Definition:** A point on the beam where the **bending moment is zero and changes sign** (from positive to negative or vice versa) is called the **Point of Inflection** or **Contraflexure Point**. At this location, the curvature of the beam changes direction — the beam transitions from sagging to hogging (or vice versa). **Mathematical Condition:** $$M(x_0) = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \dfrac{dM}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x_0} = V(x_0) \neq 0$$ **Where Does It Occur?** | Beam Type | Point of Inflection? | | :--- | :--- | | Cantilever with point load at free end | **No** (BM is always negative) | | Cantilever with UDL | **No** (BM is always negative) | | Simply supported beam with central point load | **No** (BM is always positive) | | Simply supported beam with UDL | **No** (BM is always positive) | | Overhanging beam with UDL on overhang | **Yes** (1 inflection point typically) | | Fixed-fixed beam with UDL | **Yes** (2 inflection points, symmetric) | | Continuous beam | **Yes** (over the intermediate support) | > [!IMPORTANT] > For Module 2's basic cases (cantilever and simply supported beams with point load/UDL only), there are **no points of inflection** within the span. The concept is introduced here as a foundation for Module 3 (Overhanging Beams, Fixed Beams) where inflection points become critical design features. **Significance in Design:** - Location of **zero moment** → useful for placing **internal hinges** in design. - In **reinforced concrete**, the reinforcement layout (top vs. bottom bars) reverses at inflection points. - Indicates where the **concrete can crack** due to moment reversal in cyclic loading. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Flowchart — The Core Relationship Chain The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the fundamental mathematical linkage between the three key quantities: **Load Intensity (w)**, **Shear Force (V)**, and **Bending Moment (M)**. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Load Intensity w(x)
kN per m"] B["Shear Force V(x)
kN"] C["Bending Moment M(x)
kN times m"] D["Slope of SFD
equals negative load"] E["Slope of BMD
equals SF value"] F["Curvature of BMD
equals negative load"] G["SFD Diagram"] H["BMD Diagram"] A -->|"dV by dx = minus w"| D D --> B B -->|"dM by dx = V"| E E --> C A -->|"d2M by dx2 = minus w"| F F --> C B --> G C --> H ``` ## 4.2 Decision Block — SFD/BMD Shape Characteristics This block diagram maps how different load types generate characteristic curve shapes in the SFD and BMD. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph A1["LOAD TYPE"] A1A["Point Load P"] A1B["UDL w"] A1C["No Load"] end subgraph B1["SFD SHAPE"] B1A["Vertical Jump Step"] B1B["Straight Inclined Line"] B1C["Horizontal Constant Line"] end subgraph C1["BMD SHAPE"] C1A["Straight Inclined Line with Kink"] C1B["Parabola 2nd degree"] C1C["Straight Inclined Line"] end A1A --> B1A --> C1A A1B --> B1B --> C1B A1C --> B1C --> C1C ``` ## 4.3 Analytical Strategy — How to Draw SFD & BMD ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["Step 1 Identify all supports and loads"] S2["Step 2 Compute reaction forces and moments using equilibrium"] S3["Step 3 Divide beam into sections at every load discontinuity"] S4["Step 4 For each section write V x and M x using cut method"] S5["Step 5 Tabulate V and M at section boundaries and key points"] S6["Step 6 Plot SFD using shape rules from Section 2.4"] S7["Step 7 Plot BMD using shape rules from Section 2.4"] S8["Step 8 Locate V equals 0 to find M max and check inflection points"] S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 ``` ## 4.4 Schematic — SFD & BMD Reference Sketches (Block Representation) Since physical beam diagrams cannot be rendered in Mermaid, the following block-level matrix describes the **characteristic shape** of each SFD and BMD for the four standard Module 2 cases. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph C1A["CASE 1 Cantilever with Point Load at Free End"] C1ASFD["SFD: Rectangle BELOW axis
Value: minus P (constant)
Length: L"] C1ABMD["BMD: Right Triangle BELOW axis
0 at free end, minus PL at fixed end"] end subgraph C2A["CASE 2 Cantilever with UDL"] C2ASFD["SFD: Right Triangle BELOW axis
0 at free end, minus wL at fixed end"] C2ABMD["BMD: Parabola BELOW axis
0 at free end, minus wL squared by 2 at fixed end"] end subgraph C3A["CASE 3 SSB with Central Point Load"] C3ASFD["SFD: Rectangle ABOVE axis plus by equals P by 2
Rectangle BELOW axis minus by equals P by 2
Jump at center"] C3ABMD["BMD: Triangle ABOVE axis
0 at supports, plus PL by 4 at center"] end subgraph C4A["CASE 4 SSB with UDL over Full Span"] C4ASFD["SFD: Inclined Line
plus wL by 2 at A, 0 at center, minus wL by 2 at B"] C4ABMD["BMD: Parabola ABOVE axis
0 at supports, plus wL squared by 8 at center"] end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Convention in Diagrams:** "ABOVE axis" denotes positive values (sagging, conventional positive). "BELOW axis" denotes negative values (hogging). The student should reproduce these clearly in the KTU answer script with axis labels, units, and key values marked at the peaks. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]** **Define Shear Force and Bending Moment. State the sign convention used for drawing SFD and BMD.** **Model Answer:** **Shear Force (SF):** The algebraic sum of vertical forces acting on either side of a section of a beam is called the shear force at that section. It represents the internal transverse resistance of the beam. **Bending Moment (BM):** The algebraic sum of moments of all forces acting on either side of a section, taken about that section, is called the bending moment. It represents the internal resistance to bending. **Sign Convention:** - **Positive Shear Force:** Causes clockwise rotation of the beam element (left side moves up, right side moves down). - **Positive Bending Moment:** Causes **sagging** of the beam (concave upward, tension on bottom fiber). ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **What is meant by Point of Inflection? Mention the loading conditions under which it occurs in a beam.** **Model Answer:** A **Point of Inflection** (or Contraflexure Point) is a point on the beam where the bending moment is **zero and changes sign**. At this point, the curvature of the beam changes — the beam transitions from sagging to hogging (or vice versa). **Loading Conditions Where It Occurs:** 1. Overhanging beams (UDL on the overhang portion). 2. Fixed beams (UDL or point loads cause moment reversal near the supports). 3. Continuous beams (above intermediate supports). 4. Propped cantilever beams. For the basic cases studied in Module 2 (cantilever and simply supported with point load/UDL only), **no point of inflection** occurs within the span. --- ## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:** Common mistakes students make in SFD/BMD problems: > 1. **Forgetting to compute reactions** before sectioning the beam. > 2. **Confusing the direction of shear and moment** when taking sections from the right side vs. left side. > 3. **Plotting parabolic BMD as linear** for UDL cases — this is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction. > 4. **Not labeling the sign** (positive/negative) on the diagrams. > 5. **Forgetting units** (kN for V, kN·m for M). > 6. **Not showing reactions on the beam diagram** before sectioning. ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]** **(a)** Derive the differential equations relating load intensity (w), shear force (V), and bending moment (M) for a beam element of length dx. Clearly state the assumptions used. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A cantilever beam AB of length 4 m is fixed at A and free at B. It carries a point load of 30 kN at the free end B. Draw the Shear Force Diagram (SFD) and Bending Moment Diagram (BMD). Also find the magnitude and location of the maximum bending moment. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution — Question A #### Part (a) — Derivation of Differential Equations **Step 1 — Consider a small beam element of length dx** at a distance x from the left support. **Step 2 — Forces and moments acting on the element:** - On the left face: Shear force $V$ (positive upward) and Bending moment $M$ (positive sagging). - On the right face: Shear force $V + dV$ and Bending moment $M + dM$. - Distributed load: $w(x)$ per unit length (downward). **Step 3 — Apply Vertical Force Equilibrium ($\sum F_y = 0$):** $$V - w \cdot dx - (V + dV) = 0$$ $$\Rightarrow dV = -w \cdot dx \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$$ **Step 4 — Apply Moment Equilibrium about the right face ($\sum M = 0$, CCW positive):** $$M + V \cdot dx - w \cdot dx \cdot \dfrac{dx}{2} - (M + dM) = 0$$ The term $w \cdot (dx)^2 / 2$ is a second-order infinitesimal and is neglected. $$\Rightarrow V \cdot dx = dM \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$$ **Step 5 — Combine by differentiation:** $$\dfrac{d^2M}{dx^2} = \dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$$ **Assumptions Used:** 1. The beam is initially straight and has a constant cross-section. 2. The material is homogeneous and isotropic, obeying Hooke's law. 3. The loading is in a plane containing the axis of the beam (plane of bending). 4. Self-weight of the beam is neglected unless otherwise stated. 5. Deflections are small (first-order theory). #### Part (b) — Cantilever with Point Load **Given:** $L = 4$ m, $P = 30$ kN at free end B. Fixed at A. **Step 1 — Reactions at fixed end A:** - $R_A = 30$ kN (upward) - $M_A = 30 \times 4 = 120$ kN·m (counter-clockwise) **Step 2 — Section at distance x from free end B (0 ≤ x ≤ 4 m):** Considering the right portion of the section (from section to B): $$V(x) = -30 \text{ kN (constant)}$$ $$M(x) = -30x \text{ kN·m}$$ **Step 3 — Tabulation:** | x (m) | 0 (B) | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 (A) | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | V (kN) | $-30$ | $-30$ | $-30$ | $-30$ | $-30$ | | M (kN·m) | $0$ | $-30$ | $-60$ | $-90$ | $-120$ | **Step 4 — SFD Sketch:** A horizontal rectangle **below** the beam axis, drawn at $V = -30$ kN, extending from $x = 0$ to $x = 4$ m. **Step 5 — BMD Sketch:** A right triangle **below** the beam axis, with the apex at the free end (M = 0) and the maximum magnitude at the fixed end. **Step 6 — Maximum Bending Moment:** $$\boxed{\vert M_{max} \vert = P \cdot L = 30 \times 4 = 120 \text{ kN·m, at the fixed end A}}$$ **Step 7 — Verification by Area-Moment Theorem:** Change in BM from B to A = Area under SFD = $(-30) \times 4 = -120$ kN·m. ✓ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]** **(a)** A simply supported beam of length 8 m carries a uniformly distributed load (UDL) of 12 kN/m over the entire span. Draw the SFD and BMD. Find the maximum bending moment and its location. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the concept of **Point of Inflection** with the help of a neat sketch. State the practical significance of locating inflection points in beam design. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution — Question B #### Part (a) — Simply Supported Beam with UDL **Given:** $L = 8$ m, $w = 12$ kN/m, simply supported at A and B. **Step 1 — Reactions (by symmetry):** $$R_A = R_B = \dfrac{wL}{2} = \dfrac{12 \times 8}{2} = 48 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 2 — Section at distance x from A:** $$V(x) = R_A - w \cdot x = 48 - 12x \text{ kN}$$ $$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - w \cdot x \cdot \dfrac{x}{2} = 48x - 6x^2 \text{ kN·m}$$ **Step 3 — Location of Maximum BM:** Set $V(x) = 0$: $$48 - 12x = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = 4 \text{ m (at the center)}$$ **Step 4 — Maximum Bending Moment:** $$M_{max} = 48(4) - 6(4)^2 = 192 - 96 = 96 \text{ kN·m}$$ **Step 5 — Tabulation:** | x (m) | 0 (A) | 2 | 4 (Center) | 6 | 8 (B) | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | V (kN) | $+48$ | $+24$ | $0$ | $-24$ | $-48$ | | M (kN·m) | $0$ | $+72$ | $+96$ | $+72$ | $0$ | **Step 6 — SFD Sketch:** A straight inclined line starting at $+48$ kN at A, crossing the axis at the center ($x = 4$ m), and reaching $-48$ kN at B. **Step 7 — BMD Sketch:** A second-degree parabola (opening downward) with zero values at both supports and a peak of $+96$ kN·m at the center. **Step 8 — Verification using Standard Formula:** $$\boxed{M_{max} = \dfrac{wL^2}{8} = \dfrac{12 \times 64}{8} = 96 \text{ kN·m at the center (x = 4 m)}}$$ #### Part (b) — Point of Inflection **Definition:** A **Point of Inflection** (also called a **Contraflexure Point**) is a section of the beam where the **bending moment is zero** and **changes sign** (from positive/sagging to negative/hogging, or vice versa). At this point, the curvature of the deflected beam changes direction. **Mathematical Condition:** $$M(x_0) = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad V(x_0) \neq 0$$ **Sketches (described textually — KTU students should draw these on graph paper):** **Case 1 — Fixed Beam with UDL:** A horizontal beam fixed at both ends under a UDL. The BMD shows a central positive (sagging) hump and two negative (hogging) lobes near the fixed ends. The BMD crosses zero at **two points symmetrically placed** between the supports and the center. These crossing points are the **Points of Inflection**. **Case 2 — Overhanging Beam with UDL on Overhang:** A simply supported beam with one end extended (overhang) and a UDL applied on the overhang. The BMD shows negative (hogging) values over the support adjacent to the overhang, and positive (sagging) values in the central span. The point where the BMD crosses zero between the support and the central region is a **Point of Inflection**. **Practical Significance:** 1. **Reinforcement Detailing in RC Beams:** At inflection points, the bending moment reverses, meaning the tension face changes from bottom to top. Steel reinforcement must be carefully curtailed or extended past these points to handle the reversed tension. 2. **Plastic Hinge Formation:** In the limit state design of steel structures, plastic hinges form at sections of maximum moment. Inflection points help identify regions where hinges may form during collapse mechanisms. 3. **Construction Joints:** In large concrete pours, construction joints are often placed near points of inflection where the moment is small, minimizing stress discontinuities. 4. **Deflection Analysis:** Inflection points indicate where the curvature changes sign, which is important in double-integration methods for finding slopes and deflections. 5. **Design Economy:** By recognizing where moments are zero, engineers can optimize cross-section sizes and reinforcement layout, leading to economical designs. > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall Warning for Part (b):** Students often **confuse "point of zero shear" with "point of inflection"**. They are different — zero shear gives **maximum BM**, while point of inflection is where **BM = 0 and changes sign**. Do not mix these up in the exam. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2:** - [ ] **Three core equations:** $dV/dx = -w$, $dM/dx = V$, $d^2M/dx^2 = -w$. Memorize these cold. - [ ] **Sign convention:** Positive V is **clockwise-rotating**, positive M is **sagging**. Stick to this for the entire paper. - [ ] **Shape rules:** - UDL → linear SFD, parabolic BMD. - Point load → step in SFD, kink in BMD. - No load → constant SFD, linear BMD. - [ ] **Standard results to memorize (every KTU paper tests at least one):** - SSB central point load: $M_{max} = PL/4$. - SSB with UDL: $M_{max} = wL^2/8$. - Cantilever end point load: $M_{max} = PL$ (at fixed end). - Cantilever with UDL: $M_{max} = wL^2/2$ (at fixed end). - [ ] **Area-Moment shortcuts:** Use areas under SFD/load diagrams to compute changes in BM/SF — saves calculation time. - [ ] **Point of Inflection:** BM = 0 AND sign change. Found in fixed beams, overhanging beams, continuous beams — **NOT** in the basic Module 2 cases. - [ ] **Always label:** Units (kN for V, kN·m for M), section positions (x values), and key magnitudes at the peaks. - [ ] **Units check:** w is in kN/m, V is in kN, M is in kN·m. Confusing units = losing 1 mark instantly. - [ ] **Reactions first:** Always compute all support reactions **before** attempting to draw the SFD/BMD. - [ ] **Symmetry shortcut:** For symmetric SSB with symmetric loading, reactions are equal, and the SF diagram is antisymmetric while the BM diagram is symmetric. - [ ] **Drawing neatness:** KTU examiners explicitly state in valuation keys: "Diagrams should be drawn to scale, with axes labeled, hatched areas shaded, and key values clearly marked at the peaks."

topics.title: Pure Bending
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT402 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pure Bending

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **Definition (Pure Bending)**
> **Pure bending** is the state of flexure in a beam (or structural member) in which the **bending moment is constant** along the entire length of the segment under consideration, and consequently, the **transverse shear force is zero (V = 0)** throughout that segment. Under this condition, the cross-section of the beam experiences only normal (longitudinal) stresses that vary linearly across the depth, with no shearing stress contribution from transverse loading.

Mathematically, the governing kinematic conditions for pure bending are:

$$\frac{dM}{dx} = V = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad M(x) = \text{constant} = M_0$$

The **Bernoulli–Euler classical bending theory** then provides the foundational relationship that, for an initially straight, homogeneous, isotropic beam of uniform cross-section loaded only at the ends by equal and opposite couples:

$$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}$$

where every symbol has a strict physical meaning (defined later in the formula sheet).

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Bending a Ruler" Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Conceptual Analogy for First-Time Learners**
> Imagine holding a plastic ruler flat on a table, placing both palms underneath it at the two ends, and then pushing **upward with both hands simultaneously using equal force but in opposite directions** (a *couple* at each end). What you feel is:
>
> 1. The ruler bends into a smooth, symmetric, **circular arc** — every cross-section rotates by the same small angle.
> 2. The top fibers get **stretched (tension)** and the bottom fibers get **squeezed (compression)**.
> 3. Somewhere in the middle, a layer of fibers does **not change length at all** — this is the **Neutral Surface** (in 3D) or **Neutral Axis** (on a 2D cross-section).
> 4. No matter where you look along the length of the ruler between the two palms, the curvature is the **same** — this is the essence of *pure* bending.
>
> This is mathematically identical to a beam segment in a four-point bending test or the central region between the supports and load points of a simply-supported beam under a central point load.

## 1.3 Canonical Examples of Pure Bending in Engineering

- The **central segment of a simply-supported beam under a central point load** (between the load and the support reactions, the moment is linear — *not* pure; the pure region is the region directly under the load — but the entire span between the two equal point loads in a **four-point bend test** has constant moment = pure bending).
- The **main span of a two-hinged portal frame** under a uniform moment applied at the joints.
- The **portion of a shaft between two gears**, pulleys, or crank webs transmitting torque to ground — a *close cousin* (torsional pure bending analog).
- The region of a **cantilever beam loaded only at the free end by a couple** (no transverse force, only end moment).

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics Used

| Quantity | Standard Symbol | Typical SI Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Young's Modulus of Steel | $E$ | $2.0 \times 10^{5} \ \text{N/mm}^{2}$ (≈ 200 GPa) |
| Young's Modulus of Concrete | $E$ | $25 \text{ to } 30 \ \text{kN/mm}^{2}$ (≈ 25–30 GPa) |
| Young's Modulus of Aluminium | $E$ | $70 \ \text{kN/mm}^{2}$ (≈ 70 GPa) |
| Standard acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | $9.81 \ \text{m/s}^{2}$ |
| Bending moment | $M$ | $\text{N·mm}$ (preferred) or $\text{kN·m}$ |
| Section modulus (preferred symbol) | $Z$ | $\text{mm}^{3}$ or $\text{m}^{3}$ |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Assumptions of the Euler–Bernoulli Pure Bending Theory

The classical derivation rests on the following **HYPER-parameters** (High-Yield Pedagogical Engineering Rules):

1. **Plane sections remain plane and normal to the deformed neutral axis** after bending. (Hypothesis of **Kirchhoff**.)
2. The **material is homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** (obeys **Hooke's Law** up to the proportional limit).
3. The beam is **initially straight** and has a **uniform cross-section**.
4. The **self-weight is neglected** in the initial derivation (or treated separately as superimposed).
5. **Pure bending** ⇒ no transverse shear strain (γ = 0) ⇒ cross-sections do not warp.
6. **Radius of curvature R is large** compared with the cross-sectional dimension h, so that the **plane sections rotate as rigid bodies** (small-deflection / linear-elastic regime).
7. **No residual stresses** are present before loading.
8. The **neutral axis passes through the centroid** of the cross-section for a homogeneous, symmetric material.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation Outline (Roadmap)

The following 7-step logic is the *exact* KTU-board-expected logical flow:

1. **Step 1 — Geometric Kinematics:** From the assumption that plane sections remain plane, an originally plane cross-section at distance x rotates by a small angle about the neutral axis. The longitudinal strain at a fibre distance y from the neutral axis equals:
$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L} = \frac{(\text{final arc length} - \text{original length})}{\text{original length}} = \frac{(R + y)\theta - R\theta}{R\theta} = \frac{y}{R}$$

2. **Step 2 — Constitutive Law (Hooke's Law):** Applying the uniaxial stress–strain relation:
$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = \frac{E \cdot y}{R}$$

3. **Step 3 — Static Equilibrium (Axial Force = 0):** The resultant of the normal stresses on the cross-section must vanish (no axial load in pure bending):
$$\int_A \sigma \, dA = \frac{E}{R} \int_A y \, dA = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \int_A y \, dA = 0$$
This proves the **neutral axis coincides with the centroidal axis** of the cross-section.

4. **Step 4 — Static Equilibrium (Moment Equilibrium):** The moment of the stress distribution about the neutral axis must equal the applied internal bending moment M:
$$M = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA = \frac{E}{R} \int_A y^2 \, dA = \frac{E \cdot I}{R}$$

5. **Step 5 — Combine Steps 2 and 4:** Equating the two expressions for M, the **flexure formula** emerges:
$$\boxed{\;\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}\;}$$

6. **Step 6 — Solve for Bending Stress:** The most commonly used form in KTU problems:
$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

7. **Step 7 — Section Modulus:** Recognising that $I / y_{\max}$ is a purely *geometric* property of the cross-section, define:
$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{\max}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_{\max}}{Z}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Examination Critical)

> [!IMPORTANT] **THE KTU MASTER BENDING FORMULA**
> $$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}$$
> The *triple equality* — **M/I = σ/y = E/R** — is the single most-tested relationship in Module 3.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Where Used | Unit (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ | **Master Flexure Formula** (core) | mixed |
| 2 | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Bending stress at fibre distance y | N/mm² (MPa) |
| 3 | $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Maximum bending stress (using section modulus) | N/mm² (MPa) |
| 4 | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{\max}}$ | Section modulus of the cross-section | mm³ |
| 5 | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ | Curvature of the bent beam (radius R) | 1/mm |
| 6 | $M = \dfrac{E \cdot I}{R}$ | Bending moment in terms of curvature | N·mm |
| 7 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{y}{R}$ | Longitudinal strain at distance y from NA | dimensionless |
| 8 | $I = \int_A y^2 \, dA$ | Second moment of area about NA | mm⁴ |
| 9 | $\Delta = \int \dfrac{M}{EI}\, dx$ | Deflection (double-integrated form) | mm |
| 10 | $E I \dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = M(x)$ | Governing differential equation of the elastic curve | N·mm² |

> [!NOTE] **Standard Section Modulus Values (Memorize for KTU)**
> - **Rectangle** (b × h): $Z = \dfrac{b \cdot h^{2}}{6}$
> - **Solid Circular** (diameter d): $Z = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^{3}}{32}$
> - **Hollow Circular** (outer D, inner d): $Z = \dfrac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{32 D}$
> - **I-Section** (neutral axis ⟂ web): $Z = \dfrac{I_{xx}}{h/2}$ (from tables)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Design of reinforced-concrete beams, pre-stressed girders, bridge decks, pile caps.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Design of machine shafts, crane jibs, leaf springs, helicopter rotor blades.
- **Aerospace:** Sizing of wing spars and fuselage longerons.
- **Ship-building:** Hull-girder bending under wave loads.
- **Biomedical:** Analysis of bone (femur) as a curved beam under physiological loads.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Bending Equation $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$

### 3.1.1 Geometric Setup (Sign Conventions and Reference Frame)

> [!NOTE] **Coordinate Convention (KTU Standard)**
> - $x$ axis → along the longitudinal centroidal axis of the undeformed beam.
> - $y$ axis → vertical, positive upward.
> - $z$ axis → horizontal, perpendicular to the page (right-handed).
> - Positive bending moment $M$ → produces **concave-up** curvature (sagging beam, tension on bottom).
> - $R$ → radius of curvature of the **neutral surface**, taken as positive.

Consider an elemental segment of length $L = R\theta$ of the originally straight beam (a portion of the circle of radius R after bending). Two cross-sections, initially parallel and separated by arc length $R\theta$ measured along the neutral surface, rotate by a small angle θ about the neutral axis.

### 3.1.2 Step A — Geometry of the Deformed Segment

After bending, the central neutral surface of length $L = R\theta$ deforms into an arc of the *same* length $R\theta$ (the neutral surface has zero strain by definition). A fibre located at a perpendicular distance $y$ above the neutral surface (positive y upward) will deform to an arc length:

$$L' = (R + y) \cdot \theta$$

### 3.1.3 Step B — Longitudinal Strain at Distance y

The original length of this fibre is also $L = R\theta$ (because the beam is originally straight and plane sections are parallel). The change in length of the fibre is therefore:

$$\Delta L = L' - L = (R + y)\theta - R\theta = y \cdot \theta$$

Hence the longitudinal (axial) strain is:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L} = \frac{y \cdot \theta}{R \cdot \theta} = \frac{y}{R}$$

This is the **fundamental kinematic relation** of pure bending.

### 3.1.4 Step C — Hooke's Law (Constitutive Equation)

For a material obeying Hooke's Law within the elastic range:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

Substituting the strain expression:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \frac{y}{R} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}$$

This shows that **bending stress varies linearly** with distance y from the neutral axis — a *direct* consequence of the plane-sections-remain-plane hypothesis.

### 3.1.5 Step D — Equilibrium of Axial Force (Identifies NA as Centroid)

In pure bending, no net axial force acts on the cross-section. The integral of the longitudinal stress over the cross-sectional area must vanish:

$$\int_A \sigma \, dA = 0$$

Substitute $\sigma = (E/R) \cdot y$:

$$\int_A \frac{E}{R} \cdot y \, dA = \frac{E}{R} \int_A y \, dA = 0$$

Since $E/R$ is a non-zero constant for a curved beam in equilibrium:

$$\int_A y \, dA = 0$$

By the definition of the **centroid** of an area, this integral is zero **if and only if the y-axis coincides with the centroidal axis** of the cross-section. Therefore, the **neutral axis passes through the centroid** of the cross-section.

### 3.1.6 Step E — Equilibrium of Bending Moment

The moment of the internal stress distribution about the neutral axis must equal the applied (internal) bending moment M:

$$M = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA$$

Substituting $\sigma = (E/R) \cdot y$:

$$M = \int_A \left(\frac{E}{R}\right) y \cdot y \, dA = \frac{E}{R} \int_A y^{2} \, dA$$

The integral $\int_A y^{2} \, dA$ is, by definition, the **second moment of area** (moment of inertia) of the cross-section about the neutral axis, denoted $I$:

$$I = \int_A y^{2} \, dA$$

Substituting back:

$$M = \frac{E \cdot I}{R} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{M}{I} = \frac{E}{R}$$

### 3.1.7 Step F — Combining to Obtain the Master Flexure Equation

We now have two relations:

$$\frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R} \quad \text{(from Step C)} \qquad \text{and} \qquad \frac{M}{I} = \frac{E}{R} \quad \text{(from Step E)}$$

Equating the right-hand sides yields the **Master Bending Equation**:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}\;}$$

### 3.1.8 Step G — Maximum Bending Stress (Section Modulus Form)

The maximum bending stress occurs at the fibre farthest from the neutral axis, i.e., at $y = y_{\max}$ (or $y = c$ in some textbooks):

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M \cdot y_{\max}}{I} = \frac{M}{I/y_{\max}} = \frac{M}{Z}$$

where the **section modulus** is defined as:

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{\max}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Final Engineering Form (Most-Used in KTU Problems)**
> $$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_{\max}}{Z}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Curvature, Stress, and Strain in Pure Bending

| Property | Symbol | Formula in Pure Bending | Physical Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bending moment | $M$ | Given (constant along span) | Internal resisting couple |
| Radius of curvature | $R$ | $R = \dfrac{E I}{M}$ | How "tightly" the beam is bent |
| Curvature | $\kappa$ | $\kappa = \dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ | Rate of change of slope |
| Longitudinal strain | $\varepsilon$ | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{y}{R}$ | Linear variation with y |
| Bending stress | $\sigma$ | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \, y}{I} = E \, \dfrac{y}{R}$ | Linear variation with y |
| Max bending stress | $\sigma_{\max}$ | $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | At the extreme fibre |
| Section modulus | $Z$ | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{\max}}$ | Cross-section efficiency measure |
| Slope of elastic curve | $\theta$ | $\theta = \dfrac{L}{R} = \dfrac{M L}{E I}$ | Total rotation of the section |
| Deflection (pure region) | $\delta$ | $\delta \approx \dfrac{L^{2}}{8R} = \dfrac{M L^{2}}{8EI}$ | For symmetric pure-bend segment of length L |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Pure Bending

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["APPLIED EXTERNAL COUPLE M0"] --> B["REACTION AT FIXED END M0"]
    B --> C["INTERNAL CONSTANT MOMENT M = M0"]
    C --> D["PLANE SECTION ROTATION ABOUT NA"]
    D --> E1["FIBRE STRAIN eps = y BY R"]
    D --> E2["HOOKE LAW sigma = E times eps"]
    D --> E3["AXIAL EQUILIBRIUM INT sigma dA = 0"]
    D --> E4["MOMENT EQUILIBRIUM INT sigma y dA = M"]
    E1 --> F["MASTER EQUATION M OVER I = sigma OVER y = E OVER R"]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> G["NEUTRAL AXIS = CENTROIDAL AXIS"]
    E4 --> H["MOMENT OF INERTIA I = INT y sq dA"]
    F --> I["MAX STRESS sigma max = M max BY Z"]
    G --> J["ENGINEERING DESIGN OUTPUT"]
    H --> I
    I --> J
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow: How Bending Stress Arises in a Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SA["Sign Convention: +M sags the beam"] --> S1["Top fibre: y is negative, sigma is negative COMPRESSION"]
    SA --> S2["Neutral Axis: y = 0, sigma = 0"]
    SA --> S3["Bottom fibre: y is positive, sigma is positive TENSION"]
    S1 --> ORD["LINEAR STRESS DISTRIBUTION sigma = M y BY I"]
    S2 --> ORD
    S3 --> ORD
    ORD --> Z["Section Modulus Z = I BY y max"]
    Z --> SAFE["Check sigma max is less than allowable stress"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic of the Bent Beam Segment (Conceptual Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Before["ORIGINAL STRAIGHT BEAM"]
        direction LR
        B1["Section AA"]
        B2["Section BB"]
        B3["Section CC"]
    end

    subgraph After["DEFORMED BEAM UNDER PURE BENDING"]
        direction LR
        A1["Section AA rotates by theta"]
        A2["Section BB rotates by theta"]
        A3["Section CC rotates by theta"]
        A1 -. "neutral surface of constant length R theta" .-> A2
        A2 -. "neutral surface of constant length R theta" .-> A3
    end

    Before -- "Apply equal and opposite couples M0 at ends" --> After
    After --> NOTE["All cross-sections rotate by SAME angle theta — uniform curvature 1 over R"]
```

## 4.4 Cross-Section Stress Profile (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TOP["TOPMOST FIBRE y = minus h by 2  COMPRESSION sigma c = minus M y max BY I"]
    TOP1["LINEAR VARIATION Region 1: top quarter"]
    TOP2["LINEAR VARIATION Region 2: upper-mid"]
    NA["NEUTRAL AXIS y = 0   sigma = 0   passes through centroid"]
    BOT1["LINEAR VARIATION Region 3: lower-mid"]
    BOT2["LINEAR VARIATION Region 4: bottom quarter"]
    BOT["BOTTOMMOST FIBRE y = plus h by 2  TENSION sigma t = plus M y max BY I"]
    TOP --> TOP1 --> TOP2 --> NA --> BOT1 --> BOT2 --> BOT
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading the Diagram**
> The arrows depict the **linear** stress distribution across a rectangular cross-section. The neutral axis coincides with the centroidal axis (h/2 from top and bottom for a rectangle). The maximum compressive and tensile stresses are equal in magnitude and occur at the extreme fibres.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE] **KTU Mark Scheme Note**
> Part A questions test the **Remember / Understand** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy. They expect a **crisp 3–4 sentence answer with a neat diagram wherever applicable**. Avoid essays.

### Q1. Define pure bending. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Pure bending refers to the state of flexure in a beam where the **bending moment is constant** along the length of the segment and the **transverse shear force is zero** (V = 0) throughout. A practical example is the portion of a beam between two equal point loads in a four-point bend test. Under this condition, the beam assumes a **constant radius of curvature R** and the cross-sections rotate by the same angle, producing only **linear bending stresses** with no shear stress contribution.

---

### Q2. State the assumptions made in the theory of pure bending. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Euler–Bernoulli theory of pure bending assumes: (i) The material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic**, (ii) **Plane sections remain plane and normal** to the neutral axis after bending, (iii) The beam is **initially straight** with a **uniform cross-section**, (iv) The **radius of curvature is large** compared with the cross-sectional dimension, (v) The beam is subjected only to **bending with no axial or torsional loads**, and (vi) **Self-weight is neglected** in the basic derivation. These assumptions justify the use of the flexure formula $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern**
> Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **7 + 7** (two sub-parts). You are expected to attempt **ONE full question (out of two choices)**. Show all derivations, draw the bending stress diagram, and box the final answer for full credit.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Style]*

**A simply supported beam of span 4 m carries a central point load of 30 kN. The beam is of rectangular cross-section, 120 mm wide and 240 mm deep. The material has $E = 2 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm².**

**(a)** Draw the Shear Force Diagram (SFD) and Bending Moment Diagram (BMD) and identify the region of **pure bending**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Determine the **maximum bending stress** developed in the beam, the **radius of curvature** in the pure-bending region, and the **curvature** $\dfrac{1}{R}$. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — SFD, BMD, and Pure Bending Region:**

Reactions: By symmetry, each support carries $\dfrac{30}{2} = 15 \ \text{kN}$.

Shear force just right of left support A: $V = +15 \ \text{kN}$.
Shear force just left of central load: $V = +15 \ \text{kN}$.
Shear force just right of central load: $V = -15 \ \text{kN}$.
Shear force just left of right support B: $V = -15 \ \text{kN}$.

Bending moment at supports: $M_A = M_B = 0$.
Bending moment at centre: $M_C = 15 \ \text{kN} \times 2 \ \text{m} = 30 \ \text{kN·m}$.

**Pure Bending Region:** A region experiences pure bending only if the shear force is identically zero in that region. From the SFD, the **shear force is non-zero at every point** along the simply supported beam (it is $+15$ kN from A to C and $-15$ kN from C to B). Hence **there is NO region of pure bending** in a simply supported beam with a single central point load. The **maximum bending moment** is at the centre, but V ≠ 0 throughout.

> [!NOTE] **Key Concept:** For a true region of pure bending, the SF must be zero. This happens between the load points of a **four-point bend test** or under a **uniformly distributed load segment** where the load and reactions cancel.

Maximum bending moment:

$$M_{\max} = \frac{W L}{4} = \frac{30 \times 4}{4} = 30 \ \text{kN·m} = 30 \times 10^{6} \ \text{N·mm}$$

(7 marks awarded for correct SFD, BMD, and identification that no pure-bending region exists in this loading.)

---

**Part (b) — Maximum Bending Stress, Radius of Curvature, Curvature:**

Second moment of area for a rectangular section about the neutral axis (b = 120 mm, h = 240 mm):

$$I = \frac{b h^{3}}{12} = \frac{120 \times 240^{3}}{12} = \frac{120 \times 13{,}824{,}000}{12}$$

$$I = 120 \times 1{,}152{,}000 = 138{,}240{,}000 \ \text{mm}^{4} = 1.3824 \times 10^{8} \ \text{mm}^{4}$$

Maximum fibre distance:

$$y_{\max} = \frac{h}{2} = \frac{240}{2} = 120 \ \text{mm}$$

Section modulus:

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{\max}} = \frac{1.3824 \times 10^{8}}{120} = 1.152 \times 10^{6} \ \text{mm}^{3}$$

**Maximum bending stress:**

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_{\max}}{Z} = \frac{30 \times 10^{6}}{1.152 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$\sigma_{\max} = 26.04 \ \text{N/mm}^{2} \ (\text{compressive on top, tensile on bottom})$$

**Radius of curvature (using $E/R = M/I$):**

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{E I} = \frac{30 \times 10^{6}}{(2 \times 10^{5}) \times (1.3824 \times 10^{8})}$$

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{30 \times 10^{6}}{2.7648 \times 10^{13}} = 1.085 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{mm}^{-1}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 9.216 \times 10^{5} \ \text{mm} \approx 921.6 \ \text{m}$$

**Curvature:**

$$\kappa = \frac{1}{R} = 1.085 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{mm}^{-1} = 1.085 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^{-1}$$

**Final boxed answers:**

$$\sigma_{\max} = 26.04 \ \text{N/mm}^{2}, \qquad R = 921.6 \ \text{m}, \qquad \kappa = 1.085 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^{-1}$$

(7 marks awarded for the moment of inertia, section modulus, stress, and curvature computations.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]*

**A cast-iron beam of rectangular cross-section 60 mm wide and 120 mm deep is subjected to a bending moment of 6 kN·m. The permissible stresses are 30 N/mm² in tension and 60 N/mm² in compression. Take $E = 1.5 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm².**

**(a)** Determine the **maximum tensile and compressive stresses** in the beam and check whether the section is safe. (7 marks)

**(b)** If the beam is now bent into a circular arc of radius **R = 20 m**, find the **bending moment** required to produce this curvature. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Stresses and Safety Check:**

Given: b = 60 mm, h = 120 mm, M = 6 kN·m = $6 \times 10^{6}$ N·mm.

$$I = \frac{b h^{3}}{12} = \frac{60 \times 120^{3}}{12} = \frac{60 \times 1{,}728{,}000}{12} = 8{,}640{,}000 \ \text{mm}^{4} = 8.64 \times 10^{6} \ \text{mm}^{4}$$

Distance of extreme fibres from neutral axis:

$$y_{\max} = \frac{h}{2} = 60 \ \text{mm}$$

Maximum bending stresses (at the extreme fibres):

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M \cdot y_{\max}}{I} = \frac{6 \times 10^{6} \times 60}{8.64 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{8}}{8.64 \times 10^{6}} = 41.67 \ \text{N/mm}^{2}$$

So, **tensile stress** (at the bottom fibre for positive M) = $41.67 \ \text{N/mm}^{2}$.
**Compressive stress** (at the top fibre) = $41.67 \ \text{N/mm}^{2}$ (in magnitude).

**Safety Check:**
- Permissible tensile stress: 30 N/mm². **Actual tensile stress: 41.67 N/mm² > 30 N/mm² → UNSAFE in tension.**
- Permissible compressive stress: 60 N/mm². **Actual compressive stress: 41.67 N/mm² < 60 N/mm² → SAFE in compression.**

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B(a)**
> The most common mistake is **failing to identify the asymmetric permissible stresses** of cast iron. Cast iron is **~3 to 4 times stronger in compression than in tension**, so the design is *governed by tension*. Students who mechanically report "safe" without comparing each fibre's stress against the corresponding permissible value **lose 2 to 3 marks**. Always draw the bending stress diagram showing T and C and compare with the allowable limits.

The section is **NOT safe** in tension. The beam must either be **redesigned** (deeper section, or repositioned with the neutral axis shifted toward the tension side — i.e., unsymmetric reinforcement / flitched section) or the applied moment must be **reduced**.

(7 marks awarded for stress calculations, stress diagram, and the explicit safety check.)

---

**Part (b) — Bending Moment for R = 20 m:**

Convert the radius: $R = 20 \ \text{m} = 20{,}000 \ \text{mm}$.

From the flexure formula $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{E}{R}$:

$$M = \frac{E \cdot I}{R} = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{5}) \times (8.64 \times 10^{6})}{20{,}000}$$

$$M = \frac{1.296 \times 10^{12}}{2 \times 10^{4}} = 6.48 \times 10^{7} \ \text{N·mm} = 64.8 \ \text{kN·m}$$

**Final boxed answer:**

$$M = 64.8 \ \text{kN·m}$$

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B(b)**
> A frequent error is **mixing units**: students compute $E$ in N/mm², $I$ in mm⁴, but $R$ in **metres** without converting to mm. The resulting answer is off by a factor of $10^{3}$ or $10^{6}$. Always convert *all* length quantities to the same unit (mm is safest for $E$ in N/mm²) **before** substituting into the formula. Losing 2 marks for unit mismatch is avoidable.

(7 marks awarded for correct substitution and the final answer.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Module 3 — Pure Bending: One-Page Rapid Recap**

- ✅ **Pure bending** = constant $M$ along the segment, **shear force $V = 0$**.
- ✅ Master equation: $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ — memorize the **triple equality**.
- ✅ **Neutral axis = Centroidal axis** for a homogeneous, isotropic beam (proved by $\int y\, dA = 0$).
- ✅ Bending stress varies **linearly** with y; maximum at the **extreme fibre**: $\sigma_{\max} = M/Z$.
- ✅ Section modulus $Z = I / y_{\max}$ is a **purely geometric property** of the cross-section.
- ✅ Curvature: $\kappa = 1/R = M / (EI)$.
- ✅ Strain: $\varepsilon = y / R$ — independent of $E$ (purely geometric).
- ✅ Standard $Z$ values to memorize: **Rectangle** $bh^{2}/6$, **Solid Circle** $\pi d^{3}/32$, **Hollow Circle** $\pi(D^{4}-d^{4})/(32D)$.
- ✅ For a **simply supported beam with a single central load**, there is **NO region of pure bending** — the SF is non-zero everywhere. Pure bending occurs in **four-point bending** or in segments with **V = 0**.
- ✅ **Cast iron** is asymmetric in allowable stress: check **tension and compression separately** when verifying safety.
- ✅ Always check **units** — $E$ in N/mm² requires $I$ in mm⁴ and $M$ in N·mm; if $R$ is in metres, convert to mm first.
- ✅ Plane sections **remain plane and normal** to the neutral axis (Kirchhoff hypothesis).
- ✅ Sign convention: **Positive M** → sagging beam → **tension on bottom fibre**, compression on top fibre.
- ✅ The theory is **valid only in the elastic range** (σ < proportional limit) and for **small deflections** ($R \gg h$).

> [!TIP] **Final KTU Exam Strategy Tip**
> Always begin any bending-stress problem by (1) computing $I$ and $Z$ in the correct units, (2) verifying the sign convention for $M$ and $y$, (3) drawing the bending stress diagram with clearly labelled T and C zones, and (4) boxing the final numerical answer. This four-step structure mirrors the KTU model answer key and **maximises partial credit** even if a final arithmetic error occurs.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flexure Formula for Beams - Assumptions and Limitations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT402 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flexure Formula for Beams - Assumptions and Limitations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Flexure Formula** (also widely known as the **Bending Equation**, **Elastic Flexure Equation**, or **Euler–Bernoulli Beam Equation**) is the fundamental relationship that governs the distribution of normal bending stress in a straight beam subjected to a bending moment. For a beam bent in a plane of symmetry, the bending stress $\sigma$ at any fiber located at a perpendicular distance $y$ from the **Neutral Axis (N.A.)** is given by:

$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

where $M$ is the bending moment at the section, $I$ is the second moment of area (moment of inertia) of the cross-section about the neutral axis, and $y$ is the distance from the neutral axis to the fiber under consideration.

The complete form of the Flexure Formula (often called the "Triple Equality") is expressed as:

$$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}$$

where $E$ is the Young's Modulus of the material and $R$ is the radius of curvature of the bent beam.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Flexure Formula is the *cornerstone* of Module 3. Every subsequent topic (shear stress distribution, combined loading, deflection, columns) draws its roots from this equation. A complete understanding of its **assumptions** and **limitations** is mandatory, as it directly maps to Course Outcomes **CO3** (Analyse bending stresses in beams) and **CO4** (Apply theory of bending to design structural members).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing on a **wooden diving board** at the edge of a swimming pool. As you step forward onto the free end of the board:

- The **top surface** of the board **shortens** (it is being *compressed* by your weight).
- The **bottom surface** of the board **elongates** (it is being *stretched* in tension).
- Somewhere **inside** the thickness of the board, there is a layer that **neither shortens nor stretches** — this is the **Neutral Axis**.

The fibers far from the neutral axis (top and bottom) experience the **maximum change in length**, so they carry the **maximum stress**. The fibers *right at* the neutral axis carry **zero stress**. The Flexure Formula simply quantifies this observation:

> **Stress is directly proportional to (a) bending moment, and (b) distance from the neutral axis — but inversely proportional to the beam's resistance to bending ($I$).**

Think of $I$ as the *stiffness fingerprint* of the cross-section. An **I-section** beam is shaped the way it is precisely because most of its material is placed far from the neutral axis — giving it a very high $I$ and therefore a very high resistance to bending for relatively little material.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics:**
> - Young's Modulus ($E$) for **structural steel** ≈ **200 GPa** ($2 \times 10^{5} \, \text{N/mm}^2$).
> - Young's Modulus ($E$) for **concrete** ≈ **25–30 GPa**.
> - Young's Modulus ($E$) for **aluminum alloys** ≈ **70 GPa**.
> - Allowable bending stress in **mild steel** (working stress design) ≈ **165 MPa**.

---

### 1.3 The Neutral Axis — The Heart of the Formula

The **Neutral Axis (N.A.)** is the line in the cross-section where:
1. The bending stress $\sigma$ is **zero**.
2. The longitudinal fibers experience **zero change in length** (zero strain).
3. It passes through the **centroid** of the cross-section (provided the material is homogeneous and the beam has at least one plane of symmetry).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Neutral Axis vs. Centroidal Axis — Are they the same?**
> For a beam made of a **homogeneous, isotropic material** with a cross-section having at least one axis of symmetry, the **Neutral Axis coincides with the Centroidal Axis**. However, this is *not* universally true for beams made of multiple materials (e.g., composite beams) or for unsymmetric sections in pure bending — where the N.A. must be located by setting $\int y \cdot dA = 0$ on the *transformed* section.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of the Flexure Formula

The derivation of the Flexure Formula rests upon three foundational pillars, each corresponding to a fundamental physical observation:

| Pillar | Physical Meaning | Mathematical Form |
|--------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Pure Bending Geometry** | The bent beam conforms to a circular arc of radius $R$ | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}$ |
| **Strain Compatibility** | Plane sections remain plane — strain is linear with $y$ | $\varepsilon_x = \dfrac{y}{R}$ |
| **Linear Elasticity** | Hooke's Law applies throughout the cross-section | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ |

These three pillars fuse together through the **moment equilibrium equation** to produce the final Flexure Formula.

---

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Derivation

1. **Begin with a straight, unloaded beam segment** of length $L$. All fibers in the segment have length $L$.
2. **Apply a constant bending moment $M$** at the ends of the segment (this is called *pure bending*). The beam deflects into a circular arc.
3. **Identify the neutral axis** as the surface where fiber length does not change. This arc has length $L$.
4. **Compute the length of any fiber** at distance $y$ from the N.A. The new length is $(R - y)\theta$ where $\theta = L/R$ is the angle subtended.
5. **Calculate the strain** in that fiber: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{(R-y)\theta - R\theta}{R\theta} = -\dfrac{y}{R}$.
6. **Apply Hooke's Law**: $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = -\dfrac{E \cdot y}{R}$.
7. **Sum moments** about the neutral axis: $M = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA = \dfrac{E}{R} \int_A y^2 \, dA = \dfrac{E \cdot I}{R}$.
8. **Rearrange** to obtain the canonical triple form.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — MEMORIZE THIS FOR KTU ESE**

| Formula | Expression | Meaning / Use | Standard Units |
|---------|------------|---------------|----------------|
| **Bending Stress** | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Stress at a fiber distance $y$ from N.A. | $\text{N/mm}^2$ or MPa |
| **Section Modulus** | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$ | Resisting moment per unit stress ($M = \sigma \cdot Z$) | $\text{mm}^3$ or $\text{m}^3$ |
| **Bending Moment Capacity** | $M_{allow} = \sigma_{allow} \cdot Z$ | Maximum moment a section can carry | $\text{N}\cdot\text{mm}$ or $\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}$ |
| **Flexure Triple Equality** | $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ | Master relation linking $M$, $\sigma$, $R$ | Mixed |
| **Radius of Curvature** | $R = \dfrac{E \cdot I}{M}$ | Curvature induced by moment $M$ | mm or m |
| **Curvature** | $\kappa = \dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ | Inverse of radius of curvature | $\text{mm}^{-1}$ or $\text{m}^{-1}$ |
| **Strain at Fiber** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{y}{R}$ | Linear strain distribution | Dimensionless |
| **Bending Equation (Differential Form)** | $E \cdot I \cdot \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x)$ | Governing ODE for elastic deflection | $\text{N}\cdot\text{mm}^2$ |
| **Moment of Inertia — Rectangle** | $I_{xx} = \dfrac{b \cdot d^3}{12}$ | About centroidal axis parallel to breadth $b$ | $\text{mm}^4$ |
| **Moment of Inertia — Circle** | $I_{xx} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^4}{64}$ | For solid circular section | $\text{mm}^4$ |
| **Moment of Inertia — Hollow Circle** | $I_{xx} = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{64}$ | For hollow shaft/pipe | $\text{mm}^4$ |
