$$\tau_{max} = \frac{6V}{b d^3} \cdot \frac{d^2}{4} = \frac{3V}{2bd} = \frac{3V}{2A}$$

**Step 7: Average shear stress.**

$$\tau_{avg} = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{V}{bd}$$

**Step 8: Ratio.**

$$\frac{\tau_{max}}{\tau_{avg}} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Rectangular Section:** Shear stress varies **parabolically** across the depth, with $\tau = 0$ at $y = \pm d/2$ (top/bottom) and $\tau_{max} = 1.5\,\tau_{avg}$ at the N.A.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Shear Stress Distribution in a Circular Section

For a solid circular cross-section of diameter $d$:

**Step 1: Geometry.** Consider a fiber at distance $y$ from N.A. The width of the section at this fiber is obtained from the chord length of the circle:

$$b(y) = 2\sqrt{\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2 - y^2}$$

**Step 2: Moment of Inertia.**

$$I = \frac{\pi d^4}{64}$$

**Step 3: First Moment of Area above fiber.**
The area above is a circular segment. Using the standard geometric result:

$$Q = \frac{2}{3}\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^3 \left[\cos^3\theta - \text{(complex segment form)}\right]$$

A more direct derivation via $Q = \int_{y}^{d/2} y \cdot b\,dy$:

$$Q = \int_{y}^{d/2} y \cdot 2\sqrt{\frac{d^2}{4} - y^2}\,dy$$

Using substitution $u = d^2/4 - y^2$, $du = -2y\,dy$:

$$Q = -\int 2\sqrt{u}\,du = -\frac{4}{3}u^{3/2}$$

Evaluating between $y$ and $d/2$:

$$Q = \frac{4}{3}\left[\left(\frac{d^2}{4}\right)^{3/2} - \left(\frac{d^2}{4} - y^2\right)^{3/2}\right] = \frac{d^3}{12}\left[1 - \left(1 - \frac{4y^2}{d^2}\right)^{3/2}\right]$$

**Step 4: Shear stress.**

$$\tau = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b} = \frac{V \cdot Q}{\frac{\pi d^4}{64} \cdot 2\sqrt{\frac{d^2}{4} - y^2}}$$

**Step 5: Maximum at $y = 0$.**

At $y = 0$: $Q = d^3/12$, $b = d$, $I = \pi d^4/64$

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{V \cdot d^3/12}{(\pi d^4/64) \cdot d} = \frac{64 V}{12 \pi d^2} = \frac{16 V}{3 \pi d^2} = \frac{4V}{3A}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Circular Section Result:** $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{4V}{3A} \approx 1.333\,\tau_{avg}$ at the N.A.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Shear Stress Distribution in a Symmetrical I-Section

A symmetrical I-section has **flange** (top and bottom) of width $B$, thickness $t_f$, and **web** of depth $h_w$, thickness $t_w$.

**Critical Levels to Check:**
1. At the Neutral Axis (in the web).
2. At the flange–web junction.

**Step 1: Moment of Inertia (about N.A., symmetry assumed).**

$$I = \frac{B D^3}{12} - \frac{(B - t_w) h_w^3}{12}$$

where $D$ = overall depth, $h_w$ = depth between flanges.

**Step 2: Compute $Q$ at Neutral Axis.**
Area above N.A. consists of top flange + top half of web:

$$Q_{NA} = (B \cdot t_f) \cdot \bar{y}_f + (t_w \cdot h_w/2) \cdot \bar{y}_{web}$$

For a symmetrical section with N.A. at mid-depth:
- $\bar{y}_f = \dfrac{D}{2} - \dfrac{t_f}{2}$
- $\bar{y}_{web} = \dfrac{h_w}{4} = \dfrac{D - 2t_f}{4}$

Hence:

$$Q_{NA} = B \cdot t_f \cdot \left(\frac{D - t_f}{2}\right) + t_w \cdot \frac{(D - 2t_f)}{2} \cdot \frac{(D - 2t_f)}{4}$$

**Step 3: Shear stress at N.A.**

$$\tau_{NA} = \frac{V \cdot Q_{NA}}{I \cdot t_w}$$

This is typically the **maximum** shear stress in an I-section because the width $b = t_w$ is very small.

**Step 4: Compute $Q$ at Flange-Web Junction.**
Area above is just the flange:

$$Q_{f} = B \cdot t_f \cdot \left(\frac{D - t_f}{2}\right)$$

**Step 5: Shear stress at flange-web junction.**

$$\tau_{f} = \frac{V \cdot B \cdot t_f \cdot (D - t_f)/2}{I \cdot B} = \frac{V \cdot t_f (D - t_f)}{2I}$$

This shear stress is **lower** than $\tau_{NA}$ in most practical I-sections (because the web concentrates shear stress).

**Step 6: Shear stress in the flange (just above the junction).**
Note that the *width* changes from $t_w$ to $B$ at the junction. There is a **discontinuity** in $\tau$ (sharp drop) at the junction — this is a key I-section characteristic!

> [!WARNING]
> **I-Section Shear Stress Jump:** At the flange-web junction, shear stress suddenly drops because $b$ jumps from $t_w$ (web) to $B$ (flange). Both stresses are valid; they represent the respective fibers. Always compute both!

---

### 3.5 Shear Stress in T-Section and Channel Section

For **T-section**, the neutral axis is **NOT** at mid-depth. Centroid must first be located (taking moment of areas about top or bottom edge). Then:

$$I = I_{flange} + I_{web} \text{ about N.A. (using parallel axis theorem)}$$

The maximum shear stress still occurs at the N.A., but the value of $Q$ depends on which side of N.A. is larger (the side with larger area × distance contribution).

For **Channel Section**, the procedure is identical, but the shear center is offset from the centroid — important for beams loaded in a plane not containing the centroid.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Shear Stress Distribution

| **Cross-Section Type** | **Variation Law** | **$\tau_{max}$ Position** | **$\tau_{max} / \tau_{avg}$ Ratio** | **Key Formula** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rectangle | Parabolic | Neutral Axis | $1.5$ | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{3V}{2A}$ |
| Solid Circle | Parabolic-like | Neutral Axis | $\dfrac{4}{3} \approx 1.333$ | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{4V}{3A}$ |
| Hollow Circle | Parabolic-like | Neutral Axis | $\dfrac{4}{3}$ (approx, for thin tube → 2) | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{4V}{3A}\left(\dfrac{R_o^2 + R_i^2}{R_o^2 - R_i^2}\right)$ |
| I-Section | Discontinuous | N.A. in web | Often 5–10× $\tau_{avg}$ | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{VQ_{NA}}{I t_w}$ |
| T-Section | Parabolic segments | N.A. (computed) | Variable (typically 1.5–2.5) | Apply formula carefully |
| Triangular | Parabolic | At $h/2$ from base | $1.5$ (at centroidal level, not N.A.) | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{3V}{2A}$ (when N.A. = base) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Conceptual Roadmap of Shear Stress Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Beam Subjected to Transverse Load V] --> B[Consider Two Adjacent Cross Sections at dx apart]
    B --> C[Bending Moments M and M+dM cause differential fiber stress]
    C --> D[Fibers above chosen level try to slide longitudinally]
    D --> E[Horizontal Force Imbalance dFH integrated over area above fiber]
    E --> F[Apply Equilibrium: tau x b x dx = dM/I x Q]
    F --> G[Substitute dM/dx = V from Euler Bernoulli Relation]
    G --> H[Master Formula: tau = VQ / I b]
    H --> I[Apply to Specific Cross Section Geometry]
    I --> J[Rectangular Section]
    I --> K[Circular Section]
    I --> L[I Section Symmetrical]
    I --> M[T Section Unsymmetrical]
    J --> J1[tau parabolic, tau max = 1.5 V/A at NA]
    K --> K1[tau quasi parabolic, tau max = 4V/3A at NA]
    L --> L1[tau max in web at NA, jump at flange web junction]
    M --> M1[NA shifted from centroid, tau max at NA after locating centroid]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Shear Stress Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input[Given Beam Section and Shear Force V] --> StepA[Step 1: Locate Centroid and NA]
    StepA --> StepB[Step 2: Compute Second Moment of Area I about NA]
    StepB --> StepC[Step 3: Identify Critical Fiber Levels y1 y2 y3]
    StepC --> StepD[Step 4: Compute First Moment Q for each fiber]
    StepD --> StepE[Step 5: Note width b at each fiber]
    StepE --> StepF[Step 6: Apply tau = VQ / I b]
    StepF --> StepG[Step 7: Compare and Identify tau max]
    StepG --> Output[Result: Shear Stress Distribution Curve and Critical Value]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Description of Parabolic Distribution (Rectangular Section)

Imagine a rectangular cross-section standing vertically (depth $d$, width $b$). The shear stress distribution looks like:

- At top fiber ($y = +d/2$): $\tau = 0$
- At $y = +d/4$: $\tau = \tau_{max} \cdot \left[1 - (1/2)^2\right] = 0.75\,\tau_{max}$
- At Neutral Axis ($y = 0$): $\tau = \tau_{max} = 1.5\,V/A$
- At $y = -d/4$: $\tau = 0.75\,\tau_{max}$
- At bottom fiber ($y = -d/2$): $\tau = 0$

The curve is a **parabola** opening sideways, symmetric about the N.A., with vertex at $(0, \tau_{max})$ and zero at $\pm d/2$.

### 4.4 Schematic Description of I-Section Distribution

The shear stress curve for an I-section has a **dramatic spike** in the web region:

- In the **flange**: $\tau$ is small and varies parabolically (because $b = B$ is large, dividing by large $b$ reduces $\tau$).
- At the **flange–web junction**: There is a sharp **downward jump** in $\tau$ as width changes from $B$ (in flange) to $t_w$ (in web).
- In the **web**: $\tau$ rises parabolically to its maximum at the N.A., then falls symmetrically.
- The **maximum shear stress** is therefore almost always in the **web at the N.A.**, not in the flange.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** I-beams are deliberately designed with a thin web and wide flanges because bending resistance requires material far from the N.A. (high $I$), but shear is efficiently carried by the web. This optimizes weight and material usage.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` State and explain the assumptions made in deriving the shear stress formula $\tau = VQ/(Ib)$.**

**Model Answer:**
The shear stress formula $\tau = VQ/(Ib)$ is derived under the following assumptions:
1. The beam material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's Law**.
2. Bending stress varies **linearly** across the depth ($\sigma = My/I$).
3. The shear stress is **uniformly distributed** across the width $b$ at the level considered.
4. **Plane sections remain plane** (neglecting warping due to shear).
5. The shear force $V$ is constant over the small element of length $dx$.

These assumptions give rise to a clean engineering formula widely used in KTU board examinations.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` For a rectangular beam cross-section of width $b$ and depth $d$, derive the ratio of maximum shear stress to average shear stress.**

**Model Answer:**
Average shear stress:
$$\tau_{avg} = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{V}{bd}$$

Maximum shear stress occurs at the N.A. ($y = 0$) for a rectangular section:
$$\tau_{max} = \frac{3V}{2bd}$$

Hence the ratio is:
$$\frac{\tau_{max}}{\tau_{avg}} = \frac{3V/2bd}{V/bd} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5$$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**Q3. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` Question A (14 Marks):**

> A simply supported beam of span $4\,\text{m}$ carries a uniformly distributed load of $30\,\text{kN/m}$ over the entire span. The cross-section of the beam is rectangular, $200\,\text{mm}$ wide and $400\,\text{mm}$ deep. Determine:
> **(a)** The maximum shear stress in the beam. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** The shear stress at a fiber $50\,\text{mm}$ above the Neutral Axis and sketch the shear stress distribution. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** Span $L = 4\,\text{m}$, UDL $w = 30\,\text{kN/m}$, width $b = 200\,\text{mm}$, depth $d = 400\,\text{mm}$.

**Reactions:** By symmetry, $R_A = R_B = wL/2 = 30 \times 4/2 = 60\,\text{kN}$.

**Maximum Shear Force:** $V_{max} = 60\,\text{kN} = 60 \times 10^3\,\text{N}$ (at the supports).

**(a) Maximum Shear Stress:**

For a rectangular section, $\tau_{max}$ occurs at the N.A.:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{3V}{2A} = \frac{3 \times 60 \times 10^3}{2 \times 200 \times 400}$$

$$A = 200 \times 400 = 80{,}000\,\text{mm}^2$$

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{180 \times 10^3}{160{,}000} = 1.125\,\text{N/mm}^2 = 1.125\,\text{MPa}$$

**(b) Shear Stress at $y = +50\,\text{mm}$ above N.A.:**

Using the parabolic distribution formula:

$$\tau(y) = \frac{6V}{bd^3}\left(\frac{d^2}{4} - y^2\right)$$

Substituting $y = +50\,\text{mm}$, $d = 400\,\text{mm}$, $b = 200\,\text{mm}$, $V = 60 \times 10^3\,\text{N}$:

$$\tau(50) = \frac{6 \times 60 \times 10^3}{200 \times (400)^3}\left(\frac{(400)^2}{4} - (50)^2\right)$$

$$\tau(50) = \frac{360 \times 10^3}{200 \times 64 \times 10^6}\left(40{,}000 - 2{,}500\right)$$

$$\tau(50) = \frac{360 \times 10^3}{12.8 \times 10^9} \times 37{,}500$$

$$\tau(50) = \frac{13.5 \times 10^9}{12.8 \times 10^9} \times 10^{-3}\,\text{MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\tau(50) = 1.055\,\text{MPa (approximately)}}$$

**Sketch:** Parabolic curve from $\tau = 0$ at top to $\tau_{max} = 1.125\,\text{MPa}$ at N.A., back to $\tau = 0$ at bottom.

---

**Q4. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative:**

> An I-section beam has the following dimensions: Overall depth $D = 500\,\text{mm}$, flange width $B = 250\,\text{mm}$, flange thickness $t_f = 25\,\text{mm}$, and web thickness $t_w = 25\,\text{mm}$. A shear force of $120\,\text{kN}$ acts on the section. Determine:
> **(a)** The moment of inertia of the section about the Neutral Axis. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** The maximum shear stress and shear stress at the flange-web junction. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $D = 500\,\text{mm}$, $B = 250\,\text{mm}$, $t_f = 25\,\text{mm}$, $t_w = 25\,\text{mm}$, $V = 120\,\text{kN}$.

For a symmetrical I-section, $I$ is computed by subtracting the "missing rectangles" from the outer rectangle:

$$I = \frac{B D^3}{12} - \frac{(B - t_w)(D - 2t_f)^3}{12}$$

$$I = \frac{250 \times (500)^3}{12} - \frac{(250 - 25)(500 - 50)^3}{12}$$

$$I = \frac{250 \times 1.25 \times 10^8}{12} - \frac{225 \times 9.11 \times 10^7}{12}$$

Wait — let me recompute carefully.

$$(500)^3 = 1.25 \times 10^8\,\text{mm}^3$$

$$(500 - 50)^3 = (450)^3 = 91.125 \times 10^6 = 9.1125 \times 10^7\,\text{mm}^3$$

$$I = \frac{250 \times 1.25 \times 10^8}{12} - \frac{225 \times 9.1125 \times 10^7}{12}$$

$$I = \frac{3.125 \times 10^{10}}{12} - \frac{2.0503 \times 10^{10}}{12}$$

$$I = \frac{1.0747 \times 10^{10}}{12} = 8.956 \times 10^8\,\text{mm}^4$$

**(b) Maximum Shear Stress at N.A.:**

$Q_{NA}$ = First moment of area above N.A. (top flange + top half of web):

$$Q_{flange} = (B \cdot t_f) \cdot \bar{y}_f = 250 \times 25 \times \left(\frac{500}{2} - \frac{25}{2}\right) = 6250 \times 237.5 = 1.484 \times 10^6\,\text{mm}^3$$

$$Q_{half-web} = (t_w \cdot h_w/2) \cdot \bar{y}_{web} = 25 \times 225 \times \frac{225}{2} = 5625 \times 112.5 = 6.328 \times 10^5\,\text{mm}^3$$

$$Q_{NA} = 1.484 \times 10^6 + 6.328 \times 10^5 = 2.117 \times 10^6\,\text{mm}^3$$

At the N.A., $b = t_w = 25\,\text{mm}$:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{V Q_{NA}}{I \cdot t_w} = \frac{120 \times 10^3 \times 2.117 \times 10^6}{8.956 \times 10^8 \times 25}$$

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{2.540 \times 10^{11}}{2.239 \times 10^{10}} = 11.34\,\text{MPa}$$

**Shear Stress at Flange-Web Junction:**

$Q_{f} = $ first moment of flange area only:
$$Q_f = 1.484 \times 10^6\,\text{mm}^3$$

At the flange-web junction, $b = B = 250\,\text{mm}$ (since we are considering the fiber just above the junction, where width = flange width):

$$\tau_f = \frac{V \cdot Q_f}{I \cdot B} = \frac{120 \times 10^3 \times 1.484 \times 10^6}{8.956 \times 10^8 \times 250}$$

$$\tau_f = \frac{1.781 \times 10^{11}}{2.239 \times 10^{11}} = 0.795\,\text{MPa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion error:** Many students mix cm and mm, leading to incorrect answers by a factor of 1000. Always state units in *every step*.
> 2. **Forgot to add area of half-web to flange area:** $Q_{NA}$ is *not* just the flange's $Q$ — include the top half of the web too.
> 3. **Width confusion at flange-web junction:** The width on the *flange side* of the junction is $B$; on the *web side* it is $t_w$. Use the appropriate width for the fiber being analyzed.
> 4. **Symmetry assumption:** Don't assume N.A. is at mid-depth unless the section is symmetric. For T-sections, compute centroid first!
> 5. **Final answer units:** Always express shear stress in **MPa (N/mm²)** for consistency.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Master Formula:** $\tau = \dfrac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}$ — must be memorized with all four variable meanings.
- **$Q$ calculation:** First Moment of Area of the portion of the cross-section *lying beyond the fiber level* about the N.A.
- **Rectangular Section:** Parabolic distribution; $\tau_{max} = 1.5\,V/A$ at N.A.; $\tau = 0$ at top and bottom.
- **Circular Section:** $\tau_{max} = (4/3)\,V/A$ at N.A.; distribution is parabolic-like.
- **I-Section:** $\tau_{max}$ occurs in the **web** at the **N.A.**, NOT in the flange. There is a **jump** in $\tau$ at the flange-web junction due to sudden change in width $b$.
- **T-Section:** First locate centroid (N.A.) before computing $I$ and $Q$. The deeper stem side usually contributes more to $Q_{max}$.
- **Units:** Always use **internally consistent units** (N, mm → MPa). Never mix.
- **Direction of shear stress:** Acts parallel to the shear force on the cross-section (transverse direction). On horizontal planes, by complementary shear, it acts longitudinally.
- **Quick estimate:** For a solid rectangular timber beam, $\tau_{max} \approx 1.5\,\tau_{avg}$.
- **Design implication:** To minimize weight, use sections with most material far from the N.A. (high $I$); shear is then handled by thin webs — but **check web shear**!
- **Assumptions to state in derivation-based answers:** Homogeneous, isotropic, linear-elastic material; plane sections remain plane; $V$ constant over $dx$.
- **Common 3-mark question:** "State the assumptions of shear stress formula" — must list at least 4–5.
- **Common 14-mark question pattern:** Given shear force + cross-section geometry → compute $I$, $Q$ at multiple levels, $\tau_{max}$, sketch distribution.
- **Watch out for:** Hollow sections (use $I_{hollow} = I_{outer} - I_{inner}$), composite sections (transform widths using modular ratio — typically outside Module 3 scope but good to know).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Derivation and Problems for Rectangular Section Only - Assumptions and Limitations. Deflection of Beams: Moment-Curvature Relation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCSFT402 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Derivation and Problems for Rectangular Section Only - Assumptions and Limitations. Deflection of Beams: Moment-Curvature Relation # 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Shear Stress Distribution Across a Beam Cross-Section (Rectangular Section) ### 🔹 Formal Academic Definition When a beam is subjected to transverse loading, the **internal shear force (V)** is not uniformly distributed across the cross-section. The intensity of shear stress ($\tau$) at any fibre located at a distance **y** from the **neutral axis (N.A.)** is given by the classical flexure formula for shear: $$\tau = \frac{V \cdot A \bar{y}}{I \cdot b}$$ Where: - $\tau$ = Shear stress at the fibre (N/mm² or MPa) - $V$ = Transverse shear force at the section (N) - $A\bar{y}$ = First moment of area of the portion of cross-section beyond the fibre about the Neutral Axis (mm³) - $I$ = Second moment of area of the entire cross-section about the N.A. (mm⁴) - $b$ = Width of the section at the fibre level (mm) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** The derivation is restricted to a **Rectangular Cross-Section only**. The formula must be derived from the equilibrium of an elemental block of the beam using complementary shear (a direct consequence of the **principle of complementary shear stresses**, credited to **Augustin-Louis Cauchy**). ### 🔹 Real-World Analogy — "The Sliced Bread Stack" 🍞 Imagine a thick stack of bread slices glued together. When you push the top slice horizontally (representing **shear force V**), every glue layer experiences a shear. The **glue at the Neutral Axis** (middle layer) has the **largest area of bread above it** that needs to be dragged along, so the shear stress there is **maximum**. The glue at the **top and bottom surfaces** has nothing to drag, so the shear stress drops to **zero**. This parabolic variation is exactly what we see in a rectangular beam — a **parabolic shear stress distribution** with $\tau_{max}$ at the Neutral Axis and zero at the free surfaces. --- ## 1.2 Moment-Curvature Relation (Foundation of Beam Deflection) ### 🔹 Formal Academic Definition The **Moment-Curvature Equation** establishes the fundamental geometric relationship between the internal bending moment ($M$) and the curvature ($\frac{1}{R}$) of the deflected neutral axis of a beam: $$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}$$ The simplified engineering form used universally for deflection analysis is: $$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{EI}$$ This equation is the **backbone of the entire deflection theory** (double integration, Macaulay's method, moment-area method, conjugate beam method — all of them derive from this single relation). ### 🔹 Real-World Analogy — "The Flexible Ruler Bend" 📏 Take a long flexible plastic ruler and clamp one end to a table edge. Press down at the free end. The ruler **bends into a curve**. The more you press (more moment M), the **tighter the curve** (smaller radius R, larger curvature 1/R). If the ruler is made of stiffer material (higher E) or is thicker (larger I), it resists bending — the curve becomes gentler (larger R, smaller 1/R). This is exactly $M = EI \cdot (1/R)$ — the **flexural rigidity** $EI$ governs how much the beam "fights" the curvature. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in This Module:** > - **Young's Modulus (E)** for steel ≈ **200 GPa (2 × 10⁵ N/mm²)** > - **Young's Modulus (E)** for concrete ≈ **25–30 GPa** > - **Standard Metric:** SI units throughout — Newtons (N), mm, MPa (N/mm²) > - **Maximum shear stress for rectangular section** = **1.5 × Average shear stress** (a critical board-exam constant) # 📘 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Assumptions Underlying the Shear Stress Derivation (Rectangular Section) The classical derivation of shear stress distribution in a rectangular beam rests on **five foundational assumptions**. These MUST be written verbatim in the answer script to score full marks on the 14-mark question. ### 🔸 Assumption Set > [!IMPORTANT] > **Five Mandatory Assumptions (Board-Exam Verbatim Required):** 1. **The material is homogeneous and isotropic** — mechanical properties (E, G) are uniform in all directions at every point. 2. **The beam is initially straight and unloaded** — no pre-stress, residual stresses, or initial curvature exists. 3. **The cross-section is uniform (prismatic beam)** — same geometry along the entire length. 4. **Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis after bending** — this is **Euler-Bernoulli beam theory** (shear deformation neglected). 5. **The beam material obeys Hooke's Law** — stress is linearly proportional to strain within the elastic limit (σ = E·ε). ### 🔸 Additional Implicit Assumptions - **Stress concentration at points of load application is neglected.** - **Self-weight of the beam is either negligible or uniformly distributed.** - **Width 'b' is small compared to depth 'd'** (so plane sections assumption holds). --- ## 2.2 Limitations of the Shear Stress Formula ($\tau = \frac{VQ}{Ib}$) > [!WARNING] > **Limitations — Frequently Asked in KTU Board Exams:** | # | Limitation | Engineering Implication | |---|------------|-------------------------| | 1 | Applicable only in the **elastic range** | Fails for plastic design or beyond yield | | 2 | Neglects **local stress concentrations** near holes, notches, or sudden load points | Gives inaccurate results at discontinuities | | 3 | Assumes **plane sections remain plane** | Inaccurate for **deep beams** (L/d < 5) where shear deformation dominates (Timoshenko beam theory needed) | | 4 | Does not account for **transverse normal stress** σ_y from concentrated loads | Causes error near supports and load points (St. Venant's principle violated) | | 5 | For **non-rectangular sections**, formula needs modification | For I, T, channel sections, b varies with y | | 6 | Neglects **warping of cross-sections** (especially in thin-walled open sections) | Inaccurate for thin-walled beams | --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Module 3 — Cheat Sheet) ### 🔸 A. Shear Stress in Beams | Formula | Expression | Meaning | |---------|------------|---------| | **General Shear Formula** | $\tau = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}$ | Q = First moment of area beyond the fibre | | **Average Shear Stress** | $\tau_{avg} = \frac{V}{A}$ | Uniform distribution assumption | | **Rectangular: τ at distance y** | $\tau = \frac{6V}{bd^{3}}\left(\frac{d^{2}}{4} - y^{2}\right)$ | Parabolic distribution | | **Rectangular: Max τ (at N.A., y = 0)** | $\tau_{max} = \frac{3V}{2A} = 1.5 \cdot \tau_{avg}$ | Always at neutral axis | | **Rectangular: τ at top/bottom (y = d/2)** | $\tau = 0$ | Free surface condition | ### 🔸 B. Moment-Curvature & Deflection | Formula | Expression | Application | |---------|------------|-------------| | **Flexure Formula** | $\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}$ | Stress–moment–curvature link | | **Curvature Equation** | $\frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{EI}$ | Master equation for deflection | | **Differential Equation** | $EI \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = \pm M(x)$ | Double integration method | | **Macaulay Bracket Function** | $\langle x - a \rangle^{n}$ | For discontinuous loading (UDL, point loads) | | **Slope–Deflection Relation** | $EI \frac{dy}{dx} = \theta(x)$ | $\theta$ = slope of beam | ### 🔸 C. Critical Engineering Constants > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Maximum shear stress in rectangular section:** $\tau_{max} = \frac{3V}{2bd}$ — occurs at Neutral Axis > - **Zero shear stress:** at extreme fibres (top and bottom surfaces) > - **Shape factor (max/avg shear):** $\tau_{max}/\tau_{avg} = 1.5$ (rectangular only) --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility ### 🔸 Shear Stress Distribution - **Design of timber beams, RCC beams, steel gantry girders** — shear governs web design. - **Punching shear** in slab-column connections uses the same $VQ/Ib$ principle. - **Failure mode identification:** Brittle shear failure (diagonal cracking) in deep beams versus flexural failure (ductile cracking at bottom). ### 🔸 Moment-Curvature Relation - **Foundation of structural analysis software** (STAAD.Pro, ETABS, SAP2000) — all deflection calculations trace back to $1/R = M/EI$. - **Bridge design:** Camber calculations use $EI \cdot (d²y/dx²) = M$ to pre-curve beams against expected deflection. - **Mechanical spring design:** Helical and leaf springs rely on curvature analysis. # 📘 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 DERIVATION: Shear Stress Distribution Across a Rectangular Beam ### 🔸 Step 1 — Consider an Elemental Slice of the Beam Consider a beam of **breadth b** and **depth d**. Take two cross-sections **m₁m₂** and **n₁n₂** separated by an infinitesimal distance **dx**. The shear force on these sections is $V$ (assuming uniform load between them, the shear differs by $dV$). Let **PQRS** be an elemental block of length **dx**, depth **h** (measured from the top extreme fibre to the fibre where shear stress is required), located at distance **y** from the Neutral Axis. ### 🔸 Step 2 — Bending Stresses on the Two Vertical Faces By the flexure formula $\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$, the bending (normal) stresses on the left face **PQ** and right face **RS** are: $$\sigma_1 = \frac{M \cdot y}{I} \quad \text{(at section m₁m₂)}$$ $$\sigma_2 = \frac{(M + dM) \cdot y}{I} \quad \text{(at section n₁n₂)}$$ ### 🔸 Step 3 — Horizontal Force on Each Vertical Face Area of each vertical face = $b \cdot dy$ (per unit strip) integrated over the depth h. For a strip at height y: - Force on **PQ** = $\sigma_1 \cdot b \cdot dy = \frac{M \cdot y}{I} \cdot b \cdot dy$ - Force on **RS** = $\sigma_2 \cdot b \cdot dy = \frac{(M+dM) \cdot y}{I} \cdot b \cdot dy$ Net horizontal force on the two faces (RS − PQ): $$dF_H = \frac{dM \cdot y \cdot b \cdot dy}{I}$$ ### 🔸 Step 4 — Integrate Over the Area Above the Fibre To get the total horizontal force on the top portion (from extreme top fibre to the level where shear stress is being computed at distance y₁ from N.A.), integrate: $$F_H = \int_{y_1}^{d/2} \frac{dM \cdot b}{I} \cdot y \, dy$$ $$\implies F_H = \frac{dM \cdot b}{I} \cdot \left[\frac{y^{2}}{2}\right]_{y_1}^{d/2} = \frac{dM \cdot b}{2I}\left[\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2} - y_1^{2}\right]$$ ### 🔸 Step 5 — Apply First Moment of Area (Q) For a rectangular section, the **first moment of area Q** of the portion above the fibre (depth = $\frac{d}{2} - y_1$, width = b) about the Neutral Axis is: $$Q = A \bar{y} = \left[b\left(\frac{d}{2} - y_1\right)\right] \cdot \left[\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{d}{2} + y_1\right)\right]$$ $$Q = \frac{b}{2}\left[\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2} - y_1^{2}\right]$$ Comparing with Step 4: $F_H = \frac{dM}{I} \cdot Q$ ### 🔸 Step 6 — Equilibrium in Horizontal Direction The horizontal force $F_H$ on the element must be balanced by the shear force on the **horizontal plane** at the level of interest. Let $\tau$ be the shear stress on this plane, and the area of the horizontal face is $b \cdot dx$: $$\tau \cdot (b \cdot dx) = F_H = \frac{dM}{I} \cdot Q$$ ### 🔸 Step 7 — Introduce Shear Force Relation From equilibrium of an infinitesimal beam element: $\frac{dM}{dx} = V$ Therefore: $dM = V \cdot dx$, and substituting: $$\tau \cdot b \cdot dx = \frac{V \cdot dx}{I} \cdot Q$$ $$\boxed{\tau = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}}$$ > This is the **general shear stress formula** (sometimes called the **Zhuravskii–Collignon equation**). ### 🔸 Step 8 — Specialize to Rectangular Section For a rectangular section of depth $d$ at a fibre distance $y$ from N.A.: $$\tau = \frac{V}{I \cdot b} \cdot \frac{b}{2}\left[\frac{d^{2}}{4} - y^{2}\right] = \frac{V}{2I}\left[\frac{d^{2}}{4} - y^{2}\right]$$ Substituting $I = \frac{bd^{3}}{12}$: $$\tau = \frac{6V}{bd^{3}}\left[\frac{d^{2}}{4} - y^{2}\right]$$ $$\boxed{\tau_{rect} = \frac{6V}{bd^{3}}\left(\frac{d^{2}}{4} - y^{2}\right)}$$ ### 🔸 Step 9 — Maximum Shear Stress (at Neutral Axis, y = 0) $$\tau_{max} = \frac{6V}{bd^{3}} \cdot \frac{d^{2}}{4} = \frac{6V}{4bd} = \frac{3V}{2bd}$$ Since Area $A = bd$, average shear stress is $\tau_{avg} = \frac{V}{bd}$: $$\boxed{\tau_{max} = 1.5 \cdot \tau_{avg}}$$ ### 🔸 Step 10 — Shear Stress at Top/Bottom Fibre (y = ±d/2) $$\tau = \frac{6V}{bd^{3}}\left[\frac{d^{2}}{4} - \frac{d^{2}}{4}\right] = 0$$ ✅ **Verification:** Shear stress is **zero** at the free surfaces (matches the bread-stack analogy). --- ## 3.2 DERIVATION: Moment-Curvature Relation ($M = EI/R$) ### 🔸 Step 1 — Pure Bending Assumption Consider a beam segment of length **dx** subjected to pure bending moment $M$. The neutral axis (N.A.) is along the centroidal axis. ### 🔸 Step 2 — Geometry of Deformation After bending, the originally straight segment subtends an angle $d\theta$ at the centre of curvature O, located at perpendicular distance **R** (radius of curvature) from the N.A. The deformed length of the N.A. fibre remains $dx$ (no strain at N.A.). So: $$dx = R \cdot d\theta \quad \implies \quad d\theta = \frac{dx}{R}$$ ### 🔸 Step 3 — Strain at Distance y from N.A. A fibre at distance $y$ above the N.A. has an original length $dx$. After deformation, its length becomes $(R + y) \cdot d\theta$. Change in length: $(R + y)d\theta - dx = (R + y)d\theta - R \cdot d\theta = y \cdot d\theta$ Therefore, longitudinal strain: $$\varepsilon = \frac{\text{change in length}}{\text{original length}} = \frac{y \cdot d\theta}{dx} = \frac{y \cdot dx / R}{dx}$$ $$\boxed{\varepsilon = \frac{y}{R}}$$ ### 🔸 Step 4 — Apply Hooke's Law Within the elastic limit, stress and strain are related by Young's Modulus E: $$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = E \cdot \frac{y}{R}$$ ### 🔸 Step 5 — Equate with Flexure Formula From the **theory of simple bending** (already established): $\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$ Equating both expressions for σ: $$\frac{M \cdot y}{I} = E \cdot \frac{y}{R}$$ The $y$ cancels (non-trivial fibres): $$\boxed{\frac{M}{I} = \frac{E}{R}}$$ Or, equivalently (the form most used in deflection analysis): $$\boxed{\frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{EI}}$$ ### 🔸 Step 6 — Express 1/R in Cartesian Form For small deflections, the slope $\frac{dy}{dx}$ is small, so: $$\frac{1}{R} \approx \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}}$$ Therefore, the **governing differential equation** for beam deflection: $$\boxed{EI \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = M(x)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sign Convention:** The negative sign is introduced in some textbooks as $EI \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = -M(x)$ depending on whether downward deflection is taken as positive. The KTU convention typically uses **$EI \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = M(x)$** with appropriate sign of M. --- ## 3.3 SOLVED PROBLEM: Rectangular Section Shear Stress **Problem:** A simply supported rectangular beam of span 4 m, breadth b = 120 mm, depth d = 240 mm carries a uniformly distributed load of 30 kN/m over its entire span. Find the maximum shear stress and shear stress at a fibre 60 mm above the neutral axis. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Reactions and Shear Force** Total load $W = 30 \times 4 = 120$ kN. Reactions at each support $R_A = R_B = 60$ kN. Maximum shear force at supports: $V_{max} = 60$ kN $= 60 \times 10^{3}$ N. **Step 2 — Cross-sectional properties** $$A = b \cdot d = 120 \times 240 = 28800 \text{ mm}^{2}$$ $$I = \frac{bd^{3}}{12} = \frac{120 \times 240^{3}}{12} = 1.3824 \times 10^{8} \text{ mm}^{4}$$ **Step 3 — Maximum shear stress (at N.A., y = 0)** $$\tau_{max} = \frac{3V}{2A} = \frac{3 \times 60 \times 10^{3}}{2 \times 28800} = 3.125 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$ **Step 4 — Shear stress at y = 60 mm** $$\tau = \frac{6V}{bd^{3}}\left(\frac{d^{2}}{4} - y^{2}\right) = \frac{6 \times 60 \times 10^{3}}{120 \times 240^{3}}\left(\frac{240^{2}}{4} - 60^{2}\right)$$ $$\tau = \frac{360000}{120 \times 1.3824 \times 10^{8}}(14400 - 3600) = 2.17 \times 10^{-5} \times 10800$$ $$\boxed{\tau_{y=60} = 0.2344 \text{ N/mm}^{2}}$$ ✅ **Sanity Check:** Stress decreases from 3.125 MPa at N.A. to 0.2344 MPa at y=60 mm — confirms parabolic distribution. # 📘 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Schematic Diagram — Shear Stress Distribution Across Rectangular Beam The physical beam geometry and the resulting parabolic shear stress distribution are mapped below using a **block-level functional flow**: ```mermaid flowchart TD A["RECTANGULAR BEAM CROSS SECTION
Breadth b = 120 mm, Depth d = 240 mm"] --> B["Top extreme fibre y = +d by 2"] A --> C["Neutral Axis y = 0"] A --> D["Bottom extreme fibre y = -d by 2"] B --> E["Shear stress tau = 0 N per mm squared"] C --> F["Shear stress tau max = 3V by 2bd"] D --> E F --> G["PARABOLIC VARIATION
tau = 6V by bd cubed times d squared by 4 minus y squared"] E --> G G --> H["AREA UNDER CURVE equals SHEAR FORCE V"] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Moment-Curvature Derivation Logic Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["STEP 1 Pure Bending Geometry
Original length dx deforms to R times d theta"] --> S2["STEP 2 Strain Calculation
epsilon equals y by R"] S2 --> S3["STEP 3 Hookes Law
sigma equals E epsilon equals E y by R"] S3 --> S4["STEP 4 Flexure Formula
sigma equals M y by I"] S4 --> S5["STEP 5 Equate and Cancel y
M by I equals E by R"] S5 --> S6["MASTER EQUATION
1 by R equals M by EI"] S6 --> S7["DEFLECTION GOVERNING ODE
EI d2y by dx2 equals M of x"] S6 --> S8["STRESS AT ANY FIBRE
sigma equals M y by I"] S6 --> S9["CURVATURE PHYSICAL MEANING
1 by R is the bending intensity"] ``` ## 4.3 Schematic Block — Shear Stress Derivation Logic Chain ```mermaid flowchart TD P1["Elemental block of length dx"] --> P2["Bending stresses on vertical faces from M and M plus dM"] P2 --> P3["Net horizontal force difference equals dM times y times b times dy divided by I"] P3 --> P4["Integrate over area above fibre"] P4 --> P5["Express using First Moment of Area Q equals A y bar"] P5 --> P6["Equilibrium tau times b times dx equals dM by I times Q"] P6 --> P7["Use dM by dx equals V"] P7 --> P8["GENERAL FORMULA tau equals V Q divided by I b"] P8 --> P9["Substitute I for rectangle equals b d cubed by 12"] P9 --> P10["RECTANGULAR tau equals 6V by bd cubed times d squared by 4 minus y squared"] P10 --> P11["At y = 0 tau max equals 1.5 times V by A"] P10 --> P12["At y equals plus minus d by 2 tau equals 0"] ``` # 📘 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **State the assumptions made in the derivation of shear stress distribution in a rectangular beam.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The derivation of shear stress distribution in a rectangular beam is based on the following assumptions: 1. **The beam material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's Law** — stress is linearly proportional to strain within the elastic limit. *(1 mark)* 2. **The beam is initially straight, prismatic, and unloaded** — uniform cross-section with no residual stresses. *(1 mark)* 3. **Plane sections before bending remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis after bending** — Euler-Bernoulli hypothesis (neglects shear deformation). *(1 mark)* --- ### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **State the moment-curvature relation. What is its significance in deflection analysis?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The moment-curvature relation is: $$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{EI}$$ where R is the radius of curvature of the deflected neutral axis, M is the bending moment, E is Young's modulus, and I is the second moment of area. *(1.5 marks)* **Significance:** *(1.5 marks)* - It is the **fundamental governing equation** from which all beam deflection formulae are derived. - Using $\frac{1}{R} \approx \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}}$, it becomes $EI \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = M(x)$, the **differential equation of the deflection curve**. - The product **EI** is called **flexural rigidity** — the beam's resistance to bending. - It forms the basis of **double integration, Macaulay's method, moment-area method, and conjugate beam method**. --- ## 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### 📌 **Question A** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model] **(a)** Derive the expression for the shear stress distribution across the cross-section of a **rectangular beam**. State clearly the assumptions and limitations of the derivation. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A rectangular timber beam of width 150 mm and depth 250 mm is simply supported over a span of 5 m. It carries a uniformly distributed load of 25 kN/m. Calculate: - (i) The maximum shear stress in the beam. - (ii) The shear stress at a level 75 mm above the neutral axis. *(7 marks)* --- ### **Model Solution for Question A** ### **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)** **Assumptions (2 marks):** State all five assumptions from Section 2.1 verbatim. **Derivation (5 marks):** Follow the 10-step derivation from Section 3.1 above. Write out each step with proper diagram description. The final result is: $$\tau = \frac{6V}{bd^{3}}\left(\frac{d^{2}}{4} - y^{2}\right)$$ $$\tau_{max} = \frac{3V}{2A} \quad \text{(at Neutral Axis)}$$ **Limitations (within the 7 marks):** Mention at least three from Section 2.2 — restricted to elastic range, neglects stress concentration, fails for deep beams. --- ### **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)** **Given:** - Width $b = 150$ mm - Depth $d = 250$ mm - Span $L = 5$ m - UDL $w = 25$ kN/m **Step 1: Reactions and Maximum Shear Force** $$R_A = R_B = \frac{wL}{2} = \frac{25 \times 5}{2} = 62.5 \text{ kN}$$ $$V_{max} = 62.5 \text{ kN} = 62500 \text{ N} \quad \text{(at supports)}$$ **Step 2: Section Properties** $$A = b \times d = 150 \times 250 = 37500 \text{ mm}^{2}$$ **Step 3: (i) Maximum Shear Stress** *(3.5 marks)* $$\tau_{max} = \frac{3V}{2A} = \frac{3 \times 62500}{2 \times 37500}$$ $$\boxed{\tau_{max} = 2.5 \text{ N/mm}^{2} = 2.5 \text{ MPa}}$$ **Step 4: (ii) Shear Stress at y = 75 mm** *(3.5 marks)* $$\tau = \frac{6V}{bd^{3}}\left(\frac{d^{2}}{4} - y^{2}\right) = \frac{6 \times 62500}{150 \times 250^{3}}\left(\frac{250^{2}}{4} - 75^{2}\right)$$ $$= \frac{375000}{2.344 \times 10^{9}} \times (15625 - 5625)$$ $$= 1.6 \times 10^{-4} \times 10000$$ $$\boxed{\tau_{y=75} = 1.6 \text{ N/mm}^{2}}$$ --- ### 📌 **Question B** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model] *(Alternative Choice)* **(a)** Derive the moment-curvature relation $M/I = E/R$ for a beam under pure bending. Starting from this relation, obtain the differential equation of the deflection curve. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A simply supported beam of span 6 m carries a central point load of 40 kN. Using the moment-curvature relation and double integration method, determine the maximum slope and maximum deflection at the centre. Take $EI = 9 \times 10^{12}$ N·mm². *(7 marks)* --- ### **Model Solution for Question B** ### **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)** **Derivation (5 marks):** Follow Section 3.2 above — Steps 1 to 6 — to obtain: $$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{E}{R} \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{EI}$$ **Differential Equation of Deflection Curve (2 marks):** For small slopes, $\frac{1}{R} = \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}}$. Therefore: $$\boxed{EI \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = M(x)}$$ This is the **governing differential equation** used in the double integration method. --- ### **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)** **Given:** - Span $L = 6$ m $= 6000$ mm - Central point load $P = 40$ kN $= 40000$ N - $EI = 9 \times 10^{12}$ N·mm² **Step 1: Reactions and Bending Moment Equation** Reactions: $R_A = R_B = \frac{P}{2} = 20$ kN. Taking origin at left support A, for $0 \le x \le 3000$ mm: $$M(x) = R_A \cdot x = 20000 \cdot x$$ By symmetry, the same equation (with reversed sign convention) applies to the right half. **Step 2: Differential Equation** $$EI \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = -20000 \cdot x$$ $$\frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = -\frac{20000 \cdot x}{EI}$$ **Step 3: First Integration (Slope Equation)** $$\frac{dy}{dx} = -\frac{20000 \cdot x^{2}}{2EI} + C_1 = -\frac{10000 \cdot x^{2}}{EI} + C_1$$ **Step 4: Boundary Conditions** By symmetry, slope at centre (x = 3000 mm) = 0: $$0 = -\frac{10000 \times (3000)^{2}}{EI} + C_1 \implies C_1 = \frac{9 \times 10^{10}}{EI}$$ $$C_1 = \frac{9 \times 10^{10}}{9 \times 10^{12}} = 0.01$$ So: $\frac{dy}{dx} = -\frac{10000 x^{2}}{EI} + 0.01$ **Step 5: Second Integration (Deflection Equation)** $$y = -\frac{10000 x^{3}}{3EI} + 0.01x + C_2$$ **Step 6: Boundary Condition at Support** At $x = 0$, $y = 0$: $\implies C_2 = 0$ $$y = -\frac{10000 x^{3}}{3EI} + 0.01x$$ **Step 7: Maximum Deflection at Centre (x = 3000 mm)** $$y_{max} = -\frac{10000 \times (3000)^{3}}{3 \times 9 \times 10^{12}} + 0.01 \times 3000$$ $$= -\frac{2.7 \times 10^{14}}{2.7 \times 10^{13}} + 30 = -10 + 30 = 20 \text{ mm}$$ $$\boxed{y_{max} = 20 \text{ mm (downward)}}$$ **Step 8: Maximum Slope at Supports (x = 0 or x = 6000 mm)** At $x = 0$: $$\theta_A = \left.\frac{dy}{dx}\right|_{x=0} = 0.01 \text{ rad}$$ At $x = 6000$ mm: $$\theta_B = -\frac{10000 \times (6000)^{2}}{EI} + 0.01 = -\frac{3.6 \times 10^{11}}{9 \times 10^{12}} + 0.01 = -0.04 + 0.01 = -0.03 \text{ rad}$$ $$\boxed{\theta_{max} = 0.03 \text{ rad} \quad \text{(at right support, by symmetry)}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!** > > | Common Mistake | Marks Lost | Correct Approach | > |----------------|------------|------------------| > | Forgetting to write the **assumptions** before starting derivation | Up to **2 marks deducted** | Always write assumptions FIRST, before equation | > | Using $dM/dx = V$ but writing $V$ instead of $dM$ during integration | **1 mark** | Carry $dM = V dx$ clearly through steps 6–7 | > | Sign error in moment-curvature differential equation | **1 mark** | Verify sign convention from your module textbook | > | Missing the **first moment of area Q** derivation | **2 marks** | Derive $Q = A\bar{y}$ explicitly — examiners mark this step | > | Writing $\tau_{max} = V/A$ for rectangular section | **1–2 marks** | Correct: $\tau_{max} = 1.5 V/A$ | > | Skipping units in numerical problems | **0.5 mark** | Always write $V$ in N, $b,d$ in mm, $\tau$ in MPa | > | Not stating that **shear stress is zero at the extreme fibres** | **1 mark** | Always mention as boundary verification | --- ## ✅ TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > 🎯 **MUST REMEMBER Before Walking Into the KTU Exam Hall:** - [x] **Five Assumptions** for shear stress derivation — write them verbatim first. - [x] **General Shear Formula:** $\tau = \frac{VQ}{Ib}$ — *always* start from this. - [x] **Rectangular Shear Formula:** $\tau = \frac{6V}{bd^{3}}\left(\frac{d^{2}}{4} - y^{2}\right)$ - [x] **Maximum Shear Stress (Rectangular):** $\tau_{max} = \frac{3V}{2A} = 1.5 \cdot \tau_{avg}$ — at Neutral Axis. - [x] **Zero Shear Stress** at top and bottom fibres (y = ±d/2). - [x] **Limitations:** elastic range only, fails for deep beams, no stress concentration, no warping considered. - [x] **Moment-Curvature Equation:** $\frac{M}{I} = \frac{E}{R}$ ⟹ $\frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{EI}$. - [x] **Deflection Differential Equation:** $EI \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = M(x)$. - [x] **Flexural Rigidity:** $EI$ — measure of beam's resistance to bending. - [x] **Small deflection approximation:** $\frac{1}{R} \approx \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}}$. - [x] **Standard E values:** Steel ≈ 200 GPa, Concrete ≈ 25–30 GPa, Timber ≈ 10–15 GPa. - [x] **Sanity check formulas:** $\tau_{max}/\tau_{avg} = 1.5$ (rectangle), 4/3 (circle), 3/2 (thin tube). > 📚 **Examiner's Final Tip:** A 14-mark derivation question fetches full marks only when (i) assumptions are listed first, (ii) a labeled diagram is provided, (iii) every algebraic step is shown without skipping, and (iv) the final formula is boxed with units mentioned.

topics.title: Assumptions and Limitations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT402 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Assumptions and Limitations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is the Theory of Simple Bending?

The **Theory of Simple Bending** (also called the **Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory**) is a fundamental classical theory in Strength of Materials that establishes the relationship between the **bending moment** acting on a straight beam and the **resulting bending stress (flexural stress)** developed in the beam cross-section. The governing bending equation is:

$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

where every term has a specific physical meaning that we shall explore rigorously in Section 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Module 3 of PCSFT402 explicitly requires students to *derive* the bending equation starting from geometric, kinematic, and static considerations, and to *state, understand, and apply* the underlying assumptions. The "Assumptions and Limitations" question is a **guaranteed 3 to 7 mark question** in every KTU University Examination.

## 1.2 What do "Assumptions" and "Limitations" mean in this context?

| Term | Definition | Engineering Perspective |
|---|---|---|
| **Assumption** | An idealized condition that is **deliberately imposed** on a real beam so that the mathematical derivation of the bending equation becomes tractable. | These are the "ground rules" we *choose to accept* before the theory is applied. |
| **Limitation** | A condition where the **theory fails** to give accurate results when compared to real experimental behaviour. | These are the "boundaries" *beyond which* the theory cannot be trusted. |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Stack of Playing Cards"**
> Imagine a deck of playing cards glued along their flat sides to form a slender bar. When you bend this bar (like a diving board):
> - The cards on the **top** slide over one another and *stretch* → fibres in **tension**.
> - The cards at the **bottom** compress and *squish* together → fibres in **compression**.
> - The cards in the **middle** do not change length at all → this is the **Neutral Axis**.
> - A flat cross-section drawn on the deck *before* bending will still be *flat and perpendicular* to the deck's length *after* bending.
> 
> This intuitive picture captures **Assumption 4** (plane sections remain plane) and the very *existence* of a neutral axis. The "Limitations" are equivalent to saying: "What if the deck of cards is too tall, or the glue breaks, or the cards are made of rubber?"

**In one sentence:** Assumptions are the *idealized rules of the game*; Limitations tell us *when to stop playing that game* and switch to a more advanced theory.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eleven Standard Assumptions of Simple Bending Theory

Below is the **canonical list** of assumptions adopted in the KTU-accepted derivation of the simple bending equation. Each is examined for its physical *why*.

### Assumption Group A — Geometric & Material Idealization

**1. The beam is initially straight and prismatic.**
- *Why:* Curvature at zero load is zero. A constant cross-section makes moment of inertia $I$ independent of $x$.
- *Real beam:* A slight initial curvature (sweep) is always present in rolled sections.

**2. The beam material is homogeneous and isotropic.**
- *Why:* Homogeneous → density and modulus $E$ are same at every point. Isotropic → $E$ is the *same in all directions* at any point.
- *Real beam:* Wood is anisotropic; fibre-reinforced composites are deliberately anisotropic.

**3. The beam material obeys Hooke's Law (linear elasticity).**
- *Why:* Stress $\sigma$ is directly proportional to strain $\varepsilon$ via $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$. This *linearizes* the entire derivation.
- *Real beam:* Beyond the elastic limit, the linear relation breaks down and **plasticity theory** must be used.

### Assumption Group B — Kinematic Idealization (The "Plane Sections" Postulate)

**4. Plane cross-sections, originally perpendicular to the longitudinal axis, remain plane and perpendicular to the deformed axis after bending.**
- *Why:* This is the **Euler-Bernoulli hypothesis** — it implies that *shear deformation* across the section is *zero* and that *fibre strain varies linearly* with distance from the neutral axis.
- *Consequence:* $\varepsilon_x = -\frac{y}{\rho}$ (this is the *kinematic* starting point of the derivation).

**5. The radius of curvature $\rho$ is large compared to the depth of the beam.**
- *Why:* Ensures that the small-slope approximation $\sin\theta \approx \theta \approx \tan\theta$ is valid, and the beam does not undergo large deflection (geometric non-linearity).
- *Numerical rule of thumb:* $\rho \geq 10 \cdot d$ where $d$ is the beam depth.

### Assumption Group C — Symmetry & Loading Idealization

**6. The beam has at least one longitudinal plane of symmetry.**
- *Why:* It guarantees that bending occurs in a *known plane* and prevents lateral-torsional buckling from contaminating the pure bending result.

**7. All applied loads (and reactions) act in the plane of symmetry and are perpendicular to the longitudinal axis.**
- *Why:* Prevents **unsymmetrical bending** and ensures that the neutral axis *coincides* with the centroidal axis.

**8. The self-weight of the beam is neglected (or treated as a separate uniformly distributed load).**
- *Why:* Simplifies the moment equation to depend only on applied loads.

### Assumption Group D — Stress & Strain Field Simplifications

**9. The fibres on the tension and compression sides possess the same Young's Modulus ($E$).**
- *Why:* Required so that the neutral axis passes through the *centroid*. For materials like cast iron, this is *violated* and the neutral axis *shifts* from the centroid.

**10. Shear stress on transverse cross-sections is zero (i.e., pure bending, $V = 0$).**
- *Why:* Pure bending is the cleanest case. When shear force exists, **transverse shear deformation** is no longer negligible and the cross-section *warps*.

**11. Lateral buckling, local buckling, and stress concentrations are absent.**
- *Why:* The theory predicts stress from bending alone; it cannot account for buckling instability or stress risers near holes/notches.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "PHIL-SP-SSR-LL"**
> **P**rismatic, **H**omogeneous-Isotropic, **I**deal hookean, **L**arge radius, **S**ymmetry plane, **P**lane sections, **S**ame E, **S**hear-free, **R**ectangular cross-section check, **L**ateral buckling absent, **L**oads in symmetry plane.

## 2.2 The Seven Principal Limitations

| # | Limitation | What Goes Wrong | Corrective Theory |
|---|---|---|---|
| L1 | **Deep beams (L/d < 10):** Shear deformation becomes comparable to bending deformation. | Plane sections *warp*; assumption 4 fails. | **Timoshenko Beam Theory** (includes shear). |
| L2 | **Curved beams (ρ / c < 10):** Neutral axis does *not* pass through the centroid. | Centroid ≠ Neutral axis. | **Winkler-Bach Theory** for curved beams. |
| L3 | **Unsymmetrical bending:** Loads not in the principal plane. | Bending about a *non-principal* axis induces *coupled* bending and twisting. | **Unsymmetrical Bending Theory** using product of inertia. |
| L4 | **Beyond elastic limit (σ > σ_yield):** Material yields. | Linear Hooke's Law fails. | **Plastic Bending Theory** — uses perfectly plastic moment $M_p = Z_p \cdot \sigma_y$. |
| L5 | **Beams with abrupt change in section / holes / notches:** | **Stress concentration** at the geometric discontinuity. | **Stress Concentration Factor** $K_t$ from charts. |
| L6 | **Composite / anisotropic beams:** | Different $E$ in tension vs compression; or fibre-reinforced materials. | **Transformed Section Method**; Classical Laminate Theory. |
| L7 | **Large deflection beams (δ > L/200):** | Geometry of the beam mid-span changes significantly, second-order effects. | **Non-linear large-deflection theory.** |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **bible** for KTU Module 3 problems. Memorize it. In the KTU exam, *all* marks for derivation or numerical problems trace back to one of these expressions.

| # | Formula | Name | Variables & Units | Boundary Conditions / Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\varepsilon_x = -\dfrac{y}{\rho}$ | Pure bending strain equation | $y$ in m; $\rho$ in m; $\varepsilon$ dimensionless | Valid **only** when plane sections remain plane. |
| 2 | $\sigma_x = -\dfrac{E \cdot y}{\rho}$ | Flexure stress from Hooke's law | $E$ in Pa (N/m²); $y$ in m; $\rho$ in m | Linear-elastic regime; $\sigma \leq \sigma_{yield}$. |
| 3 | $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{\rho}$ | **Bending Equation** (the master relation) | $M$ in N·m; $I$ in m⁴ | Master equation; derived from static equilibrium. |
| 4 | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Flexural stress at distance $y$ from N.A. | $y$ in m; $I$ in m⁴ | Sign convention: tension positive. |
| 5 | $M = \dfrac{E \cdot I}{\rho}$ | Moment–curvature relation | $EI$ in N·m² | $EI$ is called **flexural rigidity**. |
| 6 | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$ | **Section Modulus** | $Z$ in m³ | Used to find *maximum* bending stress. |
| 7 | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Maximum bending stress | $M$ in N·m; $Z$ in m³ | Top and bottom fibres for a symmetric section. |
| 8 | $\dfrac{1}{\rho} = \dfrac{M}{E \cdot I}$ | Curvature from moment | $\rho$ in m | Foundation for the deflection equation. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU LaTeX Pitfall:** In the KTU answer sheet, students often write $\sigma = M/I$ (a common catastrophic mistake). The **correct** form is the *two-variable* form $\sigma = My/I$ or the *three-term* proportional form $M/I = \sigma/y = E/\rho$. Examiners *deduct full marks* if $y$ is missing in the formula. Always write the full proportional form!

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Design of reinforced-concrete beams, I-girders in bridges, lintels.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Shafts under combined bending and torsion, leaf springs, machine tool beds.
- **Aerospace:** Wing spars, helicopter rotor blades.
- **Civil Infrastructure:** Crane jibs, railway sleepers, pile foundations.
- Every **building code** (IS 456, Eurocode 2, AISC 360) uses the assumptions in this Module to compute *allowable bending stress*. A senior structural designer must know exactly *when* these assumptions break down to avoid catastrophic failure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: The Bending Equation from First Principles

This derivation is the **single most-asked derivation** in KTU University Examinations for Module 3. We shall proceed in **eight (8) rigorous steps**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A prismatic straight beam of length $L$, symmetric cross-section of area $A$, made of a homogeneous, isotropic, linear-elastic material with Young's Modulus $E$, is subjected to a *pure* bending moment $M$ at its ends.

### STEP 1 — Consider a Fibre at Distance $y$ from the Neutral Axis

Consider an arbitrary longitudinal fibre, originally parallel to the centroidal axis and located at a perpendicular distance $y$ from the neutral axis (which we will later prove coincides with the centroidal axis). Let the original length of this fibre be $L$.

### STEP 2 — Apply the "Plane Sections Remain Plane" Assumption (Kinematics)

When the beam bends, the originally straight longitudinal axis deforms into a circular arc of radius $\rho$. The cross-sections remain plane, so the fibre of length $L$ at distance $y$ now deforms to a length $L'$, where:

$$L' = (\rho - y) \cdot \theta$$

Here, $\theta$ is the angle subtended at the centre of curvature by the deformed beam segment, and $\rho$ is the radius of curvature of the neutral surface.

### STEP 3 — Compute the Longitudinal Strain $\varepsilon_x$

The longitudinal strain in the fibre is defined as the change in length divided by the original length:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{L' - L}{L} = \frac{(\rho - y)\theta - \rho\theta}{\rho \cdot \theta}$$

Cancelling the common factor $\rho \cdot \theta$ in the denominator, we obtain the **kinematic (geometric) bending strain equation**:

$$\varepsilon_x = -\frac{y}{\rho}$$

The negative sign indicates that fibres *above* the neutral axis ($+y$) shorten (compression), while fibres *below* ($-y$) elongate (tension) — consistent with a *sagging* moment convention.

### STEP 4 — Apply Hooke's Law (Constitutive Relation)

The fibre is in uniaxial stress (we have no other stress in a pure bending analysis), so Hooke's Law applies directly:

$$\sigma_x = E \cdot \varepsilon_x$$

Substituting the strain expression:

$$\sigma_x = -\frac{E \cdot y}{\rho}$$

This is the **elastic flexural stress** in a fibre at distance $y$ from the neutral axis.

### STEP 5 — Apply Static Equilibrium — Sum of Axial Forces = 0

For pure bending, no net axial force acts on the cross-section. The resultant of all normal stresses $\sigma_x$ over the cross-sectional area $A$ must vanish:

$$\int_A \sigma_x \, dA = 0$$

Substituting $\sigma_x = -E \cdot y / \rho$:

$$\int_A \left( -\frac{E \cdot y}{\rho} \right) dA = 0$$

Since $E$ and $\rho$ are constants at a given section:

$$-\frac{E}{\rho} \int_A y \, dA = 0$$

For the integral to vanish with a non-zero prefactor:

$$\int_A y \, dA = 0$$

This is the **first moment of area about the neutral axis**. For it to vanish, the neutral axis **must pass through the centroid** of the cross-section.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical insight:** This step *proves* the geometric coincidence of the neutral axis and the centroidal axis — provided Assumptions 2, 3, and 9 hold. The proof fails for materials with different $E$ in tension and compression (e.g., cast iron).

### STEP 6 — Apply Static Equilibrium — Sum of Moments = M

The internal moment of the stress distribution about the neutral axis must equal the applied bending moment $M$:

$$\int_A \sigma_x \cdot y \, dA = M$$

Substituting $\sigma_x = -E \cdot y / \rho$:

$$\int_A \left( -\frac{E \cdot y}{\rho} \right) y \, dA = M$$

$$-\frac{E}{\rho} \int_A y^2 \, dA = M$$

By definition, $\int_A y^2 \, dA$ is the **second moment of area (moment of inertia)**, denoted $I$:

$$I = \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

Therefore:

$$-\frac{E \cdot I}{\rho} = M$$

Taking magnitudes and acknowledging the sign convention (sagging $M$ produces *compressive* stress on top):

$$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{E}{\rho}$$

### STEP 7 — Combine the Three Equivalent Expressions (The Bending Equation)

From Steps 4 and 6, we have:

$$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{\rho}$$

This is the **canonical form of the simple bending equation**. Three equivalent statements of the same physics:

- $M/I$ = *moment per unit area moment of inertia* (a property of the *load and section*).
- $\sigma/y$ = *stress per unit distance from N.A.* (a property of the *material response*).
- $E/\rho$ = *modulus per radius of curvature* (a property of the *beam curvature*).

### STEP 8 — Solve for the Bending Stress

From $\sigma/y = M/I$:

$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

The maximum stress occurs at the *outermost fibre*, $y = y_{max}$:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M \cdot y_{max}}{I} = \frac{M}{Z}$$

where $Z = I / y_{max}$ is the **section modulus**.

$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} = \frac{M}{Z}}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question (KTU Pattern — 7 marks):** A simply supported beam of span 3 m carries a uniformly distributed load of 20 kN/m over its entire span. The beam has a rectangular cross-section 200 mm wide × 400 mm deep. Determine the maximum bending stress in the beam, and verify the validity of the simple-bending-theory assumptions.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Maximum Bending Moment**
For a simply supported beam with UDL, the maximum bending moment occurs at mid-span:

$$M_{max} = \frac{w \cdot L^2}{8} = \frac{20 \times 10^3 \cdot 3^2}{8} = 22.5 \text{ kN·m} = 22.5 \times 10^3 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2 — Moment of Inertia of Rectangular Section**

$$I = \frac{b \cdot d^3}{12} = \frac{0.200 \cdot 0.400^3}{12} = 1.0667 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 3 — Section Modulus**

$$Z = \frac{I}{d/2} = \frac{1.0667 \times 10^{-3}}{0.200} = 5.333 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Bending Stress**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{22.5 \times 10^3}{5.333 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.219 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Verification of Slenderness Ratio**
Span-to-depth ratio:

$$\frac{L}{d} = \frac{3000}{400} = 7.5$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Assumption Check Failed:** Since $L/d = 7.5 < 10$, the beam is classified as a **deep beam**. The simple bending theory assumption (Assumption 5) is **marginally valid**. For a more accurate result, the **Timoshenko beam theory** should be applied, which accounts for transverse shear deformation. The shear stress contribution to deflection can be up to 10–15% for such beams.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — "When to use which theory?"

| Condition | Recommended Theory | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| $L/d \geq 10$ and elastic material | **Euler-Bernoulli (Simple Bending)** | All assumptions fully satisfied. |
| $L/d < 10$ | **Timoshenko Beam Theory** | Shear deformation non-negligible. |
| Curved beam with $\rho/c < 10$ | **Winkler-Bach Theory** | Neutral axis shifts from centroid. |
| $\sigma \geq \sigma_y$ | **Plastic Bending Theory** | Hooke's Law violated. |
| Non-principal-plane loading | **Unsymmetrical Bending** | Two bending components + product of inertia. |
| Beam with holes / notches | **Simple Bending + $K_t$ factor** | Stress concentration at discontinuity. |
| Composite section (e.g., steel + concrete) | **Transformed Section Method** | Different $E$ in different fibres. |
| Lateral slenderness $L/b$ large | **Lateral-Torsional Buckling** check | Beam may fail by instability, not stress. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow: Assumptions Hierarchy

This diagram visualizes the **logical hierarchy** of the eleven assumptions, grouped into four functional families that feed into the final bending equation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    G3 --> A7[7 Loads in Plane of Symmetry]
    G3 --> A8[8 Self Weight Neglected]
    G4[Group 4 Stress and Strain Field]:::g4 --> A9[9 Same E in Tension and Compression]
    G4 --> A10[10 Pure Bending Shear Force Zero]
    G4 --> A11[11 No Lateral Buckling]
    A1 --> EQ[Derivation of Bending Equation]
    A2 --> EQ
    A3 --> EQ

    A10 --> EQ
    A11 --> EQ

    EQ --> O2[Stress Equation sigma equals Ey over rho]
    EQ --> O3[Moment Curvature Relation M equals EI over rho]
    EQ --> O4[Master Bending Equation M over I equals sigma over y equals E over rho]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Limitations Decision Tree

This decision tree helps a KTU student identify *which theory* to apply when an assumption is *violated* — a question that frequently appears in the 14-mark Part-B of KTU ESE.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Start Is Simple Bending Theory Applicable]) --> Q1{Large Span to Depth Ratio L slash d greater than 10}
    Q1 -- NO --> L1[Deep Beam Use Timoshenko Beam Theory]
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2{Is the Beam Initially Straight}
    Q2 -- NO --> L2[Curved Beam Use Winkler Bach Theory]
    Q2 -- YES --> Q3{Is Stress below Yield Stress}
    Q3 -- NO --> L3[Plastic Behaviour Use Plastic Bending Theory]
    Q3 -- YES --> Q4{Loads in Principal Plane of Symmetry}
    Q4 -- NO --> L4[Unsymmetrical Bending Use Unsym Bending Theory]
    Q4 -- YES --> Q5{Material Same E in Tension and Compression}
    Q5 -- NO --> L5[Cast Iron Type Material Neutral Axis Shifts]
    Q5 -- YES --> Q6{Cross Section Has Holes or Notches}
    Q6 -- YES --> L6[Apply Stress Concentration Factor Kt]
    Q6 -- NO --> Q7{Large Deflection check delta less than L over 200}
    Q7 -- NO --> L7[Large Deflection Theory]
    Q7 -- YES --> OK([All Assumptions Valid Apply Simple Bending Theory M over I equals sigma over y equals E over rho])
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Derivation Roadmap (Kinematic → Constitutive → Static)

This block diagram illustrates the *three pillars* of the bending-equation derivation, the topic where the largest single mark allotment in KTU Module 3 is concentrated.

```mermaid
    P2[Pillar 2 Constitutive Hookes Law]:::p2 --> S2[Stress equals E times Strain]
    P3[Pillar 3 Static Force and Moment Equilibrium]:::p3 --> S3[Integral of sigma dA equals 0 and Integral of sigma y dA equals M]
    S1 --> MERGE[Merge Step]
    S2 --> MERGE
```
