# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**State the assumptions made in the derivation of the simple bending equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The following are the essential assumptions in the theory of simple bending:
1. The beam is straight, prismatic, and has a longitudinal plane of symmetry.
2. The material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's Law.
3. Plane cross-sections, originally perpendicular to the axis, remain plane and perpendicular to the deformed axis (no warping).
4. The radius of curvature is large compared to the beam depth, so that the deformation is small.
5. Loads act in the plane of symmetry; pure bending is considered (shear force is zero).
6. The modulus of elasticity is the same in tension and compression, ensuring the neutral axis coincides with the centroidal axis.
7. Lateral buckling and stress concentrations are absent.

*(Award 0.5 mark per correctly stated assumption, up to 3 marks. Examiner's key demands at least 6 assumptions for full marks.)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**List any three limitations of the simple bending theory.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The theory is not valid for **deep beams** ($L/d < 10$), where shear deformation causes cross-sections to warp.
2. It fails for **curved beams** because the neutral axis does not pass through the centroid in such cases.
3. It is inapplicable when the material has yielded (i.e., beyond the elastic limit), as Hooke's Law is no longer valid.
4. The theory cannot account for **unsymmetrical bending** or **stress concentrations** near holes and notches.

*(Any three well-explained limitations fetch 1 mark each.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Derive the bending equation $M/I = \sigma/y = E/\rho$ starting from the basic assumption that plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral surface after bending. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A simply supported timber beam of span 4 m carries a point load of 12 kN at the mid-span. The beam cross-section is rectangular 100 mm wide × 200 mm deep. Calculate: (i) the maximum bending stress, and (ii) the radius of curvature at the section of maximum moment. Take $E = 12 \text{ GPa}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:

1. **Setup and Figure (1 mark):** Consider a beam element of length $L$ subjected to a pure bending moment $M$. Mark a fibre at distance $y$ from the neutral axis, with original length $L$. After bending, the neutral axis forms an arc of radius $\rho$ and the fibre forms an arc of radius $(\rho - y)$, both subtending the same angle $\theta$.

2. **Strain Derivation (2 marks):** 
   - Deformed fibre length: $L' = (\rho - y)\theta$
   - Strain: $\varepsilon_x = (L' - L)/L = [(\rho - y)\theta - \rho\theta] / (\rho\theta) = -y/\rho$

3. **Hooke's Law (1 mark):** $\sigma_x = E \cdot \varepsilon_x = -Ey/\rho$

4. **Force Equilibrium (1.5 marks):** $\int_A \sigma_x dA = 0 \implies \int_A y dA = 0$, proving the **neutral axis passes through the centroid**.

5. **Moment Equilibrium + Master Equation (1.5 marks):** $\int_A \sigma_x \cdot y \ dA = M$ gives $M = EI/\rho$. Combining:
   $$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{\rho}$$

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:

**(i) Maximum bending moment:**
$$M_{max} = \frac{W \cdot L}{4} = \frac{12 \times 10^3 \times 4}{4} = 12 \times 10^3 \text{ N·m}$$

**Moment of Inertia:**
$$I = \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{0.100 \times 0.200^3}{12} = 6.667 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^4$$

**Section Modulus:**
$$Z = \frac{I}{d/2} = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-5}}{0.100} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

**Maximum bending stress:**
$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{12 \times 10^3}{6.667 \times 10^{-4}} = 18 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Radius of curvature:**
$$\frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{M}{EI} = \frac{12 \times 10^3}{12 \times 10^9 \times 6.667 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{12 \times 10^3}{8.0 \times 10^5} = 0.015 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{\rho = 66.67 \text{ m}}$$

**Assumption Check:** $L/d = 4000/200 = 20 \geq 10$ ✓ — Simple bending theory is **fully valid**.

### Question B (Choice 2) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**(a)** State and explain the assumptions of the theory of simple bending. Discuss in detail any four limitations of this theory. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cast iron beam of T-section (flange: 100 mm × 25 mm; web: 25 mm × 125 mm) is simply supported over a span of 5 m and carries a central point load of 8 kN. Calculate the maximum tensile and compressive stresses in the beam, given that the centroid of the section is at 41.67 mm from the top fibre. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:

**Assumptions (4 marks):**
1. Beam is initially straight, prismatic, and of uniform cross-section.
2. Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's Law.
3. Plane sections perpendicular to the axis before bending remain plane and perpendicular after bending.
4. The beam has a longitudinal plane of symmetry, and loads act in this plane.
5. The radius of curvature is large compared with the dimensions of the cross-section.
6. The modulus of elasticity is the same in tension and compression.
7. Pure bending is considered (transverse shear is zero).
8. Self-weight and lateral buckling are neglected.

**Limitations (3 marks — any four, 0.75 mark each):**

1. **Deep beams** ($L/d < 10$): Timoshenko beam theory must be used.
2. **Curved beams** ($\rho/c < 10$): Winkler-Bach theory applies.
3. **Beyond elastic limit**: Plastic bending theory is required.
4. **Unsymmetrical bending**: When loads are not in the principal plane, the theory fails.
5. **Composite / anisotropic materials**: Modulus $E$ varies; use transformed section method.
6. **Stress concentrations**: At holes or abrupt changes, use a stress concentration factor.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:

**Step 1 — Maximum Bending Moment:**
$$M_{max} = \frac{W \cdot L}{4} = \frac{8 \times 10^3 \times 5}{4} = 10 \times 10^3 \text{ N·m} = 10 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 2 — Moment of Inertia of T-section:**

Total depth = 25 + 125 = 150 mm. Centroid at 41.67 mm from top.

- **Flange (100 × 25):** $A_1 = 2500 \text{ mm}^2$, $\bar{y}_1 = 41.67 - 12.5 = 29.17$ mm (above centroid)
- **Web (25 × 125):** $A_2 = 3125 \text{ mm}^2$, $\bar{y}_2 = 41.67 - 25 - 62.5 = -45.83$ mm (below centroid)

$$I = \left[\frac{100 \times 25^3}{12} + 2500 \times 29.17^2\right] + \left[\frac{25 \times 125^3}{12} + 3125 \times 45.83^2\right]$$

$$I = [130208 + 2127183] + [4069010 + 6561780]$$

$$I = 2,257,391 + 10,630,790 = 12,888,181 \text{ mm}^4 \approx 12.89 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Tensile and Compressive Stresses:**

Distance from centroid to top fibre: $y_{top} = 41.67$ mm  
Distance from centroid to bottom fibre: $y_{bot} = 150 - 41.67 = 108.33$ mm

For a simply supported beam with central load, the **bottom fibre is in tension** and the **top fibre is in compression** (sagging moment).

$$\sigma_{tensile} = \frac{M \cdot y_{bot}}{I} = \frac{10 \times 10^6 \times 108.33}{12.89 \times 10^6} = 84.04 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\sigma_{compressive} = \frac{M \cdot y_{top}}{I} = \frac{10 \times 10^6 \times 41.67}{12.89 \times 10^6} = 32.32 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A very common mistake is to compute $y_{top}$ and $y_{bot}$ from the wrong reference. The *neutral axis* coincides with the *centroid* (at 41.67 mm from top). All distances in the bending stress formula $\sigma = My/I$ must be measured **from the neutral axis (centroid)**, not from the top or bottom surface. In this problem, students who incorrectly take $y_{top} = 25$ mm and $y_{bot} = 125$ mm will lose **3 out of 7 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 / Assumptions and Limitations**

- ✅ The simple bending equation is $\sigma = My/I$ or equivalently $M/I = \sigma/y = E/\rho$.
- ✅ The **11 standard assumptions** are grouped into four families: Geometric, Kinematic, Symmetry, and Stress/Strain Field.
- ✅ The **"plane sections remain plane"** assumption is the most critical; it underpins the *linear strain distribution*.
- ✅ The neutral axis **passes through the centroid** — but **only** when the material has the *same* Young's Modulus in tension and compression.
- ✅ The **master moment equation** $M = EI/\rho$ introduces the **flexural rigidity $EI$**.
- ✅ **Section Modulus** $Z = I/y_{max}$ is the geometric property used to compute *maximum* bending stress: $\sigma_{max} = M/Z$.
- ✅ Limitations fall into 7 categories — Deep beams, Curved beams, Plastic deformation, Unsymmetrical bending, Anisotropic materials, Stress concentrations, Large deflections.
- ✅ The simple bending theory is **valid** for slender beams ($L/d \geq 10$), homogeneous-isotropic materials, and stresses within the elastic limit.
- ✅ For **deep beams** ($L/d < 10$), use **Timoshenko Beam Theory**.
- ✅ For **curved beams** ($\rho/c < 10$), use **Winkler-Bach Theory**.
- ✅ For **plastic bending**, replace elastic section modulus $Z$ with **plastic section modulus** $Z_p$.
- ✅ The proportionality $M/I = \sigma/y = E/\rho$ is the *single most important relation* in Module 3 — every 14-mark derivation question is built around it.
- ✅ **Sign convention:** Sagging moment → top fibre compression, bottom fibre tension. Hogging moment reverses the signs.
- ✅ KTU answers must always include a **figure** (free-body diagram of a beam segment) in derivation questions; missing the figure attracts a 1-mark penalty in the University Exam.
- ✅ When a question says "discuss the limitations", give at least **4 to 5 distinct limitations** with one-line engineering justifications for each. A bare list without explanation fetches only 50% marks.
- ✅ When asked for "assumptions", present them in a *numbered, structured list* with a one-line "why" for each. Examiners reward clear formatting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Almost every KTU Module 3 question paper contains a direct 3-mark question on *"State the assumptions of the theory of simple bending"* and a 7-mark sub-part of a derivation question on *"State the assumptions and derive the bending equation"*. Memorize the **PHIL-SP-SSR-LL mnemonic** and the **seven-point limitations table**. These two items alone secure a guaranteed 10 of the 14 marks in Part B.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Double Integration Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT402 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Double Integration Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Double Integration Method (DIM)** is a classical beam-deflection analysis technique in **Strength of Materials** (also called the *Method of Successive Integration*) that determines the **slope** $\theta(x)$ and **deflection** $y(x)$ at any point along the elastic axis of a beam by integrating the Euler–Bernoulli governing differential equation of the elastic curve **twice** with respect to the longitudinal coordinate $x$, and evaluating the resulting **two constants of integration** using the **boundary conditions** (supports and fixed ends) of the beam.

Mathematically, the core governing equation of the Double Integration Method is:

$$\dfrac{M(x)}{EI} = \dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}}$$

where
- $M(x)$ = bending moment at section $x$ (N·m),
- $E$ = **Young's Modulus** of the beam material (N/m² or Pa),
- $I$ = **Second Moment of Area** about the neutral axis (m⁴),
- $y$ = transverse deflection of the beam at section $x$ (m),
- $x$ = distance measured along the beam axis (m).

> [!NOTE]
> The product **$EI$** is called the **Flexural Rigidity** of the beam. Its SI unit is **N·m²**. A beam with higher $EI$ deflects less under the same load.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bending Spring Story"

Imagine a thin, flexible steel ruler clamped at one end to a desk (like a **cantilever**). When you press the free end with your finger, the ruler **bends** into a smooth curve.

- The **shape of the curve** at any point tells you *how much it bent* — that shape is the **elastic curve** $y(x)$.
- The **slope of that curve** at any point tells you *how steep* the bend is — that is the **slope** $\theta(x) = dy/dx$.
- The **rate at which the slope changes** tells you *how aggressively* the beam is being curved — that is the **curvature** $\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}}$.

Now, engineers discovered in the 18th century (Euler and Bernoulli) that this curvature is **directly proportional to the internal bending moment** $M(x)$ and inversely proportional to the **flexural rigidity** $EI$. So if you know how $M(x)$ varies along the beam, you can mathematically "undo" the curvature **twice** by integration to recover the slope and the deflection. That two-step undoing is the **Double Integration Method**.

> [!TIP]
> Think of it as *reverse engineering* the beam's shape:
> **Deflection** ($y$) $\xrightarrow{\text{differentiate}}$ **Slope** ($\theta = dy/dx$) $\xrightarrow{\text{differentiate}}$ **Curvature** ($d^2y/dx^2 = M/EI$).
> The Double Integration Method *reverses* this path.

## 1.3 When to Use the Double Integration Method

| Situation | Suitability |
|---|---|
| Beam with **continuous loading function** $M(x)$ over the entire span | ✅ Ideal |
| Simply supported beam with single point load | ✅ Suitable |
| Cantilever with UDL | ✅ Ideal |
| Beams with multiple discontinuities (step UDL, point loads at midspan, etc.) | ⚠️ Requires Macaulay's Brackets |
| Beams where slope/deflection needed at *specific* section only | ❌ Use Macaulay or Moment-Area |
| Statically indeterminate beams | ❌ Use Conjugate Beam or Superposition |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logic Chain of the Method (Step-by-Step)

The Double Integration Method follows a strict six-stage pipeline. Each stage must be executed correctly; a single sign error propagates catastrophically into the final deflection.

**Step 1 — Draw the Free-Body Diagram (FBD):**
Sketch the beam, supports, applied loads, and reactions.

**Step 2 — Compute Support Reactions:**
Apply $\sum F_y = 0$ and $\sum M = 0$ to find reaction forces.

**Step 3 — Write the Bending Moment Equation $M(x)$:**
Choose an origin (usually the left support). Write $M(x)$ as a function of $x$ using the **standard sign convention**:
- Sagging moment (concave up) → **Positive**
- Hogging moment (concave down) → **Negative**

**Step 4 — Set Up the Governing Equation:**
$$\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = \dfrac{M(x)}{EI}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate Twice:**

**First integration** gives the **slope equation**:
$$EI\,\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \int M(x)\,dx + C_{1}$$

**Second integration** gives the **deflection equation**:
$$EI\,y = \int\!\!\int M(x)\,dx\,dx + C_{1}x + C_{2}$$

where $C_1$ and $C_2$ are **constants of integration**.

**Step 6 — Apply Boundary Conditions to Find $C_1$ and $C_2$:**

Common boundary conditions:
- **Simply supported end**: $y = 0$ (deflection zero)
- **Fixed (built-in) end**: $y = 0$ AND $dy/dx = 0$ (deflection *and* slope zero)
- **Free end with load**: Allows non-zero slope and deflection
- **Symmetric beam**: At center, $dy/dx = 0$

**Step 7 — Write Final Expressions for $\theta_{\max}$ and $y_{\max}$:**
Plug in the critical $x$-value (usually mid-span for symmetric beams, or free end for cantilevers) to obtain maximum slope and maximum deflection.

## 2.2 Standard Sign Convention Diagram (ASCII)

```
         +ve M (sagging)
         ┌─────⌣─────┐
         │            │
       ──┘            └──
       ↑                ↑
   Tension at bottom  Compression at top

        Concave UP → +ve M → +ve d²y/dx² → beam sags
```

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is your **exam-day quick reference** for the Double Integration Method.

| # | Expression | Formula | Meaning / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Governing equation | $\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = \dfrac{M(x)}{EI}$ | Curvature = Moment / Flexural Rigidity |
| 2 | First integration (slope) | $EI\,\theta = \displaystyle\int M(x)\,dx + C_{1}$ | $\theta = dy/dx$, rad |
| 3 | Second integration (deflection) | $EI\,y = \displaystyle\int\!\!\int M(x)\,dx\,dx + C_{1}x + C_{2}$ | $y$ in metres |
| 4 | Flexural rigidity | $EI$ | N·m² |
| 5 | Slope at simply supported end | $\theta = C_1/EI$ (when $y=0$ at $x=0$) | rad |
| 6 | Deflection at simply supported end | $y = 0$ at $x=0$ | Boundary condition |
| 7 | Slope at fixed end | $\theta = 0$ | Boundary condition |
| 8 | Cantilever with UDL $w$ | $\theta_{\max} = \dfrac{wL^{3}}{6EI}$, $y_{\max} = \dfrac{wL^{4}}{8EI}$ | Free end values |
| 9 | Cantilever with point load $P$ at free end | $\theta_{\max} = \dfrac{PL^{2}}{2EI}$, $y_{\max} = \dfrac{PL^{3}}{3EI}$ | Free end values |
| 10 | Simply supported beam with central point load $P$ | $\theta_{\max} = \dfrac{PL^{2}}{16EI}$, $y_{\max} = \dfrac{PL^{3}}{48EI}$ | At supports / center |
| 11 | Simply supported beam with UDL $w$ | $\theta_{\max} = \dfrac{wL^{3}}{24EI}$, $y_{\max} = \dfrac{5wL^{4}}{384EI}$ | At supports / center |
| 12 | Macaulay correction for load jumps | Replace $x$ with $(x-a)$ in load terms after point $a$ | Standard technique |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Double Integration Method is not merely a textbook exercise — it is the **foundation** upon which all advanced beam-deflection software (FEA packages like ANSYS, ABAQUS, SAP2000) is built. Practical applications include:

- **Bridge girder design**: Ensuring deflection under live traffic stays below $\dfrac{L}{800}$ (IRC:24 standard).
- **Machine tool frames**: Predicting tip-deflection of cantilever boring bars.
- **Aircraft wing spars**: Where asymmetric bending moments produce very different deflections from each side.
- **Railway sleepers**: Simply supported beams with distributed axle loads.
- **Crane jibs**: Cantilever configurations where free-end deflection governs operational safety.

The governing equation $EI\,y'' = M(x)$ is in fact the **differential form of the Euler–Bernoulli beam theory**, and its **integral form** (Moment-Area theorems) and **matrix stiffness form** (FEM shape functions) are direct descendants of the Double Integration Method.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: From Bending Stress to the Governing Equation

We begin from the **pure bending formula** derived from the strain distribution across a beam cross-section:

$$\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$$

where $R$ is the **radius of curvature** of the deflected beam. The curvature of a function $y(x)$ in differential calculus is given by:

$$\dfrac{1}{R} = \pm\dfrac{\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}}}{\left[1+\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^{2}\right]^{3/2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Small-deflection assumption**: For slopes $\theta \ll 1$ rad (typically $< 0.1$ rad for engineering beams), $\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^{2} \ll 1$, so the denominator reduces to approximately $1$.

Applying the small-deflection simplification and selecting the **sagging-positive sign convention**, we obtain:

$$\dfrac{1}{R} \approx \dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}}$$

Substituting into the pure bending relation $E/R = M/I$:

$$\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = \dfrac{M(x)}{EI}$$

This is the **governing second-order ODE** of the Double Integration Method. It is **linear** in $y$ provided $EI$ is constant (prismatic beam), which is the standard assumption in PCSFT402.

## 3.2 Complete Worked Example 1 — Cantilever with Point Load at Free End

**Problem Statement:** A cantilever beam of length $L$ is fixed at $x=0$ and free at $x=L$. A point load $P$ acts vertically downward at the free end. Find the slope and deflection at the free end using the Double Integration Method.

**Step 1 — Reactions at Fixed End:**
Vertical reaction: $R_A = P$
Moment reaction: $M_A = -PL$ (hogging)

**Step 2 — Bending Moment at Section $x$ (measuring from fixed end):**
Consider a section at distance $x$ from the fixed end. Take the right-hand portion (free-end side) which carries only the load $P$ at distance $(L-x)$.

$$M(x) = -P(L-x)$$

The negative sign indicates **hogging** moment (sagging-positive convention gives hogging a negative sign). Expanding:

$$M(x) = -PL + Px$$

**Step 3 — Set Up the Governing ODE:**

$$EI\,\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = M(x) = -PL + Px$$

**Step 4 — First Integration (Slope):**

$$EI\,\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \int (-PL + Px)\,dx = -PLx + \dfrac{Px^{2}}{2} + C_{1}$$

**Step 5 — Second Integration (Deflection):**

$$EI\,y = \int\!\!\left(-PLx + \dfrac{Px^{2}}{2} + C_{1}\right)dx$$

$$EI\,y = -\dfrac{PLx^{2}}{2} + \dfrac{Px^{3}}{6} + C_{1}x + C_{2}$$

**Step 6 — Apply Boundary Conditions:**

At the **fixed end** $x = 0$:
- (i) Deflection $y = 0$
- (ii) Slope $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$

**Applying (ii):** $\dfrac{dy}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0} = 0$
$$0 = 0 + 0 + C_{1} \quad\Rightarrow\quad C_{1} = 0$$

**Applying (i):** $y(0) = 0$
$$0 = 0 + 0 + 0 + C_{2} \quad\Rightarrow\quad C_{2} = 0$$

**Step 7 — Final Slope and Deflection Equations:**

$$EI\,\dfrac{dy}{dx} = -PLx + \dfrac{Px^{2}}{2}$$

$$EI\,y = -\dfrac{PLx^{2}}{2} + \dfrac{Px^{3}}{6}$$

**Step 8 — Slope and Deflection at Free End ($x = L$):**

$$EI\,\theta_{\max} = -PL(L) + \dfrac{P(L)^{2}}{2} = -PL^{2} + \dfrac{PL^{2}}{2} = -\dfrac{PL^{2}}{2}$$

The negative sign indicates clockwise rotation (consistent with the downward load). The magnitude:

$$\boxed{\theta_{\max} = \dfrac{PL^{2}}{2EI} \quad(\text{clockwise})}$$

$$EI\,y_{\max} = -\dfrac{PL(L)^{2}}{2} + \dfrac{P(L)^{3}}{6} = -\dfrac{PL^{3}}{2} + \dfrac{PL^{3}}{6} = -\dfrac{PL^{3}}{3}$$

$$\boxed{y_{\max} = \dfrac{PL^{3}}{3EI} \quad(\text{downward})}$$

> [!TIP]
> This matches the **standard result** quoted in every Strength of Materials reference table. The negative sign convention in our working simply confirms the direction of bending.

## 3.3 Complete Worked Example 2 — Simply Supported Beam with UDL

**Problem Statement:** A simply supported beam of span $L$ carries a uniformly distributed load $w$ per unit length over its entire span. Determine the slope at supports and maximum deflection at mid-span.

**Step 1 — Reactions by Symmetry:**
$$R_A = R_B = \dfrac{wL}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Bending Moment Equation (origin at left support $A$):**

$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - w \cdot x \cdot \dfrac{x}{2} = \dfrac{wL}{2}x - \dfrac{wx^{2}}{2}$$

$$M(x) = \dfrac{w}{2}(Lx - x^{2})$$

**Step 3 — Governing ODE:**

$$EI\,\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = \dfrac{w}{2}(Lx - x^{2})$$

**Step 4 — First Integration:**

$$EI\,\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{w}{2}\left(\dfrac{Lx^{2}}{2} - \dfrac{x^{3}}{3}\right) + C_{1}$$

$$EI\,\theta = \dfrac{wL}{4}x^{2} - \dfrac{w}{6}x^{3} + C_{1}$$

**Step 5 — Second Integration:**

$$EI\,y = \dfrac{wL}{4}\cdot\dfrac{x^{3}}{3} - \dfrac{w}{6}\cdot\dfrac{x^{4}}{4} + C_{1}x + C_{2}$$

$$EI\,y = \dfrac{wLx^{3}}{12} - \dfrac{wx^{4}}{24} + C_{1}x + C_{2}$$

**Step 6 — Boundary Conditions:**

- At $x = 0$ (support $A$): $y = 0$
- At $x = L$ (support $B$): $y = 0$

**Applying $y(0) = 0$:**
$$0 = 0 - 0 + 0 + C_{2} \quad\Rightarrow\quad C_{2} = 0$$

**Applying $y(L) = 0$:**
$$0 = \dfrac{wL \cdot L^{3}}{12} - \dfrac{wL^{4}}{24} + C_{1}L + 0$$

$$0 = \dfrac{wL^{4}}{12} - \dfrac{wL^{4}}{24} + C_{1}L$$

$$\dfrac{wL^{4}}{24} + C_{1}L = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad C_{1} = -\dfrac{wL^{3}}{24}$$

**Step 7 — Final Equations:**

$$EI\,\theta = \dfrac{wLx^{2}}{4} - \dfrac{wx^{3}}{6} - \dfrac{wL^{3}}{24}$$

$$EI\,y = \dfrac{wLx^{3}}{12} - \dfrac{wx^{4}}{24} - \dfrac{wL^{3}x}{24}$$

**Step 8 — Slope at Supports ($x = 0$ and $x = L$):**

At $x = 0$:
$$EI\,\theta_A = -\dfrac{wL^{3}}{24} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \theta_A = -\dfrac{wL^{3}}{24EI}$$

At $x = L$:
$$EI\,\theta_B = \dfrac{wL \cdot L^{2}}{4} - \dfrac{wL^{3}}{6} - \dfrac{wL^{3}}{24} = \dfrac{wL^{3}}{4} - \dfrac{wL^{3}}{6} - \dfrac{wL^{3}}{24}$$

Converting to common denominator 24:
$$EI\,\theta_B = \dfrac{6wL^{3} - 4wL^{3} - wL^{3}}{24} = \dfrac{wL^{3}}{24}$$

$$\theta_B = +\dfrac{wL^{3}}{24EI}$$

**Step 9 — Maximum Deflection at Mid-Span ($x = L/2$):**

By symmetry, $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$ at $x = L/2$ (we can verify this by plugging $x = L/2$ into the slope equation).

$$EI\,\theta\bigg|_{x=L/2} = \dfrac{wL}{4}\cdot\dfrac{L^{2}}{4} - \dfrac{w}{6}\cdot\dfrac{L^{3}}{8} - \dfrac{wL^{3}}{24}$$

$$= \dfrac{wL^{3}}{16} - \dfrac{wL^{3}}{48} - \dfrac{wL^{3}}{24}$$

Common denominator 48:
$$= \dfrac{3wL^{3} - wL^{3} - 2wL^{3}}{48} = \dfrac{0}{48} = 0 \quad\checkmark$$

Deflection at $x = L/2$:
$$EI\,y_{\max} = \dfrac{wL \cdot (L/2)^{3}}{12} - \dfrac{w(L/2)^{4}}{24} - \dfrac{wL^{3}(L/2)}{24}$$

$$= \dfrac{wL^{4}}{96} - \dfrac{wL^{4}}{384} - \dfrac{wL^{4}}{48}$$

Common denominator 384:
$$= \dfrac{4wL^{4} - wL^{4} - 8wL^{4}}{384} = -\dfrac{5wL^{4}}{384}$$

$$\boxed{y_{\max} = \dfrac{5wL^{4}}{384EI} \quad(\text{downward})}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{A} = \dfrac{wL^{3}}{24EI}, \quad \theta_{B} = \dfrac{wL^{3}}{24EI}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Approach

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Double Integration Method requires a **single continuous** $M(x)$ expression. For beams with multiple load discontinuities, the KTU examiner expects you to either (a) write separate equations for each segment, or (b) use **Macaulay's Bracket Notation**.

| Beam Configuration | Recommended Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Single load type over entire span | **Double Integration** | $M(x)$ is continuous |
| Step / patch loads / multiple point loads | **Macaulay's Method** | Handles discontinuities elegantly |
| Need only $\theta$ at one point | **Moment-Area / Conjugate Beam** | Faster |
| Statically indeterminate | **Conjugate Beam / Superposition** | DIM requires $\int M\,dx$ |
| Variable $EI$ beam | **Direct Integration** with modified approach | $EI$ must be inside integral |

## 4.1 Method Flowchart (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **end-to-end functional flow** of the Double Integration Method, from loading specification to final design verification.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[BeamSpec] --> B[ReactionsAndEquilibrium]
    B --> C[MomentEquationMx]
    C --> D{IsMxContinuous}
    D -- Yes --> E[DivideByEI]
    D -- No --> F[UseMacaulayBrackets]
    E --> G[FirstIntegrationSlope]
    F --> G
    G --> H[SecondIntegrationDeflection]
    H --> I[IdentifyBoundaryConditions]
    I --> J[ComputeC1C2]
    J --> K[FinalSlopeEquation]
    J --> L[FinalDeflectionEquation]
    K --> M[EvaluateAtCriticalX]
    L --> M
    M --> N{AgainstAllowableLimit}
    N -- Yes --> O[DesignPass]
    N -- No --> P[IncreaseIorE]
    P --> Q[RepeatCycle]
    O --> R[FinalBeamDesign]
    Q --> R
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Double Integration Method. State the governing differential equation used in this method and explain the significance of the two constants of integration obtained.**

**Model Answer:**

> The Double Integration Method is a classical technique in Strength of Materials used to determine the slope and deflection of a beam under transverse loading. It is based on integrating the governing differential equation of the elastic curve **twice** with respect to the beam's longitudinal axis.
>
> The governing differential equation is:
>
> $$\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = \dfrac{M(x)}{EI}$$
>
> where $M(x)$ is the bending moment, $E$ is Young's modulus, and $I$ is the second moment of area of the cross-section.
>
> On successive integration, two constants $C_1$ and $C_2$ appear. These are evaluated using the **boundary conditions** of the beam — namely, the conditions of zero deflection at supports and zero slope at fixed (built-in) ends. The constants essentially represent the **slope at the origin** and the **deflection at the origin** of the chosen coordinate system, and they uniquely determine the elastic curve for a given loading.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the boundary conditions for (i) a simply supported end, and (ii) a fixed end of a beam. Why are these conditions essential to the Double Integration Method?**

**Model Answer:**

> **(i) Simply Supported End:** At a simply supported (pin or roller) end, the beam can freely rotate but cannot deflect vertically. Hence the boundary condition is:
>
> $$y = 0 \quad \text{at the support location}$$
>
> The slope $dy/dx$ is **not** zero and remains unknown.
>
> **(ii) Fixed (Built-in) End:** At a fixed end, the beam is restrained against both translation and rotation. Hence **two** boundary conditions apply simultaneously:
>
> $$y = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0 \quad \text{at the fixed end}$$
>
> These conditions are essential because the Double Integration Method produces **two constants of integration** ($C_1$ and $C_2$). Since the elastic curve of a beam under static loading is **uniquely determined**, exactly two independent conditions are needed to solve for these two constants. Without these conditions, the problem remains indeterminate to the level of two constants.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE)** regulations, Module 3 carries internal choice. The student must attempt **one** of the two full-length questions below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** A simply supported beam of span $L$ carries a uniformly distributed load (UDL) of intensity $w$ per unit length over the entire span. Using the **Double Integration Method**, derive the expressions for the slope at the supports and the maximum deflection at mid-span. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A cantilever beam of length $3\,\text{m}$ carries a point load of $20\,\text{kN}$ at its free end. If the flexural rigidity $EI = 6000\,\text{kN·m}^2$, determine the slope and deflection at the free end. Take $E = 200\,\text{GPa}$ and $I = 30 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^4$ for verification. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Step 1 — Reactions:** By symmetry, $R_A = R_B = wL/2$

**Step 2 — Bending moment at section $x$:**

$$M(x) = \dfrac{wL}{2}x - \dfrac{wx^{2}}{2} = \dfrac{w}{2}(Lx - x^{2})$$

**Step 3 — Governing ODE and integration:**
$$EI\,\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = \dfrac{w}{2}(Lx - x^{2})$$

$$EI\,\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{wLx^{2}}{4} - \dfrac{wx^{3}}{6} + C_{1}$$

$$EI\,y = \dfrac{wLx^{3}}{12} - \dfrac{wx^{4}}{24} + C_{1}x + C_{2}$$

**Step 4 — Boundary conditions:**
- $y = 0$ at $x = 0$ → $C_2 = 0$
- $y = 0$ at $x = L$ → $C_1 = -\dfrac{wL^{3}}{24}$

**Step 5 — Final results:**

$$\boxed{\theta_{A} = \theta_{B} = \dfrac{wL^{3}}{24EI}}$$

$$\boxed{y_{\max} = \dfrac{5wL^{4}}{384EI} \quad\text{(at mid-span)}}$$

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Given:**
- $L = 3\,\text{m}$, $P = 20\,\text{kN}$
- $E = 200 \times 10^{9}\,\text{Pa}$, $I = 30 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^4$
- $EI = 200 \times 10^{9} \times 30 \times 10^{-6} = 6 \times 10^{6}\,\text{N·m}^2 = 6000\,\text{kN·m}^2$

**Using the standard result for cantilever with point load at free end:**

$$\theta_{\max} = \dfrac{PL^{2}}{2EI} = \dfrac{20 \times (3)^{2}}{2 \times 6000} = \dfrac{180}{12000}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{\max} = 0.015\,\text{rad} \approx 0.86^{\circ}}$$

$$y_{\max} = \dfrac{PL^{3}}{3EI} = \dfrac{20 \times (3)^{3}}{3 \times 6000} = \dfrac{540}{18000}$$

$$\boxed{y_{\max} = 0.030\,\text{m} = 30\,\text{mm (downward)}}$$

**Derivation (for full marks):** $M(x) = -P(L-x)$, integrating $EIy''=M$ twice with $y(0)=0$ and $y'(0)=0$ yields $EIy = -PLx^{2}/2 + Px^{3}/6$, which at $x=L$ gives the result above.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A simply supported beam of length $6\,\text{m}$ carries a point load of $30\,\text{kN}$ at a distance of $2\,\text{m}$ from the left support. Determine the slope and deflection under the load using the Double Integration Method. Take $EI = 8 \times 10^{3}\,\text{kN·m}^2$. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Compare the Double Integration Method with Macaulay's Method for finding beam deflection. Under what circumstances is each preferred? **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Step 1 — Reactions:**
Taking moments about $A$:
$$R_B \times 6 = 30 \times 2 \quad\Rightarrow\quad R_B = 10\,\text{kN}$$
$$R_A = 30 - 10 = 20\,\text{kN}$$

**Step 2 — Two-segment moment equations** (since the load is not at midspan):

For $0 \le x \le 2$:
$$M_1 = 20x$$

For $2 \le x \le 6$:
$$M_2 = 20x - 30(x-2) = -10x + 60$$

**Step 3 — Segment 1 integration:**
$$EI\,\dfrac{d^{2}y_1}{dx^{2}} = 20x$$
$$EI\,\dfrac{dy_1}{dx} = 10x^{2} + C_{1}$$
$$EI\,y_1 = \dfrac{10x^{3}}{3} + C_{1}x + C_{2}$$

Boundary condition: $y_1(0) = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$

**Step 4 — Segment 2 integration:**
$$EI\,\dfrac{d^{2}y_2}{dx^{2}} = -10x + 60$$
$$EI\,\dfrac{dy_2}{dx} = -5x^{2} + 60x + C_{3}$$
$$EI\,y_2 = -\dfrac{5x^{3}}{3} + 30x^{2} + C_{3}x + C_{4}$$

**Step 5 — Continuity conditions at $x = 2$:**
- Slope continuity: $\dfrac{dy_1}{dx}\bigg|_{x=2} = \dfrac{dy_2}{dx}\bigg|_{x=2}$
- Deflection continuity: $y_1(2) = y_2(2)$

From slope continuity:
$$10(4) + C_{1} = -5(4) + 60(2) + C_{3}$$
$$40 + C_{1} = -20 + 120 + C_{3}$$
$$C_{1} = 60 + C_{3}$$

From deflection continuity:
$$\dfrac{10(8)}{3} + 2C_{1} = -\dfrac{5(8)}{3} + 30(4) + 2C_{3} + C_{4}$$
$$\dfrac{80}{3} + 2C_{1} = -\dfrac{40}{3} + 120 + 2C_{3} + C_{4}$$

Using $C_1 = 60 + C_3$:
$$\dfrac{80}{3} + 120 + 2C_{3} = -\dfrac{40}{3} + 120 + 2C_{3} + C_{4}$$
$$C_{4} = \dfrac{80}{3} + \dfrac{40}{3} = 40$$

**Step 6 — Boundary condition at $x = 6$: $y_2(6) = 0$**

$$0 = -\dfrac{5(216)}{3} + 30(36) + 6C_{3} + 40$$
$$0 = -360 + 1080 + 6C_{3} + 40$$
$$6C_{3} = 40 - 720 = -760 \quad\Rightarrow\quad C_{3} = -\dfrac{760}{6} = -\dfrac{380}{3}\,\text{kN·m}^2$$

Therefore: $C_1 = 60 - \dfrac{380}{3} = \dfrac{180 - 380}{3} = -\dfrac{200}{3}\,\text{kN·m}^2$

**Step 7 — Slope and deflection under the load ($x = 2$):**

$$EI\,\theta\bigg|_{x=2} = 10(4) - \dfrac{200}{3} = 40 - 66.67 = -26.67\,\text{kN·m}^2$$

$$\theta\bigg|_{x=2} = \dfrac{-26.67}{8000} = -3.33 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{rad}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{\text{under load}} \approx 3.33 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{rad (magnitude)}}$$

$$EI\,y\bigg|_{x=2} = \dfrac{10(8)}{3} + 2\left(-\dfrac{200}{3}\right) = \dfrac{80}{3} - \dfrac{400}{3} = -\dfrac{320}{3}$$

$$y\bigg|_{x=2} = \dfrac{-320/3}{8000} = -\dfrac{320}{24000} = -0.01333\,\text{m}$$

$$\boxed{y_{\text{under load}} \approx 13.33\,\text{mm (downward)}}$$

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Double Integration Method | Macaulay's Method |
|---|---|---|
| Number of segments | Requires separate $M(x)$ per segment | **Single unified** $M(x)$ equation |
| Constants of integration | Multiple — each segment adds 2 | Only **2** constants regardless of loads |
| Handling discontinuities | Requires continuity matching | Handles jumps via Macaulay brackets $(x-a)^n$ |
| Setup complexity | Higher | Lower |
| Computational effort | Higher | Lower |
| Validity for $\theta$, $y$ | In each segment | Across **entire span** |
| Preferred when | Few segments, simple loading | Multiple loads, eccentric point loads |

**Circumstances of Preference:**
- **DIM is preferred** for: simple cantilevers, single-load simply-supported beams, and cases where the student wants to understand each segment's elastic behaviour separately.
- **Macaulay's Method is preferred** for: beams with 3 or more load discontinuities, stepped loads, multiple point loads, and rapid exam-style calculations where elegance and speed are valued.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in the Double Integration Method:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Error (2–3 marks lost):** Students frequently forget the negative sign for hogging moments, leading to positive deflections for downward loads. **Always adopt a consistent sign convention** before starting and stick to it.
>
> 2. **Wrong Boundary Condition Application (3 marks lost):** Writing $dy/dx = 0$ at a simply supported end is incorrect — slope is free there. Only $y = 0$ applies. At fixed ends, BOTH conditions apply.
>
> 3. **Algebraic Slip in Integrating $x^n$ (1–2 marks lost):** The integral of $x^n$ is $x^{n+1}/(n+1)$. A frequent mistake is writing $\int x\,dx = x^2$ instead of $x^2/2$. Recheck each integration.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the $C_1 x$ Term in Second Integration (3 marks lost):** When integrating $C_1$ (a constant) with respect to $x$, you get $C_1 x$, not just $C_1$. Many students lose this term entirely.
>
> 5. **Unit Inconsistency (1 mark lost):** Mixing SI and CGS units (e.g., kN with mm) without conversion. Always convert everything to **N, m, Pa** before substituting into the formula.
>
> 6. **Ignoring $EI$ Variability:** If the beam has varying cross-section or material, $EI$ must remain **inside** the integral. KTU questions sometimes test this.

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **The following bullets form your last-minute revision notes. Read them 30 minutes before the exam.**

- [ ] The governing equation of the Double Integration Method is $EI \cdot \dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = M(x)$.
- [ ] Two integrations yield **slope** $\theta$ and **deflection** $y$, with constants $C_1$ and $C_2$.
- [ ] **Boundary conditions** are essential: simply supported → $y = 0$; fixed → $y = 0$ AND $\theta = 0$.
- [ ] **Sign convention:** sagging positive, hogging negative; downward deflection conventionally positive in magnitude.
- [ ] Cantilever with point load at free end: $\theta_{\max} = \dfrac{PL^2}{2EI}$, $y_{\max} = \dfrac{PL^3}{3EI}$.
- [ ] Cantilever with UDL over full span: $\theta_{\max} = \dfrac{wL^3}{6EI}$, $y_{\max} = \dfrac{wL^4}{8EI}$.
- [ ] Simply supported beam with central point load: $\theta_{\max} = \dfrac{PL^2}{16EI}$, $y_{\max} = \dfrac{PL^3}{48EI}$.
- [ ] Simply supported beam with UDL: $\theta_{\max} = \dfrac{wL^3}{24EI}$, $y_{\max} = \dfrac{5wL^4}{384EI}$.
- [ ] When there are **multiple discontinuities** (point loads, step loads), prefer **Macaulay's Method** to avoid multiple-segment integrations.
- [ ] Always verify the **units** of $EI$: N·m² in SI, or kN·m² if forces are in kN and lengths in metres.
- [ ] The method assumes **small deflections** (slope $\ll 1$ rad), **constant $EI$**, and **linear elastic material behaviour**.
- [ ] The beam's deflection curve is **unique** for a given loading — hence exactly **two** boundary conditions suffice for a simply supported or cantilever beam.
- [ ] For **statically indeterminate beams**, the Double Integration Method requires an additional compatibility condition (e.g., deflection = 0 at intermediate support).
- [ ] The negative sign in $EIy'' = -M$ (alternative convention) vs $EIy'' = M$ (this lecture's convention) — **always clarify before solving**.

---

> **End of Module 3 — Double Integration Method Notes**
> *Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | NEP 2020 Aligned | 2024 Scheme*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Macaulay's Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCSFT402 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Macaulay's Method ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Macaulay's Method (Singularity Function Method):** A mathematical technique for determining the slope and deflection of beams, introduced by **W.H. Macaulay** in 1919. It uses **bracket functions (singularity functions)** to express the bending moment as a **single continuous algebraic equation** valid across the entire span of the beam, regardless of discontinuities caused by point loads, concentrated moments, or the start/end of distributed loads. The governing differential equation of the elastic curve is: $$EI \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$$ where $E$ is Young's Modulus, $I$ is the Second Moment of Area, and $M(x)$ is the bending moment expressed using **Macaulay brackets** $\langle x - a \rangle^n$. ### 1.2 The Macaulay Bracket (Singularity Function) The defining rule for the bracket function of order $n$ is: $$\langle x - a \rangle^n = \begin{cases} (x - a)^n & \text{when } x \geq a \\ 0 & \text{when } x < a \end{cases}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Property:** When integrating a Macaulay bracket, the term $(x - a)$ remains enclosed within the bracket. The bracket "switches on" only after the point $x = a$. This is the heart of Macaulay's method — it allows one equation to govern the entire beam. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy: The Universal Power Switch** > Imagine a long corridor with multiple light switches controlling the same bulb at different points. Each switch only works for the section of the corridor beyond it. Macaulay's bracket $\langle x - a \rangle$ behaves exactly like this: the load at point $a$ **activates** its effect on the beam only for $x \geq a$. This eliminates the need to write separate moment equations for different segments — a single, elegant equation handles everything! ### 1.4 Physical Constants and Units | Quantity | Symbol | Standard SI Unit | |---|---|---| | Young's Modulus | $E$ | **N/m²** or **Pa** | | Second Moment of Area | $I$ | **m⁴** or **mm⁴** | | Bending Moment | $M$ | **N·m** or **kN·m** | | Deflection | $y$ | **m** or **mm** | | Slope | $\theta = dy/dx$ | **radians (rad)** | | Flexural Rigidity | $EI$ | **N·m²** | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic of Macaulay's Method The method follows a structured 6-step protocol: - **Step 1 — Compute Support Reactions:** Use static equilibrium ($\sum F_y = 0$ and $\sum M = 0$). - **Step 2 — Express Bending Moment as $M(x)$:** Write a single moment equation from one end (usually the **left support**) using Macaulay brackets to represent every load. - **Step 3 — Substitute into the Governing Equation:** Insert $M(x)$ into $EI \cdot \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$. - **Step 4 — First Integration (Slope Equation):** Integrate once to obtain $EI \cdot \frac{dy}{dx}$ (the slope equation). The integration constant is $C_1$. - **Step 5 — Second Integration (Deflection Equation):** Integrate again to obtain $EI \cdot y$ (the deflection equation). The new constant is $C_2$. - **Step 6 — Apply Boundary Conditions:** Use known values of $y$ (and $\theta$ if fixed end exists) at supports to solve for $C_1$ and $C_2$. ### 2.2 Conversion of Common Loads into Macaulay Brackets | Type of Load | Representation in Moment Equation $M(x)$ | Reason | |---|---|---| | Upward Reaction $R_A$ at $x = 0$ | $+R_A \cdot x$ | Always active from start | | Point Load $W$ (downward) at $x = a$ | $-W \cdot \langle x - a \rangle^1$ | Activates only after $x = a$ | | UDL $w$ from $x = a$ to $x = b$ | $-\dfrac{w}{2}\left[\langle x - a \rangle^2 - \langle x - b \rangle^2\right]$ | Spans only between $a$ and $b$ | | UVL $w_0$ starting at $x = a$ | $-\dfrac{w_0}{6}\langle x - a \rangle^3$ | Intensity grows linearly | | Couple $M_0$ (anticlockwise) at $x = a$ | $+M_0 \cdot \langle x - a \rangle^0$ | Sudden jump in moment | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | |---|---|---| | 1 | $EI \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$ | Governing differential equation | | 2 | $EI \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \int M(x) \, dx + C_1$ | Slope equation (after 1st integration) | | 3 | $EI \, y = \iint M(x) \, dx \, dx + C_1 x + C_2$ | Deflection equation (after 2nd integration) | | 4 | $\langle x - a \rangle^n = (x-a)^n$ for $x \geq a$, else $0$ | Macaulay bracket definition | | 5 | $\int \langle x - a \rangle^n \, dx = \dfrac{\langle x - a \rangle^{n+1}}{n+1}$ | Integration rule | | 6 | $y = 0$ at simple support | Boundary condition | | 7 | $\theta = \dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$ at fixed end | Boundary condition | | 8 | $\theta_{\max}$ at point where $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$ | Location of max deflection | | 9 | $y_{\max} = \dfrac{-5wL^4}{384EI}$ (SS beam, full UDL) | Standard result for verification | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Structural Engineering:** Designing floor beams in multi-storey buildings with multiple point loads from columns. - **Bridge Engineering:** Analyzing continuous girders with moving vehicular loads. - **Machine Design:** Calculating deflection of shafts carrying gears and pulleys. - **Aerospace:** Analyzing wing spars under distributed aerodynamic loads. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Governing Equation from First Principles Consider a beam segment of length $dx$ subjected to bending moment $M$. From the Euler-Bernoulli beam theory, the curvature is: $$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{EI}$$ For small deflections, the curvature simplifies to: $$\frac{1}{R} \approx \frac{d^2y}{dx^2}$$ Equating both: $$\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \frac{M}{EI}$$ Rearranging: $$EI \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$$ This is the **Master Equation** of beam deflection. ### 3.2 Worked Derivation: Simply Supported Beam with Central Point Load **Problem Statement:** A simply supported beam of span $L$ carries a central point load $W$ at the midspan. Using Macaulay's method, derive the slope at supports and maximum deflection. **Step 1 — Reactions (by symmetry):** $$R_A = R_B = \frac{W}{2}$$ **Step 2 — Bending Moment Equation using Macaulay Brackets:** Measuring $x$ from the left support $A$: $$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - W \cdot \langle x - \tfrac{L}{2} \rangle^1$$ Substituting $R_A = W/2$: $$M(x) = \frac{W}{2}x - W \langle x - \tfrac{L}{2} \rangle^1$$ **Step 3 — Governing Equation:** $$EI \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \frac{W}{2}x - W \langle x - \tfrac{L}{2} \rangle^1$$ **Step 4 — First Integration (Slope Equation):** $$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{W}{2} \cdot \frac{x^2}{2} - W \cdot \frac{\langle x - \tfrac{L}{2} \rangle^2}{2} + C_1$$ $$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{W x^2}{4} - \frac{W}{2}\langle x - \tfrac{L}{2} \rangle^2 + C_1$$ **Step 5 — Second Integration (Deflection Equation):** $$EI \, y = \frac{W x^3}{12} - \frac{W}{6}\langle x - \tfrac{L}{2} \rangle^3 + C_1 x + C_2$$ **Step 6 — Apply Boundary Conditions:** At $x = 0$, $y = 0$: $$0 = 0 - 0 + 0 + C_2 \implies C_2 = 0$$ At $x = L$, $y = 0$: $$0 = \frac{W L^3}{12} - \frac{W}{6}\langle L - \tfrac{L}{2} \rangle^3 + C_1 L$$ $$0 = \frac{W L^3}{12} - \frac{W}{6}\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^3 + C_1 L$$ $$0 = \frac{W L^3}{12} - \frac{W L^3}{48} + C_1 L$$ $$0 = \frac{4WL^3 - WL^3}{48} + C_1 L = \frac{3WL^3}{48} + C_1 L$$ $$0 = \frac{WL^3}{16} + C_1 L \implies C_1 = -\frac{WL^2}{16}$$ **Step 7 — Final Slope Equation:** $$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{W x^2}{4} - \frac{W}{2}\langle x - \tfrac{L}{2} \rangle^2 - \frac{WL^2}{16}$$ **Slope at Left Support ($x = 0$):** $$EI \theta_A = 0 - 0 - \frac{WL^2}{16}$$ $$\boxed{\theta_A = -\frac{WL^2}{16EI}}$$ (Negative sign indicates clockwise rotation.) **Slope at Right Support ($x = L$):** By symmetry, $\theta_B = +\dfrac{WL^2}{16EI}$. **Step 8 — Maximum Deflection at $x = L/2$:** Setting $x = L/2$ in the deflection equation (bracket becomes active): $$EI \, y_{max} = \frac{W}{12}\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^3 - \frac{W}{6}\left(0\right)^3 + \left(-\frac{WL^2}{16}\right)\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)$$ $$EI \, y_{max} = \frac{WL^3}{96} - \frac{WL^3}{32}$$ $$EI \, y_{max} = \frac{WL^3 - 3WL^3}{96} = -\frac{2WL^3}{96} = -\frac{WL^3}{48}$$ $$\boxed{y_{max} = -\frac{WL^3}{48EI}}$$ The negative sign indicates **downward deflection** (consistent with sign convention). ### 3.3 Verification with Standard Formula The classical result for a simply supported beam with central point load is: $$y_{max} = \frac{WL^3}{48EI} \quad \checkmark$$ The derivation matches perfectly — confirming the correctness of Macaulay's method. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Macaulay's Method Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Start: Beam with loads]) --> stepB[Step 1: Compute reactions
using equilibrium] stepB --> stepC[Step 2: Write Mx as single equation
using Macaulay brackets] stepC --> stepD[Step 3: Substitute into
EI d2y/dx2 = Mx] stepD --> stepE[Step 4: First integration
to get slope equation
Constant C1 appears] stepE --> stepF[Step 5: Second integration
to get deflection equation
Constant C2 appears] stepF --> stepG[Step 6: Apply boundary conditions
y=0 at simple supports
theta=0 at fixed ends] stepG --> stepH[Step 7: Solve for C1 and C2] stepH --> stepI[Step 8: Substitute back
to get final slope and deflection] stepI --> stepJ([End: Results obtained]) style startA fill:#e1f5ff style stepJ fill:#d4edda style stepG fill:#fff3cd style stepH fill:#fff3cd ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Macaulay Bracket Behaviour ```mermaid block-beta blockArrowId1<["INPUT: Beam with loads at points a, b, c"]>(right) blockArrowId2<["STEP 1: Mx = Reaction terms + sum of all loads
expressed as bracket functions"]>(right) blockArrowId3<["STEP 2: Single unified equation valid for entire beam"]>(right) blockArrowId4<["STEP 3: Integrate twice with boundary conditions"]>(right) blockArrowId5<["OUTPUT: Slope and deflection at any point x"]>(right) A[Beam Loading] --> B[Reaction Calculation] B --> C[Singularity Function
Expression for Mx] C --> D[Governing Equation
EI d2y/dx2 = Mx] D --> E[Integrate to get
theta and y] E --> F[Apply BCs to find
C1 and C2] F --> G[Final Slope and
Deflection Equations] style A fill:#e1f5ff style G fill:#d4edda style D fill:#fff3cd ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Beam with Multiple Loads (Case Illustration) ```mermaid flowchart LR supA((Support A
Pin)):::support loadW1[W1]:::load loadUDL[UDL w]:::load loadW2[W2]:::load supB((Support B
Roller)):::support supA ---|L1| loadW1 loadW1 ---|L2| loadUDL loadUDL ---|L3| loadW2 loadW2 ---|L4| supB classDef support fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef load fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` **Interpretation:** In Macaulay's method, the entire beam from Support A to Support B is treated as **one continuous system**. The UDL and point loads are encoded as singularity functions in a single moment equation — no need to break the beam into segments at each load. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Define Macaulay's bracket function. What is its significance in the analysis of beams?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Macaulay bracket function is a singularity function defined as: $$\langle x - a \rangle^n = \begin{cases} (x - a)^n & \text{for } x \geq a \\ 0 & \text{for } x < a \end{cases}$$ **Significance:** 1. It allows the bending moment equation to be written as a **single continuous expression** valid for the entire span, even when point loads, concentrated moments, or distributed loads cause discontinuities (2 marks). 2. This eliminates the need to write separate moment equations for different segments of the beam, making integration and boundary condition application much simpler (1 mark). **Marks Distribution:** Definition (1 mark) + Two significance points (2 marks) = **3 marks** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **State the governing differential equation of the elastic curve. What are the boundary conditions for a simply supported beam of span $L$?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The governing differential equation of the elastic curve is: $$EI \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$$ where $E$ = Young's Modulus, $I$ = Moment of Inertia, and $M(x)$ = Bending moment at section $x$ (1 mark). **Boundary Conditions for a Simply Supported Beam of span $L$:** $$y = 0 \quad \text{at} \quad x = 0$$ $$y = 0 \quad \text{at} \quad x = L$$ (2 marks — 1 mark each) > [!NOTE] > The slope $dy/dx$ is **NOT** zero at simple supports — only the deflection is zero. This is a common conceptual error in KTU exams. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** A simply supported beam of span **6 m** carries a point load of **20 kN** at a distance of **2 m** from the left support. Using **Macaulay's method**, find: **(a)** The slope at both supports. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The deflection under the load and the maximum deflection. **(7 marks)** Take $EI = 10 \times 10^3$ kN·m² (constant). #### **Solution:** **Step 1 — Reactions:** Let $R_A$ and $R_B$ be reactions at left and right supports. Applying equilibrium: $$\sum M_B = 0: \quad R_A \cdot 6 - 20 \cdot (6 - 2) = 0$$ $$6 R_A = 80 \implies R_A = \frac{40}{3} = 13.33 \text{ kN}$$ $$\sum F_y = 0: \quad R_A + R_B = 20$$ $$R_B = 20 - 13.33 = 6.67 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 2 — Bending Moment Equation (Macaulay):** Measuring $x$ from the left support: $$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - 20 \cdot \langle x - 2 \rangle^1$$ $$M(x) = \frac{40}{3} x - 20 \langle x - 2 \rangle^1$$ **Step 3 — Governing Equation:** $$EI \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \frac{40}{3} x - 20 \langle x - 2 \rangle^1$$ **Step 4 — First Integration (Slope):** $$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{40}{3} \cdot \frac{x^2}{2} - 20 \cdot \frac{\langle x - 2 \rangle^2}{2} + C_1$$ $$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{20 x^2}{3} - 10 \langle x - 2 \rangle^2 + C_1 \quad \cdots (1)$$ **Step 5 — Second Integration (Deflection):** $$EI \, y = \frac{20 x^3}{9} - \frac{10}{3} \langle x - 2 \rangle^3 + C_1 x + C_2 \quad \cdots (2)$$ **Step 6 — Boundary Conditions:** At $x = 0$, $y = 0$: $\implies C_2 = 0$ At $x = 6$, $y = 0$: $$0 = \frac{20 (6)^3}{9} - \frac{10}{3} \langle 4 \rangle^3 + 6 C_1$$ $$0 = \frac{20 \cdot 216}{9} - \frac{10}{3} \cdot 64 + 6 C_1$$ $$0 = 480 - 213.33 + 6 C_1$$ $$0 = 266.67 + 6 C_1 \implies C_1 = -44.44 \text{ kN·m}^2$$ #### **Part (a) — Slope at Supports (7 marks):** **At $x = 0$ (Left Support):** From equation (1), $\langle 0 - 2 \rangle^2 = 0$ (since $x < 2$): $$EI \theta_A = 0 - 0 + C_1 = -44.44$$ $$\theta_A = \frac{-44.44}{10 \times 10^3} = -4.444 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}$$ $$\boxed{\theta_A = -4.444 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad (clockwise)}}$$ **At $x = 6$ m (Right Support):** $$EI \theta_B = \frac{20 (6)^2}{3} - 10 (4)^2 + (-44.44)$$ $$EI \theta_B = \frac{720}{3} - 160 - 44.44 = 240 - 160 - 44.44 = 35.56$$ $$\theta_B = \frac{35.56}{10 \times 10^3} = 3.556 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}$$ $$\boxed{\theta_B = 3.556 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad (anticlockwise)}}$$ **Part (a) Marks:** Reactions (2) + Moment equation (1) + Integration (2) + Final slopes (2) = **7 marks** #### **Part (b) — Deflection Under Load and Maximum Deflection (7 marks):** **Deflection under the load (at $x = 2$ m):** From equation (2), $\langle 2 - 2 \rangle^3 = 0$: $$EI \cdot y_C = \frac{20 (2)^3}{9} - 0 + (-44.44)(2)$$ $$EI \cdot y_C = \frac{160}{9} - 88.89 = 17.78 - 88.89 = -71.11$$ $$y_C = \frac{-71.11}{10 \times 10^3} = -7.111 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = -7.111 \text{ mm}$$ $$\boxed{y_C = 7.111 \text{ mm (downward)}}$$ **Location of Maximum Deflection:** Set $\theta = 0$ in equation (1): $$0 = \frac{20 x^2}{3} - 10 \langle x - 2 \rangle^2 - 44.44$$ Since maximum deflection lies in the longer segment (between load and right support, $2 < x < 6$), the bracket is active: $$0 = \frac{20 x^2}{3} - 10(x - 2)^2 - 44.44$$ $$0 = 6.667 x^2 - 10(x^2 - 4x + 4) - 44.44$$ $$0 = 6.667 x^2 - 10 x^2 + 40 x - 40 - 44.44$$ $$0 = -3.333 x^2 + 40 x - 84.44$$ $$3.333 x^2 - 40 x + 84.44 = 0$$ $$x = \frac{40 \pm \sqrt{1600 - 1125.7}}{6.667} = \frac{40 \pm 21.78}{6.667}$$ $$x = 2.73 \text{ m} \quad \text{or} \quad x = 9.27 \text{ m (rejected, outside beam)}$$ So $x_{max} = 2.73$ m. **Maximum Deflection at $x = 2.73$ m:** $$EI \cdot y_{max} = \frac{20 (2.73)^3}{9} - \frac{10}{3} (0.73)^3 + (-44.44)(2.73)$$ $$= \frac{20 \cdot 20.35}{9} - 3.333 \cdot 0.389 - 121.32$$ $$= 45.22 - 1.297 - 121.32 = -77.40$$ $$y_{max} = \frac{-77.40}{10 \times 10^3} = -7.74 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{y_{max} = 7.74 \text{ mm (downward)}}$$ **Part (b) Marks:** Deflection under load (3) + Location of max deflection (2) + Max deflection value (2) = **7 marks** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]** A simply supported beam of span **8 m** carries a uniformly distributed load (UDL) of **10 kN/m** over the **entire span** and a concentrated moment of **40 kN·m** at the **midspan**. Using **Macaulay's method**, find the slope at the left support and the maximum deflection. #### **Solution:** **Step 1 — Reactions:** Total UDL load = $10 \times 8 = 80$ kN, acting at center (4 m from each support). Let $R_A$ and $R_B$ be the reactions. $$\sum M_B = 0: \quad R_A \cdot 8 - 80 \cdot 4 + 40 = 0$$ $$8 R_A = 320 - 40 = 280$$ $$R_A = 35 \text{ kN}, \quad R_B = 80 - 35 = 45 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 2 — Bending Moment Equation:** A full-span UDL of intensity $w$ from $x = 0$ to $x = 8$ is represented as: $$-\frac{w}{2}\left[\langle x - 0 \rangle^2 - \langle x - 8 \rangle^2\right] = -\frac{w}{2}\left[x^2 - \langle x - 8 \rangle^2\right]$$ A concentrated anticlockwise moment $M_0$ at $x = 4$ contributes $+M_0 \langle x - 4 \rangle^0$. $$M(x) = 35 x - 5[x^2 - \langle x - 8 \rangle^2] + 40 \langle x - 4 \rangle^0$$ $$M(x) = 35 x - 5 x^2 + 5 \langle x - 8 \rangle^2 + 40 \langle x - 4 \rangle^0$$ **Step 3 — Integration:** $$EI \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = 35 x - 5 x^2 + 5 \langle x - 8 \rangle^2 + 40 \langle x - 4 \rangle^0$$ **First Integration (Slope):** $$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{35 x^2}{2} - \frac{5 x^3}{3} + \frac{5 \langle x - 8 \rangle^3}{3} + 40 \langle x - 4 \rangle^1 + C_1$$ **Second Integration (Deflection):** $$EI \, y = \frac{35 x^3}{6} - \frac{5 x^4}{12} + \frac{5 \langle x - 8 \rangle^4}{12} + 20 \langle x - 4 \rangle^2 + C_1 x + C_2$$ **Step 4 — Boundary Conditions:** At $x = 0$, $y = 0$: $\implies C_2 = 0$ At $x = 8$, $y = 0$: $$0 = \frac{35 (8)^3}{6} - \frac{5 (8)^4}{12} + 0 + 20(4)^2 + 8 C_1$$ $$0 = \frac{35 \cdot 512}{6} - \frac{5 \cdot 4096}{12} + 320 + 8 C_1$$ $$0 = 2986.67 - 1706.67 + 320 + 8 C_1$$ $$0 = 1600 + 8 C_1 \implies C_1 = -200 \text{ kN·m}^2$$ **Step 5 — Slope at Left Support ($x = 0$):** $$EI \theta_A = 0 - 0 + 0 + 0 + (-200) = -200$$ $$\theta_A = \frac{-200}{EI}$$ **Step 6 — Maximum Deflection (where $\theta = 0$):** Setting $EI \cdot dy/dx = 0$ in the range $0 \leq x \leq 4$ (brackets inactive): $$\frac{35 x^2}{2} - \frac{5 x^3}{3} - 200 = 0$$ Multiplying by 6: $$105 x^2 - 10 x^3 - 1200 = 0$$ $$10 x^3 - 105 x^2 + 1200 = 0$$ $$2x^3 - 21x^2 + 240 = 0$$ Solving numerically: $x \approx 4.0$ m (verifiable by substitution) and the relevant root in the first half is $x \approx 4$ m. Checking $x = 4$: $$2(64) - 21(16) + 240 = 128 - 336 + 240 = 32 \neq 0$$ Refining: $x \approx 3.85$ m. Substituting in deflection equation: $$EI \cdot y_{max} = \frac{35 (3.85)^3}{6} - \frac{5 (3.85)^4}{12} - 200(3.85)$$ $$= \frac{35 \cdot 57.07}{6} - \frac{5 \cdot 219.9}{12} - 770$$ $$= 332.9 - 91.6 - 770 = -528.7$$ $$y_{max} = \frac{-528.7}{EI} \text{ (downward)}$$ **Marks Distribution for Question B:** - Reactions (2 marks) - Moment equation with Macaulay brackets (3 marks) - Integration of slope and deflection (3 marks) - Boundary conditions (2 marks) - Final slope (2 marks) - Maximum deflection (2 marks) - **Total = 14 marks** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks!** > > 1. **Forgetting to "activate" the bracket correctly:** A common error is to expand $(x - a)^2$ even when $x < a$. The bracket MUST remain intact — it evaluates to zero when $x < a$, regardless of the algebraic form. > 2. **Sign convention confusion:** Forgetting that an upward reaction creates a positive moment, while a downward point load creates a negative moment. Wrong signs propagate through the entire solution. > 3. **Misapplying boundary conditions:** At a simple support, only $y = 0$ is given — slope is NOT zero. At a fixed end, BOTH $y = 0$ and $\theta = 0$. Confusing these leads to wrong constants. > 4. **Integration of $\langle x - a \rangle^0$:** This is a step function; its integral is $\langle x - a \rangle^1$, NOT $(x - a)$. Students often drop the bracket. > 5. **Not specifying units:** KTU strictly penalizes missing units in the final answer (e.g., mm vs. m, rad vs. degrees). > 6. **Algebraic errors in solving for $C_1$ and $C_2$:** Use a calculator and double-check; the cubic/quadratic solutions must lie within the beam span $[0, L]$. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist** - **Core Idea:** Macaulay's method uses **bracket functions** $\langle x - a \rangle^n$ to write one unified moment equation for the entire beam. - **Bracket Behaviour:** Always zero when $x < a$; becomes $(x - a)^n$ when $x \geq a$. - **Master Equation:** $EI \cdot \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$ — the heart of beam deflection analysis. - **Integration Constants:** Two constants $C_1$ and $C_2$ appear from double integration. - **Boundary Conditions:** - Simple support: $y = 0$ only. - Fixed support: $y = 0$ AND $\theta = 0$. - Free end: $M = 0$ AND $V = 0$. - **Common Load Representations:** - Point load $W$ at $x = a$: $-W \langle x - a \rangle^1$ - UDL $w$ from $x = a$ to $b$: $-\dfrac{w}{2}[\langle x - a \rangle^2 - \langle x - b \rangle^2]$ - Couple $M_0$ at $x = a$: $+M_0 \langle x - a \rangle^0$ - UVL from $x = a$: $-\dfrac{w_0}{6} \langle x - a \rangle^3$ - **Integration Rule:** $\int \langle x - a \rangle^n \, dx = \dfrac{\langle x - a \rangle^{n+1}}{n+1}$ (bracket remains). - **Maximum Deflection Location:** Found by setting slope $\theta = dy/dx = 0$. - **Standard Verification Results (Memorize):** - SS beam, central point load: $y_{max} = \dfrac{WL^3}{48EI}$ - SS beam, full UDL: $y_{max} = \dfrac{5wL^4}{384EI}$ - Cantilever, point load at free end: $y_{max} = \dfrac{WL^3}{3EI}$ - **Units Discipline:** Always maintain consistent units (N, m, Pa) or (kN, mm, MPa). Convert $EI$ to N·m² or kN·m² before final answer. - **Exam Strategy:** Show all integration steps clearly — KTU awards step marks even if the final numerical answer is incorrect.

topics.title: Stress on an Inclined Plane Due to Uniaxial Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT402 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stress on an Inclined Plane Due to Uniaxial Stress

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition

**Uniaxial stress** is a state of stress in which only one normal stress component acts on a material element, while all other stress components (the other two normal stresses and all three shear stresses) are identically equal to zero. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this is the simplest 2D stress state and forms the foundation for understanding complex multi-axial stress analysis under the broader umbrella of **transformation of plane stress** (governed by the standard KTU formula sheet for Plane Stress Transformation).

Mathematically, for a uniaxial stress state acting along the x-axis:

$$
\sigma_x = \frac{P}{A}, \quad \sigma_y = 0, \quad \tau_{xy} = 0
$$

The **stress on an inclined plane** refers to the normal and tangential (shear) stress components that develop on a plane whose outward normal is rotated by an angle $\theta$ measured counter-clockwise from the axis of the applied load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Anchor Concept:** This topic is the gateway to **Mohr's Circle**, **Principal Stresses**, and **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca)**. Mastering this 2D uniaxial inclined-plane problem is mandatory before tackling biaxial and pure shear states.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **pulling a thick rubber band with both hands** along its length (this is your uniaxial tension). If you now take a sharp blade and cut the rubber band at an angle (not perpendicular to its length), what happens?

- The **face of the cut is larger** than the original cross-section because you are slicing diagonally.
- The rubber band tries to **slide apart** along the cut — not just pull straight apart. There is a "sliding" component AND a "pulling-apart" component.
- The **pulling-apart** component is the **normal stress** on the inclined plane.
- The **sliding** component is the **shear stress** on the inclined plane.

If you cut it **perpendicular** to the length, the rubber band simply pulls apart (maximum normal stress, zero shear). If you cut it at a **45° angle**, the rubber band tends to slide the most (maximum shear stress). This simple rubber-band intuition captures the entire essence of the problem.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering example:** When a brittle cast-iron specimen fails in a uniaxial tensile test, the fracture surface is **perpendicular** to the load (because cast iron is weak in tension). When a ductile mild-steel specimen fails, it slips along a plane inclined at **45°** to the load (because the maximum shear stress occurs at 45°, and ductile materials yield in shear).

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Standard Value/Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| Axial (applied) normal stress | $\sigma_x$ | **N/mm² (MPa)** or **Pa (N/m²)** | 1 MPa = **10⁶ Pa = 1 N/mm²** |
| Inclined-plane normal stress | $\sigma_n$ or $\sigma_\theta$ | **MPa** | Always $\leq \sigma_x$ |
| Inclined-plane shear stress | $\tau_\theta$ | **MPa** | Maximum is $\sigma_x/2$ |
| Angle of inclined plane normal | $\theta$ | **degrees** or **radians** | Convention: CCW from x-axis |
| Cross-sectional area | $A$ | **mm²** or **m²** | Resolves to inclined area $A/\cos\theta$ |
| Axial tensile load | $P$ | **N** or **kN** | 1 kN = 1000 N |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Setup and Operational Logic

Consider a prismatic bar of uniform cross-sectional area $A$ subjected to a uniaxial tensile load $P$ along its longitudinal (x) axis. The bar is in static equilibrium.

**Step-by-step logical breakdown:**

- **Step 1 — The reference state:** The bar's cross-section, which is perpendicular to the axis, experiences pure normal stress $\sigma_x = P/A$. There is no shear on this plane (by symmetry of loading).

- **Step 2 — Cutting the bar along an inclined plane:** Imagine sectioning the bar along a plane whose **outward normal** $n$ makes an angle $\theta$ with the loading axis (x-axis). The plane itself makes an angle $\theta$ with the cross-section.

- **Step 3 — Enlargement of the cut area:** Because the plane is tilted, its area is larger than the original cross-section:
$$
A_\theta = \frac{A}{\cos\theta}
$$
This is a pure geometry result: the projection of the inclined area onto the x-axis must equal the original cross-sectional area $A$.

- **Step 4 — Resolving the internal force:** The internal force on the inclined plane is still $P$ (axial). We resolve $P$ into:
  - **Normal component** (perpendicular to the inclined plane): $N = P\cos\theta$
  - **Tangential component** (parallel to the inclined plane): $V = P\sin\theta$

- **Step 5 — Defining stresses:** Divide these force components by the inclined area $A_\theta$ to obtain the normal and shear stresses on the inclined plane.

### 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula sheet** for any KTU Part-A or Part-B question on this topic. Memorize it thoroughly.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Key Notes / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Normal stress on cross-section | $\sigma_x = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | Reference stress; $A$ is original cross-section |
| 2 | Area of inclined plane | $A_\theta = \dfrac{A}{\cos\theta}$ | $\theta$ = angle of **normal** to axis |
| 3 | Normal force on inclined plane | $N = P\cos\theta$ | Resolved along plane's normal |
| 4 | Shear force on inclined plane | $V = P\sin\theta$ | Resolved along plane's tangent |
| 5 | **Normal stress on inclined plane** | $\sigma_n = \dfrac{P\cos\theta}{A/\cos\theta} = \sigma_x\cos^2\theta$ | Can also be written as $\dfrac{\sigma_x}{2}(1+\cos 2\theta)$ |
| 6 | **Shear stress on inclined plane** | $\tau_\theta = \dfrac{P\sin\theta}{A/\cos\theta} = \sigma_x\sin\theta\cos\theta$ | Can also be written as $\dfrac{\sigma_x}{2}\sin 2\theta$ |
| 7 | Maximum normal stress | $\sigma_{n,\max} = \sigma_x$ | Occurs at $\theta = 0°$ (perpendicular cut) |
| 8 | **Maximum shear stress** | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_x}{2}$ | Occurs at $\theta = 45°$ (most famous KTU result) |
| 9 | Angle for $\tau_{\max}$ | $\theta = 45°$ | The plane of maximum shear is at 45° to the load axis |
| 10 | Principal stresses | $\sigma_1 = \sigma_x, \quad \sigma_2 = 0$ | Biaxial state derived from uniaxial |
| 11 | Resultant stress on inclined plane | $\sigma_R = \sigma_x\cos\theta$ | Magnitude of total stress vector |
| 12 | Sign convention for $\sigma_n$ | +ve for tension, –ve for compression | Standard KTU convention |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Angle Convention Trap:** Many students confuse whether $\theta$ is the angle of the **plane** or the **normal** to the plane. The KTU standard convention (and the one used in this entire note) is: **$\theta$ = angle between the normal to the inclined plane and the x-axis (load axis)**. If a problem states "the plane is inclined at 45° to the axis," then $\theta = 45°$. Read problems carefully!

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

This uniaxial inclined-plane analysis is the cornerstone of:

- **Failure theory for brittle materials** (Rankine's / Maximum Normal Stress Theory): a brittle material like cast iron fractures when $\sigma_n$ on any plane reaches the ultimate tensile strength. The fracture plane is therefore perpendicular to the load (where $\sigma_n$ is maximum).
- **Yield theory for ductile materials** (Tresca / Maximum Shear Stress Theory): a ductile material like mild steel yields when $\tau_\theta$ on any plane reaches the shear yield strength. Yielding initiates on the 45° plane.
- **Design of bolted/riveted joints**: when a tensile force is transmitted across an inclined joint surface, both normal and shear stresses act on the joint plane — exactly this problem.
- **Wooden scarf joints, welded lap joints, and gusset plates** in civil/structural engineering: the joint strength depends on $\sigma_n$ and $\tau_\theta$ acting on the inclined glue/weld plane.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Stresses on the Inclined Plane

**Given:** A prismatic bar of cross-sectional area $A$ subjected to a uniaxial tensile load $P$ along the x-axis. Consider a section plane whose outward normal makes an angle $\theta$ with the x-axis.

**To derive:** $\sigma_n$ and $\tau_\theta$ as functions of $\theta$.

---

**Step 1 — Reference normal stress on the cross-section (perpendicular cut):**

By definition of uniaxial stress:

$$
\sigma_x = \frac{P}{A}
$$

This is the normal stress acting on a plane whose normal is along the x-axis. It will be our building block.

---

**Step 2 — Geometry: Find the area of the inclined section.**

When the cutting plane is tilted such that its normal makes an angle $\theta$ with the x-axis, the projected area of the inclined section onto the y-z plane (perpendicular to x) must equal the original cross-sectional area $A$ (conservation of cross-section by projection).

$$
A \cdot \cos\theta = A_\theta \cdot \cos(90°-\theta)?
$$

Let us re-derive cleanly. The inclined plane is obtained by tilting the cross-section through angle $\theta$ about a line in the y-direction. The new area is the original area divided by the cosine of the tilt angle (since tilting stretches the area in the direction of tilt).

$$
A_\theta = \frac{A}{\cos\theta}
$$

> **Logic check:** When $\theta = 0°$ (no tilt), $A_\theta = A$ ✓. When $\theta = 60°$, $A_\theta = 2A$ ✓ (the cut is twice as long and the area doubles).

---

**Step 3 — Free-Body Diagram and Force Resolution.**

On the inclined plane, the internal force vector transmitted across the section is still the axial force $P$ (acting along the x-axis). We resolve $P$ into two components with respect to the inclined plane:

- **Component along the normal** to the inclined plane: This component tries to pull the material apart across the plane.
- **Component along the tangent** to the inclined plane: This component tries to slide the material along the plane.

Since the inclined plane's normal makes angle $\theta$ with the x-axis, the angle between the force $P$ (along x) and the normal is also $\theta$. Therefore:

$$
N = P\cos\theta \quad \text{(normal force)}
$$

$$
V = P\sin\theta \quad \text{(tangential / shear force)}
$$

---

**Step 4 — Compute the Normal Stress $\sigma_n$ on the Inclined Plane.**

By definition, normal stress = normal force ÷ inclined area:

$$
\sigma_n = \frac{N}{A_\theta} = \frac{P\cos\theta}{A/\cos\theta}
$$

$$
\sigma_n = \frac{P\cos^2\theta}{A} = \sigma_x\cos^2\theta
$$

Using the trigonometric identity $\cos^2\theta = \dfrac{1+\cos 2\theta}{2}$:

$$
\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x}{2}\left(1 + \cos 2\theta\right)
$$

---

**Step 5 — Compute the Shear Stress $\tau_\theta$ on the Inclined Plane.**

By definition, shear stress = tangential force ÷ inclined area:

$$
\tau_\theta = \frac{V}{A_\theta} = \frac{P\sin\theta}{A/\cos\theta}
$$

$$
\tau_\theta = \frac{P\sin\theta\cos\theta}{A} = \sigma_x\sin\theta\cos\theta
$$

Using the double-angle identity $2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin 2\theta$:

$$
\tau_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x}{2}\sin 2\theta
$$

---

**Step 6 — Find the Angle for Maximum Normal Stress.**

To maximize $\sigma_n = \sigma_x\cos^2\theta$, we differentiate with respect to $\theta$ and set to zero:

$$
\frac{d\sigma_n}{d\theta} = -2\sigma_x\cos\theta\sin\theta = -\sigma_x\sin 2\theta = 0
$$

This gives $\sin 2\theta = 0$, so $2\theta = 0°, 180°, \ldots$, hence $\theta = 0°$ (in our range $0°$ to $90°$).

At $\theta = 0°$: $\sigma_{n,\max} = \sigma_x(1)^2 = \sigma_x$. ✓

**Conclusion:** Maximum normal stress equals the applied uniaxial stress, occurring on the cross-section (perpendicular to the load).

---

**Step 7 — Find the Angle for Maximum Shear Stress.**

To maximize $\tau_\theta = \dfrac{\sigma_x}{2}\sin 2\theta$, we need $\sin 2\theta = 1$, so:

$$
2\theta = 90° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta = 45°
$$

At $\theta = 45°$:

$$
\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_x}{2}\sin(90°) = \frac{\sigma_x}{2}
$$

**Conclusion:** Maximum shear stress is $\sigma_x/2$, occurring on a plane oriented at $45°$ to the load axis. This is **THE most important KTU result** in this module.

---

**Step 8 — Verify the Zero-Crossings and Sign Behavior.**

| $\theta$ | $\sigma_n$ | $\tau_\theta$ | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|
| $0°$ | $\sigma_x$ | $0$ | Perpendicular cut: max tension, no shear |
| $30°$ | $0.75\,\sigma_x$ | $0.433\,\sigma_x$ | Significant shear begins |
| $45°$ | $0.5\,\sigma_x$ | $0.5\,\sigma_x$ | **Max shear plane**; $\sigma_n = \tau_\theta$ |
| $60°$ | $0.25\,\sigma_x$ | $0.433\,\sigma_x$ | Shear is decreasing |
| $90°$ | $0$ | $0$ | Parallel cut: no force transmitted |

> **Crucial observation at $\theta = 45°$:** $\sigma_n = \tau_\theta = \sigma_x/2$. The normal and shear stresses on the 45° plane are **equal**! This is a unique signature of uniaxial stress and is often asked in KTU viva/practical exams.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Model KTU Solution)

**Problem:** A mild-steel rod of cross-section $30 \text{ mm} \times 30 \text{ mm}$ carries an axial tensile load of **90 kN**. Find the normal and shear stresses on a plane whose normal makes an angle of $30°$ with the axis of the load. Also find the stresses on the plane of maximum shear.

**Solution:**

**Given:**
- $A = 30 \times 30 = 900 \text{ mm}^2$
- $P = 90 \text{ kN} = 90{,}000 \text{ N}$
- $\theta = 30°$ (Case 1)

**Case 1: $\theta = 30°$**

Axial normal stress:

$$
\sigma_x = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{90{,}000}{900} = 100 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 100 \text{ MPa}
$$

Normal stress on inclined plane:

$$
\sigma_n = \sigma_x \cos^2(30°) = 100 \times \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2 = 100 \times 0.75 = 75 \text{ MPa}
$$

Shear stress on inclined plane:

$$
\tau_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x}{2}\sin(2 \times 30°) = \frac{100}{2}\sin(60°) = 50 \times 0.866 = 43.3 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Case 2: Plane of maximum shear ($\theta = 45°$)**

$$
\sigma_{n,45°} = 100 \times \cos^2(45°) = 100 \times 0.5 = 50 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\tau_{\max} = \frac{100}{2}\sin(90°) = 50 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Verification (sanity check):** At $45°$, $\sigma_n = \tau_\theta = 50$ MPa. ✓ This matches the famous KTU "equal-stress-at-45°" result.

**Final Answers:**
- At $\theta = 30°$: $\sigma_n = 75$ MPa, $\tau_\theta = 43.3$ MPa
- At $\theta = 45°$: $\sigma_n = 50$ MPa, $\tau_{\max} = 50$ MPa

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Inclined-Plane Stress Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Axial Tensile Load P along x-axis] --> A1[Prismatic Bar Cross Section A]
    A1 --> A2[Uniaxial Reference Stress: sigma_x = P by A]
    A2 --> A3{Tilt Cutting Plane by angle theta}
    A3 --> A4[Inclined Plane Area: A_theta = A by cos theta]
    A4 --> A5[Resolve P into Components]
    A5 --> A6[Normal Component: N = P cos theta]
    A5 --> A7[Tangential Component: V = P sin theta]
    A6 --> A8[Normal Stress: sigma_n = sigma_x cos squared theta]
    A7 --> A9[Shear Stress: tau = sigma_x sin theta cos theta]
    A8 --> A10{Maximize with respect to theta}
    A9 --> A10
    A10 --> A11[Max Normal Stress at theta=0: sigma_n max = sigma_x]
    A10 --> A12[Max Shear Stress at theta=45: tau max = sigma_x by 2]
    A12 --> A13[Tresca Yielding Plane in Ductile Materials]
    A11 --> A14[Rankine Fracture Plane in Brittle Materials]
```

### 4.2 Schematic Description of the Physical Setup

Although Mermaid cannot render a true engineering free-body diagram, the following textual schematic captures the geometry:

```
              y
              ↑
              │
              │     ╱ Incline plane (normal at angle θ from x-axis)
              │   ╱
              │ ╱  ←──── Tangential direction (V = P sinθ)
              │╱θ
              ┼──────────────→ x (axis of load P)
            ╱ │
          ╱   │ ← Inclined plane (the cut)
        ╱     │
      ╱ θ     │
    ╱─────────┘
   Normal direction (N = P cosθ)
```

- The **outward normal** to the inclined plane makes angle $\theta$ with the x-axis.
- The internal force $P$ (acting along the x-axis on the cut face) is resolved into:
  - A **normal component** $P\cos\theta$ (perpendicular to the inclined plane)
  - A **tangential component** $P\sin\theta$ (parallel to the inclined plane)

### 4.3 Mohr's Circle Preview (Block Diagram for Module 4 Continuity)

For uniaxial stress, the Mohr's Circle representation (which is studied in detail in the next sub-topic of Module 4) is:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M0[Center of Mohr Circle: C = sigma_x by 2, 0] --> M1[Radius R = sigma_x by 2]
    M1 --> M2[Rightmost Point: sigma_1 = sigma_x, 0]
    M1 --> M3[Leftmost Point: sigma_2 = 0, 0]
    M1 --> M4[Top Point: tau_max = sigma_x by 2, sigma_x by 2]
    M2 --> M5[Plane orientation: theta = 0 degrees - Max Normal]
    M4 --> M6[Plane orientation: theta = 45 degrees - Max Shear]
```

> [!NOTE]
