> This Mohr's Circle preview directly links the inclined-plane formulas to the graphical method that KTU expects you to master in the next sub-section of Module 4. The radius $R = \sigma_x/2$ appears in **both** the Mohr's Circle and the $\tau_{\max}$ formula — this is no coincidence!

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, Dec 2024 Style]**

**Q1. [3 Marks]** Define uniaxial stress. Derive the expression for normal stress on an inclined plane whose normal makes an angle $\theta$ with the axis of the applied load.

**Model Answer:**

**Uniaxial stress** is a state of stress in which only one normal stress component exists (e.g., $\sigma_x \neq 0$) while all other components ($\sigma_y, \sigma_z, \tau_{xy}, \tau_{yz}, \tau_{zx}$) are zero. It typically arises in a prismatic bar under axial loading.

**Derivation:** Consider a bar of cross-section $A$ under axial tensile load $P$ giving $\sigma_x = P/A$. For a section plane whose normal makes angle $\theta$ with the x-axis, the inclined area is $A_\theta = A/\cos\theta$. The force $P$ resolves into normal component $N = P\cos\theta$ along the plane's normal. Therefore:

$$
\sigma_n = \frac{N}{A_\theta} = \frac{P\cos\theta}{A/\cos\theta} = \frac{P}{A}\cos^2\theta = \sigma_x\cos^2\theta
$$

This is the required expression. **(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for derivation)**

---

**Q2. [3 Marks]** At what angle is the shear stress maximum on an inclined plane under uniaxial stress? State the corresponding expression for maximum shear stress.

**Model Answer:**

Under uniaxial stress $\sigma_x$, the shear stress on an inclined plane is $\tau_\theta = (\sigma_x/2)\sin 2\theta$. This is maximum when $\sin 2\theta = 1$, i.e., $2\theta = 90°$, hence:

$$
\boxed{\theta = 45° \quad \text{and} \quad \tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_x}{2}}
$$

The plane of maximum shear is inclined at $45°$ to the load axis. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Following the KTU ESE pattern, **Part B carries 14 marks** with internal choice. You must attempt **ONE** of the two alternatives below. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**A (a) [7 Marks]** A circular steel rod of diameter 25 mm is subjected to an axial tensile load of 80 kN. Determine the normal and shear stresses on a plane whose normal is inclined at $40°$ to the axis of the rod. Also compute the maximum possible shear stress in the material and the inclination of the plane on which it acts.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Diameter $d = 25$ mm → $A = \pi d^2/4 = \pi(25)^2/4 = 490.87$ mm²
- Axial load $P = 80$ kN = 80,000 N
- Inclination $\theta = 40°$

**Step 1 — Compute the reference uniaxial stress:**

$$
\sigma_x = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{80{,}000}{490.87} = 162.97 \text{ MPa} \approx 163 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\sigma_n$ and $\tau_\theta$ at $\theta = 40°$:**

$$
\sigma_n = \sigma_x \cos^2(40°) = 162.97 \times (0.766)^2 = 162.97 \times 0.5868 = 95.62 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\tau_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x}{2}\sin(2 \times 40°) = \frac{162.97}{2}\sin(80°) = 81.485 \times 0.9848 = 80.24 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum shear stress:**

Maximum shear occurs at $\theta = 45°$:

$$
\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} = \frac{162.97}{2} = 81.49 \text{ MPa} \approx 81.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Final Answers (Sub-part a):**
- $\sigma_n = 95.62$ MPa
- $\tau_\theta = 80.24$ MPa
- $\tau_{\max} = 81.49$ MPa at $\theta = 45°$

---

**A (b) [7 Marks]** A bar of cross-section $50 \text{ mm} \times 50 \text{ mm}$ is subjected to an axial compressive load of 250 kN. Plot the variation of normal stress and shear stress on planes whose normals are inclined at $0°, 30°, 45°, 60°,$ and $90°$ to the axis of loading. Identify the plane on which the normal stress is maximum and the plane on which the shear stress is maximum.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $A = 50 \times 50 = 2500$ mm²
- $P = 250$ kN = 250,000 N (compressive)
- $\sigma_x = -P/A = -250{,}000/2500 = -100$ MPa (negative for compression)

**Compute stresses at each angle (using $\sigma_x = -100$ MPa):**

| $\theta$ | $\cos^2\theta$ | $\sin 2\theta$ | $\sigma_n$ (MPa) | $\tau_\theta$ (MPa) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $0°$ | 1.000 | 0.000 | $-100$ | $0$ |
| $30°$ | 0.750 | 0.866 | $-75$ | $-43.3$ |
| $45°$ | 0.500 | 1.000 | $-50$ | $-50$ |
| $60°$ | 0.250 | 0.866 | $-25$ | $-43.3$ |
| $90°$ | 0.000 | 0.000 | $0$ | $0$ |

(Note: Negative sign indicates compression / direction reversal — magnitude is what's important for KTU problems.)

**Observations from the table:**
- **Maximum normal stress** (in magnitude): $|\sigma_n| = 100$ MPa, occurs at $\theta = 0°$ (plane perpendicular to load axis).
- **Maximum shear stress**: $|\tau_\theta| = 50$ MPa, occurs at $\theta = 45°$ (plane inclined at $45°$ to load axis).

**Plots (described):**
- $\sigma_n$ vs $\theta$ follows a $\cos^2\theta$ curve, starting at $\sigma_x = -100$ MPa at $\theta = 0°$ and decaying to $0$ at $\theta = 90°$.
- $\tau_\theta$ vs $\theta$ follows a $\sin 2\theta$ curve, starting at $0$, peaking at $\theta = 45°$ with $\tau_{\max} = 50$ MPa, and returning to $0$ at $\theta = 90°$.

**Final Answers (Sub-part b):**
- Max normal stress plane: $\theta = 0°$ (perpendicular to axis), $|\sigma_n| = 100$ MPa.
- Max shear stress plane: $\theta = 45°$, $|\tau| = 50$ MPa.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**B (a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expressions for normal stress and shear stress on an inclined plane under uniaxial stress. Using the derived expressions, prove that the maximum shear stress is half the maximum normal stress.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Setup:** Bar of area $A$ under axial load $P$ → $\sigma_x = P/A$. Inclined section has area $A_\theta = A/\cos\theta$.

**Step 2 — Force resolution:** $N = P\cos\theta$, $V = P\sin\theta$.

**Step 3 — Stress formulas:**

$$
\sigma_n = \frac{P\cos\theta}{A/\cos\theta} = \frac{P}{A}\cos^2\theta = \sigma_x\cos^2\theta
$$

$$
\tau_\theta = \frac{P\sin\theta}{A/\cos\theta} = \frac{P}{A}\sin\theta\cos\theta = \frac{\sigma_x}{2}\sin 2\theta
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum normal stress:** $\sigma_{n,\max} = \sigma_x$ at $\theta = 0°$ (since $\cos^2 0° = 1$).

**Step 5 — Maximum shear stress:** $\tau_{\max} = \sigma_x/2$ at $\theta = 45°$ (since $\sin 90° = 1$).

**Step 6 — Ratio proof:**

$$
\frac{\tau_{\max}}{\sigma_{n,\max}} = \frac{\sigma_x/2}{\sigma_x} = \frac{1}{2}
$$

$$
\therefore \tau_{\max} = \frac{1}{2}\sigma_{n,\max} = \frac{\sigma_x}{2}
$$

Hence, maximum shear stress is exactly **half** of the maximum normal stress. **(7 marks: 3 for derivation + 2 for max conditions + 2 for ratio proof)**

---

**B (b) [7 Marks]** An axial pull of 60 kN is applied to a rectangular bar of cross-section $40 \text{ mm} \times 60 \text{ mm}$. Find the magnitude and direction of the resultant stress on a plane whose normal makes an angle of $60°$ with the axis of the bar. Also find the maximum shear stress developed in the bar.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $A = 40 \times 60 = 2400$ mm²
- $P = 60$ kN = 60,000 N
- $\theta = 60°$

**Step 1 — Reference stress:**

$$
\sigma_x = \frac{60{,}000}{2400} = 25 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Stresses on inclined plane ($\theta = 60°$):**

$$
\sigma_n = \sigma_x\cos^2(60°) = 25 \times (0.5)^2 = 25 \times 0.25 = 6.25 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\tau_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x}{2}\sin(2 \times 60°) = \frac{25}{2}\sin(120°) = 12.5 \times 0.866 = 10.825 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Resultant stress magnitude:**

The resultant stress vector on the inclined plane is the vector sum of $\sigma_n$ (normal) and $\tau_\theta$ (tangential):

$$
\sigma_R = \sqrt{\sigma_n^2 + \tau_\theta^2} = \sqrt{(6.25)^2 + (10.825)^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_R = \sqrt{39.0625 + 117.18} = \sqrt{156.24} = 12.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Direction of resultant:** The resultant makes an angle $\alpha$ with the normal to the plane (or with the bar axis):

$$
\tan\alpha = \frac{\tau_\theta}{\sigma_n} = \frac{10.825}{6.25} = 1.732 = \tan(60°)
$$

$$
\therefore \alpha = 60° \quad \text{(with respect to the normal to the inclined plane)}
$$

Equivalently, the resultant makes an angle $\theta - \alpha = 60° - 60° = 0°$ with the x-axis — confirming that the resultant stress vector points along the load direction, as expected (since it is just $P/A_\theta = \sigma_x\cos\theta = 25 \times 0.5 = 12.5$ MPa, which matches $\sigma_R$ ✓).

**Step 4 — Maximum shear stress:**

$$
\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} = \frac{25}{2} = 12.5 \text{ MPa} \text{ at } \theta = 45°
$$

**Final Answers (Sub-part b):**
- $\sigma_n = 6.25$ MPa, $\tau_\theta = 10.825$ MPa
- Resultant stress $\sigma_R = 12.5$ MPa at $60°$ to the plane normal (i.e., along the load axis)
- Maximum shear stress $\tau_{\max} = 12.5$ MPa at $\theta = 45°$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
>
> 1. **Angle Convention Errors (–2 marks):** Confusing the angle of the plane with the angle of the normal. KTU uses $\theta$ as the angle of the **normal**, NOT the plane. If a question says "plane is inclined at 30° to the cross-section," then $\theta = 30°$ (and not 60°).
>
> 2. **Sign of Compressive Stress (–1 mark):** Forgetting to mark compressive $\sigma_x$ as negative. KTU follows strict sign convention: tension = positive, compression = negative.
>
> 3. **Area of Inclined Plane Error (–2 marks):** Using $A_\theta = A\cos\theta$ instead of $A_\theta = A/\cos\theta$. This is the most common error and is heavily penalized.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to find the Inclination of $\tau_{\max}$ plane (–1 mark):** KTU almost always asks "**at what angle does the maximum shear stress occur?**" The answer is $\theta = 45°$ — never omit it.
>
> 5. **Mixing up $\tau = \sigma_x\sin\theta\cos\theta$ with $\tau = \sigma_x/2 \cdot \sin 2\theta$ (–1 mark):** Both forms are correct, but you must show the conversion via the double-angle identity to get full marks.
>
> 6. **Units Mismatch (–1 mark):** Mixing N with mm² vs. MPa, or kN with m². Always check that final stress is in MPa (or N/mm²) when using N and mm.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin This Before Every KTU Exam!**

- **Definition:** Uniaxial stress = only $\sigma_x$ exists; all other components are zero.

- **Core Formulas (memorize verbatim):**
  - $\sigma_n = \sigma_x \cos^2\theta = \dfrac{\sigma_x}{2}(1+\cos 2\theta)$
  - $\tau_\theta = \sigma_x \sin\theta\cos\theta = \dfrac{\sigma_x}{2}\sin 2\theta$
  - $\sigma_R = \sigma_x \cos\theta$ (resultant stress magnitude)

- **Three Famous Angles:**
  - $\theta = 0°$ → Perpendicular cut → $\sigma_n = \sigma_x$, $\tau = 0$ (Rankine fracture plane for brittle materials).
  - $\theta = 45°$ → $\sigma_n = \sigma_x/2$, $\tau = \sigma_x/2$ (Tresca yield plane for ductile materials).
  - $\theta = 90°$ → Parallel cut → $\sigma_n = 0$, $\tau = 0$.

- **Three Maximum/Minimum Facts:**
  - $\sigma_{n,\max} = \sigma_x$ at $\theta = 0°$.
  - $\sigma_{n,\min} = 0$ at $\theta = 90°$.
  - $\tau_{\max} = \sigma_x/2$ at $\theta = 45°$.

- **The "Equal Stress at 45°" Signature:** On the 45° plane under uniaxial stress, $\sigma_n = \tau_\theta = \sigma_x/2$. This is a unique and often-tested result.

- **Inclined Area:** Always $A_\theta = A/\cos\theta$, never $A\cos\theta$. The tilted plane has **larger** area than the cross-section.

- **Angle Convention:** $\theta$ = angle between the **normal** to the inclined plane and the loading (x) axis. KTU standard.

- **Sign Convention:** Tension = positive, Compression = negative (consistent with KTU's default sign convention in Strength of Materials).

- **Linkage to Mohr's Circle:** For uniaxial stress, the Mohr's Circle has center $(\sigma_x/2, 0)$ and radius $\sigma_x/2$. The rightmost point is the principal stress $\sigma_1 = \sigma_x$, the leftmost is $\sigma_2 = 0$, and the top of the circle is the maximum shear point $(\sigma_x/2, \sigma_x/2)$ corresponding to $\theta = 45°$.

- **Failure Theory Linkage:**
  - Brittle materials (cast iron, concrete) → fail by **maximum normal stress** at $\theta = 0°$ → fracture is flat and perpendicular to load.
  - Ductile materials (mild steel, aluminum) → yield by **maximum shear stress** at $\theta = 45°$ → slip lines are at 45° to load axis.

- **Practical Engineering Use:** Bolted joints, welded lap joints, glued wooden joints, gusset plates, riveted connections — all designed using this inclined-plane stress analysis.

- **Common KTU Question Patterns to Expect:**
  1. "Find $\sigma_n$ and $\tau_\theta$ on a plane inclined at angle X°." (Direct substitution — 3 to 7 marks)
  2. "Find the plane of maximum shear and its value." (Apply double-angle condition — 7 marks)
  3. "Prove that $\tau_{\max} = \sigma_x/2$." (Differential calculus or trigonometric argument — 7 marks)
  4. "Plot the variation of $\sigma_n$ and $\tau_\theta$ with $\theta$." (Tabular + sketch — 7 marks)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stress on an Inclined Plane Due to Biaxial Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCSFT402 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Stress on an Inclined Plane Due to Biaxial Stress # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Biaxial Stress State:** A state of stress in a two-dimensional (plane stress) element where normal stresses $\sigma_x$ and $\sigma_y$ act on two mutually perpendicular planes, with or without an accompanying shear stress $\tau_{xy}$ on those planes. The third principal stress (out-of-plane) is taken as **zero** for thin plates. **Stress on an Inclined Plane:** The transformation of stresses (both normal and tangential) acting on an arbitrary plane whose normal is oriented at an angle $\theta$ with respect to the reference x-axis. In KTU 2024 Scheme Strength of Materials, this topic forms the foundation for: - Principal Stress analysis - Mohr's Circle construction - Failure theories (Maximum Normal Stress, Maximum Shear Stress) - Design of pressure vessels, dams, retaining walls, and structural joints --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The Inflated Balloon Analogy:** > Imagine a rectangular rubber patch cut from a fully inflated balloon. The patch is being pulled in two perpendicular directions (biaxial tension). Now, if you cut a small triangular piece at any **inclined angle** from the patch, the forces on that inclined cut will be different from the original vertical/horizontal pulls. This is exactly what happens in: - A **thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel** — hoop stress ($\sigma_h$) and longitudinal stress ($\sigma_l$) act perpendicularly on an element. - A **concrete dam** — vertical gravity load and horizontal water pressure act on a small element. - A **rotating disc** — radial and tangential stresses form a biaxial system. The **inclined plane stress** problem asks: *"What is the normal pull and the sliding force on a tilted cut made through this element?"* --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!TIP] > - **Stress units:** $\text{N/m}^2$ (Pa), $\text{MPa}$ ($10^6$ Pa), $\text{GPa}$ ($10^9$ Pa) > - **Angle convention:** $\theta$ is measured **counter-clockwise** from the x-axis to the outward normal of the inclined plane. > - **Sign convention:** Tensile stress = **Positive**, Compressive stress = **Negative** (Standard KTU/IS convention). --- ## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations > [!WARNING] > - This is one of the **highest weightage topics** in Module 4 (typically 7–14 marks). > - It is a **prerequisite** for Mohr's Circle, Principal Stresses, and Combined Loading. > - Students frequently lose marks on **sign errors** when applying $\cos(2\theta)$ and $\sin(2\theta)$ terms. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Problem Setup — The Inclined Plane Element Consider a rectangular element subjected to biaxial stresses $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, and shear stress $\tau_{xy}$ (positive on the +x face pointing in the +y direction). When we slice this element along a plane whose **outward normal** makes an angle $\theta$ with the **x-axis**, we isolate a **wedge-shaped free body**. ### 2.1.1 Equilibrium of the Wedge Let the inclined face have: - Area = $dA$ - Normal stress = $\sigma_n$ (perpendicular to the cut) - Shear stress = $\tau$ (along the cut) The other two faces (x-face and y-face) have areas $dA \cos\theta$ and $dA \sin\theta$ respectively. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **definitive reference** for KTU Board Examinations on this topic. | # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Notes | |---|----------|---------|--------------------| | 1 | **Normal stress on inclined plane** | $\sigma_n = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)$ | General biaxial case | | 2 | **Shear stress on inclined plane** | $\tau = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)$ | Sign indicates direction | | 3 | **Principal stress (max)** | $\sigma_1 = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Always the largest | | 4 | **Principal stress (min)** | $\sigma_2 = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Always the smallest | | 5 | **Principal plane angle** | $\tan(2\theta_p) = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ | Gives **two** values 90° apart | | 6 | **Maximum shear stress** | $\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Occurs at 45° to principal planes | | 7 | **Plane of max shear** | $\tan(2\theta_s) = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}}$ | Perpendicular to principal planes | | 8 | **Pure biaxial (no shear)** | $\sigma_n = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta)$ | When $\tau_{xy} = 0$ | | 9 | **Pure biaxial shear stress** | $\tau = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta)$ | When $\tau_{xy} = 0$ | | 10 | **Invariant quantity** | $\sigma_x + \sigma_y = \sigma_n + \sigma_n'$ | Sum unchanged on any two perpendicular planes | > [!TIP] > **Units Check:** All stresses in MPa (or N/mm²); Angles in radians inside calculators, but degrees in final answer. --- ## 2.3 Logical Flow of Reasoning (Why and How) - **Why equilibrium?** → The wedge must be in static equilibrium; sum of forces in x and y directions must be zero. - **How to project stresses?** → Each face force must be resolved along x and y axes using trigonometry. - **Why two unknowns?** → We have $\sigma_n$ and $\tau$ as unknowns, and two equilibrium equations ($\Sigma F_x = 0$, $\Sigma F_y = 0$) — yielding a **unique solution**. - **Why does the result depend on $2\theta$?** → Because the inclined area projects as $\cos\theta$ and the stress directions rotate by $\theta$, producing double-angle identities. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Engineering Field | Application | |-------------------|-------------| | Pressure Vessel Design | Hoop & longitudinal stress analysis on inclined weld planes | | Geotechnical Engineering | Stress on inclined failure planes in soil/rock | | Aerospace | Stress on fiber-reinforced laminates at any ply angle | | Civil (Concrete) | Principal stress trajectories in beams and dams | | Mechanical (Shafts) | Combined bending + torsion creating biaxial state | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Normal Stress ($\sigma_n$) and Shear Stress ($\tau$) on an Inclined Plane ### 3.1.1 Free Body Setup Consider an element of unit thickness with stresses $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, and $\tau_{xy}$ acting on faces perpendicular to the x and y axes. Slice the element along a plane **DE** whose outward normal is at angle $\theta$ to the x-axis. Let: - Face DE (inclined) = area $dA$ - Face OD (along y-axis) = area $dA \sin\theta$ (i.e., $dA \cos\theta$ in some texts; we follow $\theta$ from x-axis) - Face OE (along x-axis) = area $dA \cos\theta$ On the inclined face DE, the unknown stresses are $\sigma_n$ (normal, outward) and $\tau$ (shear, taken positive when it tends to rotate the wedge counter-clockwise). --- ### 3.1.2 Resolving Forces Along the x-direction Forces acting in the x-direction on the wedge: - On face OE (x-face): stress $\sigma_x$ acting on area $dA\cos\theta$ → Force = $\sigma_x \cdot dA\cos\theta$ - On face OD (y-face): shear stress $\tau_{xy}$ acting on area $dA\sin\theta$ → Force = $\tau_{xy} \cdot dA\sin\theta$ - On face DE (inclined): $\sigma_n$ and $\tau$ projected on x-axis: - $\sigma_n$ projected on x: $\sigma_n \cos\theta \cdot dA$ - $\tau$ projected on x: $-\tau \sin\theta \cdot dA$ (since $\tau$ is along DE, its x-component is opposite) **Equilibrium equation in x-direction ($\Sigma F_x = 0$):** $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_x \cdot dA\cos\theta + \tau_{xy} \cdot dA\sin\theta - \sigma_n \cos\theta \cdot dA - \tau \sin\theta \cdot dA &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ Dividing throughout by $dA$ and rearranging: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_n \cos\theta + \tau \sin\theta &= \sigma_x \cos\theta + \tau_{xy} \sin\theta \quad \text{...(Equation 1)} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### 3.1.3 Resolving Forces Along the y-direction Forces acting in the y-direction on the wedge: - On face OD (y-face): $\sigma_y$ acting on area $dA\sin\theta$ → Force = $\sigma_y \cdot dA\sin\theta$ - On face OE (x-face): shear $\tau_{xy}$ acting on area $dA\cos\theta$ → Force = $\tau_{xy} \cdot dA\cos\theta$ - On face DE: $\sigma_n$ and $\tau$ projected on y-axis: - $\sigma_n$ projected on y: $\sigma_n \sin\theta \cdot dA$ - $\tau$ projected on y: $\tau \cos\theta \cdot dA$ (positive since along DE upward) **Equilibrium equation in y-direction ($\Sigma F_y = 0$):** $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_y \cdot dA\sin\theta + \tau_{xy} \cdot dA\cos\theta - \sigma_n \sin\theta \cdot dA + \tau \cos\theta \cdot dA &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ Dividing by $dA$ and rearranging: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_n \sin\theta - \tau \cos\theta &= \sigma_y \sin\theta - \tau_{xy} \cos\theta \quad \text{...(Equation 2)} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### 3.1.4 Solving the Simultaneous Equations **Equation 1:** $\sigma_n \cos\theta + \tau \sin\theta = \sigma_x \cos\theta + \tau_{xy} \sin\theta$ **Equation 2:** $\sigma_n \sin\theta - \tau \cos\theta = \sigma_y \sin\theta - \tau_{xy} \cos\theta$ **Step 1:** Multiply Equation 1 by $\cos\theta$: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_n \cos^2\theta + \tau \sin\theta \cos\theta &= \sigma_x \cos^2\theta + \tau_{xy} \sin\theta \cos\theta \quad \text{...(1a)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Multiply Equation 2 by $\sin\theta$: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_n \sin^2\theta - \tau \sin\theta \cos\theta &= \sigma_y \sin^2\theta - \tau_{xy} \sin\theta \cos\theta \quad \text{...(2a)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Add (1a) and (2a) — the $\tau$ terms cancel: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_n (\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta) &= \sigma_x \cos^2\theta + \sigma_y \sin^2\theta \end{aligned} $$ Using the identity $\cos^2\theta = \dfrac{1+\cos(2\theta)}{2}$ and $\sin^2\theta = \dfrac{1-\cos(2\theta)}{2}$: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_n &= \sigma_x \cdot \frac{1+\cos(2\theta)}{2} + \sigma_y \cdot \frac{1-\cos(2\theta)}{2} \\ &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) \quad \text{...(Final form of } \sigma_n\text{)} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **master equation for normal stress** on an inclined plane in a biaxial stress field. **Step 4:** To find $\tau$, multiply Equation 1 by $\sin\theta$ and Equation 2 by $\cos\theta$, then subtract. From (1) × $\sin\theta$: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_n \sin\theta \cos\theta + \tau \sin^2\theta &= \sigma_x \sin\theta \cos\theta + \tau_{xy} \sin^2\theta \quad \text{...(1b)} \end{aligned} $$ From (2) × $\cos\theta$: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_n \sin\theta \cos\theta - \tau \cos^2\theta &= \sigma_y \sin\theta \cos\theta - \tau_{xy} \cos^2\theta \quad \text{...(2b)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5:** Subtract (2b) from (1b): $$ \begin{aligned} \tau (\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta) &= (\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\sin\theta \cos\theta + \tau_{xy}(\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta) \\ \tau &= (\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\sin\theta \cos\theta + \tau_{xy} \end{aligned} $$ Using $\sin\theta \cos\theta = \dfrac{\sin(2\theta)}{2}$: $$ \begin{aligned} \tau &= \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy} \end{aligned} $$ Applying KTU sign convention (shear positive when causing clockwise rotation of element), the standard form is: $$ \boxed{\tau = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)} $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Principal Stresses Principal stresses are values of $\sigma_n$ when the shear stress $\tau$ on that plane equals **zero**. **Setting $\tau = 0$:** $$ \begin{aligned} -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta_p) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta_p) &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ Rearranging: $$ \begin{aligned} \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta_p) &= \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta_p) \\ \frac{\sin(2\theta_p)}{\cos(2\theta_p)} &= \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} \\ \tan(2\theta_p) &= \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} \quad \text{...(Principal plane angle)} \end{aligned} $$ **Substituting back into $\sigma_n$:** Using identities $\cos(2\theta_p) = \pm\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2R}$ and $\sin(2\theta_p) = \pm\dfrac{\tau_{xy}}{R}$, where $R = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_{1,2} &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\left(\pm\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2R}\right) + \tau_{xy}\left(\pm\frac{\tau_{xy}}{R}\right) \\ &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \frac{1}{R}\left[\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2\right] \\ &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \frac{R^2}{R} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Shear Stress Maximum shear stress occurs when $\dfrac{d\tau}{d\theta} = 0$. $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{d\tau}{d\theta} &= -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\cos(2\theta) - 2\tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta) = 0 \end{aligned} $$ This gives: $$ \begin{aligned} \tan(2\theta_s) &= -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}} \quad \text{...(Plane of max shear)} \end{aligned} $$ Substituting into the shear formula (and using $R$ as before): $$ \begin{aligned} \tau_{max} &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Fact:** $\tau_{max}$ occurs on planes oriented at **45°** to the principal planes. The normal stresses on these planes are equal (both equal to $\dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$). --- ## 3.4 Special Case: Pure Biaxial Stress ($\tau_{xy} = 0$) When only $\sigma_x$ and $\sigma_y$ act (no shear on the original element), the formulas simplify to: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_n &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) \\ \tau &= -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) \end{aligned} $$ **Verification:** At $\theta = 0°$, $\sigma_n = \sigma_x$ and $\tau = 0$. ✓ At $\theta = 45°$, $\sigma_n = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$ and $\tau = \pm\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}$. ✓ At $\theta = 90°$, $\sigma_n = \sigma_y$ and $\tau = 0$. ✓ --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (For KTU Board Pattern) **Problem:** At a point in a stressed material, the stresses on two perpendicular planes are: $\sigma_x = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 40$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 30$ MPa. Find the normal and tangential stresses on a plane whose normal makes $30°$ with the x-axis. **Solution:** Given: $\sigma_x = 80$, $\sigma_y = 40$, $\tau_{xy} = 30$, $\theta = 30°$ → $2\theta = 60°$. Step 1: Compute trigonometric values. $$ \begin{aligned} \cos(60°) &= 0.5, \quad \sin(60°) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866 \end{aligned} $$ Step 2: Apply the $\sigma_n$ formula. $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_n &= \frac{80+40}{2} + \frac{80-40}{2}(0.5) + 30(0.866) \\ &= 60 + 15 + 25.98 \\ &= 100.98 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned} $$ Step 3: Apply the $\tau$ formula. $$ \begin{aligned} \tau &= -\frac{80-40}{2}(0.866) + 30(0.5) \\ &= -20(0.866) + 15 \\ &= -17.32 + 15 \\ &= -2.32 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > Negative sign means shear acts opposite to assumed positive direction. **Answer:** $\sigma_n \approx 100.98$ MPa, $\tau \approx -2.32$ MPa. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Stress Transformation Process ```mermaid flowchart TD A["GIVEN BIAXIAL STRESS STATE"] --> B["sigma_x, sigma_y, tau_xy"] B --> C["DEFINE INCLINATION ANGLE theta"] C --> D["ISOLATE WEDGE FREE BODY"] D --> E["APPLY SIGMA Fx EQUALS 0"] D --> F["APPLY SIGMA Fy EQUALS 0"] E --> G["SOLVE TWO EQUATIONS"] F --> G G --> H["OBTAIN sigma_n EXPRESSION"] G --> I["OBTAIN tau EXPRESSION"] H --> J["NORMAL STRESS ON PLANE"] I --> K["SHEAR STRESS ON PLANE"] H --> L["SET tau EQUALS 0 FOR PRINCIPAL"] I --> M["SET d tau d theta EQUALS 0 FOR MAX SHEAR"] L --> N["PRINCIPAL STRESSES sigma1 sigma2"] L --> O["PRINCIPAL ANGLE tan 2 theta_p"] M --> P["MAXIMUM SHEAR STRESS tau_max"] M --> Q["PLANE OF MAX SHEAR tan 2 theta_s"] N --> R["ENGINEERING DECISION"] P --> R R --> S["DESIGN OR FAILURE CHECK"] ``` --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Inclined Plane Wedge Geometry ```mermaid flowchart LR X["FACE PERPENDICULAR TO X AXIS
Stress sigma_x, tau_xy
Area dA cos theta"] --> W["WEDGE FREE BODY
O D E"] Y["FACE PERPENDICULAR TO Y AXIS
Stress sigma_y, tau_xy
Area dA sin theta"] --> W W --> I["INCLINED FACE DE
Normal stress sigma_n
Shear stress tau
Area dA"] I --> T["THETA measured CCW from x axis"] ``` --- ## 4.3 Decision Tree — Selecting the Right Formula ```mermaid flowchart TD START["GIVEN sigma_x, sigma_y, tau_xy"] --> Q1{"Shear stress
tau_xy present?"} Q1 -->|"NO"| A["Use SIMPLIFIED FORMULA
sigma_n and tau with
cos 2 theta and sin 2 theta only"] Q1 -->|"YES"| B["Use GENERAL FORMULA
All three terms present"] A --> C{"Need principal
or max shear?"} B --> C C -->|"Principal"| D["Set tau EQUALS 0
Find theta_p then sigma1, sigma2"] C -->|"Max Shear"| E["Set d tau d theta EQUALS 0
Find theta_s then tau_max"] C -->|"Stress at angle theta"| F["Direct substitution"] D --> OUT["FINAL ANSWER"] E --> OUT F --> OUT ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Pattern]** ### **Q1. Define biaxial stress system and state the formula for normal stress on an inclined plane.** [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Model Answer:** A **biaxial stress system** is a two-dimensional state of stress in which normal stresses $\sigma_x$ and $\sigma_y$ act on two mutually perpendicular planes, with or without a complementary shear stress $\tau_{xy}$. It typically arises in thin plates and pressure vessel walls. The normal stress on a plane inclined at angle $\theta$ to the x-axis is: $$ \sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta) $$ This represents the normal component of stress on the inclined plane after resolving all forces on the wedge-shaped free body in equilibrium. --- ### **Q2. Differentiate between principal plane and plane of maximum shear stress.** [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Model Answer:** | Feature | Principal Plane | Plane of Maximum Shear | |---------|-----------------|------------------------| | **Shear stress on plane** | $\tau = 0$ (no shear) | $\tau = \tau_{max}$ (maximum possible) | | **Normal stress on plane** | $\sigma_n = \sigma_1$ or $\sigma_2$ (extreme values) | $\sigma_n = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$ (average) | | **Angle condition** | $\tan(2\theta_p) = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ | $\tan(2\theta_s) = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}}$ | | **Orientation** | Two planes, mutually perpendicular | At **45°** to principal planes | | **Engineering use** | Maximum/minimum stress location | Failure initiation in ductile materials | --- ## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **(a)** Derive the expressions for normal stress and tangential stress on an inclined plane in a biaxial stress system. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** A point in a structural member is subjected to stresses $\sigma_x = 120$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 60$ MPa, and $\tau_{xy} = 40$ MPa. Determine the **principal stresses** and the orientation of the principal planes. **\[7 Marks\]** --- #### **Model Solution to Question A:** **Part (a) — Derivation \[7 Marks\]** *Full derivation as detailed in SECTION 3.1 above. Key steps to be shown:* 1. Free body diagram of wedge with normal at angle $\theta$. 2. Area relations: $dA \cos\theta$ and $dA \sin\theta$ for the two perpendicular faces. 3. Equilibrium in x-direction: $\Sigma F_x = 0$ → Equation (1). 4. Equilibrium in y-direction: $\Sigma F_y = 0$ → Equation (2). 5. Solving simultaneously using double-angle identities. 6. Final boxed expressions: - $\sigma_n = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)$ - $\tau = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)$ **Part (b) — Principal Stresses \[7 Marks\]** **Given:** $\sigma_x = 120$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 60$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 40$ MPa **Step 1:** Compute center of Mohr's circle (average stress): $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_{avg} &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{120 + 60}{2} = 90 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Compute radius: $$ \begin{aligned} R &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} \\ &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{120-60}{2}\right)^2 + (40)^2} \\ &= \sqrt{(30)^2 + (40)^2} = \sqrt{900 + 1600} = \sqrt{2500} = 50 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Principal stresses: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_1 &= \sigma_{avg} + R = 90 + 50 = 140 \text{ MPa} \\ \sigma_2 &= \sigma_{avg} - R = 90 - 50 = 40 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Principal plane angle: $$ \begin{aligned} \tan(2\theta_p) &= \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2 \times 40}{120 - 60} = \frac{80}{60} = 1.333 \\ 2\theta_p &= \tan^{-1}(1.333) = 53.13° \\ \theta_p &= 26.57° \quad \text{(or } 116.57° \text{ for the other principal plane)} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** $\sigma_1 = 140$ MPa, $\sigma_2 = 40$ MPa, $\theta_p = 26.57°$. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **INTERNAL CHOICE ALTERNATIVE** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** At a point in a stressed material, the stresses on two perpendicular planes are: $\sigma_x = 90$ MPa (tensile), $\sigma_y = 30$ MPa (compressive), and $\tau_{xy} = 25$ MPa. Find the **stresses on a plane inclined at 40°** to the x-axis. Also find the **maximum shear stress** and its orientation. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** A thin cylindrical pressure vessel has internal diameter 500 mm and wall thickness 10 mm. If the internal pressure is 2 MPa, determine the **hoop and longitudinal stresses**. Also compute the **maximum shear stress** in the wall. **\[7 Marks\]** --- #### **Model Solution to Question B:** **Part (a) — Stresses on Inclined Plane \[7 Marks\]** **Given:** $\sigma_x = 90$ MPa, $\sigma_y = -30$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 25$ MPa, $\theta = 40°$, $2\theta = 80°$. **Step 1:** Compute trig values: $$ \begin{aligned} \cos(80°) &\approx 0.1736, \quad \sin(80°) \approx 0.9848 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Normal stress on inclined plane: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_n &= \frac{90 + (-30)}{2} + \frac{90-(-30)}{2}(0.1736) + 25(0.9848) \\ &= 30 + 60(0.1736) + 24.62 \\ &= 30 + 10.42 + 24.62 \\ &= 65.04 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Shear stress on inclined plane: $$ \begin{aligned} \tau &= -\frac{90-(-30)}{2}(0.9848) + 25(0.1736) \\ &= -60(0.9848) + 4.34 \\ &= -59.09 + 4.34 = -54.75 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Maximum shear stress: $$ \begin{aligned} \tau_{max} &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{90-(-30)}{2}\right)^2 + (25)^2} = \sqrt{60^2 + 25^2} = \sqrt{3600 + 625} = \sqrt{4225} = 65 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5:** Orientation of $\tau_{max}$: $$ \begin{aligned} \tan(2\theta_s) &= -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}} = -\frac{90-(-30)}{2 \times 25} = -\frac{120}{50} = -2.4 \\ 2\theta_s &= \tan^{-1}(-2.4) = -67.38° \\ \theta_s &= -33.69° \quad \text{or equivalently } 56.31° \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** $\sigma_n = 65.04$ MPa, $\tau = -54.75$ MPa, $\tau_{max} = 65$ MPa at $\theta_s = -33.69°$. **Part (b) — Pressure Vessel Analysis \[7 Marks\]** **Given:** $d = 500$ mm, $t = 10$ mm, $p = 2$ MPa. **Step 1:** Hoop (circumferential) stress: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_h &= \frac{p \cdot d}{2t} = \frac{2 \times 500}{2 \times 10} = 50 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Longitudinal stress: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_l &= \frac{p \cdot d}{4t} = \frac{2 \times 500}{4 \times 10} = 25 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Since pressure vessels have no shear on the principal planes ($\tau_{xy} = 0$), the principal stresses are $\sigma_1 = 50$ MPa and $\sigma_2 = 25$ MPa. **Step 4:** Maximum shear stress (occurs on plane at 45° to vessel axis): $$ \begin{aligned} \tau_{max} &= \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \frac{50 - 25}{2} = 12.5 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** $\sigma_h = 50$ MPa, $\sigma_l = 25$ MPa, $\tau_{max} = 12.5$ MPa. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Sign Error in $\sigma_y$** — Always check whether the stress is tensile (positive) or compressive (negative). A common error is entering $\sigma_y = +30$ instead of $-30$ for compression. > 2. **Angle Convention** — $\theta$ is measured from **x-axis to normal**, not from y-axis. Inverting this swaps $\cos$ and $\sin$ contributions. > 3. **Forgetting $2\theta$ in trig** — Students write $\cos(\theta)$ instead of $\cos(2\theta)$ in the formula, costing **2–3 marks** instantly. > 4. **Principal Plane Ambiguity** — $\tan(2\theta_p)$ gives an angle in one quadrant. The other principal plane is **always 90° away**, not just adding 180°. > 5. **Units Mix-up** — Pressure in MPa and dimensions in mm must yield stress in MPa (since $\frac{N/mm^2}{1} = MPa$). Watch the unit consistency. > 6. **Shear Sign Confusion** — A negative $\tau$ result does not mean wrong; it just means shear acts opposite to the assumed positive direction. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Revision Checklist:** - Biaxial stress = 2D stress with $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, and optionally $\tau_{xy}$. - Inclined plane normal makes angle $\theta$ with x-axis; **always use $2\theta$** in trig functions. - **Master Formula 1:** $\sigma_n = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)$ - **Master Formula 2:** $\tau = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)$ - **Principal stresses** occur when $\tau = 0$: $\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ - **Principal plane:** $\tan(2\theta_p) = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ - **Max shear stress:** $\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ - **Max shear plane:** $\tan(2\theta_s) = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}}$ (45° rotated from principal planes) - **Invariant:** $\sigma_x + \sigma_y = \sigma_1 + \sigma_2$ (always true) - **Special case** ($\tau_{xy}=0$): $\sigma_1 = \max(\sigma_x, \sigma_y)$, $\sigma_2 = \min(\sigma_x, \sigma_y)$, $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{\vert \sigma_x - \sigma_y \vert}{2}$. - **Tensile = Positive**, **Compressive = Negative** (KTU/IS convention). - Pressure vessel: $\sigma_h = \dfrac{pd}{2t}$, $\sigma_l = \dfrac{pd}{4t}$, $\sigma_h = 2\sigma_l$. - Always verify $\sigma_n$ at $\theta = 0°$ gives $\sigma_x$ and at $\theta = 90°$ gives $\sigma_y$ — a powerful **self-check** technique.

topics.title: Stress on an Inclined Plane Due to Two Normal Stresses Accompanied by Shear Stresses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT402 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stress on an Inclined Plane Due to Two Normal Stresses Accompanied by Shear Stresses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stress on an Inclined Plane** is the combined state of **normal stress** (perpendicular to the inclined surface) and **tangential shear stress** (acting along the inclined surface) that arises when a two-dimensional stress element, originally loaded with orthogonal normal stresses $\sigma_x$ and $\sigma_y$ together with complementary shear stresses $\tau_{xy}$, is rotated by an angle $\theta$ about an axis perpendicular to the plane of the element.

When a structural member such as a pressure vessel shaft, a riveted plate, or a dam is subjected to **biaxial normal stresses accompanied by shear**, the stresses on planes not aligned with the loading axes are no longer purely normal. They split into:

- **Normal Stress on the inclined plane** ($\sigma_n$ or $\sigma_\theta$)
- **Shear Stress on the inclined plane** ($\tau_n$ or $\tau_\theta$)

This is the foundational concept of **Stress Transformation** in 2D solid mechanics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Punching Through a Log" Model

Imagine you are trying to cut a thick wooden log using a chisel.

- If you push the chisel **straight down (along the grain axis)**, the force is purely **compressive normal stress**.
- If you push the chisel **sideways (parallel to the surface)**, the force is purely **shear stress**.
- But if you tilt the chisel at an **angle**, the same applied force now splits into two components:
    - One pushing **into the wood (normal stress on the tilted plane)**
    - One sliding **along the wood grain (shear stress on the tilted plane)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** The *same* applied force produces **different stress components depending on the orientation of the plane** on which we observe it. This is exactly what happens inside a stressed material element.

## 1.3 Reference Stress Element

Consider a 2D rectangular element of unit thickness with:
- $\sigma_x$ — normal stress on the **x-face** (positive = tensile)
- $\sigma_y$ — normal stress on the **y-face** (positive = tensile)
- $\tau_{xy}$ — shear stress on the **x-face** acting in the **y-direction**
- $\tau_{yx}$ — shear stress on the **y-face** acting in the **x-direction**

By **Complementary Shear Stress Theorem**: $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Units

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard SI Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Normal Stress | $\sigma$ | $\text{N/m}^2$ or **Pascal (Pa)** |
| Shear Stress | $\tau$ | $\text{N/m}^2$ or **Pa** |
| Angle of Inclination | $\theta$ | **radians (rad)** or **degrees** |
| Thickness | $t$ | **m (metres)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU numerical problems, the unit **MPa = N/mm²** is most commonly used. **1 MPa = 10⁶ Pa = 1 N/mm²**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Free Body of an Inclined Section — Setup

To find stresses on an inclined plane, we isolate a **wedge-shaped element** by cutting the original rectangular element along a plane whose **outward normal makes an angle $\theta$ with the x-axis**.

Let the inclined plane have:
- **Outward normal** at angle $\theta$ with x-axis
- **Inclined surface length** = $dA$
- **Horizontal projection** = $dA \cos\theta$
- **Vertical projection** = $dA \sin\theta$

The wedge is in equilibrium under:
- $\sigma_x$ on the x-face (area = $dA \cos\theta$)
- $\sigma_y$ on the y-face (area = $dA \sin\theta$)
- $\tau_{xy}$ on the x-face
- $\tau_{yx}$ on the y-face
- Unknown $\sigma_\theta$ and $\tau_\theta$ on the inclined face (area = $dA$)

## 2.2 Equilibrium Equations

### Step 1: Resolve forces in the direction **along the inclined plane normal** (gets $\sigma_\theta$)
$$\sum F_n = 0$$

### Step 2: Resolve forces in the direction **along the inclined plane tangent** (gets $\tau_\theta$)
$$\sum F_t = 0$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following two equations are the **most tested formulas** in KTU ESE and internal assessments for this module.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Normal Stress on Inclined Plane** | $\sigma_\theta = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)$ | Valid for any angle $\theta$ measured from x-axis |
| 2 | **Shear Stress on Inclined Plane** | $\tau_\theta = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)$ | Negative sign convention varies by textbook |
| 3 | **Principal Stress (Major)** | $\sigma_1 = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Maximum normal stress |
| 4 | **Principal Stress (Minor)** | $\sigma_2 = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Minimum normal stress |
| 5 | **Maximum Shear Stress** | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Occurs on 45° planes to principal planes |
| 6 | **Principal Plane Angle** | $\tan(2\theta_p) = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ | Gives orientation of principal planes |
| 7 | **Radius of Mohr's Circle** | $R = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Geometric representation |
| 8 | **Centre of Mohr's Circle** | $C = \left(\dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2},\ 0\right)$ | On the $\sigma$-axis |
| 9 | **Average Normal Stress** | $\sigma_{avg} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$ | Invariant under rotation |
| 10 | **Invariant Sum** | $\sigma_x + \sigma_y = \sigma_\theta + \sigma_{\theta+90°}$ | Stress invariant property |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Used:** Tensile normal stresses are **positive**, compressive are **negative**. Shear stresses that tend to rotate the element **clockwise** are **positive** (in some textbooks; KTU follows the convention where positive shear produces **clockwise couple** on the element face).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why Stress Transformation Matters |
|---|---|
| **Pressure Vessels** | Hoop and longitudinal stresses act on different planes; failure analysis requires inclined-plane stresses |
| **Bridges & Trusses** | Diagonal members carry combined normal + shear loads |
| **Shafts under Combined Loading** | Bending + torsion = inclined plane stresses at 45° |
| **Weld/Joint Design** | Welds fail along inclined planes governed by $\sigma_\theta$, $\tau_\theta$ |
| **Dam Concrete** | Principal stress directions dictate cracking patterns |
| **Soil Mechanics** | Mohr-Coulomb failure uses inclined plane analysis |

## 2.5 Key Observations from the Formulas

1. **Both $\sigma_\theta$ and $\tau_\theta$ are functions of $2\theta$** — meaning stresses repeat every **180°**, not 360°.
2. **The sum $\sigma_\theta + \sigma_{\theta+90°} = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$** — this is the **stress invariant**.
3. **There exist orientations where $\tau_\theta = 0$** — these are the **principal planes**, where stress is purely normal.
4. **There exist orientations where $\sigma_\theta$ is maximum or minimum** — these are also the principal planes.
5. **Maximum shear stress occurs at 45° to principal planes**, not necessarily 45° to x-axis.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Normal Stress $\sigma_\theta$ on Inclined Plane

### Geometry of the Wedge

Take a 2D element of unit thickness. Cut it along a plane whose **outward normal** makes an angle $\theta$ with the x-axis. Let the inclined face have area $dA$.

- Area of the **horizontal face** (perpendicular to x-axis) = $dA \cos\theta$
- Area of the **vertical face** (perpendicular to y-axis) = $dA \sin\theta$

### Step 1: List All Forces Acting on the Wedge

**On the horizontal (x) face:**
- Normal force: $\sigma_x \cdot (dA \cos\theta)$ acting in +x direction
- Shear force: $\tau_{xy} \cdot (dA \cos\theta)$ acting in +y direction

**On the vertical (y) face:**
- Normal force: $\sigma_y \cdot (dA \sin\theta)$ acting in +y direction
- Shear force: $\tau_{yx} \cdot (dA \sin\theta)$ acting in +x direction

**On the inclined face (area $dA$):**
- Normal force: $\sigma_\theta \cdot dA$ acting along the outward normal
- Shear force: $\tau_\theta \cdot dA$ acting along the inclined plane

### Step 2: Resolve Forces Along the Normal Direction (n-direction)

The **unit vector along the normal** is $(\cos\theta,\ \sin\theta)$.

Sum of force components along this direction equals zero:

$$\sum F_n = \sigma_x (dA\cos\theta)\cos\theta + \tau_{xy}(dA\cos\theta)\sin\theta + \sigma_y(dA\sin\theta)\sin\theta + \tau_{yx}(dA\sin\theta)\cos\theta - \sigma_\theta dA = 0$$

> **Logic Note:** Each force is dotted with the unit normal $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$ to extract its component along $n$. The final $\sigma_\theta dA$ is subtracted because it acts outward (opposite to inward equilibrium).

### Step 3: Substitute $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$ and Divide by $dA$

$$\sigma_x \cos^2\theta + \sigma_y \sin^2\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\sin\theta\cos\theta - \sigma_\theta = 0$$

### Step 4: Apply Double-Angle Trigonometric Identities

Using:
$$\cos^2\theta = \frac{1 + \cos(2\theta)}{2}, \quad \sin^2\theta = \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}, \quad 2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin(2\theta)$$

Substituting:

$$\sigma_\theta = \sigma_x \cdot \frac{1 + \cos(2\theta)}{2} + \sigma_y \cdot \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2} + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)$$

### Step 5: Separate and Rearrange

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x \cos(2\theta)}{2} + \frac{\sigma_y}{2} - \frac{\sigma_y \cos(2\theta)}{2} + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)$$

Group the $\cos(2\theta)$ terms:

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)}$$

> **Logic Note:** This is the **governing KTU formula** for normal stress on an inclined plane.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Shear Stress $\tau_\theta$ on Inclined Plane

### Step 1: Resolve Forces Along the Tangent Direction (t-direction)

The **unit vector along the tangent** (perpendicular to normal, in the rotated plane) is $(-\sin\theta,\ \cos\theta)$.

Sum of tangential components:

$$\sum F_t = -\sigma_x (dA\cos\theta)\sin\theta + \tau_{xy}(dA\cos\theta)\cos\theta - \sigma_y(dA\sin\theta)\cos\theta + \tau_{yx}(dA\sin\theta)\sin\theta - \tau_\theta dA = 0$$

> **Logic Note:** Each force is dotted with $(-\sin\theta, \cos\theta)$ to get its tangential component. The shear force $\tau_\theta dA$ acts in the tangent direction and is subtracted.

### Step 2: Substitute $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$ and Divide by $dA$

$$-\sigma_x \sin\theta\cos\theta + \sigma_y\sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_{xy}(\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta) - \tau_\theta = 0$$

### Step 3: Apply Double-Angle Identities

Using:
$$\sin\theta\cos\theta = \frac{\sin(2\theta)}{2}, \quad \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta = \cos(2\theta)$$

$$\tau_\theta = (\sigma_y - \sigma_x)\frac{\sin(2\theta)}{2} + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)$$

### Step 4: Rearrange to Standard Form

$$\boxed{\tau_\theta = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Principal Stresses

### Condition for Principal Stress

Principal stresses occur where $\tau_\theta = 0$ (no shear stress on the plane, only normal stress).

Setting $\dfrac{d\sigma_\theta}{d\theta} = 0$:

$$\frac{d\sigma_\theta}{d\theta} = -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\sin(2\theta) + 2\tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta) = 0$$

Solving:

$$\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

Substitute this $2\theta_p$ into the $\sigma_\theta$ equation. Using trigonometric identity:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_1 = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + R, \quad \sigma_2 = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} - R}$$

where $R = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ is the radius of Mohr's circle.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Maximum Shear Stress

Maximum shear stress occurs when $\dfrac{d\tau_\theta}{d\theta} = 0$:

$$\frac{d\tau_\theta}{d\theta} = -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\cos(2\theta) - 2\tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta) = 0$$

$$\tan(2\theta_s) = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}}$$

Note: $2\theta_s = 2\theta_p \pm 90°$ — meaning **maximum shear planes are at 45° to principal planes**.

Substituting back:

$$\tau_{\max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{\max} = R}$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** At a point in a stressed material, the stresses on two mutually perpendicular planes are:
- $\sigma_x = 80\ \text{MPa}$ (tensile)
- $\sigma_y = 40\ \text{MPa}$ (tensile)
- $\tau_{xy} = 30\ \text{MPa}$

Find:
1. Normal stress and shear stress on a plane inclined at **30°** to the vertical plane (i.e., $\theta = 30°$ measured from x-axis).
2. Principal stresses and their orientations.
3. Maximum shear stress.

### Part 1: Stresses on Inclined Plane ($\theta = 30°$)

Given: $2\theta = 60°$

Compute values:
$$\cos(60°) = 0.5, \quad \sin(60°) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.8660$$

Apply $\sigma_\theta$ formula:

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{80 + 40}{2} + \frac{80 - 40}{2}(0.5) + 30(0.8660)$$

$$\sigma_\theta = 60 + 15 + 25.98 = 100.98\ \text{MPa}$$

Apply $\tau_\theta$ formula:

$$\tau_\theta = -\frac{80 - 40}{2}(0.8660) + 30(0.5)$$

$$\tau_\theta = -20(0.8660) + 15 = -17.32 + 15 = -2.32\ \text{MPa}$$

> **Interpretation:** $\sigma_\theta \approx 101$ MPa (tensile, large), $\tau_\theta \approx -2.32$ MPa (negative sign indicates direction opposite to assumed positive).

### Part 2: Principal Stresses

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{80 + 40}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{80 - 40}{2}\right)^2 + 30^2}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 60 \pm \sqrt{400 + 900} = 60 \pm \sqrt{1300}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 60 \pm 36.06\ \text{MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_1 = 96.06\ \text{MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = 23.94\ \text{MPa}}$$

Principal plane angle:

$$\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2(30)}{80 - 40} = \frac{60}{40} = 1.5$$

$$2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(1.5) = 56.31° \Rightarrow \theta_p = 28.15°$$

### Part 3: Maximum Shear Stress

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \frac{96.06 - 23.94}{2} = \frac{72.12}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{\max} = 36.06\ \text{MPa}}$$

This occurs at $\theta_s = \theta_p + 45° = 73.15°$.

---

## 3.6 Verification Table — Special Cases

| Case | $\theta$ | $\sigma_\theta$ | $\tau_\theta$ | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Plane perpendicular to x-axis | $0°$ | $\sigma_x$ | $\tau_{xy}$ | Original state |
| Plane perpendicular to y-axis | $90°$ | $\sigma_y$ | $-\tau_{xy}$ | Original state |
| Principal plane 1 | $\theta_p$ | $\sigma_1$ | $0$ | Max normal stress |
| Principal plane 2 | $\theta_p + 90°$ | $\sigma_2$ | $0$ | Min normal stress |
| Max shear plane | $\theta_p + 45°$ | $\sigma_{avg}$ | $\tau_{\max}$ | Pure shear state |
| Invariant check | $\theta$ and $\theta + 90°$ | sum = $\sigma_x + \sigma_y$ | – | Stress invariant |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Original 2D Stress Element with Inclined Cut

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **functional stress architecture** for the inclined-plane stress transformation problem — illustrating the rotation of axes, the wedge isolation, and the resolution of stresses.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Element] --> B[Sigma x on x-face]
    A --> C[Sigma y on y-face]
    A --> D[Tau xy on x-face]
    A --> E[Tau yx on y-face]
    F[Make Inclined Cut at Angle theta] --> G[Isolate Wedge Shaped Element]
    G --> H[Force Resolution along Normal Direction n]
    G --> I[Force Resolution along Tangent Direction t]
    H --> J[Sum F_n equals zero]
    I --> K[Sum F_t equals zero]
    J --> L[Derive sigma theta formula]
    K --> M[Derive tau theta formula]
    L --> N[Compute Numerical Stress Values]
    M --> N
    N --> O{Special Cases}
    O --> P[Principal Stresses when tau theta equals zero]
    O --> Q[Maximum Shear Stress at 45 degrees to principal planes]
    O --> R[Verify via Mohrs Circle Geometry]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This flowchart represents the systematic approach for solving any inclined-plane stress problem in KTU examinations — start with the wedge, resolve forces in two perpendicular directions, apply equilibrium, obtain the two key formulas, and then specialize.

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Mohr's Circle Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Plot Point X: sigma x, tau xy] --> Q[Plot Point Y: sigma y, minus tau xy]
    Q --> R[Draw Diameter XY]
    R --> S[Center C: avg of sigma x and sigma y, zero]
    S --> T[Radius R: square root of terms squared]
    T --> U[Rotate by 2 theta on circle to get sigma theta, tau theta]
    U --> V[Extreme Right Point: sigma 1 principal]
    U --> W[Extreme Left Point: sigma 2 principal]
    U --> X[Top and Bottom Points: tau max shear stress]
```

> **Mohr's Circle:** A graphical representation where **stress transformation is visualized as rotation by $2\theta$** on a circle. The center is at $\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}, 0\right)$ and radius is $\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$.

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Sign Convention on a Stress Element

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stress Element x-y] --> B[Top face: sigma y acts outward tensile positive]
    A --> C[Right face: sigma x acts outward tensile positive]
    A --> D[Right face: tau xy acts downward positive shear]
    A --> E[Top face: tau yx acts leftward positive shear]
    B --> F[Complementary Shear: tau xy equals tau yx]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Shows the **standard sign convention** used universally in KTU/Indian engineering curricula. Tensile normal stresses are positive; shear stresses that produce a clockwise couple on the element are positive.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define normal stress and shear stress on an inclined plane. Mention the condition at which shear stress on an inclined plane becomes zero.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Normal stress on an inclined plane ($\sigma_\theta$):** The component of the resultant stress vector acting **perpendicular** to the inclined plane.

**Shear stress on an inclined plane ($\tau_\theta$):** The component of the resultant stress vector acting **tangentially** (parallel) to the inclined plane.

**Condition for zero shear stress:** When the inclined plane coincides with a **principal plane**. This occurs at angle:

$$\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

On this plane, the normal stress attains its extreme value (maximum or minimum), and the shear stress vanishes completely.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is meant by principal stresses? Write the expression for principal stresses in a 2D stress system.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Principal stresses** are the **maximum and minimum normal stresses** acting on mutually perpendicular planes called principal planes, on which the **shear stress is zero**.

The two principal stresses in a 2D stress system are:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

where $\sigma_1$ is the major (maximum) principal stress and $\sigma_2$ is the minor (minimum) principal stress. The orientation of the principal planes is given by:

$$\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**At a point in a stressed material, the stresses on two mutually perpendicular planes are:**
- $\sigma_x = 100\ \text{MPa}$ (tensile)
- $\sigma_y = 60\ \text{MPa}$ (compressive)
- $\tau_{xy} = 40\ \text{MPa}$

**Find:**
**(a)** Normal and shear stresses on a plane inclined at **30°** to the x-axis. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Principal stresses and their orientations. Also find maximum shear stress. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $\sigma_x = +100\ \text{MPa}$ (tensile, positive)
- $\sigma_y = -60\ \text{MPa}$ (compressive, negative)
- $\tau_{xy} = 40\ \text{MPa}$
- $\theta = 30°$, so $2\theta = 60°$

**Trigonometric values:**
- $\cos(60°) = 0.5$
- $\sin(60°) = 0.8660$

**Step 1: Apply $\sigma_\theta$ formula**

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)$$

Substitute values:

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{100 + (-60)}{2} + \frac{100 - (-60)}{2}(0.5) + 40(0.8660)$$

$$\sigma_\theta = 20 + \frac{160}{2}(0.5) + 34.64$$

$$\sigma_\theta = 20 + 40 + 34.64 = 94.64\ \text{MPa}$$

**Step 2: Apply $\tau_\theta$ formula**

$$\tau_\theta = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)$$

$$\tau_\theta = -\frac{100 - (-60)}{2}(0.8660) + 40(0.5)$$

$$\tau_\theta = -\frac{160}{2}(0.8660) + 20$$

$$\tau_\theta = -80(0.8660) + 20 = -69.28 + 20 = -49.28\ \text{MPa}$$

**Result:**
- $\boxed{\sigma_\theta = 94.64\ \text{MPa (tensile)}}$
- $\boxed{\tau_\theta = -49.28\ \text{MPa}}$

**Interpretation:** The negative shear stress means the actual shear acts in the opposite direction to the assumed positive direction.

---

### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Find principal stresses**

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{100 + (-60)}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{100 - (-60)}{2}\right)^2 + 40^2}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 20 \pm \sqrt{(80)^2 + 40^2}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 20 \pm \sqrt{6400 + 1600} = 20 \pm \sqrt{8000}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 20 \pm 89.44\ \text{MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_1 = 109.44\ \text{MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = -69.44\ \text{MPa}}$$

**Step 2: Principal plane orientation**

$$\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2(40)}{100 - (-60)} = \frac{80}{160} = 0.5$$

$$2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(0.5) = 26.57°$$

$$\boxed{\theta_p = 13.28°\ \text{(for major principal stress)}}$$

The minor principal plane is at $\theta_p + 90° = 103.28°$.

**Step 3: Maximum shear stress**

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \frac{109.44 - (-69.44)}{2} = \frac{178.88}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{\max} = 89.44\ \text{MPa}}$$

This occurs at $\theta_s = \theta_p + 45° = 58.28°$ from the x-axis.

**Step 4: Verification using stress invariant**

$$\sigma_x + \sigma_y = 100 + (-60) = 40\ \text{MPa}$$
$$\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = 109.44 + (-69.44) = 40\ \text{MPa} \quad \checkmark$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**At a point in a structural member, the stresses are:**
- $\sigma_x = 50\ \text{MPa}$ (tensile)
- $\sigma_y = 30\ \text{MPa}$ (tensile)
- $\tau_{xy} = 25\ \text{MPa}$

**Find:**
**(a)** The magnitudes of normal and shear stresses on a plane whose normal makes an angle of **45°** with the x-axis. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Principal stresses, their directions, and the maximum shear stress using analytical formulas. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Given:** $\theta = 45°$, so $2\theta = 90°$
- $\cos(90°) = 0$
- $\sin(90°) = 1$

**Normal stress:**

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{50 + 30}{2} + \frac{50 - 30}{2}(0) + 25(1)$$

$$\sigma_\theta = 40 + 0 + 25 = 65\ \text{MPa}$$

**Shear stress:**

$$\tau_\theta = -\frac{50 - 30}{2}(1) + 25(0) = -10\ \text{MPa}$$

**Result:** $\boxed{\sigma_\theta = 65\ \text{MPa},\ \tau_\theta = -10\ \text{MPa}}$

---

### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Principal stresses:**

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{50 + 30}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{50 - 30}{2}\right)^2 + 25^2}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 40 \pm \sqrt{100 + 625} = 40 \pm \sqrt{725}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 40 \pm 26.93\ \text{MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_1 = 66.93\ \text{MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = 13.07\ \text{MPa}}$$

**Principal plane angle:**

$$\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2(25)}{50 - 30} = \frac{50}{20} = 2.5$$

$$2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(2.5) = 68.20°$$

$$\boxed{\theta_p = 34.10°}$$

**Maximum shear stress:**

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \frac{66.93 - 13.07}{2} = 26.93\ \text{MPa}$$

This occurs at $\theta_s = 34.10° + 45° = 79.10°$.

**Verification:** $\sigma_x + \sigma_y = 80 = \sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = 66.93 + 13.07 = 80\ \checkmark$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (KTU Board Pattern)**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in $\sigma_y$:** Many students write compressive $\sigma_y = +60$ instead of $-60$. **Always** apply sign convention before substitution.
>
> 2. **Angle Identification:** The angle $\theta$ is between the **outward normal of the inclined plane** and the **x-axis**, NOT the plane itself. Misreading the question costs 1–2 marks.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Double-Angle:** Plugging in $\theta$ directly instead of $2\theta$ in trigonometric functions is the **#1 mistake** in this module. Always compute $2\theta$ first.
>
> 4. **Incorrect Trigonometric Values:** Mistaking $\sin(60°)$ for $\sin(30°) = 0.5$ is common. Use standard values or calculator properly.
>
> 5. **Units Inconsistency:** Mixing MPa and N/mm² without conversion loses marks. **1 MPa = 1 N/mm²** (same numerically).
>
> 6. **Missing Principal Plane Verification:** Always verify $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$ as a sanity check.
>
> 7. **Forgetting Complementary Shear:** Not mentioning $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$ when setting up equilibrium — partial marks deduction.
>
> 8. **Maximum Shear Plane Orientation:** Writing "45° to x-axis" instead of "45° to principal planes" is technically wrong. The 45° is from the principal plane, not from x.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize**

- **Two Master Formulas:**
    - $\sigma_\theta = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)$
    - $\tau_\theta = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)$

- **Always use $2\theta$**, never $\theta$ directly in trig functions.

- **Sign convention:** Tensile = +ve, Compressive = -ve, Clockwise shear = +ve.

- **Complementary Shear Theorem:** $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$ (mandatory before writing equilibrium).

- **Principal Stress Formula:**
$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

- **Principal Plane Angle:**
$$\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

- **Maximum Shear Stress:**
$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

- **Maximum shear occurs on planes 45° to principal planes**, not necessarily 45° to x or y axes.

- **Stress Invariant:** $\sigma_x + \sigma_y = \sigma_\theta + \sigma_{\theta + 90°}$ — use as a verification tool.

- **Average Stress (Centroid of Mohr's Circle):** $\sigma_{avg} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$ — invariant under rotation.

- **For pure shear ($\sigma_x = \sigma_y = 0$):** $\sigma_1 = +\tau$, $\sigma_2 = -\tau$, $\tau_{\max} = \tau$, principal planes at 45° to x-axis.

- **For uniaxial stress ($\sigma_y = \tau_{xy} = 0$):** Maximum shear on 45° plane = $\dfrac{\sigma_x}{2}$.

- **Units:** Use MPa or N/mm² consistently. Avoid mixing.

- **Always draw the wedge FBD** in 14-mark problems to fetch full marks in equilibrium setup.

- **Mention complementary shear** explicitly in derivation questions.

- **Verification step:** Check $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$ before finalizing your answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
