<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Principal Stresses and Principal Planes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> **Principal Stresses** are the maximum and minimum normal stresses that occur at a point in a stressed body, acting on planes (called **Principal Planes**) on which the **shear stress is zero**. These planes are mutually perpendicular and are oriented such that only normal stresses act upon them.

In a 2D stress element, there are always **two principal stresses**:
- **σ₁** = Major (maximum) principal stress
- **σ₂** = Minor (minimum) principal stress

The corresponding planes are:
- **Plane 1 (Major principal plane)**: The plane on which σ₁ acts and where shear stress τ = 0.
- **Plane 2 (Minor principal plane)**: The plane on which σ₂ acts and where shear stress τ = 0.

### 🔭 Conceptual Analogy (The Mountain Cross-Section)
Imagine you are standing inside a stressed rock at a point. If you cut a small slice in any random direction, you will see some normal force pushing perpendicular to your cut, and some sliding (shear) force. Now, imagine *rotating* your imaginary slice until the sliding force completely vanishes — what remains is purely a push or a pull. **That direction of cutting is a principal plane, and the pure push/pull you measure is the principal stress.** One slice will give you the maximum push (σ₁), and the perpendicular slice will give you the minimum (σ₂).

---

## 1.2 Mohr's Circle of Stress

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> **Mohr's Circle of Stress** is a **two-dimensional graphical representation** invented by the German civil engineer **Christian Otto Mohr (1882)** that depicts the transformation of normal stress (σ) and shear stress (τ) acting on different planes passing through a point. It is constructed in the **σ–τ plane** with normal stress (σ) on the abscissa (x-axis) and shear stress (τ) on the ordinate (y-axis). The circle uniquely defines the state of stress at a point and provides a powerful visual tool to determine **principal stresses, maximum shear stress, and the orientation of principal planes**.

### 🔭 Conceptual Analogy (The Spinning Coin)
Think of a coin spinning on a table. At any instant, you can see its "width" (like normal stress) and the "twist angle" you observe (like shear stress). As the coin rotates, the visible width changes — sometimes wide, sometimes narrow. Mohr's circle is essentially a **map** of how the apparent normal stress and apparent shear stress change as you rotate the slicing plane through 360°. The circle captures *all possible* orientations in one neat picture.

---

## 1.3 Buckling and Stability of Long Columns

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> **Buckling** is the **sudden lateral (sideways) deflection** that occurs in slender structural members (called **columns**) when they are subjected to a compressive axial load exceeding a certain critical value, called the **Euler's Critical Load (Pcr)**. Beyond this load, the column, which was previously stable in its straight equilibrium position, becomes **unstable** and collapses laterally even though the compressive stress may remain well below the yield strength of the material.

### 🔭 Conceptual Analogy (The Plastic Ruler Test)
Hold a thin plastic ruler vertically on your desk and press down on its top with your finger. For small forces, the ruler remains straight. Apply a slightly larger force, and suddenly — *snap!* — it bows sideways. You did not break the ruler's material; you simply crossed a **stability threshold**. That critical force depends on how *tall* the ruler is, how *stiff* it is (its flexural rigidity *EI*), and how its ends are held. This is buckling.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Why this topic is critical for the Board Exam:**
> - Principal stress problems and Mohr's circle constructions appear in **almost every KTU University ESE** (typically 7 to 14 marks).
> - Column buckling problems are **favourite 14-mark questions** in Part B.
> - Numerical substitution errors (sign conventions and orientation angles) are the **#1 reason students lose marks**, so pay careful attention.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Stress Transformation Theory — The Foundation

Consider a 2D plane stress element subjected to:
- Normal stress **σₓ** (acting on the x-face in the x-direction)
- Normal stress **σᵧ** (acting on the y-face in the y-direction)
- Shear stress **τₓᵧ** (acting on the x-face in the y-direction)

For a plane inclined at an angle **θ** counter-clockwise from the x-axis, the stresses on the inclined plane are obtained by **static equilibrium** of the wedge element:

### Stress Transformation Equations

$$\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$$

$$\tau_n = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta$$

Where:
- **σₙ** = Normal stress on the inclined plane
- **τₙ** = Shear stress on the inclined plane
- **θ** = Angle of inclination of the plane (CCW from x-axis)

### Why these equations matter in engineering:
These equations are used universally in **pressure vessel design, shaft design, truss analysis, dam design, retaining walls**, and **machine component analysis** to find the most critical loading direction.

---

## 2.2 Principal Stresses — Finding the Maximum and Minimum

Setting **dσₙ/dθ = 0** (condition for extremum):

$$\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

This gives **two values of 2θₚ separated by 180°**, i.e., **two values of θₚ separated by 90°**.

The principal stresses are obtained by substituting back:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

By convention: **σ₁ ≥ σ₂**.

The **center of Mohr's circle** is **C = (σₓ + σᵧ)/2**, and the **radius** is **R = √[(σₓ − σᵧ)/2]² + τₓᵧ²**.

### Maximum In-Plane Shear Stress

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = R$$

The plane of maximum shear is oriented at **45°** from the principal plane:

$$\tan 2\theta_s = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}}$$

The normal stress on the plane of maximum shear equals the center value: **σₙ = (σₓ + σᵧ)/2**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Stress Transformation)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Normal stress on inclined plane | σₙ = (σₓ + σᵧ)/2 + (σₓ − σᵧ)/2·cos 2θ + τₓᵧ·sin 2θ | Pa, N/mm² |
| 2 | Shear stress on inclined plane | τₙ = −(σₓ − σᵧ)/2·sin 2θ + τₓᵧ·cos 2θ | Pa, N/mm² |
| 3 | Principal plane angle | tan 2θₚ = 2τₓᵧ / (σₓ − σᵧ) | θ in degrees |
| 4 | Major principal stress | σ₁ = (σₓ + σᵧ)/2 + √[((σₓ − σᵧ)/2)² + τₓᵧ²] | Pa, N/mm² |
| 5 | Minor principal stress | σ₂ = (σₓ + σᵧ)/2 − √[((σₓ − σᵧ)/2)² + τₓᵧ²] | Pa, N/mm² |
| 6 | Maximum in-plane shear stress | τ_max = √[((σₓ − σᵧ)/2)² + τₓᵧ²] | Pa, N/mm² |
| 7 | Mohr's circle center | C = (σₓ + σᵧ)/2, 0 | Coordinate on σ-axis |
| 8 | Mohr's circle radius | R = √[((σₓ − σᵧ)/2)² + τₓᵧ²] | Same units as σ |
| 9 | Angle on Mohr's circle | Rotation by 2θ on Mohr's circle = 2θ in physical plane | Note: Angle doubled |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention (KTU Standard):**
> - **Tensile stresses** → **Positive**
> - **Compressive stresses** → **Negative**
> - **Shear stress τₓᵧ** → **Positive** if it acts in the **+y direction on the +x face**
> - **Angle θ** → **Positive** for **Counter-Clockwise (CCW)** rotation from x-axis
> - **On Mohr's circle**, shear axis is plotted **downward positive** by convention (some books use upward positive — KTU follows **downward positive**).

---

## 2.4 Column Buckling — The Theory

### 2.4.1 Euler's Critical Load (Pcr)

For a long, slender, perfectly straight column with pin-ended supports, the critical load at which buckling initiates is given by **Leonhard Euler's formula (1757)**:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{L_{eff}^2}$$

Where:
- **P_cr** = Euler's critical buckling load (N)
- **E** = Young's Modulus of the column material (Pa or N/mm²)
- **I** = Minimum second moment of area of the cross-section about the buckling axis (mm⁴)
- **L_eff** = Effective length of the column = **k · L**, where **k** = effective length factor depending on end conditions.

### 2.4.2 Effective Lengths for Different End Conditions

| # | End Condition | Sketch | Effective Length (L_eff) | k Factor |
|---|---------------|--------|--------------------------|----------|
| 1 | Both ends pinned (hinged) | ↕ pin—column—pin | L | k = 1 |
| 2 | Both ends fixed | ↕ fixed—column—fixed | L/2 | k = 0.5 |
| 3 | One end fixed, other pinned | ↕ fixed—column—pin | L/√2 ≈ 0.707 L | k = 1/√2 |
| 4 | One end fixed, other free (cantilever) | ↕ fixed—column—free | 2L | k = 2 |

### 2.4.3 Euler's Critical Stress

Dividing P_cr by cross-sectional area **A**:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{P_{cr}}{A} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{(L_{eff}/r)^2}$$

Where **r** = radius of gyration = √(I/A), and **L_eff/r** = **slenderness ratio (λ)**.

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** Euler's formula is **valid only for long, slender columns** where buckling occurs in the **elastic range** (i.e., σ_cr ≤ proportional limit). For short columns, the material yields before buckling, so Euler's formula is NOT applicable.

### 2.4.4 Slenderness Ratio (λ)

$$\lambda = \frac{L_{eff}}{r} = \frac{L_{eff}}{\sqrt{I/A}}$$

### 2.4.5 Crippling / Critical Slenderness Ratio (λ_c)

The transition point between **Euler (long)** and **Johnson / Rankine (short)** columns:

$$\lambda_c = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2 E}{\sigma_c}}$$

Where **σ_c** is the **crushing stress** (yield stress for ductile materials, ultimate stress for brittle materials).

If **λ > λ_c** → **Long column** → Use **Euler's formula**.
If **λ < λ_c** → **Short column** → Use **Johnson's** or **Rankine's** formula.

### 2.4.6 Rankine's Formula (Empirical, applicable to all column lengths)

$$P_R = \frac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a\left(\frac{L_{eff}}{k}\right)^2}$$

Where:
- **σ_c** = Crushing (ultimate compressive) stress of material
- **A** = Cross-sectional area
- **a** = Rankine's empirical constant = **σ_c / (π²·E)**
- **k** = Least radius of gyration

$$\sigma_R = \frac{P_R}{A} = \frac{\sigma_c}{1 + a\left(\frac{L_{eff}}{k}\right)^2}$$

### 2.4.7 Johnson's Formula (For intermediate columns)

$$\sigma_{cr} = \sigma_y - \left(\frac{\sigma_y - \sigma_c}{\sigma_c}\right) \cdot \sigma_y \cdot \left(\frac{L_{eff}}{k}\right)^2$$

Or equivalently:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \sigma_y - \alpha \cdot \sigma_y \cdot \lambda^2 \quad \text{where} \quad \alpha = \frac{\sigma_y - \sigma_c}{\sigma_c \cdot \lambda_c^2}$$

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Column Buckling)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Applicable Range |
|---|----------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | Euler's critical load | P_cr = π²EI / L²_eff | Long columns (λ > λ_c) |
| 2 | Euler's critical stress | σ_cr = π²E / λ² | Long columns (elastic buckling) |
| 3 | Slenderness ratio | λ = L_eff / r = L_eff / √(I/A) | All columns |
| 4 | Critical slenderness ratio | λ_c = √(π²E / σ_c) | Transition point |
| 5 | Rankine's critical load | P_R = σ_c·A / [1 + a(L_eff/k)²] | All column lengths |
| 6 | Rankine's constant | a = σ_c / (π²·E) | Material property |
| 7 | Johnson's critical stress | σ_cr = σ_y − [((σ_y − σ_c)/σ_c)]·σ_y·(L_eff/k)² | Intermediate columns |
| 8 | Effective length (pinned-pinned) | L_eff = L | k = 1 |
| 9 | Effective length (fixed-fixed) | L_eff = L/2 | k = 0.5 |
| 10 | Effective length (fixed-pinned) | L_eff = L/√2 | k = 0.707 |
| 11 | Effective length (fixed-free) | L_eff = 2L | k = 2 |

### Real-World Applications:
- **Euler buckling** is critical in the design of **building columns, bridge piers, towers, masts, and long drill strings**.
- **Rankine's formula** is widely used in **mechanical and structural engineering handbooks** for quick, conservative estimates.
- **Mohr's circle** is used in **failure analysis, machine design, geotechnical engineering, and fracture mechanics**.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Principal Stress Formula

### Starting Point — Stress Transformation Equations

Consider a 2D plane stress element with stresses σₓ, σᵧ, τₓᵧ. Cut a wedge at an angle θ from the x-axis. The forces on the inclined face (length ds) are obtained from equilibrium.

**Step 1: Resolving forces along the inclined normal (n-direction):**

For a wedge of unit thickness, on the inclined plane of area ds, the normal stress σₙ acts. The equilibrium equation in the n-direction is:

$$\sigma_n \cdot ds = \sigma_x \cdot ds \cdot \cos\theta \cdot \cos\theta + \sigma_y \cdot ds \cdot \sin\theta \cdot \sin\theta + 2\tau_{xy} \cdot ds \cdot \cos\theta \cdot \sin\theta$$

**Step 2: Simplifying using trigonometric identities:**

Using cos²θ = (1 + cos 2θ)/2, sin²θ = (1 − cos 2θ)/2, and 2·sin θ·cos θ = sin 2θ:

$$\sigma_n = \sigma_x \cos^2\theta + \sigma_y \sin^2\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\sin\theta\cos\theta$$

$$\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x(1 + \cos 2\theta)}{2} + \frac{\sigma_y(1 - \cos 2\theta)}{2} + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$$

$$\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$$

This is the **Normal Stress Transformation Equation**.

**Step 3: Setting derivative to zero for extremum:**

$$\frac{d\sigma_n}{d\theta} = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2} \cdot 2\sin 2\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta = 0$$

$$\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta = \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta$$

$$\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

This is the equation for the **principal plane angle**.

**Step 4: Solving for principal stresses:**

Construct a right triangle from tan 2θₚ where:
- Opposite side = 2τₓᵧ
- Adjacent side = (σₓ − σᵧ)
- Hypotenuse = √[(σₓ − σᵧ)² + (2τₓᵧ)²]

Therefore:
- cos 2θₚ = (σₓ − σᵧ) / √[(σₓ − σᵧ)² + (2τₓᵧ)²]
- sin 2θₚ = 2τₓᵧ / √[(σₓ − σᵧ)² + (2τₓᵧ)²]

**Step 5: Substituting back into σₙ equation:**

$$\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2} \cdot \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + (2\tau_{xy})^2}} + \tau_{xy} \cdot \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + (2\tau_{xy})^2}}$$

$$\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + (2\tau_{xy})^2}{2\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + (2\tau_{xy})^2}}$$

$$\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + (2\tau_{xy})^2}}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}}$$

✅ **Derivation complete.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Euler's Critical Buckling Load (Pinned-Pinned Column)

**Step 1: Setup — Consider a long column of length L, pin-ended at both ends, subjected to an axial compressive load P.**

At the instant of buckling, the column deflects laterally by a small amount **y** at distance **x** from the bottom.

**Step 2: Bending Moment at any section:**

At a section x from the bottom, the bending moment **M** is:

$$M = -P \cdot y$$

(Negative sign indicates that the moment creates concavity opposite to the deflection y. The exact sign depends on the coordinate system, but the magnitude is what matters for buckling.)

**Step 3: Euler-Bernoulli Beam Equation:**

The differential equation governing the deflected shape of the column is:

$$EI \cdot \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M = -P \cdot y$$

$$EI \cdot \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} + P \cdot y = 0$$

**Step 4: Solving the differential equation:**

Let **k² = P/(EI)**. Then:

$$\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} + k^2 y = 0$$

The general solution is:

$$y = A\cos(kx) + B\sin(kx)$$

**Step 5: Applying Boundary Conditions:**

At **x = 0** (bottom pin): **y = 0** → A·1 + B·0 = 0 → **A = 0**.
At **x = L** (top pin): **y = 0** → A·cos(kL) + B·sin(kL) = 0.

Since A = 0:
$$B\sin(kL) = 0$$

**Step 6: Non-trivial solution (B ≠ 0):**

For buckling to occur (i.e., non-zero deflection), we need:

$$\sin(kL) = 0 \implies kL = n\pi \quad \text{where} \quad n = 1, 2, 3, ...$$

**Step 7: Finding the critical load:**

The **smallest critical load** corresponds to **n = 1** (the first buckling mode):

$$k = \frac{\pi}{L} \implies \frac{P}{EI} = \frac{\pi^2}{L^2}$$

$$\boxed{P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2}}$$

✅ **Euler's critical load derived for pinned-pinned column.**

**Step 8: Generalizing to other end conditions:**

The general form of Euler's formula is:

$$\boxed{P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{L_{eff}^2} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{(kL)^2}}$$

Where **k** is the effective length factor based on end conditions.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Effective Length for Fixed-Free Column

**Setup:** A column of length L, fixed at the bottom and free at the top. Subjected to axial compressive load P.

When it buckles, the bottom is fixed (no deflection, no rotation), and the top deflects sideways by amount **δ**.

The deflected shape has zero slope at the bottom (fixed end), and the entire column behaves as the **upper half** of a pinned-pinned column of length **2L**.

Therefore, the effective length is **L_eff = 2L**, and:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{(2L)^2} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{4L^2}$$

**For fixed-pinned column (one end fixed, other pinned):**
The effective length is obtained from the transcendental equation **tan(kL) = kL**, yielding **kL ≈ 4.493**, hence:

$$L_{eff} = \frac{\pi}{k} \cdot L = \frac{\pi}{4.493} \cdot L \cdot \pi \approx 0.6992 \cdot L \cdot \pi$$

A simpler standard result is **L_eff = L/√2 ≈ 0.707 L**.

**For fixed-fixed column (both ends fixed):**
Both ends fixed means no rotation. The deflected shape has an inflection point at mid-height. So the buckling mode resembles a pinned-pinned column of half the length:

$$L_{eff} = \frac{L}{2}$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{(L/2)^2} = \frac{4\pi^2 EI}{L^2}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Principal Stress

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** At a point in a stressed body, the stresses on the x-face and y-face are: σₓ = 80 MPa, σᵧ = 40 MPa, τₓᵧ = 30 MPa. Determine: (a) Principal stresses, (b) Principal plane angle, (c) Maximum shear stress.

**Given:**
σₓ = 80 MPa, σᵧ = 40 MPa, τₓᵧ = 30 MPa.

**Step 1: Center of Mohr's circle:**
$$C = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{80 + 40}{2} = 60 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Radius of Mohr's circle:**
$$R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{80 - 40}{2}\right)^2 + (30)^2}$$

$$R = \sqrt{(20)^2 + (30)^2} = \sqrt{400 + 900} = \sqrt{1300} \approx 36.06 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Principal stresses:**
$$\sigma_1 = C + R = 60 + 36.06 = 96.06 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_2 = C - R = 60 - 36.06 = 23.94 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Principal plane angle:**
$$\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2 \times 30}{80 - 40} = \frac{60}{40} = 1.5$$

$$2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(1.5) = 56.31° \implies \theta_p = 28.15°$$

**Step 5: Maximum shear stress:**
$$\tau_{max} = R = 36.06 \text{ MPa}$$

The plane of maximum shear is at θ_s = θ_p − 45° = 28.15° − 45° = −16.85° (or 16.85° clockwise from x-axis).

On this plane, the normal stress equals the center value: σₙ = 60 MPa.

✅ **Numerical example solved.**

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Euler Buckling

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A solid circular steel column has diameter 50 mm, length 3 m, and is pinned at both ends. Take E = 200 GPa. Determine: (a) Euler's critical load, (b) Euler's critical stress, (c) Slenderness ratio. Also verify if Euler's formula is applicable.

**Given:**
Diameter d = 50 mm → A = π·d²/4 = π·(50)²/4 = 1963.5 mm²
Length L = 3 m = 3000 mm
E = 200 GPa = 200,000 N/mm² = 2×10⁵ MPa
End condition: Both ends pinned → L_eff = L = 3000 mm

**Step 1: Second moment of area for solid circular section:**
$$I = \frac{\pi d^4}{64} = \frac{\pi \times (50)^4}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^6}{64} = 306,796 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2: Euler's critical load:**
$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 306,796}{(3000)^2}$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 6.13592 \times 10^{10}}{9 \times 10^6}$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{6.0553 \times 10^{11}}{9 \times 10^6} = 67,281 \text{ N} \approx 67.28 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Euler's critical stress:**
$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{P_{cr}}{A} = \frac{67,281}{1963.5} \approx 34.26 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Slenderness ratio:**
$$r = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{306,796}{1963.5}} = \sqrt{156.25} = 12.5 \text{ mm}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{L_{eff}}{r} = \frac{3000}{12.5} = 240$$

**Step 5: Verification — Is Euler's formula applicable?**
Assuming σ_y = 250 MPa for mild steel, λ_c ≈ 80. Since λ = 240 >> 80, the column is a **long column**, and Euler's formula **IS applicable**. ✅

✅ **Buckling example solved.**

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Stress Transformation Pipeline

This flow chart shows the **decision pipeline** a student/analyst should follow when solving a principal stress / Mohr's circle problem:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1: Read Given Stresses sigma_x sigma_y tau_xy] --> B[Step2: Compute Center C equals sigma_x plus sigma_y divided by 2]
    B --> C[Step3: Compute Radius R equals sqrt of sigma_x minus sigma_y over 2 squared plus tau_xy squared]
    C --> D[Step4: Compute Principal Stresses sigma1 equals C plus R and sigma2 equals C minus R]
    C --> E[Step5: Compute Max Shear Stress tau_max equals R]
    D --> F[Step6: Compute Principal Plane Angle tan2theta_p equals 2 tau_xy divided by sigma_x minus sigma_y]
    F --> G[Step7: Identify sigma1 is major and sigma2 is minor]
    G --> H[Step8: Plot Mohr Circle with center C and radius R]
    H --> I[Step9: Verify by substituting theta_p back into sigma_n equation to confirm tau equals zero]
    I --> J[Final Answer Box: sigma1 sigma2 theta_p tau_max]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Column Buckling Selection Flow

This flow chart shows the **decision tree** for selecting the right column formula based on slenderness ratio:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1: Identify End Conditions of the Column] --> B[Step2: Compute Effective Length L_eff using k factor]
    B --> C[Step3: Compute Cross-Sectional Properties A I r]
    C --> D[Step4: Compute Slenderness Ratio lambda equals L_eff divided by r]
    D --> E[Step5: Compute Critical Slenderness Ratio lambda_c equals sqrt pi squared E over sigma_c]
    E --> F{Decision: Is lambda greater than lambda_c?}
    F -->|YES Long Column| G[Use EULER Formula P_cr equals pi squared EI over L_eff squared]
    F -->|NO Short Column| H{Decision: Is lambda between lambda_c and some lower bound?}
    H -->|YES Intermediate Column| I[Use JOHNSON Formula or RANKINE Formula]
    H -->|NO Very Short Column| J[Material YIELD or CRUSHING governs]
    G --> K[Final Answer P_cr in Newtons]
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Mohr's Circle Construction Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mark Center C on sigma axis] --> B[From X-face Point plot X sigma_x minus tau_xy]
    A --> C[From Y-face Point plot Y sigma_y plus tau_xy]
    B --> D[Draw Diameter XY through circle]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Circle complete radius R]
    E --> F[Read sigma1 at right intersection with sigma axis]
    E --> G[Read sigma2 at left intersection with sigma axis]
    E --> H[Read tau_max at top and bottom of circle]
    F --> I[Final Principal Stresses Obtained]
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mohr's Circle Construction — Key Steps (Physical Plane vs. Mohr's Plane):**
> 1. **Plot points** X = (σₓ, −τₓᵧ) and Y = (σᵧ, +τₓᵧ) on σ–τ axes. (Note: shear axis convention — downward positive in KTU.)
> 2. **Draw the circle** with diameter XY.
> 3. **The intersections** of the circle with the σ-axis are the principal stresses σ₁ and σ₂.
> 4. **The topmost/bottommost points** of the circle give ±τ_max.
> 5. **Angle on Mohr's circle** = **2 × (angle in physical plane)**. Rotation direction is the same.
