# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 📝 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define principal stresses and principal planes. Show that the sum of two perpendicular normal stresses on a plane remains invariant.**

**Model Answer:**

**Principal stresses:** The maximum and minimum normal stresses occurring at a point in a stressed material on planes where the shear stress is zero.

**Principal planes:** The planes on which principal stresses act; these planes are mutually perpendicular and have zero shear stress.

**Proof of invariance:**
The sum of normal stresses on two perpendicular planes:

$$\sigma_n + \sigma_{n'} = \left[\frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta\right] + \left[\frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} - \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta - \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta\right]$$

$$\sigma_n + \sigma_{n'} = \sigma_x + \sigma_y \quad \text{(independent of } \theta \text{)}$$

Hence, the sum is **invariant** (= σₓ + σᵧ). ✅

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**State Euler's formula for buckling of a long column. How does the effective length change with different end conditions?**

**Model Answer:**

**Euler's Formula:** The critical buckling load for a long, slender column is:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{L_{eff}^2}$$

Where E = Young's modulus, I = minimum moment of inertia, L_eff = effective length.

**Effective length for different end conditions:**

| End Condition | Effective Length L_eff |
|---|---|
| Both ends pinned | L |
| Both ends fixed | L/2 |
| One end fixed, other pinned | L/√2 |
| One end fixed, other free | 2L |

The effective length is smallest when both ends are fixed (most stable) and largest when one end is free (least stable). ✅

---

## 📝 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

At a point in a structural member, the stresses on two mutually perpendicular planes are:
- On plane X-face: σₓ = 120 MPa (tensile), τₓᵧ = 40 MPa
- On plane Y-face: σᵧ = −60 MPa (compressive)

**(a)** Determine the **principal stresses** and the **orientation of the principal planes**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Construct **Mohr's circle** of stress and hence find the **maximum shear stress** and the **normal stress on the plane of maximum shear**. Also determine the **angle of obliquity** for a plane at θ = 30°. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a): Principal Stresses and Their Orientations

**Given:**
σₓ = 120 MPa, σᵧ = −60 MPa, τₓᵧ = 40 MPa

**Step 1: Center of Mohr's circle:**
$$C = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{120 + (-60)}{2} = \frac{60}{2} = 30 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Radius of Mohr's circle:**
$$R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{120 - (-60)}{2}\right)^2 + (40)^2}$$

$$R = \sqrt{(90)^2 + (40)^2} = \sqrt{8100 + 1600} = \sqrt{9700} \approx 98.49 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Principal stresses:**
$$\sigma_1 = C + R = 30 + 98.49 = 128.49 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_2 = C - R = 30 - 98.49 = -68.49 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Orientation of principal planes:**
$$\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2 \times 40}{120 - (-60)} = \frac{80}{180} = 0.4444$$

$$2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(0.4444) = 23.96°$$

$$\theta_{p1} = 11.98° \approx 12° \quad \text{(Major principal plane — direction of σ₁)}$$

$$\theta_{p2} = 12° + 90° = 102° \quad \text{(Minor principal plane — direction of σ₂)}$$

✅ **Part (a) solved.**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b): Mohr's Circle, Max Shear Stress, Obliquity at θ = 30°

**Step 1: Construct Mohr's circle.**

- **Center:** C = (30, 0)
- **Plot point X-face:** (σₓ, −τₓᵧ) = (120, −40)
- **Plot point Y-face:** (σᵧ, +τₓᵧ) = (−60, +40)
- **Radius:** R = 98.49 MPa
- **The circle** passes through X and Y, with diameter XY.

**Step 2: Maximum shear stress:**
$$\tau_{max} = R = 98.49 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Normal stress on plane of maximum shear:**
$$\sigma_n = C = 30 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Plane of maximum shear orientation:**
$$\tan 2\theta_s = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}} = -\frac{180}{80} = -2.25$$

$$2\theta_s = -66.04° \implies \theta_s = -33.02° \quad \text{(or 56.98°)}$$

This is consistent with θ_s = θ_p − 45° = 12° − 45° = −33° ✅.

**Step 5: Stresses on plane at θ = 30° (using transformation equations):**

$$\sigma_n = C + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$$

At θ = 30°, 2θ = 60°, cos 60° = 0.5, sin 60° = 0.866:

$$\sigma_n = 30 + 90 \times 0.5 + 40 \times 0.866 = 30 + 45 + 34.64 = 109.64 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\tau_n = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta$$

$$\tau_n = -90 \times 0.866 + 40 \times 0.5 = -77.94 + 20 = -57.94 \text{ MPa}$$

(Negative sign means shear stress is in the opposite direction to assumed positive convention.)

**Step 6: Angle of obliquity (φ):**
$$\tan \phi = \frac{\vert \tau_n \vert}{\sigma_n} = \frac{57.94}{109.64} = 0.5285$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.5285) = 27.86°$$

✅ **Part (b) solved.**

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Alternative]`

A **hollow circular cast iron column** has:
- Outer diameter D = 200 mm
- Inner diameter d = 150 mm
- Length L = 6 m
- Both ends **fixed**
- E = 200 GPa, σ_c (crushing stress) = 550 MPa
- Rankine's constant a = 1/1600

**(a)** Determine the **crippling load** using **Euler's formula** and **Rankine's formula**. State which is more appropriate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the **critical slenderness ratio** and check the **applicability** of Euler's formula. Also calculate the **Euler's critical stress**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a): Crippling Load by Euler and Rankine

**Given:**
D = 200 mm, d = 150 mm, L = 6 m = 6000 mm, both ends fixed → L_eff = L/2 = 3000 mm
E = 200,000 N/mm², σ_c = 550 MPa, a = 1/1600

**Step 1: Cross-sectional area A:**
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(200^2 - 150^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(40000 - 22500) = \frac{\pi}{4}(17500)$$

$$A = 13,744.47 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Second moment of area I:**
$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}(D^4 - d^4) = \frac{\pi}{64}(200^4 - 150^4) = \frac{\pi}{64}(1.6 \times 10^9 - 5.0625 \times 10^8)$$

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}(1.09375 \times 10^9) = \frac{\pi \times 1.09375 \times 10^9}{64} = 53,679,290 \text{ mm}^4 \approx 5.368 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 3: Radius of gyration k (using k to avoid confusion with factor):**
$$k_{gyr} = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{5.368 \times 10^7}{13,744.47}} = \sqrt{3905.56} \approx 62.50 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Euler's critical load:**
$$P_{Euler} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{L_{eff}^2} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 200,000 \times 5.368 \times 10^7}{(3000)^2}$$

$$P_{Euler} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 1.0736 \times 10^{13}}{9 \times 10^6} = \frac{1.059 \times 10^{14}}{9 \times 10^6} \approx 1.177 \times 10^7 \text{ N} = 11,768 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Rankine's critical load:**
$$P_{Rankine} = \frac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a(L_{eff}/k_{gyr})^2}$$

$$L_{eff}/k_{gyr} = 3000 / 62.50 = 48$$

$$P_{Rankine} = \frac{550 \times 13,744.47}{1 + (1/1600) \times (48)^2} = \frac{7,559,460}{1 + (2304/1600)} = \frac{7,559,460}{1 + 1.44} = \frac{7,559,460}{2.44}$$

$$P_{Rankine} \approx 3,098,131 \text{ N} \approx 3,098 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6: Comparison:**
Euler's load (11,768 kN) >> Rankine's load (3,098 kN). Since the slenderness ratio is only 48, this is **not a long column** — Euler's formula **overestimates** the strength here. **Rankine's formula is more appropriate** as it accounts for both elastic buckling and material crushing.

✅ **Part (a) solved.**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b): Critical Slenderness Ratio and Euler's Critical Stress

**Step 1: Critical slenderness ratio:**
$$\lambda_c = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2 E}{\sigma_c}} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2 \times 200,000}{550}} = \sqrt{\frac{1,973,920.88}{550}} = \sqrt{3589.86} \approx 59.92$$

**Step 2: Actual slenderness ratio:**
$$\lambda = \frac{L_{eff}}{k_{gyr}} = \frac{3000}{62.50} = 48$$

**Step 3: Check applicability:**
Since λ = 48 < λ_c = 59.92, the column is an **intermediate/short column**, NOT a long column. Therefore, **Euler's formula is NOT directly applicable** in the strict sense. This **validates our choice of Rankine's formula** from Part (a).

**Step 4: Euler's critical stress (theoretical, for reference):**
$$\sigma_{cr, Euler} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 200,000}{(48)^2} = \frac{1,973,920.88}{2304} \approx 856.74 \text{ MPa}$$

**Note:** This is much greater than σ_c = 550 MPa, confirming that Euler's formula is **inapplicable** here because yielding/crushing would occur before elastic buckling.

**Step 5: Rankine's critical stress (for comparison):**
$$\sigma_{cr, Rankine} = \frac{\sigma_c}{1 + a\lambda^2} = \frac{550}{1 + (1/1600)(48)^2} = \frac{550}{2.44} \approx 225.41 \text{ MPa}$$

✅ **Part (b) solved.**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cause Marks Deduction (Read Carefully!):**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** Many students plot Mohr's circle with shear axis **upward positive** instead of the KTU standard of **downward positive**. This reverses the orientation and gives a wrong value of θ_p. **Lose 1–2 marks.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Double the Angle:** On Mohr's circle, the rotation is **2θ**, not θ. If you measure the angle from the X-point to the principal point as, say, 24°, the physical plane angle is **12°**, not 24°. **Lose 2–3 marks.**
>
> 3. **Using L instead of L_eff in Euler's formula:** Students frequently forget to use the effective length factor for different end conditions. **Lose 2 marks minimum.**
>
> 4. **Confusing Pinned-Pinned (k=1) vs Fixed-Pinned (k=1/√2):** A very common error is using L for a fixed-pinned column instead of L/√2. **Lose 2 marks.**
>
> 5. **Not Checking Euler's Applicability:** Computing P_cr using Euler without checking λ vs λ_c and getting a value that is unrealistic. Always compute λ and compare with λ_c. **Lose 1–2 marks.**
>
> 6. **Wrong units of E:** Using E = 2×10⁵ N/mm² vs 2×10¹¹ N/m² vs 200 GPa — students mix units. **Lose 1 mark per occurrence.**
>
> 7. **Forgetting I_min:** Buckling occurs about the **weakest axis**, so I should be the **minimum** second moment of area. For unequal sections, this is critical. **Lose 2 marks.**
>
> 8. **Order of σ₁ and σ₂:** Convention says σ₁ ≥ σ₂. Students sometimes label them the other way around. **Lose 1 mark.**

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Print This for Last-Minute Revision:**

✅ **Principal Stresses:** Maximum and minimum normal stresses at a point, occurring on planes where **shear stress = 0**.

✅ **Principal Plane Angle:** tan 2θₚ = 2τₓᵧ / (σₓ − σᵧ) → **two values 90° apart**.

✅ **Principal Stresses Formula:** σ₁,₂ = (σₓ + σᵧ)/2 ± √[((σₓ − σᵧ)/2)² + τₓᵧ²].

✅ **Mohr's Circle Center:** C = (σₓ + σᵧ)/2, on σ-axis.

✅ **Mohr's Circle Radius:** R = √[((σₓ − σᵧ)/2)² + τₓᵧ²] = τ_max.

✅ **Mohr's Circle Angle:** **2θ** on circle = **θ** in physical plane (same direction of rotation).

✅ **Invariant Sum:** σₙ + σₙ' = σₓ + σᵧ (independent of θ).

✅ **Euler's Critical Load:** P_cr = π²EI / L²_eff → for **long columns only (λ > λ_c)**.

✅ **Effective Length L_eff:** Pinned-Pinned = L, Fixed-Fixed = L/2, Fixed-Pinned = L/√2, Fixed-Free = 2L.

✅ **Euler's Critical Stress:** σ_cr = π²E / λ², where λ = L_eff/r = L_eff/√(I/A).

✅ **Slenderness Ratio:** λ = L_eff / r — **higher λ means more slender → buckling-prone**.

✅ **Critical Slenderness Ratio:** λ_c = √(π²E / σ_c) — transition between long and short columns.

✅ **Rankine's Formula (universal):** P_R = σ_c·A / [1 + a(L_eff/k)²], a = σ_c / (π²·E).

✅ **Johnson's Formula (intermediate columns):** σ_cr = σ_y − [((σ_y − σ_c)/σ_c)]·σ_y·λ².

✅ **Selection Rule:** λ > λ_c → Euler; λ < λ_c → Rankine or Johnson; very small λ → material failure.

✅ **Sign Convention (KTU):** Tensile = +ve, Compressive = −ve, CCW θ = +ve, Shear plotted **downward positive** on Mohr's circle.

✅ **Engineering Applications:** Building columns, bridge piers, masts, drill strings (buckling); pressure vessels, shafts, machine parts (Mohr's circle).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Euler's Buckling/Crippling Load for Columns with Different End Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT402 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Euler's Buckling/Crippling Load for Columns with Different End Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Buckling Load (Crippling Load):** The axial compressive load at which a long, slender structural member (column) suddenly undergoes lateral deflection (transverse displacement) perpendicular to its axis, causing it to fail by instability rather than by direct crushing of the material.

Mathematically, it is the critical compressive load $P_e$ at which the column transitions from a state of stable **straight equilibrium** to an unstable **bent (buckled) equilibrium** configuration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Leonhard Euler (1757)** first derived the mathematical theory of column buckling. He established that a perfectly straight, axially loaded, slender column will buckle elastically when the load reaches a specific critical threshold — independent of the material's crushing strength.

The classical Euler Crippling Load equation is:

$$P_e = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$$

Where:
- $P_e$ = Euler's critical (crippling/buckling) load, in **Newtons (N)**
- $E$ = Young's Modulus of Elasticity of the column material, in **N/m² or Pa**
- $I$ = Area Moment of Inertia of the cross-section about the **axis of buckling (neutral axis)**, in **m⁴**
- $L_e$ = **Effective Length** of the column (depends on end conditions), in **m**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Ruler on the Table" Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Ruler Trick:**
> Take a thin plastic ruler. Hold it vertically on a table and press down gently on top.
> - If your fingers are **loose (pinned)**, the ruler bends easily at a very small load — it buckles.
> - If you **grip it firmly at top and bottom (fixed)**, it resists much more load before it buckles.
> - If you let the **top slide (free)**, it buckles almost instantly under its own weight.

This everyday experiment precisely demonstrates how the **end-fixity of a column governs its buckling strength**. The mathematical embodiment of this physical intuition is the *Effective Length ($L_e$)* concept.

## 1.3 Key Physical Concepts & Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Slenderness Ratio ($\lambda$)** | Ratio of effective length to the radius of gyration: $\lambda = \dfrac{L_e}{k}$ |
| **Radius of Gyration ($k$)** | $k = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}}$, where $A$ is cross-sectional area |
| **Effective Length ($L_e$)** | Length of an equivalent *pinned-pinned* column having the same buckling load |
| **Column** | A structural member whose primary load is axial compression |
| **Critical Stress ($\sigma_{cr}$)** | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{P_e}{A} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Crippling Load vs Crushing Load:** Crippling load causes failure by *instability (geometric)*, while crushing load causes failure by *material yielding*. For slender columns, crippling governs; for short columns, crushing governs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Background Assumptions in Euler's Theory

Euler's classical theory is built upon the following idealizing assumptions:

1. The column is **initially perfectly straight** (no initial curvature).
2. The load is **truly axial** (passes through the centroid — no eccentricity).
3. The column material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** (obeys Hooke's Law).
4. The **self-weight** of the column is neglected in comparison to the applied load.
5. The **ends are frictionless** in the case of pinned supports (or fully restrained in fixed cases).
6. **Shortening due to direct compression** is neglected — only bending deformation is considered.
7. Failure occurs by **bending only** (no twisting or lateral-torsional buckling considered).

## 2.2 The Concept of Effective Length ($L_e$)

The effective length is the most critical concept that captures the influence of end conditions. It is defined as:

$$L_e = \beta \cdot L$$

where $\beta$ is a dimensionless constant called the **Effective Length Factor (or Coefficient of End Fixity)** that depends solely on the rotational and translational restraints at the column ends.

The critical insight: **Any column buckling problem can be reduced to the standard pinned-pinned case** by substituting $L$ with $L_e$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — All End Conditions at a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD CHEAT SHEET — Memorize This Table for KTU Exams**

| **End Condition** | **Diagram (Bent Shape)** | **Effective Length ($L_e$)** | **Effective Length Factor ($\beta$)** | **Crippling Load ($P_e$)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Both Ends Hinged** *(Pinned-Pinned)* | Half sine wave | $L_e = L$ | $\beta = 1.0$ | $P_e = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L^2}$ |
| **Both Ends Fixed** *(Fixed-Fixed)* | Quarter wave (S-shape with inflexion at mid) | $L_e = \dfrac{L}{2}$ | $\beta = 0.5$ | $P_e = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 E I}{L^2}$ |
| **One End Fixed, Other Free** *(Fixed-Free / Cantilever)* | Quarter wave | $L_e = 2L$ | $\beta = 2.0$ | $P_e = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{4 L^2}$ |
| **One End Fixed, Other Hinged** *(Fixed-Pinned)* | Curve with off-centre inflection point | $L_e = \dfrac{L}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 L$ | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ | $P_e = \dfrac{2 \pi^2 E I}{L^2}$ |

### 2.3.1 Equivalent Formulas (Critical Stress Form)

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{P_e}{A} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{\left(L_e / k\right)^2} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}$$

This is the **Euler critical stress formula**, expressed in terms of the slenderness ratio $\lambda$.

### 2.3.2 Inflection Point (Point of Contraflexure) — Why $L_e$ Works

> [!TIP]
> An **inflection point** is where the curvature changes sign — it is physically equivalent to a **pinned support** in terms of internal bending moment.
> Therefore, $L_e$ is simply the **distance between two consecutive inflection points** (or between an end support and the nearest inflection point) on the buckled shape.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Euler buckling formula is foundational in:
- **Structural Engineering:** Design of building columns, bridge piers, and tower legs.
- **Aerospace Engineering:** Sizing of struts in aircraft fuselages and rocket motor casings.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Design of piston rods in hydraulic presses, push-rods in IC engines, and jack-screws.
- **Civil Engineering:** Slenderness checks as mandated by **IS 800:2007 (Indian Steel Code)** and **IS 456 (Concrete Code)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Code Limit:** IS 800 recommends that the slenderness ratio $\lambda$ for compression members should **not exceed 180** for general columns and **250** for bracing members (to prevent excessive flexibility).

## 2.5 Slenderness Ratio Classification of Columns

| Classification | Slenderness Ratio $\lambda$ | Governing Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Long (Slender) Column** | $\lambda \geq \lambda_c$ (typically > 80–100) | **Euler Buckling** (Elastic Instability) |
| **Intermediate Column** | Moderately high | Empirical formulas (e.g., Rankine, IS code) |
| **Short Column** | $\lambda$ small (< 30–40) | **Crushing / Yielding** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Euler's formula is **valid ONLY for long (slender) columns**. Using it for short columns gives dangerously non-conservative results because the actual failure load (crushing) is much lower than the predicted buckling load.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Case 1: Both Ends Hinged (Pinned-Pinned) — The Standard Case

**Setup:** A column of length $L$ is pin-supported at both ends. An axial compressive load $P$ is applied at the centroid.

### 3.1.1 Governing Differential Equation

Consider the column buckled into a slightly curved shape. Take the origin at the bottom pin and let $y$ be the lateral deflection. The bending moment at any section $x$ is:

$$M = -P \cdot y$$

(negative because the moment from axial load $P$ opposes the restoring moment $EI \, y''$).

Using the **Euler-Bernoulli Beam Equation**:

$$EI \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M = -P y$$

Rearranging:

$$EI \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + P y = 0$$

Dividing by $EI$:

$$\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + \frac{P}{EI} y = 0$$

### 3.1.2 Solution of the Differential Equation

Let $k^2 = \dfrac{P}{EI}$. The equation becomes:

$$\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + k^2 y = 0$$

This is a standard second-order linear ODE. Its general solution is:

$$y(x) = A \cos(kx) + B \sin(kx)$$

### 3.1.3 Applying Boundary Conditions

**At $x = 0$** (bottom pin): $y(0) = 0$
$$y(0) = A \cos(0) + B \sin(0) = A = 0$$
So $A = 0$.

**At $x = L$** (top pin): $y(L) = 0$
$$y(L) = 0 \cdot \cos(kL) + B \sin(kL) = B \sin(kL) = 0$$

For a non-trivial (buckled) solution, $B \neq 0$, hence:
$$\sin(kL) = 0$$

The non-trivial roots of $\sin(kL) = 0$ are $kL = n\pi$ for $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$.

### 3.1.4 Finding the Critical (Smallest) Load

The smallest value corresponds to $n = 1$ (the *first* buckling mode), giving:

$$kL = \pi \implies k = \frac{\pi}{L}$$

Substituting back into $k^2 = \dfrac{P}{EI}$:

$$\frac{P}{EI} = \frac{\pi^2}{L^2}$$

$$\boxed{P_e = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Hence for pinned-pinned columns:** $L_e = L$, $\beta = 1$, and $P_e = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2}$.

---

## 3.2 Case 2: Both Ends Fixed (Fixed-Fixed)

**Setup:** Both ends are perfectly clamped — no rotation and no translation allowed at either end.

### 3.2.1 Symmetry Argument — A Powerful Shortcut

By symmetry of geometry and loading, the buckled shape must be **anti-symmetric about the mid-height**. This means:
- The **tangent at mid-height is vertical** (zero slope at mid).
- The **midpoint is an inflection point** (zero bending moment at mid).

Therefore, only **the top half** of the column behaves like a pinned-pinned column of length $L/2$.

### 3.2.2 Direct Result via Effective Length

The **effective length** is the distance between inflection points:

$$L_e = \frac{L}{2}$$

Substituting into the Euler formula:

$$\boxed{P_e = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{(L/2)^2} = \frac{4\pi^2 E I}{L^2}}$$

### 3.2.3 Verification via Full Differential Equation

The ODE remains the same:
$$\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + k^2 y = 0 \implies y = A \cos(kx) + B \sin(kx)$$

**Boundary conditions:**
- $y(0) = 0$ ⟹ $A = 0$, so $y = B \sin(kx)$.
- $y'(0) = 0$ (fixed end — zero slope) ⟹ $B k \cos(0) = 0$. Since $B \neq 0$, this requires $\cos(0) = 1 \neq 0$, so this BC needs modification. Wait — we need to apply BC at **two ends**.

**Better approach:** Apply four BCs and use the **determinant-of-coefficients = 0** (characteristic equation) trick.

Use the general form: $y = A \cos(kx) + B \sin(kx)$, so $y' = -Ak \sin(kx) + Bk \cos(kx)$.

BCs:
1. $y(0) = 0 \implies A = 0$
2. $y'(0) = 0 \implies Bk = 0 \implies B = 0$

This gives a trivial solution only — because the symmetric buckling mode requires a **third-order stiffness matrix treatment**. The correct physical mode is the *anti-symmetric* one. By symmetry, mid-height has $y'(L/2) = 0$, and using antisymmetric extension:

The non-trivial condition yields: $\sin(kL) = 0$ for the half-wave, giving $kL/2 = \pi$ for the first anti-symmetric mode... 

> [!TIP]
> **Cleaner reasoning:** Treat each half as a pinned-pinned column of length $L/2$. So $k(L/2) = \pi$, giving $k = 2\pi/L$, and $P_e = k^2 EI = 4\pi^2 EI / L^2$. ✅

---

## 3.3 Case 3: One End Fixed, Other End Free (Cantilever Column)

**Setup:** Bottom is rigidly clamped, top is completely free.

### 3.3.1 Physical Reasoning

A free end has **no moment and no shear reaction**. By reflecting the buckled shape about the fixed end, the mirror image is identical. Hence the **inflection point is at the fixed end itself**, and the distance from the inflection point to the free end is $L$. So $L_e = L$ for the *upper half of a mirror-paired column of total length $2L$*:

$$L_e = 2L$$

### 3.3.2 Direct Result

$$\boxed{P_e = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{(2L)^2} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{4 L^2}}$$

### 3.3.3 Verification via ODE

General solution: $y = A \cos(kx) + B \sin(kx)$, $y' = -Ak \sin(kx) + Bk \cos(kx)$.

Origin at the fixed end, $x$ measured upward.

**Boundary Conditions:**
1. $y(0) = 0 \implies A = 0$
2. $y'(0) = 0 \implies Bk \cos(0) = 0 \implies Bk = 0 \implies B = 0$

Same issue as Case 2 — this gives only a trivial solution. **This indicates our choice of origin is wrong.** Place origin at the **free end**, with $x$ measured downward. The free end has:

- $y(0) = \delta$ (some unknown deflection at top, but moment is zero)
- $y''(0) = 0$ (no moment at free end since $M = -EI y''$, and $M = 0$ there)

General solution: $y(x) = A \cos(kx) + B \sin(kx)$, $y''(x) = -Ak^2 \cos(kx) - Bk^2 \sin(kx)$.

At $x = 0$: $y''(0) = -Ak^2 = 0 \implies A = 0$. So $y = B \sin(kx)$.

At $x = L$ (fixed end): $y(L) = 0 \implies B \sin(kL) = 0$. Non-trivial: $\sin(kL) = 0$.

For the *smallest* non-trivial root: $kL = \pi \implies k = \pi/L$.

Therefore:

$$P_e = k^2 EI = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2}$$

Wait — this gives the *same* result as pinned-pinned of length $L$. **The catch:** the *effective length* for a cantilever is $L_e = 2L$, but the ODE result above (using the cantilever buckled into a half sine-wave of length $L$) actually corresponds to $L_e = L$ mathematically. Let me reconsider.

> [!CAUTION]
> **Reconciliation:** A *cantilever column* (fixed-free) buckles such that the deflection $y$ satisfies $y(0) = y'(0) = 0$ at the fixed end and $y''(L) = y'''(L) = 0$ at the free end. Solving with all four BCs gives the characteristic equation $\tan(kL) = kL$, whose smallest non-trivial root is $kL \approx 4.493$, leading to $P_e \approx \dfrac{20.19 EI}{L^2}$.
> However, in KTU Strength of Materials (syllabus scope), the simplified **effective length approach** is followed: $L_e = 2L$, giving $P_e = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{4L^2}$.

For KTU syllabus purposes (introductory treatment), the canonical answer is:

$$\boxed{P_e = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{4 L^2} \quad \text{with } L_e = 2L}$$

---

## 3.4 Case 4: One End Fixed, Other End Hinged (Fixed-Pinned)

**Setup:** Bottom fixed (clamped), top pinned (hinged).

### 3.4.1 Effective Length Approach (KTU Syllabus)

For this case, the inflection point is **not at the mid-point** but at a distance $\dfrac{L}{\sqrt{2}}$ from the fixed end. Hence:

$$L_e = \frac{L}{\sqrt{2}}$$

### 3.4.2 Crippling Load

$$\boxed{P_e = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{(L/\sqrt{2})^2} = \frac{2 \pi^2 E I}{L^2}}$$

### 3.4.3 Verification via ODE

The exact characteristic equation for this case (with all four BCs) is $\tan(kL) = kL$, whose smallest root gives $kL \approx 4.493$ (same as the cantilever), yielding $P_e \approx \dfrac{20.19 EI}{L^2}$.

Comparing: $\dfrac{2\pi^2 EI}{L^2} \approx \dfrac{19.74 EI}{L^2}$, which is very close to the exact $20.19$ — a difference of only $\approx 2.3\%$. This is why the KTU syllabus adopts the approximate formula:

$$\boxed{P_e = \frac{2\pi^2 EI}{L^2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU exams, always use the four standard formulas from the cheat sheet above.** The transcendental equation solution is beyond the scope of the introductory Strength of Materials course.

---

## 3.5 Summary Table of All Four Cases — Analytical Matrix

| **Case** | **End Conditions** | **Effective Length $L_e$** | **Crippling Load $P_e$** | **Relative Strength** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| I | Both Hinged | $L$ | $\dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2}$ | $1.00 \, P_{ref}$ |
| II | Both Fixed | $L/2$ | $\dfrac{4\pi^2 EI}{L^2}$ | $4.00 \, P_{ref}$ |
| III | Fixed–Free | $2L$ | $\dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{4L^2}$ | $0.25 \, P_{ref}$ |
| IV | Fixed–Hinged | $L/\sqrt{2}$ | $\dfrac{2\pi^2 EI}{L^2}$ | $2.00 \, P_{ref}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Going from a pinned-pinned column to a fixed-fixed column **quadruples** the buckling capacity. Hence, in real construction, columns are deliberately designed with end restraints (e.g., welded base plates, moment-resisting connections) to enhance stability.

---

## 3.6 Limitation of Euler's Formula — Validity Range

Euler's formula assumes that the **maximum compressive stress at buckling does NOT exceed the proportional limit** of the material. This translates to:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2} \leq \sigma_{proportional \, limit}$$

Solving for the **limiting slenderness ratio**:

$$\lambda_c = \pi \sqrt{\frac{E}{\sigma_{p}}}$$

where $\lambda_c$ is the **critical slenderness ratio**. Columns with $\lambda > \lambda_c$ are classified as **long columns** and Euler's formula is directly applicable.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Buckled Shapes for All Four End Conditions (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following block-level schematic illustrates the buckled configuration, support conditions, inflection points, and effective length for each case.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CASE1["CASE 1: BOTH ENDS HINGED - Effective Length Le = L"]
        A1[Top Hinge - No Translation, Free Rotation]:::hinge
        A2[Inflection Point at Mid-Height L by 2]:::inflection
        A3[Bottom Hinge - No Translation, Free Rotation]:::hinge
        A1 --- A2
        A2 --- A3
    end

    subgraph CASE2["CASE 2: BOTH ENDS FIXED - Effective Length Le = L by 2"]
        B1[Top Fixed - No Rotation, No Translation]:::fixed
        B2[Inflection Point at L by 4 from top]:::inflection
        B3[Inflection Point at 3L by 4 from top]:::inflection
        B4[Bottom Fixed - No Rotation, No Translation]:::fixed
        B1 --- B2
        B2 --- B3
        B3 --- B4
    end

    subgraph CASE3["CASE 3: FIXED FREE CANTILEVER - Effective Length Le = 2L"]
        C1[Top Free - No Restraint, Free to Move]:::free
        C2[Inflection Point at Fixed Base]:::inflection
        C3[Bottom Fixed - No Rotation, No Translation]:::fixed
        C1 --- C2
        C2 --- C3
    end

    subgraph CASE4["CASE 4: FIXED HINGED - Effective Length Le = L by sqrt of 2"]
        D1[Top Hinged - No Translation, Free Rotation]:::hinge
        D2[Inflection Point at distance L by sqrt of 2 from fixed end]:::inflection
        D3[Bottom Fixed - No Rotation, No Translation]:::fixed
        D1 --- D2
        D2 --- D3
    end

    classDef hinge fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fixed fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef free fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef inflection fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Functional Flowchart — Column Buckling Analysis Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Column Buckling Analysis] --> B{Determine End Conditions of Column}
    B -->|Both Hinged| C1[Le = L]
    B -->|Both Fixed| C2[Le = L / 2]
    B -->|Fixed Free| C3[Le = 2L]
    B -->|Fixed Hinged| C4[Le = L / sqrt of 2]

    C1 --> D[Calculate Cross Section Properties: I, A, k]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D

    D --> E{Compute Slenderness Ratio lambda = Le / k}
    E --> F{Is lambda greater than lambda critical?}
    F -->|Yes, Long Column| G[Apply Euler Formula: Pe = pi squared E I over Le squared]
    F -->|No, Short or Intermediate| H[Apply Empirical Formula: Rankine or IS Code]

    G --> I[Calculate Critical Stress: sigma cr = pi squared E over lambda squared]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Compare Applied Load P with Critical Load Pe]
    J --> K{Is P less than Pe by Safety Factor?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Column is SAFE]
    K -->|No| M[Column will FAIL - Revise Design]
    L --> N[End]
    M --> N
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Comparison of Effective Lengths

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REF["REFERENCE: PINNED PINNED COLUMN"]
        R1[Le = 1.0 L]:::base
    end

    subgraph SHORT["STRONGEST: BOTH ENDS FIXED"]
        S1[Le = 0.5 L]:::strong
    end

    subgraph MID["INTERMEDIATE: FIXED HINGED"]
        M1[Le approx 0.707 L]:::mid
    end

    subgraph WEAK["WEAKEST: FIXED FREE CANTILEVER"]
        W1[Le = 2.0 L]:::weak
    end

    classDef base fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef strong fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef mid fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef weak fill:#FF4500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Green = strongest buckling resistance, Red = weakest. As end fixity increases (more restraints), the effective length decreases and the column becomes stronger.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1: Define Euler's Crippling Load. State the assumptions made in Euler's theory.**
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Euler's Crippling Load is defined as the axial compressive load at which a long, slender, initially straight column suddenly deflects laterally (buckles), causing failure by elastic instability rather than by direct crushing. It is given by:
$$P_e = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$$

**Assumptions (1 Mark — list any 3):**
1. The column is perfectly straight and the load is truly axial.
2. The material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law.
3. Self-weight of the column is negligible.
4. Failure occurs purely by bending (no torsion).
5. End conditions are ideal (perfectly fixed or perfectly pinned).

---

### **Question A2: What is effective length? How does it vary for different end conditions?**
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model QP]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
The effective length of a column with given end conditions is the length of an *equivalent pinned-pinned column* that would buckle under the same critical load. It accounts for the rotational and translational restraints at the column ends.

**Variation (1.5 Marks):**

| End Condition | Effective Length $L_e$ |
|---|---|
| Both Ends Hinged | $L$ |
| Both Ends Fixed | $L/2$ |
| One End Fixed, Other Free | $2L$ |
| One End Fixed, Other Hinged | $L/\sqrt{2}$ |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question B (Option A): Derivation + Problem Solving**

**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, ESE Pattern]`

**Question (A):**
**(a)** Derive the expression for Euler's crippling load for a column hinged at both ends. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A mild steel column of length 4 m is hinged at both ends. The cross-section is a hollow circular tube with outer diameter 80 mm and inner diameter 60 mm. Take $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm². Calculate:
   (i) The Euler's crippling load.
   (ii) The slenderness ratio, given that the proportional limit is 200 N/mm². Comment on the applicability of Euler's formula. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)**

**Assumptions (2 Marks):**
- Column is initially perfectly straight and the load is concentric (axial).
- Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic.
- Self-weight is neglected; failure is by bending only.

**Setup (1 Mark):** Place origin at the bottom hinge. Let $y$ be the lateral deflection at distance $x$.

**Differential Equation (2 Marks):**
At any section, the bending moment due to axial load $P$ is $M = -Py$. Equating to the flexural rigidity equation:
$$EI \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = -Py \implies \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} + \frac{P}{EI}y = 0$$

**Solution and Boundary Conditions (1.5 Marks):**
Let $k^2 = \dfrac{P}{EI}$. Solution: $y = A\cos(kx) + B\sin(kx)$.
BCs: $y(0)=0 \Rightarrow A=0$; $y(L)=0 \Rightarrow \sin(kL)=0$.
For non-trivial solution: $kL = \pi \Rightarrow k = \pi/L$.

**Final Result (0.5 Mark):**
$$P_e = k^2 EI = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2}$$

---

#### **Solution to (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- Length $L = 4$ m $= 4000$ mm
- Outer diameter $D_o = 80$ mm, Inner diameter $D_i = 60$ mm
- $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm²
- Both ends hinged $\Rightarrow L_e = L = 4000$ mm
- Proportional limit $\sigma_p = 200$ N/mm²

**Step 1: Cross-Section Properties (1 Mark)**

Cross-sectional area:
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_o^2 - D_i^2\right) = \frac{\pi}{4}(80^2 - 60^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(6400 - 3600) = \frac{\pi}{4}(2800) = 700\pi \approx 2199.1 \text{ mm}^2$$

Moment of Inertia:
$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}\left(D_o^4 - D_i^4\right) = \frac{\pi}{64}(80^4 - 60^4)$$

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}(40960000 - 12960000) = \frac{\pi}{64}(28000000) = 437500\pi \approx 1374447 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2: Euler Crippling Load (2 Marks)**
$$P_e = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2} = \frac{(9.8696)(2 \times 10^5)(1374447)}{(4000)^2}$$

$$P_e = \frac{9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 1374447}{16 \times 10^6}$$

Numerator: $9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^5 = 1.97392 \times 10^6$
Numerator: $1.97392 \times 10^6 \times 1374447 = 2.7132 \times 10^{12}$

$$P_e = \frac{2.7132 \times 10^{12}}{16 \times 10^6} = 1.6958 \times 10^5 \text{ N} \approx 169.58 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Slenderness Ratio (2 Marks)**

Radius of gyration:
$$k = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{1374447}{2199.1}} = \sqrt{625.0} = 25.0 \text{ mm}$$

Slenderness ratio:
$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{k} = \frac{4000}{25.0} = 160$$

**Step 4: Critical Slenderness Ratio and Validity Check (2 Marks)**

$$\lambda_c = \pi\sqrt{\frac{E}{\sigma_p}} = \pi\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^5}{200}} = \pi\sqrt{1000} \approx 3.1416 \times 31.623 = 99.35$$

Since $\lambda = 160 > \lambda_c = 99.35$, the column is a **long column** and **Euler's formula is valid**. ✅

---

### **Question B (Option B): End Conditions + Numerical**

**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, ESE Pattern]`

**Question (B):**
**(a)** A 3 m long cast iron column has a circular cross-section of 100 mm diameter. It is fixed at one end and free at the other end. Calculate the crippling load using Euler's formula. Take $E = 1.2 \times 10^5$ N/mm². **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the crippling loads of the column in part (a) if the end conditions were:
   (i) Both ends fixed,
   (ii) Both ends hinged,
   (iii) One end fixed and the other pinned.
   Comment on the results. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to (a) — Fixed-Free Column (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $L = 3$ m $= 3000$ mm
- Diameter $D = 100$ mm $\Rightarrow$ radius $r = 50$ mm
- Fixed-free end condition $\Rightarrow L_e = 2L = 6000$ mm
- $E = 1.2 \times 10^5$ N/mm²

**Cross-section properties (2 Marks):**
$$I = \frac{\pi D^4}{64} = \frac{\pi (100)^4}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 10^8}{64} = 4.9087 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Euler Crippling Load (3 Marks):**
$$P_e = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2} = \frac{9.8696 \times 1.2 \times 10^5 \times 4.9087 \times 10^6}{(6000)^2}$$

Numerator: $9.8696 \times 1.2 \times 10^5 = 1.1844 \times 10^6$
Numerator: $1.1844 \times 10^6 \times 4.9087 \times 10^6 = 5.8134 \times 10^{12}$
Denominator: $36 \times 10^6 = 3.6 \times 10^7$

$$P_e = \frac{5.8134 \times 10^{12}}{3.6 \times 10^7} = 1.6148 \times 10^5 \text{ N} \approx 161.48 \text{ kN}$$

**Result (2 Marks):**
$$\boxed{P_e \approx 161.48 \text{ kN}}$$

---

#### **Solution to (b) — Comparison (7 Marks)**

Using the same column ($I = 4.9087 \times 10^6$ mm⁴, $E = 1.2 \times 10^5$ N/mm²), and $L = 3000$ mm:

| Case | $L_e$ | $P_e$ | Numerical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Both Fixed | $L/2 = 1500$ mm | $\dfrac{4\pi^2 EI}{L^2}$ | $4 \times 161.48 = 645.92$ kN |
| (ii) Both Hinged | $L = 3000$ mm | $\dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2}$ | $161.48 \times 4 = 645.92$... wait, recheck |

**Recomputation for Case (i) Both Fixed (2 Marks):**
$$P_e = \frac{4\pi^2 EI}{L^2} = \frac{4 \times 9.8696 \times 1.2 \times 10^5 \times 4.9087 \times 10^6}{(3000)^2}$$
$$= \frac{4 \times 5.8134 \times 10^{12}}{9 \times 10^6} = \frac{2.325 \times 10^{13}}{9 \times 10^6} = 2.584 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 2584 \text{ kN}$$

Wait — let me recompute carefully. From part (a), $\dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2}$ for $L=3000$ mm:

$$\frac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2} = \frac{9.8696 \times 1.2 \times 10^5 \times 4.9087 \times 10^6}{(3000)^2} = \frac{5.8134 \times 10^{12}}{9 \times 10^6} = 6.459 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 645.9 \text{ kN}$$

**Now computing all four cases (3 Marks):**

| Case | End Conditions | $L_e$ | $P_e$ Formula | Value (kN) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Original (a) | Fixed-Free | $2L$ | $\dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{4L^2}$ | $\dfrac{645.9}{4} = 161.5$ |
| (i) | Both Fixed | $L/2$ | $\dfrac{4\pi^2 EI}{L^2}$ | $4 \times 645.9 = 2583.5$ |
| (ii) | Both Hinged | $L$ | $\dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2}$ | $645.9$ |
| (iii) | Fixed-Hinged | $L/\sqrt{2}$ | $\dfrac{2\pi^2 EI}{L^2}$ | $2 \times 645.9 = 1291.8$ |

**Comments (2 Marks):**
- The **fixed-fixed** condition gives the highest crippling load — 16 times the fixed-free case.
- The **fixed-free** condition is the weakest — buckling capacity is just $\dfrac{1}{16}$ of the fixed-fixed case.
- Ranking: Fixed-Fixed > Fixed-Hinged > Both Hinged > Fixed-Free.
- Therefore, **end fixity plays a major role** in column design — even a small increase in restraint at the supports dramatically improves buckling resistance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the square on $L_e$:** Students often write $L_e = L/2$ but forget to square it in the denominator. The factor is $(\beta L)^2$, not $\beta L^2$.
> 2. **Mixing up end conditions:** Fixed-Free gives $L_e = 2L$ (weakest), NOT Fixed-Fixed. Memorize the table by the **physical shape of the buckled curve**.
> 3. **Ignoring the "minimum I" rule:** For asymmetric cross-sections (e.g., rectangular), use the **smaller** moment of inertia $I_{min}$, because buckling occurs about the **weaker axis**.
> 4. **Unit inconsistencies:** Convert all lengths to **mm** (or all to m) and keep $E$ in **consistent units** (N/mm² or N/m²). Mixing m and mm is the #1 reason for arithmetic errors in KTU papers.
> 5. **Applying Euler to short columns:** If $\lambda < \lambda_c$, **DO NOT** use Euler's formula — it overpredicts the strength. Use Rankine's or IS Code formulas instead.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY QUICK REVISION — Memorize Before Exam**

### **Core Formulas:**
- ✅ Euler's Critical Load: $\boxed{P_e = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L_e^2}}$
- ✅ Critical Stress: $\boxed{\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}}$
- ✅ Slenderness Ratio: $\boxed{\lambda = \dfrac{L_e}{k} = \dfrac{L_e}{\sqrt{I/A}}}$
- ✅ Critical Slenderness Ratio: $\boxed{\lambda_c = \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\sigma_p}}}$

### **Effective Length Table (Most Important):**
- ✅ Pinned–Pinned: $L_e = L$
- ✅ Fixed–Fixed: $L_e = L/2$ (strongest)
- ✅ Fixed–Free: $L_e = 2L$ (weakest)
- ✅ Fixed–Hinged: $L_e = L/\sqrt{2}$

### **Key Conceptual Points:**
- ✅ Euler's theory is **valid only for long, slender, elastic columns** ($\lambda > \lambda_c$).
- ✅ **Buckling** = instability failure (geometric), **Crushing** = material failure.
- ✅ Buckling occurs about the **axis with the minimum moment of inertia**.
- ✅ The **effective length** is the distance between consecutive inflection points (or between an end and the nearest inflection point).
- ✅ End fixity strongly influences column strength — proper end connections can **quadruple** the load capacity.

### **Numerical Problem Strategy:**
1. ✅ Identify end conditions → write $L_e$.
2. ✅ Compute $I$ and $A$ from the given cross-section.
3. ✅ Substitute into Euler's formula; **ensure unit consistency**.
4. ✅ Compute $\lambda$ and compare with $\lambda_c$ to validate Euler's applicability.

### **Frequently Asked Exam Traps:**
- ✅ For a hollow circular tube: $I = \dfrac{\pi (D_o^4 - D_i^4)}{64}$
- ✅ For a solid circular section: $I = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{64}$
- ✅ For a rectangular section: $I = \dfrac{bd^3}{12}$ (about the axis perpendicular to side $d$)
- ✅ Don't forget the $\pi^2 \approx 9.8696$ factor — examiners expect precise calculation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Euler Equation Derivation for Both Ends Hinged Only
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT402 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Euler Equation Derivation for Both Ends Hinged Only

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Euler's Critical Load (Crippling Load) for a Column with Both Ends Hinged:**
> The Euler critical load $(P_{cr})$ is the **axial compressive load** at which a long, slender, straight column — supported by frictionless **hinges (pins) at both ends** — first becomes unstable and buckles laterally into a deflected shape described by a half-sine wave. Mathematically, for both ends hinged:
>
> $$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} \, E \, I}{L^{2}}$$

where:
- $E$ → Modulus of Elasticity of the column material (in N/m² or N/mm²)
- $I$ → Minimum Area Moment of Inertia of the cross-section about the axis of buckling (in m⁴ or mm⁴)
- $L$ → Unsupported (actual) length of the column (in m or mm)
- $P_{cr}$ → Euler's critical / crippling / buckling load (in N)

A **hinged-hinged column** is the classical reference case in column-buckling theory. Because both supports permit free rotation but restrain translation, the column buckles into **exactly one half-sine wave** between the supports, and the **effective length** of the column equals its actual length.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Foot-Ruler" Analogy:**
> Take a thin, flat plastic ruler. Pinch one end firmly between your thumb and the edge of a table, and pinch the other end similarly. Now press the two ends of the ruler towards each other along its length (this is your axial compressive load $P$). If you press **gently**, the ruler stays perfectly straight. As you increase the pushing force, there is a particular value of force at which — suddenly — the ruler snaps sideways and bows into a smooth single curve. That exact force is the **Euler critical load**.
>
> - If you make the ruler longer, less force is needed to make it bow (notice $L^{2}$ in the denominator).
> - If you make the ruler stiffer (higher $E$) or thicker (higher $I$), it needs much more force to bow.
> - The two pinched ends behave as hinges: they cannot move sideways, but they can freely rotate.

This everyday experience captures the essence of Euler buckling: long, slender structural members under axial compression fail suddenly by *bending instability*, not by *crushing of material*.

## 1.3 Key Terminology for the Hinged-Hinged Case

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Engineering Metrics (Hinged-Hinged Column):**
> - **Effective length, $L_{e}$** = **$L$** (full length, because the buckled shape is exactly one half-sine wave)
> - **End-fixity coefficient, $\alpha$** = **$1.0$** (ratio $L_{e}/L$)
> - **Critical buckling stress, $\sigma_{cr}$** = **$\dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda^{2}}$**, where $\lambda$ is the slenderness ratio
> - **Slenderness ratio, $\lambda$** = **$\dfrac{L}{r_{min}}$**, where $r_{min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{min}}{A}}$ is the **minimum radius of gyration**

These quantities are *constants* the examiner expects you to recall and apply instantly.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Assumptions Underlying Euler's Theory

The derivation of $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} EI}{L^{2}}$ rests on several idealisations. **KTU examiners frequently award marks simply for stating these clearly.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Euler's Theoretical Assumptions (Hinged-Hinged Column):**
> 1. The column is initially **perfectly straight**, and the load is **perfectly axial (concentric)**.
> 2. The column material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's Law** (i.e., the stresses remain within the elastic limit).
> 3. The **self-weight** of the column is *negligible* compared with the applied load.
> 4. The cross-section is **uniform** along the entire length.
> 5. The **end-shortening** due to direct compression is negligible.
> 6. The column fails **only by buckling** (elastic instability) — not by crushing or yielding.
> 7. Both end supports are **frictionless hinges** — they restrain lateral translation but allow free rotation.

If any of these assumptions is violated (especially #2 — yielding, or #1 — a noticeable initial crookedness), Euler's formula **must be replaced** by empirical formulae such as **Rankine's Formula** or **IS 800 / IS 456 buckling curves**.

## 2.2 Why "Both Ends Hinged" Produces a Half-Sine Wave

Physically, the deformed shape of the column must satisfy two geometric constraints simultaneously:

| Constraint | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Lower end pinned at $x = 0$ | $y(0) = 0$ | Deflection is zero at the bottom hinge |
| Upper end pinned at $x = L$ | $y(L) = 0$ | Deflection is zero at the top hinge |
| Free rotation at both ends | $M(0) = M(L) = 0$, equivalently $y''(0) = y''(L) = 0$ | No bending resistance at the hinges |

The simplest smooth function that is zero at *both* $x = 0$ **and** $x = L$ is the sine function $y = \delta \, \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi x}{L}\right)$. Hence the **buckled shape of a hinged-hinged column is a single half-sine wave**, and $L_{e} = L$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Bank — Both Ends Hinged (Memorise for KTU)**

| \# | Concept | Governing Expression | Standard Unit | Critical Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Euler Critical Load (Hinged-Hinged) | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}}$ | N | Reference / parent formula |
| 2 | Effective Length | $L_{e} = \alpha \, L = 1.0 \, L = L$ | mm or m | $\alpha = 1$ for hinged-hinged |
| 3 | Critical Buckling Stress | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda^{2}} = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{A}$ | N/mm² | $\lambda$ = slenderness ratio |
| 4 | Slenderness Ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_{e}}{r_{min}} = \dfrac{L_{e}}{\sqrt{I_{min}/A}}$ | dimensionless | Use *minimum* $I$ |
| 5 | Radius of Gyration | $r = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}}$ | mm or m | Weakest axis governs |
| 6 | Condition for "Long" Column | $\lambda \geq \lambda_{c}$ (critical slenderness) | dimensionless | Below this → short column |
| 7 | Crippling Stress | $f_{c} = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{A} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda^{2}}$ | N/mm² | Must be $< f_{y}$ (yield) |
| 8 | Moment Equation (derivation) | $M_{x} = -P \, y$ | N·mm | Sign convention: concave towards original axis |
| 9 | Governing ODE | $\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} + k^{2} \, y = 0$, where $k^{2} = \dfrac{P}{EI}$ | — | Homogeneous linear ODE |
| 10 | Characteristic Equation | $kL = n\pi$, $n \in \mathbb{Z}^{+}$ | — | $n=1$ gives the smallest load |
| 11 | Buckled Shape (mode $n$) | $y_{n}(x) = \delta_{n} \sin\!\left(\dfrac{n\pi x}{L}\right)$ | mm or m | $n=1$ ⇒ single half-wave |
| 12 | Comparison with Other End Conditions | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{(\alpha L)^{2}}$ | N | $\alpha = 1, 0.5, 0.7, 2$ are the four standard values |

> [!NOTE]
> **A handy memory aid for $\alpha$ values:**
> - Hinged–Hinged → $\alpha = 1$ (1)
> - Fixed–Fixed → $\alpha = 0.5$ (1/2)
> - Fixed–Hinged → $\alpha \approx 0.7$ (≈ 1/√2)
> - Fixed–Free → $\alpha = 2$ (2)
> The pattern *1 — 1/2 — 1/√2 — 2* is a favourite KTU short-question topic.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where Does This Formula Actually Show Up in Practice?**
> - **Steel columns** in multi-storey buildings (typically designed as hinged at both ends, even when nominally fixed, because of the conservative nature of the $\alpha = 1$ assumption).
> - **Long pin-ended struts** in trusses, bridges, and transmission towers.
> - **Machine tool columns** and **boom components of cranes** that experience axial compression in service.
> - **Drill strings, push rods, and tie-bar systems** where compression-buckling is a failure mode.
> - **Aerospace slender members** — wing struts, fuselage longerons, rocket fairing supports.

The hinged-hinged case is the **baseline reference**: every other end condition is essentially a modification of this same derivation, with the only change being the **boundary-condition values** at the supports.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem Setup (Schematic Description)

Consider a **straight, prismatic, homogeneous column AB** of length $L$, with **frictionless hinges at both ends** A and B. An **axial compressive load $P$** is applied at the centroidal axis through the hinges. When $P$ reaches the critical value $P_{cr}$, the column buckles laterally and assumes a small, smooth deflected shape.

**Coordinate convention:**

- Place the origin **O** at the lower hinge A.
- Let the $x$-axis run along the *original* (undeflected) centroidal axis of the column, with $x$ increasing from A to B.
- Let the $y$-axis be the lateral deflection axis, measured positive in the direction of the bow.

> **Sign convention for bending moment:** We adopt the standard sign convention in which a positive bending moment produces **concave-upward** curvature. For the buckled shape, the column bends such that the concave side faces the original axis, so $M$ is taken as **negative**.

## 3.2 Free-Body Diagram of the Cut Section

Imagine cutting the column at an arbitrary section X located at a distance $x$ from the lower hinge A. The portion of the column lying to the **left** of section X is in equilibrium under:

1. The **horizontal axial load $P$** acting through the hinge A (along the original axis).
2. The **horizontal reaction $H$** at hinge A (perpendicular to $P$, this is the horizontal thrust developed at the pin).
3. The **internal bending moment $M_{x}$** acting at section X.
4. The **internal shear force $V_{x}$** acting at section X.

Taking moments about the section X (counter-clockwise positive) for the lower segment:

$$M_{x} \;=\; H \cdot x \;-\; P \cdot y$$

For equilibrium of the entire column against horizontal translation, the sum of horizontal forces gives $H = P \, \tan(\theta_{0})$, where $\theta_{0}$ is the very small slope at the lower end. For a **frictionless hinge at the lower end, the bending moment there must be zero** (since a pin cannot resist moment), so $M(0) = 0$ implies $H \cdot 0 - P \cdot y(0) = 0$, and the cleaner result is obtained by considering **column-like equilibrium** in the small-deflection limit:

$$M_{x} \;=\; -P \cdot y$$

> **Why the negative sign?** The deflected column's curvature is *concave* towards the original axis. Using the convention $M = EI \, \dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}}$, the deflection $y$ is positive (lateral) while the bending moment needed to bend the column back towards the axis is negative. The sign is consistent throughout.

## 3.3 Differential Equation of the Buckled Column

Apply the **Euler-Bernoulli beam equation** to the section:

$$M_{x} \;=\; EI \, \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}}$$

Equating this with the moment from the FBD:

$$EI \, \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} \;=\; -P \cdot y$$

Rearrange into the standard form of a linear, homogeneous, second-order ODE with constant coefficients:

$$\frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} \;+\; \frac{P}{EI} \cdot y \;=\; 0$$

Define the **wave-number constant**:

$$k^{2} \;=\; \frac{P}{EI}$$

Then the governing ODE simplifies to:

$$\frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} \;+\; k^{2} \, y \;=\; 0$$

> **Why the name "wave-number"?** Because the solution of this equation is sinusoidal — exactly the form of a standing wave on a stretched string — and $k$ measures the number of half-wavelengths fitting into a unit length.

## 3.4 General Solution of the ODE

The **characteristic equation** for $\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} + k^{2} y = 0$ is:

$$m^{2} + k^{2} = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad m = \pm \, i k$$

Therefore the **complementary (general) solution** is:

$$y(x) \;=\; A \cos(k x) \;+\; B \sin(k x)$$

where $A$ and $B$ are **arbitrary constants of integration** to be determined from the **boundary conditions** of the problem.

> **Important physical meaning:**
> - The first term $A \cos(kx)$ represents a **symmetric** mode (even function of $x$).
> - The second term $B \sin(kx)$ represents an **anti-symmetric** mode (odd function of $x$).

## 3.5 Application of Boundary Conditions (Hinged at Both Ends)

A **frictionless hinge at $x = x_{0}$** enforces **two** simultaneous conditions:

1. The **deflection** at the hinge is zero: $y(x_{0}) = 0$.
2. The **bending moment** at the hinge is zero: $M(x_{0}) = EI \, y''(x_{0}) = 0$.

For our hinged-hinged column, $x_{0} = 0$ and $x_{0} = L$:

**Boundary Condition 1:** At $x = 0$, $y(0) = 0$.

$$y(0) \;=\; A \cos(0) + B \sin(0) \;=\; A \cdot 1 + B \cdot 0 \;=\; A$$

So $A = 0$.

The deflection equation now reduces to:

$$y(x) \;=\; B \sin(k x)$$

**Boundary Condition 2:** At $x = L$, $y(L) = 0$.

$$y(L) \;=\; B \sin(k L) \;=\; 0$$

This is a **trigonometric equation in $kL$**. There are two possibilities:

- **Trivial solution:** $B = 0$. But $B = 0$ would mean $y(x) = 0$ for all $x$ — i.e., the column remains perfectly straight. This corresponds to a column *that has not buckled*, which is mathematically valid but **physically uninteresting** for the buckling problem.
- **Non-trivial solution:** $\sin(kL) = 0$, which requires $B \neq 0$.

> **This non-trivial condition is the *signature* of an eigenvalue problem** — buckling problems are always eigenvalue problems in disguise.

## 3.6 The Eigenvalue Equation

The condition $\sin(kL) = 0$ is satisfied only when:

$$k L \;=\; n \pi, \quad n \;=\; 1, \, 2, \, 3, \, \ldots$$

i.e., $k = \dfrac{n\pi}{L}$.

Substituting back $k^{2} = \dfrac{P}{EI}$:

$$\frac{P}{EI} \;=\; \left(\frac{n\pi}{L}\right)^{2} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad P \;=\; \frac{n^{2} \pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}}$$

The possible **buckling loads** are therefore:

$$P_{n} \;=\; \frac{n^{2} \pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

## 3.7 Identifying the Critical (Minimum) Load

A column will buckle at the **smallest load for which the buckled configuration is possible**, since the slightest increase above this load will cause it to deflect uncontrollably. Therefore:

$$P_{cr} \;=\; \min_{n \in \mathbb{Z}^{+}} P_{n} \;=\; P_{1} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}}$$

The corresponding **buckled shape** (eigenfunction) for $n = 1$ is:

$$y_{1}(x) \;=\; B \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{L}\right)$$

This is precisely **one half-sine wave** between the hinges, with maximum deflection (amplitude) at the mid-height $x = L/2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $n = 1$ is the physical answer, even though $n = 2, 3, \ldots$ also mathematically satisfy the boundary conditions?**
> Because the column will buckle as soon as the **first** instability mode is reached. Higher modes ($n \geq 2$) are mathematical possibilities but are *not* the threshold load — they would require an additional lateral-disturbance pattern matching their mode shape, and the structure has already collapsed by then.

## 3.8 Analytical Summary of the Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 1 (Equilibrium):} && M_{x} = -P \, y \\
&\textbf{Step 2 (Beam Bending Equation):} && EI \, \dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = M_{x} \\
&\textbf{Step 3 (Governing ODE):} && \dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} + k^{2} \, y = 0,\quad k^{2} = \dfrac{P}{EI} \\
&\textbf{Step 4 (General Solution):} && y(x) = A \cos(kx) + B \sin(kx) \\
&\textbf{Step 5 (BC: } y(0) = 0\textbf{):} && A = 0 \\
&\textbf{Step 6 (BC: } y(L) = 0\textbf{):} && B \sin(kL) = 0 \\
&\textbf{Step 7 (Non-trivial eigenvalue):} && \sin(kL) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; kL = n\pi \\
&\textbf{Step 8 (Substitute } k = n\pi / L\textbf{):} && P_{n} = \dfrac{n^{2} \pi^{2} EI}{L^{2}} \\
&\textbf{Step 9 (Critical / minimum load, } n = 1\textbf{):} && \boxed{\,P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} EI}{L^{2}}\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.9 Cross-Reference: Connection to Critical Stress

Once $P_{cr}$ is known, the corresponding **average compressive stress** in the column at the moment of buckling is:

$$\sigma_{cr} \;=\; \frac{P_{cr}}{A} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} E I}{A L^{2}} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} E}{(L/r_{min})^{2}} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda^{2}}$$

This is the **classical Euler stress formula** in terms of the slenderness ratio $\lambda$. It expresses the same result in a form that is more directly comparable to the material's yield stress $f_{y}$ for checking the validity of the elastic-buckling assumption.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hinged-Hinged Column — Buckled Configuration (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

> **Note:** The following Mermaid diagram provides a clear, block-level functional depiction of the hinged-hinged column in its buckled state and the chain of internal forces and moments. This is in lieu of a hand-drawn physical schematic, ensuring the figure renders reliably across KTU-compatible markdown viewers.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["HINGE SUPPORT A: x = 0, y = 0, M = 0"] --> nodeB["COLUMN SEGMENT length dx"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["SECTION X at distance x from A"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["INTERNAL FORCES: Axial P, Shear Vx, Moment Mx"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["BENDING EQUATION: Mx = -P times y"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["ODE: d2y by dx2 + k2 y = 0, where k2 = P over EI"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["SOLUTION: y = A cos kx + B sin kx"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["BOUNDARY CONDITION y at 0 equals 0 gives A = 0"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["BOUNDARY CONDITION y at L equals 0 gives sin kL = 0"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["EIGENVALUE: kL = n pi, n = 1, 2, 3 ..."]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["BUCKLING LOAD: Pn = n squared pi squared EI over L squared"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["CRITICAL LOAD: n = 1 gives Pcr = pi squared EI over L squared"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["HINGE SUPPORT B: x = L, y = 0, M = 0"]
    nodeM --> nodeN["BUCKLED SHAPE: y1 = delta sin pi x over L"]
    nodeN --> nodeO["MAXIMUM DEFLECTION at mid-height x = L by 2"]
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow of the Mathematical Derivation

```mermaid
graph LR
    inputA["Axial Load P Applied at Hinges"] --> equilibrium["Moment Equilibrium: Mx equals -P y"]
    equilibrium --> beamEq["Euler-Bernoulli: EI y double-prime equals Mx"]
    beamEq --> ode["Linear ODE: y double-prime + k2 y = 0"]
    ode --> genSol["General Solution: y equals A cos kx + B sin kx"]
    genSol --> bcApply["Apply BCs: y equals 0 at x equals 0 and x equals L"]
    bcApply --> eigenCond["Eigenvalue Equation: kL equals n pi"]
    eigenCond --> critLoad["Critical Load: Pcr equals pi squared EI over L squared"]
    critLoad --> shapeFunc["Buckled Shape: y1 equals delta sin pi x over L"]
    shapeFunc --> verify["Verify BCs are satisfied at both hinges"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams during revision:**
> - The first diagram traces the *physical* path from the support at A to the support at B, marking every important internal-action variable.
> - The second diagram traces the *mathematical* path from the applied load to the final Euler formula. Together, they form a complete mental picture of the derivation — useful for last-minute KTU exam-day revision.

## 4.3 Diagram Interpretation Notes

- **nodeA and nodeM** represent the **boundary supports**. The labels explicitly state that at each hinge, both deflection $y$ and bending moment $M$ vanish.
- **nodeD** is the cross-section of the column where the FBD is cut; it shows the trio of internal resultants (axial $P$, shear $V_{x}$, bending $M_{x}$).
- **nodeE to nodeL** form the derivation chain — each block represents one of the algebraic transitions explained in SECTION 3.
- **nodeO** highlights the **physical location of the maximum lateral deflection**, which occurs at the mid-span for a hinged-hinged column buckled in its first mode.
